title: SteelNet: Multimodal Representation Learning for Industrial Process Optimization
name: steelnet-multimodal-industrial-optimization
short_hypothesis: A multimodal representation learning framework, named SteelNet, which incorporates cross-modal alignment and modality dropout strategies, can enable robust and consistent learning from heterogeneous industrial sensor data, even when some data modalities are missing, thereby improving anomaly detection and process optimization.
related_work: Extensive research has applied multimodal deep learning to predictive maintenance using combinations of CNNs, LSTMs, transformers, and GNNs for various data types like imagery, time-series, and text. Specific applications in steelmaking have used CNN+DNN for temperature control and reinforcement learning for process optimization. However, two key challenges persist: achieving robustness to missing or corrupted modalities and aligning model predictions with actionable operational metrics like equipment availability. Existing frameworks often perform well under ideal conditions but struggle with the modality dropout, occlusion, and noise common in real-world industrial plants.
abstract: Steel rolling mills must continuously monitor various sensors, but harsh industrial conditions often lead to sensor failures and incomplete data, making it challenging for traditional systems to detect problems. To this end, we propose SteelNet, a multimodal representation learning framework designed for robust learning from various industrial sensor data. SteelNet incorporates cross-modal alignment and modality dropout strategies that enable consistent representation learning even when modalities are partially missing. The core problem being solved is improving equipment availability and optimizing process parameters. This framework allows for the early detection of critical events by combining information from multiple sensors and effectively handling missing data. By improving the reliability of anomaly detection and predictive insights, SteelNet aims to strengthen fault tolerance and support better decision-making in process optimization.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's primary capability in identifying steel defects from multimodal sensor data.
experiments.Method: SteelNet will be trained on a novel multimodal dataset constructed from the Severstal Steel Defect Detection competition data, augmented with synthetically generated process parameters simulating realistic industrial monitoring. The task is to classify steel surface images into one of four defect classes or a non-defective class.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's ability to identify which process parameters are most influential for each type of defect, providing interpretable insights for process optimization.
experiments.Method: The parameter attribution network within SteelNet will be used to generate importance scores for each input process parameter corresponding to a predicted defect class. The predicted attributions will be compared against a physics-based ground truth.

experiments.Description: To test the robustness of the SteelNet framework under simulated sensor failure conditions.
experiments.Method: The model's performance will be evaluated while applying an industrial modality dropout strategy. Sensor-specific failure probabilities will be used to randomly drop out input modalities at varying rates (e.g., 10%, 20%, 30%, 50%) during testing.

experiments.Description: To analyze the contribution of each key component of the SteelNet architecture to the overall performance.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted by systematically removing individual components from the full SteelNet model: the self-attention mechanism, the attribution loss, the modality dropout strategy, and the physics constraints. Each ablated model variant will be trained and evaluated on the primary defect classification and parameter attribution tasks.

risk_factors: Synthetic Parameter Generation: The process parameters are computationally generated based on domain knowledge rather than measured from actual industrial sensors.
Defect Intensity Scoring Methodology: The intensity scores are derived from a regression model trained on pixel-level segmentation masks rather than direct sensor measurements of defect severity.
Temporal Dynamics Absence: Industrial monitoring systems typically involve continuous time-series sensor streams, but our dataset represents static snapshots without temporal dependencies.
Artificial Class Balancing: The decision to limit each defect class to 200 samples creates artificial balance that does not reflect realistic defect occurrence rates in industrial settings.
Limited Real-World Validation: The framework requires validation in actual industrial settings with real sensor data and operational constraints.

title: On the Generalization of Models Trained with SGD: Information-Theoretic Bounds and Implications
name: tighter-sgd-gen-bounds
short_hypothesis: By removing the local gradient sensitivity term from existing information-theoretic generalization bounds for SGD, it is possible to derive new, tighter bounds. These improved bounds are expected to better explain the generalization behavior of neural networks and can inspire a new, simple regularization scheme called Gaussian Model Perturbation (GMP) that aims to reduce the flatness term of the bounds.
related_work: Prior work, notably Neu et al. (2021), established information-theoretic generalization bounds for SGD-trained models. These bounds consist of a 'trajectory term' and a 'flatness term'. The trajectory term accumulates local gradient sensitivity and gradient dispersion over training. However, the local gradient sensitivity term does not vanish during training, which can make the bound loose. This paper aims to develop a tighter bound by removing this local sensitivity term, which should provide better insights into SGD's generalization properties and lead to new regularization techniques.
abstract: This paper follows up on a recent work of Neu et al. (2021) and presents some new information-theoretic upper bounds for the generalization error of machine learning models, such as neural networks, trained with SGD. We apply these bounds to analyzing the generalization behaviour of linear and two-layer ReLU networks. Experimental study of these bounds is intended to provide some insights on the SGD training of neural networks. They also point to a new and simple regularization scheme.

experiments.Description: To verify the proposed generalization bound (from Eq. 3) by comparing its estimated value against the empirical generalization gap under different conditions.
experiments.Method: An MLP and an AlexNet will be trained on MNIST and CIFAR-10, respectively. The experiment will involve varying the network width and the level of label noise in the data. The bound will be estimated using PyTorch, BackPack, and PyHessian libraries.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Estimated bound value', 'Empirical generalization gap (difference between training and testing loss)']

experiments.Description: To experimentally investigate the dynamics of gradient dispersion during the training of neural networks and its relationship with generalization.
experiments.Method: An MLP will be trained on MNIST and an AlexNet on CIFAR-10, with fixed learning rate, batch size, and weight initialization. The experiment will be conducted with various levels of label noise.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Gradient dispersion', 'Training accuracy', 'Testing accuracy (plotted across training epochs)']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether a dynamic gradient clipping scheme, inspired by the new bounds, can reduce the generalization error by controlling the trajectory term.
experiments.Method: A dynamic gradient clipping scheme will be applied during the training of MLP on MNIST and AlexNet on CIFAR-10, specifically on datasets containing label noise. The performance will be compared against training without the clipping scheme.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Gaussian Model Perturbation (GMP) regularization scheme against other state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: VGG16 and PreActResNet18 models will be trained on SVHN, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets. GMP will be compared against several baselines: ERM (no regularization), Dropout, Label Smoothing (L.S.), Flooding, MixUp, Adversarial Training (Adv. Tr.), and Sharpness-Aware Minimization (AMP).

risk_factors: Unfortunately, the current design of the dynamic gradient clipping algorithm does not provide a significant improvement for models trained on a clean dataset (without label noise).

There are yet promising directions for further improving these bounds, for example, via exploiting conditional mutual information bounds (Haghifam et al. (2020)), strong data processing inequalities (Wang et al. (2021a)), and the relationship between between the trajectory term and the flatness term.

title: BESPOKE: Benchmark for Search-Augmented Large Language Model Personalization via Diagnostic Feedback
name: bespoke-benchmark
short_hypothesis: A new benchmark, BESPOKE, constructed from long-term, authentic human chat and search histories paired with fine-grained diagnostic feedback, will enable a more realistic and systematic evaluation of personalization in search-augmented LLMs. An LLM-based evaluator using this data can be designed to effectively assess personalization quality along multiple dimensions.
related_work: Current evaluation methods for search-augmented LLMs, like RAG-QA Arena and Search Arena, primarily focus on factuality and general preference judgments, but neglect personalization. Existing personalized LLM evaluation benchmarks, such as LaMP-QA, rely on data from constrained domains like StackExchange (QA-style interactions) or use synthetic personas, which do not reflect realistic, long-term, and diverse user activities. This creates a gap for a benchmark that can systematically evaluate personalization in systems like ChatGPT and Gemini, which leverage rich user histories composed of both chat and search interactions.
abstract: Search-augmented large language models (LLMs) remain insufficient for fully addressing diverse user needs, where the same query can reflect different intents across users. While recent systems like ChatGPT and Gemini attempt personalization by leveraging user histories, systematic evaluation of this capability is under-explored. To address this gap, we propose BESPOKE, a realistic benchmark for evaluating personalization in search-augmented LLMs. BESPOKE is designed to be both realistic, by collecting authentic chat and search histories directly from humans, and diagnostic, by pairing responses with fine-grained preference scores and feedback. The benchmark is constructed through long-term, deeply engaged human annotation, where annotators contribute their own histories, author queries, and evaluate responses with scores and diagnostic feedback.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed LLM-based evaluator (Ep) by assessing its alignment with human judgments on personalization quality.
experiments.Method: A meta-evaluation dataset of 300 Response-Judgment (R-J) pairs is used. The proposed GPT-5-based evaluator, which uses both scores and feedback as context ('w/ Feedback'), is compared against two baselines: a generic evaluator without personalization context ('w/o Personal.') and an evaluator that uses only scores as context ('w/o Feedback').
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Score Alignment (Pearson/Spearman correlation)', 'Feedback Alignment (Accuracy of a meta-evaluator judging semantic equivalence)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of leveraging user history on the personalization performance of search-augmented LLMs and to determine the most effective method for constructing user context.
experiments.Method: Six search-augmented LLMs (GPT-4o-search, o3-search, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Perplexity-sonar, Perplexity-sonar-reasoning) are evaluated on the BESPOKE benchmark. The experiment varies four aspects of user context construction: 1) history usage (with vs. without), 2) query awareness (static vs. dynamic context), 3) history selection (full vs. query-relevant), and 4) context form (raw vs. profile).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Personalization Scores (Need Alignment, Content Depth, Tone, Explanation Style)', 'Gold Information Coverage (Recall)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate efficient methods for retrieving relevant user history sessions for personalization, comparing query expansion techniques against a baseline.
experiments.Method: A bi-encoder retriever (Stella-V5-1.5B) is used for history retrieval on the BESPOKE dataset. The performance of the original user query is compared against two query expansion methods: CoT (Chain-of-Thought) expansion and Pseudo-history expansion. Ground truth is established by designating oracle histories using an LLM.

experiments.Description: To isolate the impact of web-search quality on the personalization of model responses.
experiments.Method: An experiment is designed where the model's built-in search tool is disabled and replaced with the ground-truth 'gold information' from the BESPOKE dataset. This condition ('Personalized + Gold Information') is compared against the standard setting where models use their own web search ('Personalized').

experiments.Description: To investigate how the performance of reasoning and non-reasoning models changes as the quality of retrieved information deteriorates.
experiments.Method: A controlled comparison is conducted using Sonar and Sonar-Reasoning models. Noise is systematically injected into the gold information provided to the models at varying ratios (0%, 30%, 50%, 70%) to simulate deteriorating search quality.

risk_factors: While our dataset is relatively small in scale due to its reliance on deeply engaged human annotation, it provides rich user histories that reflect authentic and realistic preferences.

While our GPT-5-based evaluator shows strong empirical performance, this reliance on a proprietary model has two important limitations. It may reduce generalizability across model families, and it incurs non-trivial computational cost for running the benchmark end-to-end.
While leveraging LLMs can directly assess query-history relevance as discussed in Section 4.2, they are prohibitively costly and impractical to scale.
Despite these advances, none of the models in any setting surpass an average score of 60. This ceiling highlights that personalization in realistic environments remains a significant challenge.

title: Massive Memorization with Hundreds of Trillions of Parameters for Sequential Transducer Generative Recommenders
name: vista-two-stage-attention-recommender
short_hypothesis: Decomposing the traditional target attention mechanism into two stages—(1) user history summarization into a few hundred cached tokens, and (2) candidate item attention to these cached tokens—will enable recommender models to scale to lifelong user histories (up to one million items) while keeping downstream training and inference costs fixed.
related_work: Modern recommendation systems use two main approaches for handling long user sequences. The first, full user sequence modeling (e.g., HSTU), suffers from enormous computational costs and latency, making it challenging for industrial-scale deployment. The second, target-specific sequence sampling (e.g., SIM, TWIN), reduces cost but introduces a performance gap compared to full-sequence attention and has computational costs that increase linearly with the number of candidate items. Existing models do not adequately address these industrial scalability issues for ultra-long user histories.
abstract: Modern large-scale recommendation systems rely heavily on user interaction history sequences. While scaling to ultra-long user histories (10k to 100k items) improves performance, it creates significant challenges in latency, QPS, and GPU cost. Existing models do not adequately address these industrial scalability issues. We propose a novel two-stage modeling framework, Virtual Sequential Target Attention (VISTA), which decomposes traditional target attention into two distinct stages: (1) user history summarization into a few hundred tokens; followed by (2) candidate item attention to those tokens. These summarization token embeddings are then cached and utilized as sequence features for downstream model training and inference. This design is intended to enable VISTA to scale to lifelong user histories while keeping downstream training and inference costs fixed.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the VISTA attention mechanism against several baseline models on public recommendation datasets.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained and evaluated for click-through rate prediction on the Amazon-Electronics and KuaiRand-1K datasets using the FuxiCTR framework. VISTA will be compared against baselines including DIN, TTSN, MHA, SASRec, and HSTU. An ablation, VISTA-w/o-QLA, will also be tested.

experiments.Description: To evaluate model performance on longer sequences using a simplified dataset derived from production data.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained for CTR prediction on the 'Minimal Production dataset', which has sequences up to 2,000 items. VISTA will be compared against the same set of baselines: DIN, TTSN, MHA, SASRec, and HSTU.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed VISTA model with the baseline production model (HSTU) in an offline setting on an industrial-scale dataset, and to conduct ablation studies on VISTA's components.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a large-scale industrial dataset with an average sequence length of 7,000. The baseline is the HSTU model. VISTA will be evaluated in its full configuration and in several ablated versions: VISTA-128D (reduced embedding dimension), VISTA-64Seed (fewer seeds), and VISTA-w/o-Recon (without generative reconstruction loss).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the proposed quasi-linear attention (QLA) mechanism by varying sequence length.
experiments.Method: On the Minimal Production dataset, VISTA with QLA will be compared to VISTA without QLA across different sequence lengths (256, 512, 1024, 2000). On the industrial dataset, a 3-layer model on 6,000-length sequences will be compared to a 5-layer model on 16,000-length sequences.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the VISTA model in a live production environment through an online A/B test.
experiments.Method: An online A/B test will be conducted on a production video recommendation system. The baseline is the HSTU model, and the test group will be the HSTU model with the VISTA module added.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['C-Task (main consumption task)', 'O1-Task (online onboarding metric)', 'O2-Task (online onboarding metric)', 'Inference GPU resource (QPS)']

risk_factors: This approach significantly reduces the computational complexity for downstream models, especially during inference, at the cost of additional storage.
In practice, this is a worthwhile trade-off, as the cost of GPU computation remains multiple orders of magnitude higher than the cost of storage.

VISTA's practical applicability is underscored by its resilience to slight de-synchronization between its stages and its ability to approximate complex transformer architectures without their substantial computational cost.

title: Distribution Shift Detection for Deep Neural Networks
name: Coverage-Based Distribution Shift Detection
short_hypothesis: A distribution shift in a stream of data fed to a deep neural network can be detected efficiently by deriving a tight generalization coverage bound from in-distribution data using selective prediction principles. A systematic violation of this pre-computed bound by the empirical coverage of a new window of data indicates a distribution shift, without needing to perform computationally expensive two-sample tests against the full training dataset.
related_work: Standard methods for distribution shift detection often use a two-step process: dimensionality reduction of the data (e.g., using a model's embeddings) followed by a two-sample statistical test like the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test or Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). These methods, termed black-box shift detection (BBSD), are computationally intensive as they require processing the entire detection-training set for each new window of test data. This makes them impractical for very large datasets. The proposed approach aims to be significantly more efficient by summarizing the training data into a small set of coverage bounds.
abstract: To deploy and operate deep neural models in production, the quality of their predictions, which might be contaminated benignly or manipulated maliciously by input distributional deviations, must be monitored and assessed. Specifically, we study the case of monitoring the healthy operation of a deep neural network (DNN) receiving a stream of data, with the aim of detecting input distributional deviations over which the quality of the network's predictions is potentially damaged. Using selective prediction principles, we propose a distribution deviation detection method for DNNs. The proposed method is derived from a tight coverage generalization bound computed over a sample of instances drawn from the true underlying distribution. Based on this bound, our detector continuously monitors the operation of the network over a test window and fires off an alarm whenever a deviation is detected.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that the proposed coverage bound holds for in-distribution data and is violated as expected when a distribution shift occurs, using a controlled synthetic data environment.
experiments.Method: A simple binary linear classifier will be trained to discriminate between two 2D Gaussians (in-distribution, G_0). Shifted distributions will be created by moving the centers of the Gaussians closer (G_) or farther apart (G+). The detector, using softmax response as its confidence function, will be trained on G_0. Test windows will be drawn from G_0, G_, and G+ to check if the lower and a symmetric upper coverage bound are violated as hypothesized.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The relationship between the empirical coverages, the computed bounds, and the desired coverages will be plotted to visually inspect for bound violations. The p-value from the detection test will also be considered.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed coverage-based detection method against baseline methods on the CIFAR-10 dataset under various simulated distribution shifts.
experiments.Method: Pretrained ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 models will be used. The in-distribution (ID) data is CIFAR-10. Distribution shifts will be simulated using Rotations, Gaussian noise, the CIFAR-100 dataset, the SVHN dataset, and adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD, CW). The proposed method will be tested with two confidence-rate functions: Softmax Response (Ours-SR) and Soft-Entropy (Ours-Ent). These will be compared against four baselines: KS-BBSD-S, KS-BBSD-E, MMD-BBSD-S, and MMD-BBSD-E.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Area Under an Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC), Area under the Precision-Recall curve for in-distribution data (AUPR-Tr), and Area under the Precision-Recall curve for out-of-distribution data (AUPR-Te). Aggregated versions of these metrics over all window sizes will also be used (Agg-AUROC, Agg-AUPR-Tr, Agg-AUPR-Te).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed detection method on the large-scale ImageNet dataset against the best-performing baseline, considering various types of distribution shifts and different levels of contamination in the test window.
experiments.Method: A pretrained EfficientNet-B0 model will be used. The in-distribution (ID) data is ImageNet. Distribution shifts will be simulated using Rotations, ImageNet-O, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-C datasets, and adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD, CW). The experiment will also vary the proportion of shifted instances (p = 1, 2/3, 1/3) in the test window. The proposed method (Ours-SR and Ours-Ent) will be compared against the best baseline from the previous experiment, KS-BBSD-S.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Area Under an Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC), Area under the Precision-Recall curve for in-distribution data (AUPR-Tr), and Area under the Precision-Recall curve for out-of-distribution data (AUPR-Te). Aggregated versions of these metrics will also be reported.

risk_factors: Although we only considered classification, our method can be extended to regression using an appropriate confidence-rate function such as the MC-dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016).
Extensions to other tasks, such as object detection and segmentation, would be very interesting.
It would also be interesting to examine other types of shift benchmarks such as (Koh et al., 2021).
In our method, the information from the multiple coverage bounds was aggregated by averaging, but it is plausible that other statistics or weighted averages could provide more effective detections.
Finally, an interesting open question is whether one can benefit from using outlier/adversarial detection techniques combined with population-based detection techniques (as discussed here).

title: Learning Altruistic Behaviours in Reinforcement Learning without External Rewards
name: learning-altruism-by-maximizing-choice
short_hypothesis: An agent can learn to behave altruistically towards another agent, without knowing its goals or receiving external rewards, by maximizing the other agent's 'choice', defined as the variety of states it can reach in the future.
related_work: Typical methods for achieving altruistic behavior in agents rely on supervision, such as providing ground-truth actions, carefully crafted rewards, or imitation learning from expert demonstrations. These approaches often do not scale well and require explicit knowledge of the other agent's goals. While concepts like instrumental convergence and empowerment are related, this work aims to develop and experimentally evaluate an unsupervised, task-agnostic approach for learning altruism purely from observation, without needing privileged access to an environment simulator or expert data.
abstract: Can artificial agents learn to assist others in achieving their goals without knowing what those goals are? Generic reinforcement learning agents could be trained to behave altruistically by rewarding them for benefiting other agents, but this assumes the other agents' goals are known. However, explicit knowledge of other agents' goals is often difficult to acquire. Thus, it is beneficial to develop agents that do not depend on external supervision and learn altruistic behaviour in a task-agnostic manner. We propose to act altruistically towards other agents by giving them more choice. We formalize this concept and propose an altruistic agent that learns to increase the choices another agent has by preferring to maximize the number of states that the other agent can reach in its future.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed altruistic behavior in simple, discrete grid worlds with controllable gates, using two scenarios: one where the altruistic agent must open a door for a 'leader' agent, and another where it must avoid blocking the leader's path to a goal.
experiments.Method: The experiments are set in two custom gridworld environments: 'Door Scenario' and 'Dead End Scenario'. An altruistic agent is trained with Q-Learning to maximize the leader agent's choice, estimated using three proposed metrics: Discrete Choice (DC), Entropic Choice (EC), and Immediate Choice (IC). The leader agent is pretrained. The proposed method will be compared to a supervised baseline, an 'Assistance via Empowerment (AvE)' baseline, and a random-policy baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The performance will be measured by the binary success of the leader agent in reaching its goal. The emergent behavior of the altruistic agent will be analyzed under different hyperparameter settings for look-ahead horizon (n) and discount factor (γa).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed altruistic agents in a more complex, fully-observable multi-agent environment that requires cooperation to succeed.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Level-Based Foraging (LBF) environment. A pretrained 'leader' agent is paired with an 'altruistic' agent trained from scratch. The altruistic agent is trained using Deep Q-Learning (DQL) with a reward signal based on maximizing the leader's Immediate Choice (IC). This approach will be compared to a supervised shared-reward baseline, a random-policy baseline, and a maximum-entropy (MEPOL) unsupervised baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Performance will be measured by the normalized reward achieved by the leader agent. The internal reward of the altruistic agent (the estimated choice of the leader) will also be tracked to see if it correlates with the leader's success.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the capabilities of altruistic agents in a complex environment with continuous state spaces, where they must protect a leader agent from adversaries.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the multi-agent 'Tag' environment. Adversaries and a leader agent are pretrained. Three additional altruistic agents are then introduced and trained using MADDPG to maximize the leader's Immediate Choice (IC). This will be compared against several baselines: 'None' (no altruistic agents), 'Random' (altruistic agents act randomly), 'Cage' (altruistic agents are only penalized for leaving the environment), and 'Supervised' (altruistic agents receive the same sparse reward as the leader).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Performance is defined as the number of times the leader agent is caught by the adversaries per episode (lower is better).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed altruistic method in a non-spatial, non-navigation environment involving resource distribution.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a resource-based MDP environment with two 'consumer' agents with different resource preferences and one altruistic agent. The altruistic agent is trained with Q-Learning to distribute resources by maximizing the consumers' choice (estimated via IC, EC, or DC), without knowing their reward functions. This will be compared to a supervised agent that knows the consumers' reward functions and a random-policy baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Performance is measured as the average number of failed consumption attempts by the consumer agents.

risk_factors: This failure case can be prevented by having a large enough horizon n and discount factor γa, analogously to the selection of the temperature hyperparameter in maximum entropy single-agent RL.

We remark that altruistic proxy objectives do not yet come with strict guarantees of optimizing for other agents' rewards, and identify failure modes (sec. 4.1) which are hyperparameter-dependent, and which we hope provide interesting starting points for future work.

title: Wide Attention is the Way Forward for Transformers
name: transformer-aspect-ratio
short_hypothesis: An alternative approach to building deep Transformers is to build wider, shallower attention-based models. By systematically changing the model aspect ratio (balancing layers vs. heads per layer) while keeping the total number of attention heads constant, it is hypothesized that wider, single-layer models can equal or outperform their deeper counterparts on NLP tasks when trained from scratch.
related_work: Prior work on Transformer architectures has primarily focused on designing more efficient attention mechanisms or using Network Architecture Search (NAS) to find optimal operator combinations. While widening networks has been shown to be effective for CNNs, and explored to some extent in Transformers, little research has systematically investigated the trade-off between the number of attention layers and the number of attention heads per layer (the 'model aspect ratio'). This coarse-grained design space is not commonly explored in NAS and represents a research gap in understanding optimal Transformer configurations.
abstract: The Transformer is an extremely powerful and prominent deep learning architecture. In this work, we challenge the commonly held belief in deep learning that going deeper is better, and propose an alternative approach that is building wider attention Transformers. We plan to demonstrate that wide single layer Transformer models can typically equal or sometimes outperform deeper ones in a variety of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks when both are trained from scratch. The impact of changing the model aspect ratio on Transformers will be studied systematically. This ratio balances the number of layers and the number of attention heads per layer, while keeping the total number of attention heads and all other hyperparameters constant. We will also evaluate how wide models affect memory footprint, inference speed, and interpretability.

experiments.Description: To systematically investigate the influence of the 'model aspect ratio' on Transformer performance across a variety of NLP tasks and attention mechanisms, when trained from scratch.
experiments.Method: Models will be evaluated on four text classification tasks: IMDb Token Level, IMDb Byte Level, Listops, and Document Matching. For each task, a set of Transformer architectures will be created by varying the number of layers (L) and heads per layer (H) while keeping the total number of heads (L x H) constant. For example, configurations will range from a deep model (e.g., 6 layers, 8 heads) to a wide model (e.g., 1 layer, 48 heads). This comparison will be performed across 10 different attention mechanisms: dot-product, BigBird, Linear Transformer, Linformer, Local attention, Longformer, Performer, Sinkhorn, Sparse Transformer, and Synthesizer. All models will be trained from scratch without pretrained embeddings or parameters.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test accuracy', 'Model size (number of parameters)', 'Inference latency (on CPU and GPU)', 'Interpretability (via inspection of attention weights)']

experiments.Description: To test whether the findings on model aspect ratio from NLP tasks generalize to the domain of computer vision.
experiments.Method: The Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT-V2-B1) will be used as a baseline on the CIFAR10 dataset. Wider variants of this model will be created by replacing the two attention layers at each of the four stages with a single wide attention layer, thereby reducing depth while increasing width. Two wide variants will be tested: one matching the baseline's total heads, and another with more heads and a larger embedding size to be a closer match in model size.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of models that use a uniform mixture of different attention mechanisms in both wide and deep configurations.
experiments.Method: Models will be constructed using an equal mix of 8 efficient attention mechanisms (BigBird, Linear Transformer, Linformer, Local, Longformer, Performer, Sparse Transformer, and Synthesizer). These mixed-attention models will be tested in both deep (multi-layer) and wide (single-layer) variants on the NLP tasks. The number of heads for each attention type will be scaled to match the total head count of the homogeneous models.

risk_factors: Whilst our study considers the effects of attention width across many attention mechanisms and various tasks, it remains to be seen whether these results hold for much larger models on much larger datasets, for example masked langauage modelling.
As we are not using pretrainining and pretrained embedings, their results are not directly comparable to our own.

The usage of a single wide attention layer in vision Transformers is constrained by the fact that the majority of these vision Transformers still use pooling or convolution layers before the attention.

title: Schrodinger's Memory: Large Language Models
name: schrodingers-memory
short_hypothesis: The memory mechanism in Large Language Models (LLMs) can be explained by the Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT), where the Transformer architecture functions as a dynamic UAT model that adaptively fits outputs based on inputs. This memory is termed 'Schrödinger's memory' because it only becomes observable when queried. A model's memory ability, assessed by its capacity to recall information, can serve as an objective measure of its overall capabilities.
related_work: Previous research on LLM memory has primarily focused on two approaches: expanding the context length to provide more information across long sequences, and integrating external memory storage systems for retrieval and updates. However, these studies have not fully explained the intrinsic memory mechanisms within the models themselves, lacking a fundamental theoretical framework and resulting in a vague definition of memory.
abstract: With the development of Large Language Models (LLMs), their language capabilities are becoming increasingly comparable to those of humans. But do LLMs have memory? Based on current practice, LLMs do appear to exhibit memory. So, what is the underlying mechanism of this memory? Previous research lacked a deep exploration of LLMs' memory capabilities and the underlying theory. In this paper, we use the Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT) to explain the memory mechanism in LLMs. We also conduct experiments to verify the memory capabilities of various LLMs, proposing a new method to assess their abilities based on the memory ability. We argue that LLM memory operates like Schrödinger's memory, meaning that it only becomes observable when a specific memory is queried. We can only determine if the model retains a memory based on its output in response to the query; otherwise, it remains indeterminate.

experiments.Description: To verify the memory capabilities of various LLMs by fine-tuning them on a poem memorization task and testing their ability to recite poems from minimal input cues.
experiments.Method: Models from the Qwen series (Qwen1.5-0.5B-Chat, Qwen2-0.5B-Instruct, Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct) and bloom series (bloom-389m-zh, bloom-1b4-zh, bloom-560m, bloom-1b7) are to be fine-tuned on the CN Poems and ENG Poems datasets. The task involves generating the full content of a poem given its metadata (dynasty, author, and title for Chinese poems; author and title for English poems). The models will be trained for 100 epochs.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, defined as the percentage of test examples where the predicted poem content exactly matches the ground truth.']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of output text length on the memory capabilities of LLMs, under the hypothesis that longer text is more difficult to remember.
experiments.Method: Models (Qwen1.5-0.5B-Chat, Qwen2-0.5B-Instruct, Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct, bloom-389m-zh, bloom-1b4-zh) are to be fine-tuned for 100 epochs on a version of the CN Poems dataset where the combined length of the input and output text is set to be between 256 and 512 characters.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, defined as the percentage of test examples where the predicted poem content exactly matches the ground truth.']

risk_factors: Due to the complexity of the brain, many operational mechanisms remain unclear, and no reasonable conclusions currently exist regarding its specific workings.

title: Temporally coherent visualisation of time-dependent data
name: tc-tsne
short_hypothesis: By adding a temporal cost term to the t-SNE objective function, which treats the time series of each data point as its own dataset to be embedded, it is possible to produce low-dimensional embeddings that are simultaneously spatially coherent within each time frame and temporally coherent across all time frames, including long-range temporal patterns.
related_work: Existing methods for visualizing time-dependent data include applying static algorithms independently to each frame (leading to instability), embedding all timeframes globally (sacrificing spatial quality), post-hoc alignment (suffering from long-term drift), sequential initialization (inheriting issues from alignment), and penalizing inter-frame velocity (failing to capture abrupt changes or long-range patterns). These strategies often fail to balance spatial quality and temporal coherence, or only address local temporal coherence (between adjacent time steps), creating a need for a method that can capture long-range temporal patterns while maintaining high-quality spatial representations.
abstract: Dimension reduction algorithms aim to embed high-dimensional datasets into a low-dimensional space in such a way that important structural properties, such as clusters and manifolds, are preserved. Most such methods are designed for static data, and naively applying them to time-dependent data can lead to unstable embeddings which do not meaningfully capture the temporal evolution of the data. In this paper, we propose a new variant of the t-SNE algorithm for time-dependent data, TC-tSNE (Temporally Coherent t-SNE) in which an extra term is added to the cost function to promote temporal coherence: the notion that a data point which has a similar position in two time frames should be embedded to similar positions at those times. Importantly, this notion captures temporal similarities over the entire time domain and can therefore capture long-range temporal patterns, not just local ones.

experiments.Description: To create a qualitative visualization of the evolution of a dynamic network of face-to-face interactions among primary school pupils, demonstrating the ability of TC-tSNE to reveal rich underlying temporal and structural patterns that are not clear with other methods.
experiments.Method: The TC-tSNE method will be applied to a pre-processed dataset of face-to-face interactions (Stehlé et al., 2011), which has been transformed into 9-dimensional embeddings using the Unfolded Adjacency Spectral Embedding (UASE) algorithm. The resulting 1D and 2D TC-tSNE embeddings will be visually compared against several t-SNE-based baseline methods: Independent t-SNE, Global t-SNE, Aligned t-SNE, Continuous t-SNE, Landmark Dynamic t-SNE (with PCA and t-SNE guides), and Principal Component Dynamic t-SNE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Qualitative visual inspection of the generated 1D and 2D plots. The evaluation will focus on how clearly the visualizations show the separation of classes, mingling during break times, and the return of students to their classes.

experiments.Description: To quantitatively compare the performance of TC-tSNE against existing methods for time-dependent data visualization on a collection of benchmark datasets, evaluating both spatial quality and temporal coherence.
experiments.Method: TC-tSNE will be evaluated on six datasets: a synthetic 'bifurcating_gaussians' dataset, and five benchmark datasets from Vernier et al. (2020) ('cartolastd', 'fashion', 'qtables', 'sorts', 'walk'). The performance will be compared against a suite of baselines: independent, global, and aligned PCA and t-SNE; continuous t-SNE; D-tSNE (Rauber et al., 2016); Landmark Dynamic t-SNE (LD-tSNE) with PCA and t-SNE guides; and Principal Component Dynamic t-SNE (PCD-tSNE).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: A set of spatial and temporal metrics will be used. Spatial quality will be measured using a 'neighborhood metric' (an average of Neighborhood preservation, Neighborhood hit, Trustworthiness, and Continuity) and 'spatial Shepard diagram metrics' (Pearson, Spearman, Kendall Tau correlations). Temporal coherence will be measured using a proposed 'temporal Shepard diagram metric', which measures the correlation of interpoint distances for individual data points across all timeframes.

risk_factors: In future work, we hope to apply our strategy to other neighbor-embedding methods such as UMAP, and to understand how our ideas can be applied for network and word embeddings.

title: Empowering Memory Assistance: An Episodic Memory-Based Framework for Personalized Recommendations
name: episodic-memory-tgn-recommendations
short_hypothesis: A cognitively inspired episodic-memory framework, built on a Temporal Graph Network (TGN), can effectively model time-evolving personal experiences for personalized recommendations. By treating each event as a multimodal node encoding time, activity, and place, and using specialized temporal encodings like wavelets, the system can learn long-term routines and anticipate future actions without requiring retraining for memory updates.
related_work: Static graphs are insufficient for modeling temporal dynamics in recommendations. Existing dynamic graph learning approaches often rely on limited features and lack support for ordered sequences or contextual reasoning. While action anticipation models exist, they typically lack long-term memory, personalization, and require retraining for new tasks. Recent LLM agents have memory mechanisms, but these are based on short-lived context windows or static document retrieval, not a persistent, temporally ordered, multimodal episodic structure. This creates a gap for a unified framework that can handle continuous experience accumulation, track long-term patterns, and adapt to shifting contexts.
abstract: Artificial agents operating in dynamic environments require the ability to recall and contextualize past experiences to inform future behavior. Drawing inspiration from human episodic memory, we propose a cognitively grounded recommendation framework that models time-evolving personal experiences using a dynamic, multimodal memory architecture. The proposed system encodes temporally structured actions, places, and interactions into a hierarchical temporal graph network (TGN), enabling agents to disambiguate overlapping behavior patterns and anticipate future actions based on long-term experience. Unlike traditional recommendation or forecasting models that rely on static, task-specific patterns, this approach is designed to support continual memory updates without retraining and generalize across varied activity sequences. This work introduces a scalable, cognitively inspired memory architecture with broad applications in lifelong learning, assistive robotics, and human-AI collaboration.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to predict future action sequences against state-of-the-art long-term anticipation models.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Ego4D LTA dataset for the task of action anticipation. It will be compared against baselines including RUSTLM, iCVAE, CLIP+Transformer, Object Prompt, Palm, and AntGPT.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform link prediction on lifelog data compared to other temporal graph-based models.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on a structured long-horizon dataset constructed from Ego4D. The task involves predicting the next activity (Test Score) and integrating unseen activities (New Node Score). Baselines include JODIE, DyRep, TGAT, TGN, CAWN, TCL, GraphMixer, and DyGFormer.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test ROC Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 'New Node ROC Accuracy', 'New Node Precision']

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed time encoding method through an ablation study.
experiments.Method: Different temporal encoding methods will be evaluated within the framework on the link prediction task using the constructed lifelog dataset. Methods to be compared include Sinusoidal, Cosine, Wavelet, Fourier, Gaussian, and a learnable matrix.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test ROC Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 'New Node ROC Accuracy', 'New Node Precision']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effect of the backbone architecture choice on link prediction performance.
experiments.Method: Different graph network architectures will be substituted as the backbone of the framework and evaluated on the link prediction task. Architectures to be compared include JODIE, DyFormer, TGAT, TGN, CAWN, TCL, GraphMixer, and DyRep.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test ROC Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 'New Node ROC Accuracy', 'New Node Precision']

experiments.Description: To examine the effect of different loss functions on link prediction performance for temporally ordered sequences.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained with different loss functions for the link prediction task. The loss functions to be compared are Binary Cross Entropy, Cross Entropy Loss, Mean Squared Error, L1 Loss, Log Likelihood, and CTC Loss.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test ROC Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 'New Node ROC Accuracy', 'New Node Precision']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of different negative sampling techniques on model performance for link prediction tasks.
experiments.Method: The model's performance on the link prediction task will be assessed using different negative sampling strategies. The strategies to be compared are Random, Historical, and Inductive sampling.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test ROC Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 'New Node ROC Accuracy', 'New Node Precision']

experiments.Description: To analyze the performance of different general sampling techniques for link prediction tasks, focusing on temporal dynamics.
experiments.Method: The model's performance on the link prediction task will be assessed using different sampling strategies. The strategies to be compared are Uniform, Recent, and Time Interval Aware.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test ROC Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 'New Node ROC Accuracy', 'New Node Precision']

risk_factors: Limitations include dependency on recurring patterns and large datasets, which may hinder performance in sparse environments.
Additionally, the computational complexity of multimodal data processing and dynamic memory updates poses challenges in resource-constrained settings.

Future work will focus on optimizing for such scenarios, integrating reinforcement learning for context-aware decisions, and expanding the framework to incorporate additional modalities, such as physiological and environmental data.

title: Learning the Inverse Temperature of Ising Models under Hard Constraints using One Sample
name: learn-beta-truncated-ising-mple
short_hypothesis: An estimator based on the maximization of the pseudolikelihood can efficiently and consistently estimate the inverse temperature parameter β of a truncated Ising model from a single sample, even when the model has complex dependencies and hard constraints defined by a k-SAT formula. This approach is intended to generalize recent techniques to this more challenging setting by developing new concentration inequalities.
related_work: Prior work has established methods for estimating parameters of standard (untruncated) Ising models from a single sample, often using pseudolikelihood. Other research has addressed learning for simpler truncated models, such as product distributions truncated by k-SAT assignments or the hard-core model. However, the Ising model is not a product distribution, and common analytical tools used to control the concentration of measure do not apply due to two sources of interdependence: one from the model's Hamiltonian itself and another from the structure of the truncation set. This work aims to bridge this gap by developing an estimation framework specifically for truncated Ising models.
abstract: We consider the problem of estimating the inverse temperature parameter β of an n-dimensional truncated Ising model using a single sample. Given a graph G with n vertices, a truncated Ising model is a probability distribution over the n-dimensional hypercube {-1,1}^n where each configuration σ is constrained to lie in a truncation set S ⊆ {-1,1}^n and has probability Pr(σ) ∝ exp(βσᵀAσ). We adopt the setting where the truncation set S can be expressed as the set of satisfying assignments of a k-SAT formula. Given a single sample from such a model, we design an estimator based on the maximization of the pseudolikelihood. This approach is intended to generalize recent techniques to confront the more challenging setting of the truncated Ising model.

experiments.Description: To theoretically analyze the statistical consistency and computational efficiency of the proposed Maximum Pseudolikelihood Estimator (MPLE) for the inverse temperature of a truncated Ising model.
experiments.Method: The analysis is performed on a truncated Ising model defined on a graph G with n vertices and maximum degree Δ. The truncation set S is defined by the satisfying assignments of a k-SAT formula with clause size k and variable degree d. The proposed estimator, β̂, is obtained by maximizing the pseudolikelihood objective function using a Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) algorithm. The theoretical analysis plan involves proving bounds on the first and second derivatives of the log-pseudolikelihood objective to establish its properties under specific conditions on k, d, and Δ.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Statistical consistency rate of the estimator, measured by the upper bound on the estimation error |β̂ - β*|.', 'Computational complexity of the estimation algorithm, measured by its runtime.']

risk_factors: The analysis relies on an assumption that the maximum degree of the graph Δ is on the order of o(n^(1/6)).
The analysis restricts the interaction magnitudes to a uniform value |Aij| = 1/Δ to isolate the structural component of peer influence, rather than allowing arbitrary coupling strengths.

The proposed approach induces two sources of interdependence, namely from the model itself and from the structure of the truncation set, which complicates the analysis as common tools to control concentration of measure on the hypercube do not apply.

The powerful tools based on the fast mixing nature of Glauber dynamics are not applicable in our model due to the fragmented nature of the truncation set, which can render the domain of our measure into disconnected islands and make the Glauber dynamics non-Ergodic.

title: SVBench: A Benchmark with Temporal Multi-Turn Dialogues for Streaming Video Understanding
name: svbench-streaming-video-dialogue
short_hypothesis: Existing video understanding benchmarks are inadequate for evaluating the long-context, temporal reasoning capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) in streaming scenarios. We propose a new benchmark, SVBench, featuring temporal multi-turn question-answering chains, to comprehensively assess these capabilities. We also introduce a novel task, temporal multi-turn dialogue for streaming videos, and a baseline model, StreamingChat, designed to be more effective on this task.
related_work: Current video understanding benchmarks typically rely on isolated, single-instance text inputs and disjointed QA pairs tied to individual, often short, video clips. This approach fails to evaluate a model's capacity for temporally sequential reasoning and understanding of continuous, dynamic video streams. While some benchmarks use longer videos like movies, they still fall short of addressing the intricate challenges of streaming videos, which involve extended temporal contexts and dynamic scene variations. There is a need for a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate the temporal multi-turn dialogue capabilities of LVLMs for streaming videos.
abstract: Despite the significant advancements of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) on established benchmarks, there remains a notable gap in suitable evaluation regarding their applicability in the emerging domain of long-context streaming video understanding. Current benchmarks for video understanding typically emphasize isolated single-instance text inputs and fail to evaluate the capacity to sustain temporal reasoning throughout the entire duration of video streams. To address these limitations, we introduce SVBench, a pioneering benchmark with temporal multi-turn question-answering chains specifically designed to thoroughly assess the capabilities of streaming video understanding of current LVLMs. We design a semi-automated annotation pipeline to obtain a large dataset of streaming videos with QA pairs, which includes generating QA chains that represent a series of consecutive multi-turn dialogues over video segments and constructing temporal linkages between successive QA chains. We also construct a StreamingChat model as a strong baseline for this new task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the capabilities of LVLMs in understanding long-context scenarios within temporal multi-turn dialogues. In this setup, the model is provided with the full contextual history of preceding QA pairs up to the current timestamp and must answer questions chronologically.
experiments.Method:
Models: MovieChat, Video-ChatGPT, Video-LLaVA, ShareGPT4Video, VideoLLaMA2, TimeChat, InternVL2, VILA, InternLM-XComposer2.5, MiniCPM-V 2.6, StreamingChat (proposed), Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4V, GPT-4o.
Dataset: SVBench evaluation set.
Task: Dialogue Evaluation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Semantic Accuracy (SA)', 'Contextual Coherence (CC)', 'Logical Consistency (LC)', 'Temporal Understanding (TU)', 'Informational Completeness (IC)', 'Overall Score (OS)', 'METEOR', 'GPT4-Score']

experiments.Description: To assess the ability of LVLMs to perform temporal reasoning by introducing probabilistic transitions between related QA chains. When a question with a temporal linkage is encountered, there is a probability that the model will jump to the corresponding related question in the following chain.
experiments.Method:
Models: MovieChat, Video-ChatGPT, Video-LLaVA, ShareGPT4Video, VideoLLaMA2, TimeChat, InternVL2, VILA, InternLM-XComposer2.5, MiniCPM-V 2.6, StreamingChat (proposed), Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4V, GPT-4o.
Dataset: SVBench evaluation set.
Task: Streaming Evaluation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Semantic Accuracy (SA)', 'Contextual Coherence (CC)', 'Logical Consistency (LC)', 'Temporal Understanding (TU)', 'Informational Completeness (IC)', 'Overall Score (OS)', 'METEOR', 'GPT4-Score']

experiments.Description: To analyze model performance across 9 distinct long-context streaming video understanding skills defined by the question categories in SVBench.
experiments.Method:
Models: MovieChat, Video-ChatGPT, Video-LLaVA, ShareGPT4Video, VideoLLaMA2, TimeChat, InternVL2, VILA, InternLM-XComposer2.5, MiniCPM-V 2.6, StreamingChat (proposed), Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4V, GPT-4o.
Dataset: SVBench evaluation set.
Task: Performance analysis per skill category.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Intention Inference (II)', 'Potentiality Assessment (PA)', 'Relationship Inference (RI)', 'Counterfactual Reasoning (CR)', 'Character State and Transition (CST)', 'Common Sense Inference (CSI)', 'Comparison and Trend Analysis (CTA)', 'Event-Centric Analysis (ECA)', 'Spatio-Temporal Speculation (STS)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study assessing the effectiveness of dialogue evaluation (multi-turn QA) versus traditional single-instance QA evaluation.
experiments.Method:
Models: MovieChat, Video-ChatGPT, Video-LLaVA, ShareGPT4Video, VideoLLaMA2, TimeChat, InternVL2, VILA, InternLM-XComposer2.5, MiniCPM-V 2.6, StreamingChat (proposed), Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4V, GPT-4o.
Dataset: SVBench evaluation set.
Task: Compare model performance on single-instance QA (Sin.) vs. multi-turn QA (Mul.).

risk_factors: However, due to the limitations of the video understanding and text generation capabilities of current LVLMs, we have to employ human annotators to manually augment, delete, and modify the QA pairs so that the QA in the chain is connected and aligned with the video.

Given the difficulty of simultaneously meeting these stringent criteria, utilizing LLMs to establish these linkages proves to be impractical. Therefore, manual annotation is required, with the specific modification guideline outlined in Appendix C.
Due to the context window limitation, we split temporal dialogue paths exceeding 100 frames into multiple segments for training.

title: Deep Complex Spatio-Spectral Networks with Complex Visual Inputs
name: dcsnet-complex-spatio-spectral-networks
short_hypothesis: A fully complex-valued pipeline, consisting of a novel real-to-complex (R2C) color transform, a token-based spatio-spectral network (DCSNet) that operates in the frequency domain using Fourier filters, and a specialized complex-valued loss function (Lidense), will enable effective application of complex-valued neural networks to computer vision tasks like binary segmentation and classification by maintaining complex representations throughout the architecture and avoiding information loss.
related_work: Complex-valued neural networks (iCNNs) have shown promise for richer representations, particularly in domains with natural complex-valued data like MRI and radar. However, their application in mainstream computer vision is limited due to several challenges. Key research gaps include the lack of a standard method to convert real-valued RGB images into a meaningful complex-valued format, the difficulty in maintaining the complex-valued nature of information throughout a network, and the absence of suitable objective functions for dense prediction tasks like binary segmentation with complex-valued outputs. While some complex-valued transformer architectures exist, they often suffer from high computational costs and suboptimal performance on image tasks.
abstract: Complex-valued neural networks have attracted growing attention for their ability to handle complex-valued data with enhanced representational capacity. However, their potential in computer vision remains relatively untapped. In this paper, we introduce Deep Complex Spatio-Spectral Network (DCSNet), a fully complex-valued token-based, end-to-end neural network designed for binary segmentation tasks. Additionally, our DCSNet encoder can be used for image classification in the complex domain. We also propose an invertible real-to-complex (R2C) transform, which generates two complex-valued input channels, complex intensity and complex hue, while producing complex-valued images with distinct real and imaginary components. DCSNet operates in both spatial and spectral domains by leveraging complex-valued inputs and complex Fourier transform. As a result, the complex-valued representation is maintained throughout DCSNet, and we avoid the information loss typically associated with Real-Complex transformations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed DCSNet on various binary segmentation tasks (salient object detection, defocus blur detection, shadow detection) using real-valued datasets.
experiments.Method: The DCSNet model, with its encoder pre-trained on ImageNet-1k, will be evaluated on several benchmark datasets. For salient object detection, the datasets are ECSSD, PASCAL-S, HKU-IS, DUT-O, and DUTS. For defocus blur detection, CUHK and DUT datasets are used. For shadow detection, SBU and ISTD datasets are used. The performance will be compared against various real-valued baselines (e.g., PiCANet, BASNet, PoolNet, VST) and complex-valued baselines (FCCN, SCVUNet).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sm (Structure-measure)', 'maxF (maximum F-measure)', 'Emax (maximum enhanced-alignment measure)', 'MAE (mean absolute error)', 'F-measure (Fβ)', 'BER (balanced error rate)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the DCSNet encoder on image classification tasks using both real-valued and complex-valued datasets.
experiments.Method: The DCSNet encoder will be trained and evaluated on image classification. For real-valued data, ImageNet-1k and CIFAR10 will be used. For complex-valued data, the MSTAR and S1SLC_CVDL datasets will be used. Performance will be compared against other complex-valued methods like FCCN and DCN, as well as real-valued models like ResNet, ViT, and GFNet.

experiments.Description: To evaluate DCSNet on a foreground extraction task using a complex-valued dataset.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Simulated InSAR Building dataset for the task of building detection. Performance will be compared against other complex-valued methods, namely FCCN and SCVUNet.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to analyze the contribution of the proposed components: the complex input channels, the dense loss function, and the dense token.
experiments.Method: A series of ablation studies will be performed. The first analyzes the contribution of different input configurations (RGB, iHSV, and the proposed complex channels I_o and I_phi, individually and combined) on SOD benchmarks. The second analyzes the importance of the proposed dense loss (Lidense) and the dense token (T_S) by removing them from the model. A third study will compare the proposed Fourier filter module against a complex-valued self-attention module.

risk_factors: Moreover, we considered using the Fourier transform to get complex input, but the complex input generated from the Fourier transform proved unsuitable.

title: Q-FSRU: Quantum-Augmented Frequency-Spectral Fusion for Medical Visual Question Answering
name: quantum-frequency-fusion-medvqa
short_hypothesis: Combining frequency-domain analysis with quantum-inspired retrieval will improve performance and reasoning in Medical VQA. Transforming multimodal features (image and text) into the frequency domain using FFT will capture global contextual patterns missed by spatial processing, while using quantum-inspired similarity measures for retrieval will better capture nuanced semantic relationships in external medical knowledge compared to classical methods.
related_work: Current Medical Visual Question Answering (Med-VQA) models predominantly operate in the spatial domain, which may overlook subtle, global frequency-based patterns relevant in medical imaging. Concurrently, while retrieval-augmented methods have shown promise, they typically rely on classical similarity measures like cosine similarity, which may not fully capture the complex semantic relationships required for clinical reasoning. Prior work has explored frequency-domain representations for other tasks or single modalities, but not comprehensively for Med-VQA. Similarly, quantum-inspired retrieval has been established in information retrieval but has not been specifically adapted for medical knowledge retrieval in this context.
abstract: Solving tough clinical questions that require both image and text understanding is still a major challenge in healthcare AI. In this work, we propose Q-FSRU, a new model that combines Frequency Spectrum Representation and Fusion (FSRU) with a method called Quantum Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Quantum RAG) for medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). The model takes in features from medical images and related text, then shifts them into the frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). This helps it focus on more meaningful data and filter out noise or less useful information. To improve accuracy and ensure that answers are based on real knowledge, we add a quantum-inspired retrieval system. It fetches useful medical facts from external sources using quantum-based similarity techniques. These details are then merged with the frequency-based features for stronger reasoning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Q-FSRU model against existing methods on a medical visual question answering task.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the VQA-RAD dataset. Baselines for comparison will include general-purpose VQA models (MCAN, LXMERT), medical-specific models (LLaVA-Med, STLLaVA-Med), a knowledge-augmented method (LaPA), and a frequency-domain approach (FSRU).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's generalization capabilities and domain adaptation performance using zero-shot transfer between different medical imaging domains.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on the VQA-RAD (radiology) dataset and evaluated on the PathVQA (pathology) dataset without additional fine-tuning, and vice-versa. Performance will be compared against baselines including LLaVA-Med, STLLaVA-Med, LaPA, and FSRU.

experiments.Description: To investigate the contribution of individual components of the Q-FSRU architecture to its overall performance through ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies will be conducted on the VQA-RAD dataset by systematically removing or replacing key components of the full Q-FSRU model. Variants to be tested include: without Frequency Processing, without Quantum Retrieval, without Contrastive Learning, using only Spatial Fusion, using Cosine Similarity instead of quantum similarity, and without Cross-Modal Co-selection.

risk_factors: future work aiming to scale to larger datasets, include more imaging modalities, and refine the retrieval mechanism for improved grounding.

title: When Vision Transformers Outperform ResNets without Pre-training or Strong Data Augmentations
name: SAM-for-ViT-training
short_hypothesis: We hypothesize that smoothing the loss landscapes at convergence by using a sharpness-aware minimizer (SAM) can significantly improve the generalization ability of convolution-free architectures like Vision Transformers (ViTs) and MLP-Mixers, which are otherwise observed to converge to extremely sharp local minima.
related_work: Vision Transformers (ViTs) and MLP-Mixers are general-purpose architectures that lack the convolution-like inductive biases of CNNs. Consequently, when trained from scratch on datasets like ImageNet, they yield modest accuracies compared to ResNets. To boost performance, existing works rely on large-scale pre-training (e.g., on JFT-300M) or repeated strong data augmentations. These approaches result in excessive demands on data, computing, and hyperparameter tuning. Furthermore, training these models is known to be sensitive to initialization and hyperparameters, pointing to underlying optimization problems.
abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) and MLPs signal further efforts on replacing hand-wired features or inductive biases with general-purpose neural architectures. Existing works empower the models by massive data, such as large-scale pre-training and/or repeated strong data augmentations, and still report optimization-related problems. This paper investigates ViTs and MLP-Mixers from the lens of loss geometry, intending to improve the models' data efficiency at training and generalization at inference. Visualization and Hessian analysis suggest that these models converge to extremely sharp local minima. This work proposes to promote smoothness by applying a recently proposed sharpness-aware optimizer (SAM) to substantially improve the accuracy and robustness of ViTs and MLP-Mixers.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if applying the Sharpness-Aware Minimizer (SAM) optimizer improves the performance of ViTs and MLP-Mixers when trained from scratch on ImageNet without strong augmentations, and to compare them against ResNets.
experiments.Method: Train ViT models (ViT-S, ViT-B), MLP-Mixer models (Mixer-S, Mixer-B), and ResNet models (ResNet-50, -101, -152, etc.) from scratch on ImageNet. The training will use only basic Inception-style preprocessing (random crop and horizontal flip). For each architecture, a 'vanilla' version trained with a standard optimizer (AdamW or SGD) will be compared against a version trained with SAM.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, ImageNet-ReaL, and ImageNet V2.', 'Robustness measured by accuracy on ImageNet-R and ImageNet-C.', 'Loss landscape analysis via visualization, average flatness (L_train*), and the dominant Hessian eigenvalue (λ_max).']

experiments.Description: To compare the effects of SAM against strong data augmentations (mixup and RandAugment) on model generalization and loss geometry.
experiments.Method: Train ViT-B/16 and Mixer-B/16 on ImageNet using three distinct training pipelines: 1) vanilla training with basic augmentations, 2) training with SAM, and 3) training with a combination of strong augmentations (mixup and RandAugment). A fourth setup combining SAM and strong augmentations will also be tested.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on ImageNet.', 'Sharpness at convergence, measured by the Hessian dominant eigenvalue (λ_max) and average flatness (L_train*).']

experiments.Description: To evaluate how the effectiveness of SAM changes with the size of the training dataset.
experiments.Method: Train ResNet-152, ViT-B/16, and Mixer-B/16 models (with and without SAM) on subsets of ImageNet, specifically random samples of 1/4 and 1/2 of the full dataset. Additionally, evaluate SAM on models pre-trained on ImageNet-21k and then fine-tuned on ImageNet-1k.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Top-1 accuracy on the ImageNet validation set.']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of SAM when combined with adversarial training to improve model robustness.
experiments.Method: Incorporate SAM into a fast adversarial training framework (FGSM with random start). Train ViT, MLP-Mixer, and ResNet models on ImageNet using this combined three-level minimax objective and compare against standard adversarial training.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Clean accuracy on ImageNet.', 'Adversarial robustness evaluated under a 10-step PGD attack.', 'Accuracy on ImageNet-R and ImageNet-C.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate if SAM can improve performance when coupled with a supervised contrastive learning framework.
experiments.Method: Pre-train ViT-S/16 and ViT-B/16 using a supervised contrastive learning objective on ImageNet. Compare models pre-trained with and without SAM. After pre-training, the classification head will be fine-tuned.

experiments.Description: To study the role of smoothed loss geometry on the transfer learning ability of models pre-trained with SAM.
experiments.Method: Take ViT, MLP-Mixer, and ResNet models pre-trained on ImageNet (with and without SAM) and fine-tune them on several downstream image classification tasks without using SAM during the fine-tuning phase.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Oxford-IIIT Pets, and Oxford Flowers-102 datasets.']

risk_factors: First, SAM incurs another round of forward and backward propagations to update e, which will lead to around 2x computational cost per update.

Second, we notice that the effect of SAM diminishes as the training dataset becomes larger, so it is vital to develop learning algorithms that can improve/accelerate the large-scale pre-training process.

title: WorldSense: Evaluating Real-world Omnimodal Understanding for Multimodal LLMs
name: worldsense-benchmark
short_hypothesis: A new benchmark, WorldSense, which features a strong coupling of audio and video inputs, can be created to rigorously assess the omni-modal understanding capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in real-world scenarios, a gap not addressed by existing benchmarks.
related_work: Recent literature on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has shown remarkable progress, but primarily focuses on visual-language information, largely overlooking crucial modalities like audio. While some audio-visual benchmarks exist (e.g., OmniBench, AV-Odyssey Bench, Music-AVQA, AVQA, Long-VALE), they have significant limitations. These include focusing on static images, being restricted to specific domains or simple tasks like captioning, or suffering from low-quality and monotonous question patterns. This creates a critical gap in comprehensively evaluating the real-world omni-modal understanding capabilities of MLLMs.
abstract: We introduce WorldSense, the first benchmark to assess the multi-modal video understanding, that simultaneously encompasses visual, audio, and text inputs. In contrast to existing benchmarks, our WorldSense has several features: (i) collaboration of omni-modality, we design the evaluation tasks to feature a strong coupling of audio and video, requiring models to effectively utilize the synergistic perception of omni-modality; (ii) diversity of videos and tasks, WorldSense encompasses a diverse collection of 1,662 audio-visual synchronised videos, systematically categorized into 8 primary domains and 67 fine-grained subcategories to cover the broad scenarios, and 3,172 multi-choice QA pairs across 26 distinct tasks to enable the comprehensive evaluation; (iii) high-quality annotations, all the QA pairs are manually labeled by 80 expert annotators with multiple rounds of correction to ensure quality. We hope our WorldSense can provide a platform for evaluating the ability in constructing and understanding coherent contexts from omni-modality.

experiments.Description: To comprehensively assess the omni-modal understanding ability of a wide range of MLLMs using the proposed WorldSense benchmark.
experiments.Method: Models will be evaluated on the WorldSense benchmark, which consists of 1,662 audio-visual videos and 3,172 multiple-choice question-answer pairs. The evaluation will cover three types of MLLMs: (1) open-source audio-visual models (Unified-IO-2, OneLLM, VideoLLaMA2, etc.), (2) open-source video-only MLLMs (Qwen2-VL, LLaVA-OneVision, InternVL2.5, LLaVA-Video, etc.), and (3) proprietary MLLMs (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT 4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Pro). The task is multiple-choice question answering based on the provided video and audio inputs.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to dissect modality contributions and rigorously assess the necessity of multimodal integration in real-world understanding.
experiments.Method: Models will be evaluated on the WorldSense benchmark across various modality configurations. To investigate the impact of vision, performance will be compared across audio-only, audio with video captions, and audio with video frames. To investigate the impact of audio, performance will be compared across video-only, video with subtitles, and video with original audio.

risk_factors: While our WorldSense represents a significant advancement in evaluating multimodal understanding capabilities of MLLMs, the multiple-choice format inevitably constrains the assessment of models’ generative capabilities.
Real-world understanding often requires open-ended responses, explanations, and adaptability beyond selecting from predefined options.
Our WorldSense may not adequately evaluate how models perform on tasks requiring nuanced reasoning or creative problem-solving.

title: Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent with decoupled backpropagation and layer-wise updates
name: async-sgd-decoupled-bp
short_hypothesis: A novel asynchronous formulation of Backpropagation can be introduced which allows the forward pass and the backward pass to be executed separately and in parallel. By using more threads for the backward pass than the forward pass, the unequal execution time can be accounted for. Furthermore, asynchronously updating the model's parameters at a layer-wise granularity without a locking mechanism will reduce staleness and speed up training.
