related_work: Standard backpropagation (BP) suffers from update locking, where forward and backward passes are interlocked, and the backward pass is computationally more expensive. Existing asynchronous SGD methods like Hogwild! allow lock-free updates but do not address the forward-backward pass dependency and computational imbalance. Other approaches like block-local learning or communication-efficient algorithms tackle different aspects of distributed training, such as network partitioning or gradient compression, but often require architectural changes or extra memory. The proposed method aims to fill the gap by decoupling the forward and backward passes of BP into separate, parallel threads with an imbalanced ratio to account for compute disparity, combined with immediate, lock-free layer-wise updates to reduce staleness.
abstract: The increasing size of deep learning models has created the need for more efficient alternatives to the standard error backpropagation algorithm, that make better use of asynchronous, parallel and distributed computing. One major shortcoming of backpropagation is the interlocking between the forward phase of the algorithm, which computes a global loss, and the backward phase where the loss is backpropagated through all layers to compute the gradients, which are used to update the network parameters. To address this problem, we propose a method that parallelises SGD updates across the layers of a model by asynchronously updating them from multiple threads. Furthermore, since the forward pass is often much faster than the backward pass, we use separate threads for the forward and backward pass calculations, which allows us to use a higher ratio of forward to backward threads than the usual 1:1 ratio, reducing the overall staleness of the parameters. Thus, our approach performs asynchronous stochastic gradient descent using separate threads for the loss (forward) and gradient (backward) computations and performs layer-wise partial updates to parameters in a distributed way.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of asynchronous training with decoupled backpropagation on image classification tasks.
experiments.Method: Tasks: Image classification on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. Models: ResNet-18 and ResNet-50. Baselines: The proposed method with layer-wise updates (Async LU) and block updates (Async BU) will be compared against standard SGD, Locally-Asynchronous-Parallel SGD (LAPSGD), and Locally-Partitioned-Asynchronous-Parallel SGD (LPPSGD).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Best accuracy', 'Time to reach accuracy (TTA)', 'Number of epochs']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scaling properties of the proposed asynchronous training method on a large-scale image classification task.
experiments.Method: Task: Image classification on the ImageNet-1k dataset. Model: ResNet-50. Baselines: The proposed method (Async LU) will be compared against vanilla backpropagation (BP) on a single GPU.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of asynchronous backpropagation on a sequence modeling task.
experiments.Method: Task: Sentiment analysis on the IMDb dataset. Model: A 2-Layer LSTM network. Baselines: The proposed method with layer-wise updates (Async LU) will be compared against the variant with block updates (Async BU).

risk_factors: While our implementation using C++ and LibTorch demonstrated the potential of this method, we also identified some limitations related to GPU resource allocation in SIMT architectures.

title: Generating with Confidence: Uncertainty Quantification for Black-box Large Language Models
name: black-box-llm-uq-semantic-dispersion
short_hypothesis: Simple metrics based on the semantic dispersion of multiple generated responses from a black-box LLM can serve as reliable predictors for the quality of the LLM's responses, enabling effective uncertainty quantification and selective generation without white-box access.
related_work: Existing research on uncertainty quantification (UQ) for natural language generation (NLG) typically assumes white-box access to model internals like token-level logits, which is increasingly unrealistic for closed-source, API-based LLMs. Prior work often adapts UQ methods from classification, overlooking the unique generative challenges of NLG, or relies on expensive techniques like asking the model for its own confidence. The most relevant prior work computes 'semantic entropy' but still requires access to the LLM's numerical output. This paper addresses the gap by proposing and evaluating UQ methods designed specifically for a black-box setting, where only the generated text is available.
abstract: Gauging the trustworthiness of responses generated by large language models (LLMs) remains an open challenge, with limited research on uncertainty quantification (UQ) for natural language generation (NLG). Furthermore, existing literature typically assumes white-box access to language models, which is becoming unrealistic. In this work, we investigate UQ in NLG for black-box LLMs. We first differentiate uncertainty vs confidence: the former refers to the “dispersion” of the potential predictions for a fixed input, and the latter refers to the confidence on a particular prediction/generation. We then propose and compare several confidence/uncertainty measures, applying them to selective NLG where unreliable results could either be ignored or yielded for further assessment.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of proposed black-box uncertainty and confidence measures by using them to predict the correctness of LLM generations on question-answering tasks. This is framed as a selective generation task where low-confidence or high-uncertainty answers could be rejected.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on question-answering datasets: CoQA (conversational), TriviaQA (closed-book), and Natural Questions (nq). Several LLMs will be tested, including LLaMA (13B), LLaMA2 (13B), OPT (13B), and the black-box gpt-3.5-turbo. The proposed measures (NumSet, Deg, Ecc, EigV), derived from pairwise response similarities (calculated via Jaccard and an NLI model), will be compared against random chance, an oracle, and several baselines. Baselines include a black-box method (Lexical similarity - ULexisim) and white-box methods (Semantic Entropy - SE, and P(true)). The correctness of generations will be automatically assessed by gpt-3.5-turbo.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC)', 'Area Under Accuracy-Rejection Curve (AUARC)']

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the number of generated samples (m) on the performance of the proposed uncertainty and confidence measures.
experiments.Method: Using the same experimental setup as the main evaluation, the number of generated responses 'm' will be varied (e.g., m=3, 5, 10, 20). The performance of the confidence-based measures (Deg, Ecc) and uncertainty-based measures will be tracked as 'm' increases.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['AUARC for both confidence (C+IA) and uncertainty (U+EA) settings, plotted against the number of generations (m).']

risk_factors: Currently, the evaluation of uncertainty/confidence measures is restricted to question-answering tasks, because it is generally difficult to acquire labels on whether a response is 'reliable' or not for open-ended conversations.
Even for the datasets used in our paper, while our use of GPT as the judge improves upon previous heuristics (of using rougeL), the evaluation can be improved if human labels are used.
When the sampling temperature is 0 (i.e. greedy decoding), all methods will give degenerate results, and one might resort to white-box methods or external information to quantify the uncertainty.
Our methods may not identify factual errors propagated from the training corpus or identify when the LLM is being overconfident (which is related to calibration, an orthogonal research topic).

title: From Lab to Line: Deployment-Aware NMR–Text Expert Routing for Real-Time Apple Moldy Core Disease Screening and Explanation
name: deployment-aware-multimodal-amcd-diagnosis
short_hypothesis: A multimodal learning framework that fuses low-field nuclear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR) imagery with expert textual descriptions can enable real-time, interpretable, and lightweight diagnosis of internal apple disorders like Apple Moldy Core Disease (AMCD). A proposed Task-Aware Mixture-of-Experts (T-MoE) fusion method will effectively route between NMR and text experts, while a new deployment-aware metric, TAAPM, will align model evaluation with the economic constraints of industrial sorting.
related_work: Prior work in non-destructive detection of internal apple diseases has explored methods like Vis/NIR spectroscopy, acoustic vibrations, and X-ray/CT imaging, but has underutilized low-field NMR (LF-NMR). Similarly, while multimodal learning has been applied in agriculture, it has primarily focused on external symptoms (e.g., leaf diseases) using visual and text data. There is a gap in applying modern multimodal deep learning, specifically integrating LF-NMR, to detect latent internal disorders like Apple Moldy Core Disease (AMCD), which often lack external signs. Existing models also often lack interpretability and are not tailored for deployment constraints like cost and latency in industrial settings.
abstract: Real-time, interpretable diagnosis of Apple Moldy Core Disease (AMCD) under industrial sorting constraints is addressed. AppleNMR-MM V1.0, an LF-NMR–centric dataset with expert textual descriptions, is introduced to enable multi-modal learning in a small-sample regime. A Task-Aware Mixture-of-Experts (T-MoE) fusion is proposed to route among NMR and text experts conditioned on predictive uncertainty and compute budget, while a Multi-agent Collaborative Chain-of-Thought (MACCT) with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) coordinates triage→diagnose→explain agents using a domain corpus for evidence-grounded reasoning. To align modeling with production constraints, a new metric, TAAPM, is introduced as the primary deployment criterion, explicitly derived from real factory export-trade regulations and optimized for overall economic benefit.

experiments.Description: To establish unimodal baselines by evaluating the standalone classification performance of text-only and vision-only models on the AMCD detection task.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the AppleNMR-MM V1.0 dataset. For the text-only baseline, various Transformer-based language models (BERT-base-uncased, RoBERTa-base, DistilBERT-base-uncased, ALBERT-base-v2) will be trained on expert-generated diagnostic text. For the vision-only baseline, various CNN architectures (ResNet, DenseNet121, EfficientNet-B0, shufflenetv2, VGG) will be trained on the LF-NMR images.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of different multimodal fusion strategies for AMCD classification, comparing them against unimodal baselines and against each other in terms of performance and deployment efficiency.
experiments.Method: Using the AppleNMR-MM V1.0 dataset, various combinations of text encoders (e.g., ALBERT-base-v2, RoBERTa-base) and vision encoders (e.g., EfficientNet-B0, ResNet50) will be combined. The experiment will test four different fusion mechanisms: Early Fusion with Self-Attention (EF+SA), Gated Fusion, Feature concatenation, and Cross-Attention.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to systematically quantify the contribution of each modality (vision and text) and assess the robustness of the two most representative multimodal fusion architectures (Gated Fusion and Cross-Attention).
experiments.Method: The two best-performing model variants will be evaluated under five configurations: (1) Full Model, (2) Text Only (vision modality masked), (3) Vision Only (text modality masked), (4) Text with noisy vision input, and (5) Vision with noisy text input. This will be performed on the AppleNMR-MM V1.0 dataset.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the capability of the proposed NMR-Apple-Expert system to generate domain-specific diagnostic dialogues for AMCD.
experiments.Method: A Qwen3-4B model will be fine-tuned using QLoRA on a proprietary vision-language dialogue dataset. Its performance will be evaluated on a held-out set of 100 samples and compared against other open-source 4B-scale models (Mistral-4B, Gemma3-4B-IT) and a commercial API (GPT-40).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Human evaluation (pass/fail rate)', 'GPT-based scoring (1-5 scale) on dimensions of contextual coherence, factual consistency, domain-specific relevance, and language fluency']

title: Fast Training Dataset Attribution via In-Context Learning
name: fast-tda-via-icl
short_hypothesis: The contribution of training datasets to the outputs of instruction-tuned LLMs can be estimated without access to model internals by using in-context learning. We propose two methods: 1) A similarity-based approach (Shapley Context Method - SCM) which posits that providing a dataset as context to an LLM trained on it changes its output less than for an LLM not trained on it. 2) A mixture distribution model approach (Context Mixture Factorization - CMF) that frames attribution as a matrix factorization problem to separate a base model component from dataset-specific components.
related_work: Existing Training Data Attribution (TDA) methods are primarily either retraining-based or influence function-based. Retraining-based methods are computationally expensive as they require retraining the model without the target data. Influence function-based methods are more efficient but rely on a linear approximation of the model which can be inaccurate. Critically, both approaches require access to the internal workings of the model (e.g., gradients), which is not feasible for many popular black-box LLMs.
abstract: We investigate the use of in-context learning and prompt engineering to estimate the contributions of training data in the outputs of instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs). We propose two novel approaches: (1) a similarity-based approach that measures the difference between LLM outputs with and without provided context, and (2) a mixture distribution model approach that frames the problem of identifying contribution scores as a matrix factorization task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the proposed attribution methods (SCM and CMF) to identify the influence of different datasets with varying levels of exposure (seen, unseen but similar, and novel) on LLM outputs.
experiments.Method: The experiment will apply SCM and CMF to a set of instruction-tuned LLMs (GPT-4, Bloomz, Mistral 7B, Phi-3-mini). The evaluation will be conducted on a binary Q&A task using three primary datasets: BoolQ (likely seen during pretraining), FakeQ (synthetically created and unseen), and Olympic 2024 (guaranteed unseen). Five additional Wikipedia science domain datasets will be used as broad controls. The performance will be compared against the Trak attribution baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Attribution scores from SCM (Shapley values) and CMF (π coefficients).', 'The primary evaluation is based on the expectation that attribution scores will be highest for BoolQ, lower for FakeQ, and lowest for Olympic2024, reflecting the degree of data exposure.']

experiments.Description: To validate that the proposed attribution metrics can reliably capture a model's growing dependence on a dataset by measuring attribution during a controlled fine-tuning process.
experiments.Method: LLMs will be incrementally fine-tuned on the previously unseen Olympic2024 dataset using progressively increasing learning rates. After each fine-tuning step, the SCM and CMF attribution methods will be applied to measure the contribution of the Olympic2024 dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The attribution scores from SCM and CMF for the Olympic2024 dataset.', 'The metrics are considered valid if the attribution scores increase monotonically with the extent of fine-tuning.']

experiments.Description: To assess the utility of the proposed attribution metrics for evaluating and ranking the effectiveness of different machine unlearning algorithms.
experiments.Method: Three machine unlearning methods (Gradient Ascent, Fine-tuning with Random Labels, Unlearning with Adversarial Samples) will be applied to Bloomz and Phi-3 models that have been fine-tuned on the Olympic2024 dataset. The goal is to make the models 'unlearn' this dataset. The SCM and CMF attribution methods will be used to measure the influence of the Olympic2024 dataset before and after the unlearning process.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The reduction in attribution scores (from SCM and CMF) for the Olympic2024 dataset.', 'A larger reduction in the attribution score indicates a more effective unlearning method.']

risk_factors: Given the modularity of LLM structures, this assumption [that an LLM's behavior can be modeled as a mixture of a general-purpose model and specialized models for each dataset] is not fully realistic.
Model (3) can capture the scenarios where an LLM uses data from multiple sources, but does not model the scenarios where the LLM uses the interaction of data from multiple sources.
While we focus on BoolQ-style binary QA for tractability, our framework is general and can extend to multi-class or free-form generation. Future work will apply SCM and CMF to such outputs...
Retrieval design itself (e.g., chunk size, reranking, or long-context strategies) merits study, as it shapes the stability of attribution.

title: The KFIoU Loss for Rotated Object Detection
name: kf-iou-loss-for-rotated-detection
short_hypothesis: We propose an approximate SkewIoU loss, named KFIoU, based on Gaussian modeling and Gaussian product. This loss is designed to be fully differentiable, easy to implement, and handle non-overlapping cases. By using the Gaussian product to mimic the overlap calculation of SkewIoU, KFIoU is intended to achieve better trend-level alignment with the SkewIoU metric without requiring the extra hyperparameter tuning that is necessary for other Gaussian-based losses like GWD and KLD.
related_work: In rotated object detection, there is an inconsistency between the regression loss (e.g., Smooth L1) and the evaluation metric (SkewIoU). SkewIoU is complex to compute, not always differentiable, and unfriendly to gradient-based training, which prevents its direct use as a loss function. While horizontal detection uses IoU-based losses (GIoU, DIoU) to solve this, their application to rotation detection is blocked. Prior attempts to approximate SkewIoU include polygon-based, pixel-based, and Gaussian-based methods. However, recent Gaussian-based losses like GWD and KLD require specifying a distribution distance metric, which introduces extra hyperparameters that need careful tuning and vary across datasets. This work aims to create a hyperparameter-free Gaussian-based loss that more naturally simulates the SkewIoU mechanism.
abstract: Differing from the well-developed horizontal object detection area whereby the computing-friendly IoU based loss is readily adopted and well fits with the detection metrics, rotation detectors often involve a more complicated loss based on SkewIoU which is unfriendly to gradient-based training. In this paper, we propose an effective approximate SkewIoU loss based on Gaussian modeling and Gaussian product, which mainly consists of two items. The first term is a scale-insensitive center point loss, which is used to quickly narrow the distance between the center points of the two bounding boxes. In the distance-independent second term, the product of the Gaussian distributions is adopted to inherently mimic the mechanism of SkewIoU by its definition. This is in contrast to recent Gaussian modeling based rotation detectors e.g. GWD loss and KLD loss that involve a human-specified distribution distance metric which require additional hyperparameter tuning. The resulting new loss called KFIoU loss is intended to be easier to implement, fully differentiable, and able to handle non-overlapping cases. We further propose to extend our technique to the 3-D case which also suffers from the same issues as 2-D.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed KFIoU loss against other regression losses and analyze its properties, such as trend-level consistency with SkewIoU.
experiments.Method: The base model is RetinaNet. The comparison will be performed on DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, and DOTA-v2.0 datasets. Losses to be compared include Smooth L1, plain SkewIoU, GWD, KLD, and the proposed KFIoU. The analysis will also involve comparing different forms of the center point loss term within KFIoU.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of KFIoU loss compared to the standard Smooth L1 loss on a variety of 2D rotated object detection benchmarks.
experiments.Method: Using a RetinaNet base detector, experiments will be conducted on HRSC2016, MSRA-TD500, ICDAR2015, FDDB, and DOTA-v1.0 datasets. The performance of models trained with KFIoU loss will be compared against those trained with Smooth L1 loss.

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization of the KFIoU loss to 3D object detection.
experiments.Method: Using the KITTI validation split for 3D and BEV detection. The baseline model is PointPillars. The experiment will compare the baseline against PointPillars augmented with GWD loss, KLD loss, and the proposed KFIoU loss.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['mAP for 3D Detection (Easy, Moderate, Hard)', 'mAP for BEV Detection (Easy, Moderate, Hard)']

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of KFIoU loss against other published peer methods on the DOTA benchmark under a controlled setting.
experiments.Method: Using the RetinaNet model with a ResNet50 backbone on the DOTA datasets (v1.0, v1.5, v2.0). The proposed KFIoU loss will be compared against other solutions including PIoU, IoU-Smooth L1, Modulated Loss, CSL, GWD, and KLD, all trained and tested under the same environment and hyperparameters.

experiments.Description: To compare detectors equipped with KFIoU loss against state-of-the-art methods on the DOTA-v1.0 benchmark.
experiments.Method: The KFIoU loss will be integrated into various detectors (e.g., R³Det, RoI-Transformer) and compared against a range of recent single-stage, refine-stage, and two-stage detectors on the DOTA-v1.0 dataset.

risk_factors: Note that the Gaussian modeling has a limitation that it cannot be directly applied to quadrilateral/polygon detection which is also an important task in aerial images, scene text, etc.
In addition, the Gaussian distribution of the square like object is close to the isotropic circle, which is not suitable for the object heading detection.

title: Learning Constraints from Offline Dataset via Inverse Dual Values Estimation
name: IDVE for Offline ICRL
short_hypothesis: By reformulating offline Inverse Constraint Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) as a regularized policy learning problem, it is possible to learn constraints from a static offline dataset. This can be achieved by deriving dual value functions for both rewards and costs and formulating a bi-level optimization problem to estimate their values. This approach, named Inverse Dual Values Estimation (IDVE), is designed to ensure safety and conservatism by regularizing the policy's deviation from the expert policy and incentivizing it to operate within the distribution of the offline data.
related_work: Classical Inverse Constraint Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) algorithms infer constraints from expert demonstrations but typically require an online learning paradigm with boundless exploration. This is often dangerous and expensive in realistic, safety-critical applications. Existing offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods focus on learning policies or rewards from static datasets but have not been applied to the problem of offline constraint inference. This work presents itself as the first attempt to create a solution for offline ICRL, addressing the gap where no prior method exists.
abstract: To develop safe control strategies, Inverse Constrained Reinforcement learning (ICRL) infers constraints from expert demonstrations. Classical ICRL algorithms typically adopt an online learning diagram that permits boundless exploration, which is dangerous and expensive for safe-critical control tasks. To address this challenge, this work presents a novel Inverse Dual Values Estimation (IDVE) framework for offline ICRL. IDVE dynamically combines the conservative estimation inherent in offline RL and the data-driven inference in inverse RL to learn constraints from limited data. Specifically, IDVE derives dual value functions for both rewards and costs, estimating their values in a bi-level optimization problem based on the offline dataset. To derive a practical algorithm, the paper introduces methods for handling unknown transitions, scaling to continuous environments, and controlling the degree of sparsity regularization.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the algorithm's ability to recover constraints and visualize the learned value functions in discrete environments.
experiments.Method: The method is evaluated on 7x7 discrete grid-world maps, each with four unique constraint settings. The offline dataset for each environment consists of 50 trajectories, including expert demonstrations and sub-optimal data from random walks. The proposed method (IDVE) is compared against several baselines: IDVE w/o S (without sparsity control), IDVE w/o A (without the violating action buffer), Offline IL (Inverse Soft Q-Learning), and Offline RL (f-DVL).

experiments.Description: To study how well the constraints inferred from training environments can be generalized to new, unseen environments with different starting and target states.
experiments.Method: Constraints are learned using data from the initial grid-world environments. The resulting policies are then evaluated on new grid-world settings with different start and target states. The performance of IDVE is compared against IDVE w/oS and Offline RL. The Offline IL baseline is omitted for this experiment.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's performance on continuous control tasks with predefined physical constraints.
experiments.Method: The experiments are conducted on four modified MuJoCo environments: Limited Speed Ant, Limit Arm HalfCheetah, Blocked Walker, and Blocked Half-Cheetah. The offline dataset is created from an early-stopped SAC replay buffer combined with expert trajectories. The proposed method (IDVE) is compared with its ablations (IDVE w/oA, IDVE w/oS) and other baselines (Offline RL, Offline IL).

risk_factors: A promising avenue for future research involves extending our method to accommodate diverse ICRL configurations, such as soft constraints in stochastic environments.

title: MergePrint: Robust Fingerprinting against Merging Large Language Models
name: mergeprint
short_hypothesis: A novel fingerprinting method, MERGEPRINT, can embed robust fingerprints into Large Language Models that preserve ownership claims even after model merging. By optimizing the model's parameters against a pseudo-merged model (simulating post-merge weights), the generated fingerprints will remain detectable after the model is merged with others. Additionally, optimizing the fingerprint inputs themselves can minimize performance degradation.
related_work: Existing intellectual property protection techniques for LLMs have limitations. Output watermarking is ineffective for released models that are manipulated directly. Model weight watermarking, while robust to fine-tuning in some cases, has been shown not to survive model merging. Prior model fingerprinting methods have primarily focused on robustness against fine-tuning and are insufficient for the model merging scenario. This work addresses the specific, underexplored gap of creating fingerprints that are robust to model merging, a low-cost and practical method of model misappropriation.
abstract: As the cost of training large language models (LLMs) rises, protecting their intellectual property has become increasingly critical. Model merging, which integrates multiple expert models into a single model, presents a growing risk of unauthorized usage. While fingerprinting techniques have been studied for asserting model ownership, existing methods have primarily focused on fine-tuning, leaving model merging underexplored. To address this gap, we propose a novel fingerprinting method MERGEPRINT that embeds robust fingerprints designed to preserve ownership claims even after model merging. By optimizing against a pseudo-merged model, which simulates post-merged model weights, MERGEPRINT generates fingerprints that remain detectable after merging. Additionally, we optimize the fingerprint inputs to minimize performance degradation, enabling verification through specific outputs from targeted inputs.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the robustness of MERGEPRINT fingerprints when merging a fingerprinted model with a non-fingerprinted model across various merging ratios.
experiments.Method: A fingerprint is embedded into WizardMath-7B-V1.0. This model is then merged with LLaMA-2-7B-CHAT (which has no fingerprint) using Task-arithmetic, TIES-merging, and DARE methods. The merging coefficient will be varied. The approach will be compared against the Instructional Fingerprinting (IF) baseline. The base model for all is LLaMA-2-7B.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Verification Success Rate (VSR)', 'Downstream task performance on GSM8K (Math)', 'Downstream task performance on StrongReject-small (Safety)']

experiments.Description: Investigate whether individual fingerprints are preserved when merging multiple models, where more than one model is embedded with a unique fingerprint.
experiments.Method: Three models will be merged: WizardMath-7B-V1.0 (with fingerprint y1='transformers'), LLaMA-2-CHAT (with fingerprint y2='pikachu'), and Vicuna-7B (no fingerprint). The merging coefficients will be varied. Merging will be performed using Task-arithmetic and TIES-merging, with and without DARE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Verification Success Rate (VSR) for y1', 'Verification Success Rate (VSR) for y2']

experiments.Description: Evaluate fingerprint persistence when sequentially merging a fingerprinted model with a larger number of other models (up to seven total).
experiments.Method: WizardMath-7B (with an embedded fingerprint) will be sequentially merged with six other LLMs: LLaMA2-7B-CHAT, Nous-Hermes-llama-2-7B, Vicuna-7B, Pygmalion-2 7B, LLaMA2-7B-chat-Uncensored, and Swallow-7B. Merging will be done in equal proportions using Task-Arithmetic and TIES-MERGING. The approach will be compared to the Instructional Fingerprinting (IF) baseline.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the harmlessness of the fingerprinting method by measuring its impact on the model's original downstream task performance.
experiments.Method: The performance of the original models (WizardMath and LLaMA-2-CHAT) will be compared to their fingerprinted versions (with and without input optimization). Evaluation will be conducted using the lm-eval-harness on nine tasks: ARC-Challenge, ARC-Easy, CommonsenseQA, HellaSwag, OpenBookQA, PIQA, SquadCompletion, TriviaQA, and Winogrande.

experiments.Description: Verify the effectiveness and reliability of the fingerprinting method by checking that the fingerprint appears only in the intended owner's model.
experiments.Method: The presence of the embedded fingerprint pair will be checked in the owner's model and in seven other models used in the merging experiments to ensure it does not appear unintentionally.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the efficiency of the fingerprinting procedure, specifically the parameter optimization (OptP) component.
experiments.Method: The training loss during the OptP phase for WizardMath-7B will be analyzed, comparing the process with and without the initial input optimization (OptI) step.

experiments.Description: Test the generality of MERGEPRINT on a different family of models, Mistral-based LLMs, and analyze the relationship between inter-model parameter distance and fingerprint retention.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted using Mistral-7B as the base model. Fingerprints will be embedded into fine-tuned versions like WizardMath-7B, Shisa-7B, and Abel-7B-002. Merging experiments will be performed between these models to observe fingerprint retention. The parameter distance between each fine-tuned model and the base model will be calculated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Verification Success Rate (VSR)', 'L2 norm of parameter differences']

risk_factors: Highly memorized fingerprints with extremely low loss may still be vulnerable to adversarial attacks, such as membership inference, which exploit this property for estimation. This is one of the limitation our proposed method has.
The development of fingerprinting methods that are fully robust to fingerprint estimation remains as future work.
However, as we do not utilize formal methods or cryptographic techniques to verify the fingerprints, the process lacks formal validation for ownership claims.

Consequently, a model with embedded fingerprints that has a smaller distance from the base model may be unable to retain its own fingerprint when merged with a model that has a larger distance from the base model. We leave addressing this issue as future work.

title: Pixelated Butterfly: Simple and Efficient Sparse training for Neural Network Models
name: pixelated-butterfly
short_hypothesis: A simple, fixed sparsity pattern based on hardware-aware variants of butterfly matrices (flat block butterfly) combined with low-rank matrices can be used to sparsify most neural network layers, speeding up training from scratch without a drop in accuracy.
related_work: Existing sparse training methods face several challenges. Many methods use dynamic sparsity masks which incur significant overhead, slowing down training. Other methods use unstructured sparsity, which is inefficient on modern hardware like GPUs that are optimized for dense, block-based computations. Finally, most approaches target only a single type of operation, like attention, whereas models are composed of multiple modules (e.g., attention, MLP) that are bottlenecks.
abstract: Overparameterized neural networks generalize well but are expensive to train. Ideally, one would like to reduce their computational cost while retaining their generalization benefits. Sparse model training is a promising approach, but existing methods struggle with accuracy loss, slow training runtime, or difficulty in sparsifying all model components. The core problem is that searching for a sparsity mask is difficult and expensive. To address this, our main insight is to optimize over a continuous superset of sparse matrices with a fixed structure known as products of butterfly matrices. As butterfly matrices are not hardware efficient, we propose simple variants (block and flat) to take advantage of modern hardware. Our method (Pixelated Butterfly) uses a simple fixed sparsity pattern based on flat block butterfly and low-rank matrices to sparsify most network layers (e.g., attention, MLP).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality and efficiency of the proposed Pixelfly method on image classification tasks by comparing its training dynamics, end-to-end performance, and transfer learning capabilities against dense and other sparse models.
experiments.Method: Models (MLP-Mixer, Vision Transformer) will be trained from scratch on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet datasets. The performance will be compared to dense counterparts and other sparse training baselines like RigL, BigBird, and original Butterfly. A transfer learning experiment will also be conducted by fine-tuning an ImageNet-pretrained model on CIFAR-10.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Top-1 accuracy', 'Wall-clock training time speedup', 'Empirical Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) distance to the dense model', 'Downstream task accuracy']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of Pixelfly in the text domain, specifically on a language modeling task and a benchmark for long-context scenarios.
experiments.Method: A GPT-2 model will be trained on the WikiText-103 language modeling task. A vanilla Transformer will be trained on the Long Range Arena (LRA) benchmark. Performance will be compared against dense models and other efficient attention variants like BigBird and Reformer.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to demonstrate the necessity of each component of the Pixelfly method: the flat block butterfly structure, the low-rank component, hardware-alignment (block sparsity), and the budget allocation strategy.
experiments.Method: Different components of ViT and MLP-Mixer models will be sparsified on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. This includes: 1) varying the parameter allocation between the flat block butterfly and low-rank parts; 2) comparing against other sparsity patterns; 3) studying the effect of different block sizes on efficiency; 4) sparsifying only attention or MLP layers individually.

risk_factors: Our flat butterfly is a simple first order approximation of the rich class of butterfly matrices, and there could be more sophisticated approximations that retain more expressiveness.
Inspired by the remarkable success of model pruning for inference, it is possible that dynamic block sparse mask could be made efficient yet still accurate.
We do not use 2:4 sparsity (available on Ampere GPUs such as A100).

Though this result does not capture the possible regularization effect of sparsity, it shows that sparse models with small NTK difference from dense NTK preserve the generalization ability of dense models, a subject that has been studied more extensively, both from empirical and from theoretical perspectives.

title: LM$^2$otifs: An Explainable Framework for Machine-Generated Texts Detection
name: LM2OTIFS
short_hypothesis: Representing text as a graph of word co-occurrences and applying an eXplainable Graph Neural Network (XGNN) will enable both accurate detection of machine-generated text (MGT) and the extraction of interpretable motifs that explain the detection outcome. This graph-based approach is theoretically superior to sequential models for this task.
related_work: Numerous methods exist for detecting machine-generated text (MGT), categorized as white-box and black-box approaches. However, a significant gap remains in their explainability. Existing methods often act as black boxes, and traditional explainability techniques like Integrated Gradients are computationally prohibitive for large models, while attention mechanisms struggle to capture the global, complex word relationships that distinguish human from machine-generated text. This creates a need for a detector that is not only accurate but also provides clear, evidence-based explanations for its decisions.
abstract: The impressive ability of large language models to generate natural text has led to critical challenges in authorship authentication. Although numerous detection methods have been developed to differentiate between machine-generated texts (MGT) and human-generated texts (HGT), the explainability of these methods remains a significant gap. Traditional explainability techniques often fall short in capturing the complex word relationships that distinguish HGT from MGT. To address this limitation, we present LM2OTIFS, a novel explainable framework for MGT detection. Inspired by probabilistic graphical models, we provide a theoretical rationale for the effectiveness. LM2OTIFS utilizes eXplainable Graph Neural Networks to achieve both accurate detection and interpretability. The LM2OTIFS pipeline operates in three key stages: first, it transforms text into graphs based on word co-occurrence to represent lexical dependencies; second, graph neural networks are used for prediction; and third, a post-hoc explainability method extracts interpretable motifs, offering multi-level explanations from individual words to sentence structures.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the MGT detection performance of LM2OTIFS against state-of-the-art supervised and zero-shot detectors in both in-domain and cross-domain settings.
experiments.Method: The LM2OTIFS model, using a three-layer GCN architecture, will be evaluated on several benchmark datasets: HC3, M4, RAID, Yelp, Creative, and Essay, which include texts generated by various LLMs like ChatGPT, DaVinci, Llama2, GPT-4, Claude3, and Gemini-1.0-Pro. Performance will be compared against a comprehensive set of baselines, including Likelihood, Rank, LogRank, Entropy, DetectGPT, DetectLLM (LRR, NPR), DNAGPT, Fast-DetectGPT, Glimpse, Binoculars, GPTZero, RoBERTa-QA, Radar, and DeTeCtive.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness and faithfulness of the explainable motifs extracted by LM2OTIFS.
experiments.Method: The Most Relevant First (MoRF) and Least Relevant First (LeRF) protocols will be used to evaluate the motifs extracted from the HC3, M4, and RAID datasets. This involves measuring the model's prediction change as the most or least relevant input features (graph edges) are sequentially removed. The motifs from LM2OTIFS will be compared against a simple random motif baseline, as well as adapted explainability methods like LIME, SHAP, and GLTR.

experiments.Description: To conduct a qualitative analysis of the extracted motifs to visualize and understand the linguistic fingerprints that differentiate human-generated and machine-generated text.
experiments.Method: Motifs will be extracted from texts in the PubMed dataset generated by GPT-4 and Davinci. The graph motifs and corresponding text motifs will be visualized to analyze word-level, phrasal, and sentence-level patterns.

risk_factors: First, we have not explored the impact of different GNN architectures or hyperparameter settings on the resulting explanations.
Second, the quality of the explainable motifs is dependent on the quality of the graph representation, which in turn requires a sufficient number of training samples to construct effectively.

title: Learning on a Razor’s Edge: Identifiability and Singularity of Polynomial Neural Networks
name: polynomial-nn-identifiability-singularity
short_hypothesis: The function spaces (neuromanifolds) of deep MLPs and CNNs with polynomial activation functions can be formally analyzed using algebraic geometry to determine their identifiability, dimension, and the nature of their singular points, which are hypothesized to arise from sparse subnetworks.
related_work: Prior work has not fully characterized the geometric properties of the function spaces parameterized by neural networks. Specifically, a formal understanding of identifiability (the uniqueness of parameters for a given function) and singularities (points of geometric degeneracy) for networks with polynomial activations is lacking. This research aims to fill this gap using tools from algebraic geometry.
abstract: This work studies function spaces parametrized by neural networks, referred to as neuromanifolds. Specifically, it focuses on deep Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with an activation function that is a sufficiently generic polynomial. The goal is to address the identifiability problem and to describe singular points of these neuromanifolds. The proposed method is to leverage tools from algebraic geometry to formally analyze these properties.

experiments.Description: To address the identifiability problem in polynomial neural networks and compute the dimension of the neuromanifold.
experiments.Method: The study will involve a theoretical analysis of deep Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) that use a generic polynomial activation function. The analysis will use methods from algebraic geometry.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The analysis will assess whether, for a function in the neuromanifold, there exist finitely many parameter choices (for MLPs) or a one-to-one parameter-to-function mapping (for CNNs).

experiments.Description: To describe and characterize the singular points of neuromanifolds for polynomial MLPs and CNNs.
experiments.Method: A theoretical characterization of singularities will be performed for both CNNs and MLPs. The connection between these singularities, sparse subnetworks, and critical points of the mean-squared error loss will be investigated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The success of the characterization will be based on its completeness (e.g., complete for CNNs, partial for MLPs) and its ability to provide a geometric explanation for phenomena like the sparsity bias in MLPs.

risk_factors: We characterize singularities completely for CNNs, and partially for MLPs.

title: RESpecBench: How reliable is LLM-as-a-judge? Rigorous Evaluation of Specification Generation with Automated Verification
name: RESpecBench
short_hypothesis: A multi-domain benchmark with a sound and automated verifier can provide a more rigorous and reliable evaluation of an LLM's ability to produce precise, semantically equivalent specifications from natural language descriptions, compared to the prevailing LLM-as-a-Judge methodology, which is hypothesized to be unreliable and overestimate correctness.
related_work: Prior work on evaluating code generation (e.g., MBPP, HumanEval) often relies on unit tests, which are not always reliable for checking semantic correctness. Software verification benchmarks (e.g., CloverBench, MBPP-DFY, Verina) exist but are often restricted to a single domain, are not fully sound, or rely on the LLM itself to prove correctness. While LLM-as-a-Judge has been studied, its reliability for judging the correctness of an LLM's own generated specifications has been overlooked, largely due to the lack of verifiable ground-truth specifications. This creates a gap for a multi-domain benchmark that uses sound, automated verifiers to rigorously evaluate specification generation without relying on LLM-based judgments.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to assist formalization of natural language statements into formal specifications. Unlike syntax correctness, validating semantic correctness is particularly challenging and LLM-as-a-Judge has become the dominant assessment methodology due to its ease of use and great flexibility. However, the reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge has rarely been systematically evaluated. We introduce RESpecBench, a multi-domain benchmark with a sound and automated verifier, measuring the LLM's ability to produce precise, semantically equivalent specifications from informal natural language descriptions. RESpecBench spans five different domains, including Grade-School Math (GSM-Symbolic+), SQL, First-Order Logic (FOL), regular expressions (RegEx), and Rocq Prover tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the end-to-end performance of various state-of-the-art LLMs in generating formal specifications from natural language descriptions across multiple domains, using a sound, automated verifier as the baseline.
experiments.Method: Several state-of-the-art LLMs (including GPT-5, GPT-5 Mini, GPT-OSS, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash, and Qwen3-Next) will be prompted with natural language problems from the RESpecBench dataset. RESpecBench covers five domains: GSM-Symbolic+, SQL, FOL, RegEx, and Rocq Prover. The LLM-generated specifications will be compared against oracle specifications using domain-specific sound verifiers (Z3, Polygon, DFA equivalence, CoqHammer).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Success rate (where the verifier outputs a verdict of 'Success')"]

experiments.Description: To evaluate the reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge by comparing its verdicts against the ground-truth verdicts from the sound verifier.
experiments.Method: Using the RESpecBench dataset, two LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines will be evaluated: 1) 'Formalization Judge', where the LLM scores its own generated specification against the NL problem, and 2) 'Equivalence Judge', where the LLM scores the equivalence between its generated specification and the oracle specification. The judge's decisions will be compared to the sound verifier's decisions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Inflation (Judge success rate - Verifier success rate)', 'False Acceptance Rate (FAR)', 'False Rejection Rate (FRR)']

experiments.Description: To assess the Formalization Judge's ability to recognize correct specifications by evaluating its performance when scoring the ground-truth oracle specifications.
experiments.Method: The Formalization Judge will be prompted to score the oracle specifications from RESpecBench against their corresponding natural language prompts.

experiments.Description: To test the sensitivity of the automated verification process to the solver timeout budget, specifically whether increasing the timeout reduces the number of 'Unknown' verdicts.
experiments.Method: For the SQL and Rocq Prover domains, which are prone to timeouts, the verifier will be run with varying timeout budgets ranging from 4s to 256s on outputs from the best-performing models for those domains.

risk_factors: Some items remain Unknown on SQL tasks and Rocq Prover tasks do not return refutations, which are due to the limitations with the ATPs.
Further, our Rocq Prover domain is limited with only 31 tasks, since it is difficult finding hard yet CoqHammer verifiable specifications.
Additionally, we note that specification generation can be a vague task, given that natural language is highly open to interpretation.

title: SIGMA-Gen: Structure and Identity Guided Multi-Subject Assembly for Image Generation
name: sigma-gen
short_hypothesis: A single diffusion transformer model, trained on a large-scale synthetic dataset, can perform single-pass image generation of multiple subjects while preserving their identities and adhering to diverse spatial and structural constraints. These constraints can range from coarse 2D/3D bounding boxes to fine-grained pixel-level masks and depth maps. This is achieved by encoding identity, routing, and structure information into dedicated control images that are processed through a unified attention mechanism.
related_work: Prior work in controllable image generation falls into several categories. Structure control methods like ControlNet and T2I-Adapter can guide generation using depth or segmentation but lack mechanisms for controlling subject identity. Subject personalization methods like DreamBooth and Textual Inversion can learn a subject's identity but typically require per-subject optimization and struggle to compose multiple subjects with spatial control. In-context generation methods can use reference images but also face challenges with multiple subjects and lack structural guidance. Subject insertion methods like AnyDoor are often limited to a single subject per generation. A key gap is the lack of a unified framework that can jointly handle multi-subject identity preservation and multi-granularity structural control within a single forward pass, which is largely due to the scarcity of suitable training data.
abstract: We present SIGMA-GEN, a unified framework for multi-identity preserving image generation. Unlike prior approaches, SIGMA-GEN is the first to enable single-pass multi-subject identity-preserved generation guided by both structural and spatial constraints. A key strength of our method is its ability to support user guidance at various levels of precision — from coarse 2D or 3D boxes to pixel-level segmentations and depth — with a single model. To enable this, we introduce SIGMA-SET27K, a novel synthetic dataset that provides identity, structure, and spatial information for over 100k unique subjects across 27k images.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SIGMA-GEN on single- and multi-subject personalized generation against baselines when provided with precise control signals (masks and depth).
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on a custom evaluation dataset of 710 examples. SIGMA-GEN, based on the Flux.1 Kontext model, will be compared against several baselines. For single-subject generation, baselines will include UniCombine, OminiControl, a variant of Insert Anything (Insert Anything*), and a variant of Flux Kontext (Flux Kontext*). For multi-subject generation, the baseline will be an iterative version of Insert Anything*.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['DINO-I (image-to-image similarity for identity)', 'SigLIP-I (image-to-image similarity for identity)', 'SigLIP-T (text-to-image similarity)', 'MSE (mean squared error for depth)', 'CLIP-IQA (perceptual quality)', 'MUSIQ (perceptual quality)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SIGMA-GEN on single- and multi-subject personalized generation against baselines when provided with coarse control signals (2D bounding boxes and 3D bounding box masks).
experiments.Method: Using the same custom evaluation dataset and the single trained SIGMA-GEN model, performance will be compared against the MSDiffusion baseline for tasks involving 2D bounding box control.

experiments.Description: To analyze the scalability of SIGMA-GEN in terms of performance and runtime as the number of subjects increases, and compare it to an iterative baseline approach.
experiments.Method: The performance of SIGMA-GEN will be measured on generations with an increasing number of subjects (from 2 to 7). This will be compared against the Iterative Insert Anything* baseline under the same conditions.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the impact of different guidance components on the model's performance in the precise mask and depth scenario.
experiments.Method: The performance of the model will be tested under several conditions: 1) removing depth control entirely while keeping masks, 2) providing a full scene description in the text prompt versus a background-only prompt. A further ablation will probe different methods of providing depth control: via the unified structure control image (subject-only or full depth) versus using an external pre-trained ControlNet.

risk_factors: In terms of subject consistency, measured by DINO-I and SigLIP-I, SIGMA-GEN experiences some degradation as the number of subjects increases—largely due to dataset distribution effects.

title: Exploring General Intelligence of Program Analysis for Multiple Tasks
name: multi-graph-assembly-embedding
short_hypothesis: A program embedding methodology based on assembly code, which represents a program using multiple graph structures (Control Flow Graph, Call Graph, and Data Flow Graph), can create a general representation suitable for diverse program analysis tasks, including both semantics-based and compilation-related problems.
related_work: Previous machine learning approaches for program analysis often treat code as text sequences or use Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs), which ignore rich structural information and long-distance dependencies. Other methods that use Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) typically rely on a single graph structure, such as a control flow graph. Furthermore, representations built from source code or ASTs lack compile-time and architecture-specific information, making them unsuitable for tasks like binary similarity detection across different compilation configurations. This work aims to address this gap by creating a program representation from assembly code that fuses information from multiple graph structures (CFG, CG, DFG) to capture richer structural and semantic details, including compilation-specific characteristics.
abstract: As the program scale ever grows, program analysis tasks become increasingly diverse and complex. Effective learning-based program analysis tasks need to consider many factors including not only program structures and semantics but also running platforms and compilation configurations. Existing works are oftentimes on a basis of source code or abstract syntax tree, but unfortunately lack sufficient program structure and semantic information to unveil program insights. To this end, we propose a new program analysis model that takes advantage of graph neural networks at the level of assembly code. The model aims to solve both traditional program semantics-based problems and compilation-related problems by fusing the semantics of the program's instructions and the existence of multiple graph structures within programs.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to capture the key functional information of a program, it will be tested on a program classification task.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the POJ-104 dataset, which contains programs from 104 different classes. Programs will be compiled using GCC and LLVM with -O2 and -O3 optimization levels. The proposed model (MTM) will generate an embedding for each program, which is then fed to a softmax classifier. The performance will be compared against the Ins2vec baseline. Ablation studies are also planned to evaluate the contribution of individual graph components (CFG, CG, DFG).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to handle compilation-related problems, it will be tested on binary similarity detection, which is composed of two subtasks: cross-configuration binary code detection and compilation option classification.
experiments.Method: Using binaries generated from the POJ-104 dataset, the first subtask will train the model in a siamese framework to predict if two binaries, compiled with different configurations, originate from the same source code. Performance will be compared to the Order Matters Model (OM). The second subtask will be a multi-class classification task to predict the compilation option (GCC-O2, GCC-O3, LLVM-O2, LLVM-O3) of a given binary.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Similarity distance (for cross-configuration detection)', 'Classification accuracy (for compilation classification)']

risk_factors: Furthermore, these graphs are relative large for the efficient graph-based deep learning models (e.g., graph neural networks) to run. As tested, the number of nodes and edges of the DFG can be as high as 10847 nodes and 26910 edges. These large-scale graphs incur tediously long training time using graph neural networks.
However, procedure-level analysis tasks such as function name prediction can also be solved by the proposed model, which we will leave as future work.

title: Diffusion-Based Planning for Autonomous Driving with Flexible Guidance
name: diffusion-planner-for-driving
short_hypothesis: A novel transformer-based Diffusion Planner can effectively model multi-modal driving behavior and ensure high-quality trajectories for closed-loop planning without rule-based refinement. By learning the gradient of the trajectory score function and using a flexible classifier guidance mechanism, the model can achieve safe, cooperative, and adaptable planning behaviors.
related_work: Contemporary learning-based planning methods, such as those using imitation learning, often struggle to model complex, multi-modal human driving behaviors, leading to error accumulation in closed-loop scenarios. This forces a reliance on rule-based fallback strategies for trajectory refinement and safety, which limits adaptability. Furthermore, these methods lack flexibility for post-training adjustments to meet specific driving preferences. While diffusion models have been used in related domains like motion prediction, their potential for high-quality, closed-loop autonomous planning without rule-based post-processing has not been fully explored.
abstract: Achieving human-like driving behaviors in complex open-world environments is a critical challenge in autonomous driving. Contemporary learning-based planning approaches such as imitation learning methods often struggle to balance competing objectives and lack of safety assurance, due to limited adaptability and inadequacy in learning complex multi-modal behaviors commonly exhibited in human planning, not to mention their strong reliance on the fallback strategy with predefined rules. We propose a novel transformer-based Diffusion Planner for closed-loop planning, which can effectively model multi-modal driving behavior and ensure trajectory quality without any rule-based refinement. Our model supports joint modeling of both prediction and planning tasks under the same architecture, enabling cooperative behaviors between vehicles. Moreover, by learning the gradient of the trajectory score function and employing a flexible classifier guidance mechanism, Diffusion Planner is designed to achieve safe and adaptable planning behaviors.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed Diffusion Planner against state-of-the-art planning methods on a large-scale, real-world autonomous planning benchmark.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the nuPlan benchmark, using the Val14, Test14, and Test14-hard splits in both closed-loop non-reactive and reactive modes. The proposed 'Diffusion Planner' (and a variant 'Diffusion Planner w/ refine.') will be compared against three categories of baselines: Rule-based (IDM, PDM-Closed), Learning-based (PDM-Open, UrbanDriver, GameFormer w/o refine., PlanTF, PLUTO w/o refine.), and Hybrid (PDM-Hybrid, GameFormer, PLUTO).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A final score calculated as the average across all scenarios, ranging from 0 to 100.']

experiments.Description: To validate the algorithm's performance and transferability across diverse driving scenarios and vehicle types using a newly collected dataset.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on a newly collected 200-hour real-world driving dataset from a delivery vehicle, which is integrated into the nuPlan framework. This dataset features a smaller vehicle with more conservative behavior and operation in bike lanes. The proposed 'Diffusion Planner' will be compared against the same set of baselines (IDM, PDM-Closed, PDM-Hybrid, GameFormer, PLUTO, PDM-Open, GameFormer w/o refine., PlanTF, PLUTO w/o refine.), retrained on the new dataset.

experiments.Description: To empirically study the properties of the Diffusion Planner, including its ability to model multi-modal behavior and respond to flexible guidance.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies will be performed to assess the contribution of key design choices for training (z-score normalization, interpolation, data augmentation, ego state handling, number of predicted vehicles) and inference (denoise steps, temperature). The model's ability to generate multi-modal trajectories (e.g., left turn, right turn, straight) from the same initial position will be tested. The effectiveness of the classifier guidance mechanism will be evaluated by applying different energy functions at inference time to control behaviors like target speed maintenance, comfort, collision avoidance, and staying within the drivable area.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative visualization of trajectories', 'Closed-loop planning score on nuPlan benchmarks']

risk_factors: Our method relies on vectorized map information and detection results of neighboring vehicles. Compared to mainstream end-to-end pipelines, this approach involves some information loss and requires a data processing module.

We find that learning-based methods struggle with flexibility, particularly when significant lateral movement is required. In contrast, rule-based methods perform better in this aspect due to the provision of a reference trajectory.
The high performance of Diffusion comes at the cost of requiring multiple model inferences, leading to reduced sample efficiency.

title: FairCoT: Enhancing Fairness in Diffusion Models via Chain of Thought Reasoning of Multimodal Language Models
name: FairCoT
short_hypothesis: A framework, FairCoT, can enhance fairness in diffusion models by using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning within multimodal generative large language models (LLMs), employing iterative CoT refinement and attire-based attribute prediction to systematically mitigate biases.
related_work: In the domain of text-to-image generative models, biases inherent in training datasets often propagate into generated content. Existing approaches like zero-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT) have limitations in addressing these biases in sensitive scenarios.
abstract: In the domain of text-to-image generative models, biases inherent in training datasets often propagate into generated content, posing significant ethical challenges, particularly in socially sensitive contexts. We introduce FairCoT, a novel framework that enhances fairness in diffusion models through Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning within multimodal generative large language models (LLMs). FairCoT employs iterative CoT refinement and attire-based attribute prediction to systematically mitigate biases, ensuring diverse and equitable representation in generated images. By integrating iterative reasoning processes, FairCoT addresses the limitations of zero-shot CoT in sensitive scenarios, balancing creativity with ethical responsibility.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if FairCoT can improve fairness and diversity in generated images without compromising image quality or relevance.
experiments.Method: The FairCoT framework will be evaluated across multiple text-to-image models, including DALL-E and various Stable Diffusion variants.

title: Certified Robustness Under Bounded Levenshtein Distance
name: lipschitz-based-levenshtein-certification
short_hypothesis: By computing an upper bound on the Lipschitz constant of convolutional classifiers with respect to the Edit-Robustness-Proxy (ERP) distance, and using this to train 1-Lipschitz classifiers, it is possible to provide deterministic robustness certificates against Levenshtein distance perturbations efficiently in a single forward pass.
related_work: Existing verification methods for text classifiers are either probabilistic (e.g., randomized smoothing) or deterministic. Deterministic methods like Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) are limited as they assume a fixed-length input space, making them unable to handle insertions and deletions inherent to the Levenshtein distance. They are also computationally expensive, requiring multiple forward passes. Lipschitz constant-based verification, while successful in the image domain for lp-spaces, cannot be trivially applied to NLP due to the discrete and variable-length nature of text inputs. This creates a research gap for a fast, deterministic verification method that can certify robustness against Levenshtein distance constraints.
abstract: Text classifiers suffer from small perturbations, that if chosen adversarially, can dramatically change the output of the model. Verification methods can provide robustness certificates against such adversarial perturbations, by computing a sound lower bound on the robust accuracy. Nevertheless, existing verification methods incur in prohibitive costs and cannot practically handle Levenshtein distance constraints. We propose the first method for computing the Lipschitz constant of convolutional classifiers with respect to the Levenshtein distance. We use these Lipschitz constant estimates for training 1-Lipschitz classifiers. This enables computing the certified radius of a classifier in a single forward pass.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of the proposed verification method (LipsLev) against existing approaches for certifying robustness under Levenshtein distance.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained and verified on sentence classification tasks using the AG-News, SST-2, IMDB, and Fake-News datasets. The model architecture is a convolutional classifier with a single convolutional layer, character-level embeddings, and a ReLU activation function. The proposed method, LipsLev, will be compared against a brute-force verification baseline and a modified version of the Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) method adapted to handle insertions and deletions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of enforcing a 1-Lipschitz constraint during training versus regularizing the Lipschitz constant as an alternative training strategy.
experiments.Method: On the SST-2 dataset, single-layer models will be trained using two different strategies: 1) enforcing a 1-Lipschitz property on each layer as proposed (Eq. 7), and 2) standard training with an added regularization term on the global Lipschitz constant `G` with varying regularization strengths (λ).

experiments.Description: To study the performance of the proposed 1-Lipschitz training formulation on models with more than one convolutional layer.
experiments.Method: On the AG-News and SST-2 datasets, models with 1, 2, 3, and 4 convolutional layers will be trained using the 1-Lipschitz formulation from Eq. (7).

risk_factors: A problem shared with verification methods in the image domain is scalability (Wang et al., 2021).

Even though our method is the first to practically provide Levenshtein distance certificates in NLP, neither the formulation of Huang et al. (2019) or our formulation covers modern architectures as Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017).

Tokenizers: Modern Transformer-based classifiers utilize popular tokenizers such as SentencePiece (Kudo and Richardson, 2018), which aggregate contiguous characters in tokens before feeding them to the model. In order to deal with such non-differentiable piece, methods for computing the Lipschitz constant of tokenizers are needed.

Poor performance on character-level tasks: In the case no tokenizer is used, transformers are known to fail in character-level classification tasks like the IMDB classification problem of Long Range Arena (Tay et al., 2021).
Non-Lipschitzness of Transformers: Transformers are known to have a non-bounded Lipschitz constant (Kim et al., 2021).

title: A Modulation Layer to Increase Neural Network Robustness Against Data Quality Issues
name: Modulation-Layer-for-Data-Quality
short_hypothesis: A novel neural network modification, inspired by biological neuromodulation, can mitigate the impacts of missing data. This is achieved by replacing the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (e.g., a reliability score), which is jointly learned with the main task using a multi-layer perceptron. This allows the network to up- and down-weight inputs based on their quality.
related_work: Prior work on handling missing data includes various imputation methods, from simple techniques like mean imputation to more complex ones like chained equations and deep learning-based generative models (e.g., GANs, VAEs). Another line of work incorporates uncertainty into deep networks using Bayesian methods or Gaussian processes. However, these approaches can be complex, assumption-laden, or may not leverage meaningful missingness patterns effectively. The proposed modulation layer offers a more direct mechanism to handle data quality issues by dynamically adjusting input weights based on reliability, which is distinct from imputation or complex probabilistic modeling.
abstract: Data quality is a common problem in machine learning, especially in high-stakes settings such as healthcare. Missing data affects accuracy, calibration, and feature attribution in complex patterns. While some classifiers naturally handle missing data, others, such as deep neural networks, are not designed for unknown values. Making machine learning models robust to missing data is therefore crucial for practical applications. We propose a novel neural network modification to mitigate the impacts of missing data. The approach is inspired by neuromodulation that is performed by biological neural networks. Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input, mimicking the ability of cortex to up- and down-weight inputs based on the presence of other data. The modulation function is jointly learned with the main task using a multi-layer perceptron.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the proposed Modulated Fully Connected Layer (MFCL and MFCL+) in classification tasks where missing value flags are used as the modulating signal.
experiments.Method: The experiments are run on the ACTFAST dataset (for 30-day Mortality, Acute Kidney Injury, and Heart Attack prediction) and the Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset. The proposed MFCL and MFCL+ layers are tested in the place of fully-connected layers at the input level of MLP architectures. Baselines include MLPs fed with imputed values from various algorithms (chained regression with Bayesian ridge, missForest, GAIN, VAEAC), a graphical network model (GRAPE), and an MLP with mean imputation and a concatenated missingness indicator (FC+Mod).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area under receiver operating curve (AUROC)', 'Area under precision and recall curve (AUPRC)']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the modulation layer when using input reliability (quantified by noise level) as the modulating signal, instead of binary missing flags.
experiments.Method: This experiment uses the complete Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset. Gaussian noise with a variable standard deviation (SD) is added to the input features. The SD value is used as the modulating reliability signal for the proposed MFCL and MFCL+ layers. The models are compared against the same baseline architectures used in the other classification tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area under receiver operating curve (AUROC)', 'Area under precision and recall curve (AUPRC)']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the MFCL and MFCL+ layers for data imputation within an autoencoder architecture.
experiments.Method: The experiments use the ACTFAST, Breast Cancer, and Boston datasets. The MFCL and MFCL+ layers are added in place of standard fully-connected layers at the input of an autoencoder imputation system. The baseline is a standard autoencoder with fully-connected layers.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the proposed MFCL and MFCL+ layers in a regression task with missing data.
experiments.Method: This experiment uses the Boston housing dataset. The MFCL and MFCL+ layers are added in place of fully-connected layers at the input of MLP networks, using missing flags as the modulating signal. Baselines are two MLPs: one with input imputed via chained regression (Iterative Imputer) and another using constant value imputation with a concatenated missingness indicator (FC+Mod).

risk_factors: One other limitation is the lack of availability of large open tabular datasets with high missingness which limits the ability to generalize our findings.
To make our experiments with informative missingness comparable across features, we restricted our input space to numeric variables and discarded categorical variables.
We tested the application of the modulation process only in fully connected layers which are limited by nature in the types of data that they can handle.
We did not search over regularization strategies of the modulation network, which may further improve its performance.
Preliminary experiments placing MFCL layers deep in the autoencoder experiments did not yield visible improvement (data not shown).

title: What Is Missing: Interpretable Ratings for Large Language Model Outputs
name: What Is Missing (WIM) Ratings
short_hypothesis: A rating system based on the cosine similarity between a model's output and natural language feedback describing what's 'missing' will produce a better rating distribution than standard numerical ratings. This will lead to improved performance in preference learning by providing a clearer, more interpretable, and less sparse learning signal, resulting in lower training loss and better reward advantage scaling.
related_work: Current preference learning methods, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), rely on direct rankings or numerical ratings of model outputs. These approaches are subjective, lack interpretability, and often result in a poor learning signal because it is difficult to distill the worth of a complex answer into a single number. This can lead to many outputs receiving the same rating, preventing the generation of a learning signal. The key shortcomings of existing systems are the low interpretability of ratings and the issue of identical ratings for distinct outputs hindering the learning process.
abstract: Current Large Language Model (LLM) preference learning methods rely on direct rankings or numerical ratings of model outputs to learn human preferences. These rankings are subjective, and a single numerical rating is a poor metric to quantify a complex system like human language. This paper introduces the What Is Missing (WIM) rating system to create better rankings for preference learning methods. WIM is a straightforward method that can be integrated into existing training pipelines. To create a WIM rating, natural language feedback for a model output is given by a human or LLM judge. Both the output and the feedback are passed through a sentence embedding model, and the cosine similarity between the high-dimensional vectors is calculated. The proposed method is designed to be interpretable and to improve the preference data generation pipeline.

experiments.Description: To test the performance difference between the numerical rating system and the proposed WIM rating system during LLM fine-tuning.
experiments.Method: A Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct model will be fine-tuned using the ultrafeedback-prompt dataset with Online Direct Preference Optimization (ODPO). The experiment will compare the WIM rating system against a standard 1-10 numerical rating system and a random judge baseline. Two variants of WIM will be tested: one using a 'Changing Judge' (the model being trained) and one using a 'Fixed Judge'. The `all-mpnet-base-v2` model will be used for sentence embeddings.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training loss (DPO loss)', 'Mean model entropy', 'Reward advantage', 'Win rate against the base Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct model, judged by gpt-4o-mini on completions from the ultrafeedback-prompt test dataset']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the general capabilities of the models trained with different rating methods on a suite of standard benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The models trained with the Random, Numerical, WIM Changing Judge, and WIM Fixed Judge rating methods, along with the base Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct model, will be evaluated on several benchmark datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['BBH score', 'GPQA Diamond Zeroshot score', 'IfEval score', 'MMLU score']

risk_factors: Exploring the limitations of WIM while using an LLM as a judge. Analysis around the instruction following abilities of the judge and the prompt engineering required for the judge to perform the correct task.
WIM fails due to the poor instruction following of the judge LLM. The WIM feedback in the second response is given as 'wim' and demonstrates potential issues with using an LLM as judge.

title: Contextual Latent World Models for Offline Meta Reinforcement Learning
name: contextual-discrete-codebook-world-models
