short_hypothesis: Introducing Contextual Discrete Codebook World Models (C-DCWM), which conditions a discrete latent world model on a task representation from a context encoder. Jointly training the world model and the context encoder is hypothesized to yield task representations that better capture variation factors across tasks, improving generalization to out-of-distribution tasks in offline meta-RL.
related_work: Offline meta-RL (OMRL) aims to learn generalizable policies from static datasets. A common approach is context-based OMRL, which uses a context encoder to infer a task representation from transition histories to condition a policy. Separately, latent world models have advanced representation learning by mapping observations to a compact latent space and learning dynamics there. Prior OMRL methods like UNICORN use predictive models in observation space, but this work proposes to unify these two fields by extending discrete codebook world models (DCWM) to the context-based OMRL setting, conditioning the latent world model itself on the inferred task context.
abstract: Offline meta-reinforcement learning seeks to overcome the challenges of poor generalization and expensive data collection by leveraging datasets for related tasks. Context encoding is a prevalent approach, where an encoder maps transition histories to a task representation. In parallel, latent world models – which map observations into temporally consistent latent spaces – advanced self-supervised representation learning for planning and policy optimization. In this work, we unify these directions by introducing contextual latent world models: world models conditioned on the task representation and trained jointly with the context encoder. Coupling task inference with predictive modeling is proposed to yield task representations that capture variation factors across tasks.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the performance of C-DCWM in few-shot and zero-shot generalization settings on in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and new environment tasks.
experiments.Method: C-DCWM is compared against baseline methods FOCAL, CSRO, DORA, UNICORN-SS, and UNICORN-SUP. The evaluation is planned on a set of multi-task environments from MuJoCo, Contextual DeepMind Control, and Meta-World (ML1, ML10, ML45) benchmarks. Datasets are to be generated using the Dropout Q-function (DroQ) algorithm.

experiments.Description: Compare different latent space formulations to investigate the effect of classification loss, discrete codebooks, and bounding on performance.
experiments.Method: Ablation study on MuJoCo and Contextual-DMC benchmarks comparing different latent space formulations: (i) classification loss (cross-entropy) vs. regression loss (MSE, cosine similarity), (ii) discrete vs. continuous latent states, and (iii) bounded (e.g., SimNorm) vs. unbounded latent states.

experiments.Description: Evaluate how different objectives for training the context encoder affect performance and the quality of the learned task representation.
experiments.Method: Ablation study comparing different training objectives for the context encoder: a contrastive objective only (InfoNCE or FOCAL), a world model objective only (WM), or a combination of both. The evaluation is planned on MuJoCo, Contextual-DMC, and Meta-World benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average returns/success rates', 'Disentanglement metrics (DCI: disentanglement, completeness, informativeness)', 'InfoMEC metrics (modularity, explicitness, compactness)']

experiments.Description: Investigate the importance of bounding the task representation for generalization performance.
experiments.Method: Ablation study on C-DCWM comparing different methods for bounding the task representation vector 'z': Tanh activation, l2-normalization, Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ), and an unbounded identity function. The evaluation is planned on selected MuJoCo and Meta-World environments.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the impact of different underlying offline RL algorithms for policy optimization within the C-DCWM framework.
experiments.Method: Ablation study comparing the performance of C-DCWM when using different policy optimization algorithms: Implicit Q-Learning (IQL), Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), and TD3+BC. The evaluation is planned on MuJoCo and Meta-World environments.

risk_factors: Extending our framework to accommodate different state and action spaces across tasks is a promising direction for future research.
Furthermore, we evaluate C-DCWM in environments with only state information; in the future, the observation encoder architecture could be modified to support visual observations.

title: From Counseling Transcript to Mind Map: Leveraging LLMs for Effective Summarization in Mental Health Counseling
name: llm-counseling-mindmap-summarization
short_hypothesis: Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically GPT-4o Mini, can be leveraged through one-shot prompting to effectively summarize counseling transcripts and generate them as visual-based mind maps in PlantUML syntax. This visual format is proposed as an innovative method to enhance comprehension and reduce cognitive load for therapists compared to conventional text-based summaries.
related_work: Prior research in dialogue summarization has predominantly focused on generating text-based summaries, which can be cognitively demanding for therapists to interpret. While information visualization techniques have been applied in mental health, they are typically designed for patients and visualize quantitative data like mood trends. There is a research gap in developing visualization tools specifically for practitioners that can handle the complex, qualitative data from counseling sessions. The recent ability of LLMs to generate structured output based on instructions presents a new opportunity to create visual summaries from this type of data, which has been underexplored.
abstract: The increasing number of patients with mental health illness has heightened the cognitive load on therapists, making it challenging to provide personalized care. While summarizing counseling sessions can aid practitioners, existing research focuses on text-based summaries which require significant cognitive effort. Visual-based summaries like mind maps can enhance cognitive understanding, but generating them from complex qualitative data is challenging for traditional AI. Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate the capability to perform tasks based on instructions and generate outputs in various formats. In this study, we propose to develop a web-based summarization tool that serves as a pipeline for summarizing counseling transcripts into visual-based mind map summaries using LLMs.

experiments.Description: To assess the quality of LLM-generated visual summaries of counseling transcripts by comparing them against human-written text-based summaries.
experiments.Method: A web-based tool will use the GPT-4o Mini model to summarize counseling transcripts. The model will be prompted using a one-shot learning technique, providing an example and instructions to summarize the transcript with a focus on the patient's responses and to format the output in PlantUML mind map syntax. The experiment will be run on 20 randomly sub-sampled transcripts from the testing set of the MEMO dataset. The generated PlantUML syntax will then be converted into a visual mind map. Human reviewers will be asked to evaluate the quality of these 20 generated visual summaries by comparing them with the corresponding text-based reference summaries provided in the dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy: How well the visual-based summary reflects the key information from the text-based summary.', 'Completeness: Whether the visual-based summary captures all important points from the text-based summary.', 'Conciseness: Whether the visual-based summary is presented without unnecessary details or redundancy.', 'Coherence: Whether the visual-based summary is organized in a logical and understandable way.']

risk_factors: While participants from our human evaluation provided valuable perspectives, the limited number of respondents restricts the generalizability of the results.
Additionally, the participants' backgrounds are primarily in technical fields related to LLMs, rather than in mental health.

As a result, our study lacks input from professionals or actual users within the mental health sector, which may limit the relevance of the findings to the specific needs and experiences of the mental health community.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Bandits with Continuous Actions and Large Context Spaces
name: rl-for-continuous-contextual-bandits
short_hypothesis: By modifying reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms for continuous control, specifically using one-step deterministic policy gradients, we can create agents that solve contextual bandit problems with continuous actions and large context spaces (e.g., images), outperforming existing hand-crafted, tree-based contextual bandit algorithms.
related_work: Well-established methods for contextual bandits exist for discrete action spaces. For continuous actions, research has focused on methods like discretization, which suffers from the curse of dimensionality, and more recently, tree-based policies that smooth over a discretized action space (e.g., CATS). However, these tree-based methods have not been thoroughly analyzed for large context spaces like images. While deep contextual bandits have been proposed for large contexts, they have been limited to discrete actions. This work addresses the under-explored intersection of continuous actions and large context spaces, motivated by applications like personalized healthcare.
abstract: We consider the challenging scenario of contextual bandits with continuous actions and large context spaces, e.g. images. We posit that by modifying reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms for continuous control, we can outperform hand-crafted contextual bandit algorithms for continuous actions on standard benchmark datasets, i.e. vector contexts. We propose to demonstrate that parametric policy networks can be applied to this problem. Furthermore, we aim to show that RL algorithms can generalise contextual bandit problems with continuous actions to large context spaces. Lastly, we introduce a new contextual bandits domain with multi-dimensional continuous action space and image contexts which existing methods cannot handle.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed RL agent against state-of-the-art methods on standard contextual bandit benchmarks with vector contexts and a single continuous action dimension.
experiments.Method: The proposed RL agent, based on a modified DDPG algorithm for the one-step bandit setting, will be evaluated on four OpenML datasets: Wisconsin, CPU_act, Zurich Delays 5%, and Black friday. The performance will be compared against several baselines: CATS (a tree-based policy method), CATX (a JAX re-implementation of CATS), dLinear (a discretized e-greedy algorithm), and dTree (a discretized tree-based algorithm equivalent to CATS with zero bandwidth).

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the proposed RL agent's ability to handle high-dimensional image contexts and multi-dimensional continuous actions, settings where existing methods are expected to struggle.
experiments.Method: The agent will be evaluated on two image-based domains. First, a single-action regression task on the MNIST dataset, where it will be compared to CATX. Second, a new 'Tanks' benchmark domain will be introduced, featuring multi-dimensional (3D) continuous actions and image contexts. In the Tanks domain, the agent will be evaluated using both vector and image contexts under two different loss functions (smooth and sparse) and compared against a random agent.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Held-out costs (on MNIST)', 'Online regret (on Tanks)', 'Offline held-out costs (on Tanks)']

risk_factors: The adoption of nonlinear function approximators often reduces any theoretical guarantees of convergence, but works well in practice.
Whilst the motivation for this work comes from personalised healthcare, we leave the application of our method to medical images for future work.
Similarly, for even larger context spaces such as 3D scans, we leave integrating a pre-trained image representation or a curriculum for future work.

title: LUM-ViT: Learnable Under-sampling Mask Vision Transformer for Bandwidth Limited Optical Signal Acquisition
name: lum-vit-optical-undersampling
short_hypothesis: A Vision Transformer variant, LUM-ViT, can be designed with a learnable under-sampling mask to perform pre-acquisition optical modulation. This approach, integrated with a Digital Micromirror Device (DMD), is intended to reduce the acquisition volume for bandwidth-limited signals like hyperspectral data while preserving task performance.
related_work: Bandwidth constraints are a major hurdle in applications like hyperspectral imaging. Traditional methods use feature extraction for dimensionality reduction post-acquisition. Compressive Sensing (CS) allows for pre-acquisition modulation but is limited in its ability to leverage prior information and is ineffective at very low sampling rates. While deep learning models like CNNs and ViTs excel at processing such data, they typically assume negligible acquisition costs and operate post-acquisition. Existing Transformer pruning methods are not suitable for pre-acquisition under-sampling as they require the full object data to be captured first. This work proposes to bridge the gap by integrating a deep learning model directly with optical hardware (DMD) for pre-acquisition modulation.
abstract: Bandwidth constraints during signal acquisition frequently impede real-time detection applications. Hyperspectral data is a notable example, whose vast volume compromises real-time hyperspectral detection. To tackle this hurdle, we introduce a novel approach leveraging pre-acquisition modulation to reduce the acquisition volume. This modulation process is governed by a deep learning model, utilizing prior information. Central to our approach is LUM-ViT, a Vision Transformer variant. Uniquely, LUM-ViT incorporates a learnable under-sampling mask tailored for pre-acquisition modulation. To further optimize for optical calculations, we propose a kernel-level weight binarization technique and a three-stage fine-tuning strategy.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed LUM-ViT model on a large-scale image classification benchmark under various under-sampling rates.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset for the Top-1 classification task. The LUM-ViT model, based on a pre-trained MAE-base model, will be tested at under-sampling rates of 20%, 10%, 5%, and 2%. It will be compared against several baselines: a Directly Under-sampling ViT (DU-ViT), Random-mask-ViT, Magnitude-based masking (Mag-mask-ViT), and a traditional Compressive Sensing (CS) method.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the practical feasibility of LUM-ViT by deploying it on a real-world optical signal acquisition system.
experiments.Method: A real-world application experiment will be conducted using a hardware setup comprising a Digital Micromirror Device (DMD), a spectrometer, and a 7-color e-ink display. The LUM-ViT-2% model will be used to classify random images from the ImageNet-1k test set, which have been color-reduced to 7 colors to be displayed on the e-ink screen.

experiments.Description: To ascertain whether LUM-ViT can handle real-world hyperspectral data with richer spectral information.
experiments.Method: The model's performance will be evaluated on three remote sensing datasets used for hyperspectral image classification: Indian Pines, Pavia University, and Salinas. The task is pixel classification. The LUM-ViT configuration will be adjusted for the task, including matching input channels to the datasets' spectral channels and setting the patch size to 9.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to validate the key methodological design choices of LUM-ViT.
experiments.Method: Using the LUM-ViT-10% model on the ImageNet-1k dataset, several variants will be tested: 1) using full-layer binarization instead of kernel-level, 2) zeroing out masked points instead of using a learnable token, 3) using L1-norm for the mask loss instead of MSE, and 4) removing the Linear layer that follows the masking parameters.

risk_factors: Attempts to concurrently train the binary layer and the mask have thus been unsuccessful.
Future research could explore dynamic mask strategies and extend this framework to other tasks.

title: ShotSight: Explaining KGE Models with an LLM-Ready, Example-Based Heuristic
name: shotsight-kge-explanations
short_hypothesis: A novel case-based reasoning heuristic, ShotSight, can generate compelling, human-understandable, example-based explanations for Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) model predictions by leveraging the latent space representation of nodes and edges. This approach is intended to be more time-efficient than existing methods and produce explanations that are suitable for few-shot prompting in Large Language Models (LLMs).
related_work: Existing explainability methods for KGE models, such as ExplaiNE, GNNExplainer, GradientRollback (GR), and KGEx, primarily focus on identifying influential links or subgraphs whose removal would decrease a prediction's probability. However, methods like GR can be computationally expensive and memory-intensive, making them prohibitive for large-scale graphs. Furthermore, existing methods do not explicitly address integration with Large Language Models for tasks like few-shot prompting. This work aims to fill this gap by proposing a time-efficient heuristic for generating example-based explanations that are also 'LLM-ready'.
abstract: This article tackles the critical challenge of explainability in Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) models. We introduce a novel case-based reasoning approach called ShotSight, that leverages the latent space representation of nodes and edges in a knowledge graph to generate compelling, human-understandable, example-based explanations for link predictions. The motivation is to provide effective explanations and also to demonstrate that this technique can serve as a valuable aid in constructing relevant “shots” for few-shot prompting within Large Language Models (LLMs), effectively making KGE models LLM-ready.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of the explanation triples identified by ShotSight on a KGE model's predictions using a remove-and-retrain protocol. This experiment aims to test the hypothesis that removing the identified influential triples will cause a larger drop in the target triple's plausibility score compared to removing random triples.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use TransE and ComplEx models trained on the Fb15k-237 and WN18RR datasets. For a given target triple, the ShotSight method will be used to find influential explanation triples. Following the Remove-and-Retrain (ROAR) protocol, these explanation triples will be removed from the training dataset, and the model will be retrained. The outcome will be compared to a baseline where an equal number of random triples sharing the same predicate as the target triple are removed instead.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the sufficiency of the explanation triples identified by ShotSight for a KGE model to make a prediction. This experiment tests if a model can recover its predictive ability for a target triple when trained on a dataset containing only the identified influential examples for that predicate.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use TransE and ComplEx models on the Fb15k-237 and WN18RR datasets. Using a Reversed-Remove-and-Retrain (rev-ROAR) protocol, all triples with the same predicate as the target triple will be removed from the training set, and then only the influential explanation triples identified by ShotSight will be added back. The model will be retrained on this modified, sparse dataset.

experiments.Description: To create and validate a novel XAI test set for assessing the interpretability of explanations from KGE models, intended for future user studies.
experiments.Method: A subset of the Fb15k-237 benchmark dataset will be curated. The process involves scoring all triples with a trained model and selecting high-scoring triples that are deemed human-readable by multiple evaluators. This creates a new dataset, XAI-Fb15k-237.

risk_factors: One disadventage, that we are aware of, is that our approach is a heuristics, we are doing some follow-up research on how to provide estimation guarantees for this approach and if it is even possible.

Another limitation of ShotSight is its dependence on the quality of past cases and the effectiveness of criticism strategies used in example-based explanations Kim et al. (2016) which we indirectly support in the aggregation step.

title: Unlocking the Power of Function Vectors for Characterizing and Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Continual Instruction Tuning
name: fv-for-catastrophic-forgetting
short_hypothesis: Catastrophic forgetting in Large Language Models (LLMs) during continual instruction tuning is primarily caused by biases in function activation, rather than the overwriting of task-processing functions. This phenomenon can be characterized by changes in a model's 'function vector' (FV). Therefore, a novel training methodology that regularizes the FV to maintain the correct mapping of inputs to function vectors will mitigate forgetting.
related_work: Existing research on catastrophic forgetting in LLMs typically analyzes performance drops on specific skills through a single training sequence, failing to connect forgetting to the characteristics of the training data. There is a gap in understanding the internal mechanisms of forgetting. While some work has explored task conflicts, the literature lacks robust tools to track and dissect the underlying processes. Traditional methods like feature similarity or L2 parameter distance are insufficient for characterizing forgetting in LLMs, as they either overlook model-dependent information or require full training to compute.
abstract: Catastrophic forgetting (CF) poses a significant challenge in machine learning, where a model forgets previously learned information upon learning new tasks. Despite the advanced capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), they continue to face challenges with CF during continual learning. This study explores CF across various settings, proposing to interpret forgetting by examining the function vector (FV), a compact representation of functions in LLMs, as a model-dependent indicator for the occurrence of CF. The central hypothesis is that CF in LLMs primarily stems from biases in function activation rather than the overwriting of task processing functions. Leveraging these insights, we propose a novel function vector guided training methodology, incorporating a regularization technique to stabilize the FV and mitigate forgetting.

experiments.Description: To empirically examine when and how catastrophic forgetting occurs in LLMs during continual instruction tuning, investigating the influence of task types, training stages, and different language models.
experiments.Method: Four language models (Llama2-7b-chat, Llama2-13B-chat, Llama3-8B-chat, Mistral-7B-instruct-v2.0) will be continually fine-tuned using LoRA on six task sequences derived from the SuperNI benchmark. These sequences consist of pure generation tasks, pure classification tasks, and mixed tasks (NI-Seq-C1, NI-Seq-G1, NI-Seq-M1, etc.).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['GP (General Performance): Average zero-shot performance on general evaluation tasks (Hellaswag, CommonsenseQA, Alpaca, BBH-Object-Count).', 'IP (In-context Performance): Average in-context performance on the same general tasks.', 'FP (Final Performance): Average zero-shot performance across all instruction tuning tasks after the final tuning step.', 'AP (Average Performance): Average zero-shot performance when learning each instruction tuning task.', 'Forget: The difference between AP and FP (AP - FP).']

experiments.Description: To establish and validate the correlation between shifts in the Function Vector (FV) and model forgetting, testing the hypothesis that FV similarity can serve as an effective indicator of forgetting.
experiments.Method: During the continual learning process from the first experiment, the performance on four general evaluation tasks will be tracked. Concurrently, the cosine similarity between the function vector at each training step and the initial function vector (Cosine(θ_j^Te, θ_0^Te)) will be calculated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Correlation between the performance on evaluation tasks (measured by Rouge-L) and the calculated FV similarity.']

experiments.Description: To causally investigate the mechanism of forgetting by performing intervention experiments on the function vectors during inference.
experiments.Method: Using models fine-tuned on NI-Seq-G1 and NI-Seq-C1, two types of interventions will be performed during inference on evaluation tasks like Hellaswag and Object Count: 1) Adding the 'source FV' from the original pre-trained model to a specific layer's hidden state. 2) Subtracting the 'biased target FV' from the current training task from a specific layer's hidden state.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Change in model performance (Rouge-L) on evaluation tasks post-intervention.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Function Vector Guided (FVG) training methodology for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in continual learning.
experiments.Method: The FVG training approach, which includes a FV consistency loss and a FV-guided KL-divergence loss, will be applied to several continual learning baselines (IncLora, EWC, OLora, InsCL). These enhanced baselines will be tested on three SuperNI sequences (NI-Seq-G1, NI-Seq-C1, NI-Seq-M1) and the TRACE benchmark, using Llama2-7b-chat, Llama3-8b-chat, and Mistral-7b-instruct models.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['GP (General Performance)', 'IP (In-context Performance)', 'FP (Final Performance on trained tasks)']

risk_factors: Nonetheless, under certain scenarios, like when utilizing the InsCL replay method on NI-Seq-M1, our strategy yields a 2.8 drop in FP. This could be attributed to the conflict between the diverse gradient information from the memory buffer and our regularization component.

title: Characterizing ResNet's Universal Approximation Capability
name: characterizing-resnet-approximation
short_hypothesis: ResNet with bottleneck blocks (b-ResNet) can approximate popular classes of functions, such as polynomials and smooth functions, with comparable accuracy to standard feedforward ReLU networks but with a significantly reduced number of tunable weights, specifically by a factor of the input dimension, d.
related_work: Prior work, such as Lin & Jegelka (2018), established the universal approximation capability of ResNet, meaning it can approximate any Lebesgue-integrable function arbitrarily well. However, the question of how many tunable weights are needed for ResNet to approximate a function up to a specific error ε remains largely open. While related work by Oono & Suzuki (2019) focused on ResNet-type CNNs, this paper addresses the gap for ResNet-type MLPs by aiming to provide explicit upper and lower bounds on the number of weights required for approximation.
abstract: Despite its practical success, an essential theoretical question remains largely open: how well can ResNet approximate functions? This paper aims to provide answers to this question by analyzing ResNet with bottleneck blocks (b-ResNet). The goal is to show that b-ResNet can approximate various function classes, such as d-dimensional monomials and smooth functions, with a reduced number of tunable weights compared to classical ReLU networks. The analysis will also investigate whether these bounds are order-optimal and can help circumvent the curse of dimensionality for certain function classes by leveraging the Kolmogorov Superposition Theorem.

experiments.Description: To numerically validate the theoretical results on the universal approximation capability of b-ResNet by testing its performance on learning a complex, high-dimensional function.
experiments.Method: The task is to approximate a synthetic function f(x) composed of sums of products and sinusoidal terms in high dimensions (d = 100, 200, 300). The proposed b-ResNet models, denoted RN(d+1, n, d/10) with varying widths n, are compared against a baseline fully connected neural network, NN(d+1, d/10). Data is generated via Quasi Monte Carlo sampling over the input space [-1,1]^d.

risk_factors: Our results are suboptimal by an exponential factor of 1/2 due to polynomial approximation methods compared with these more modern quantization-based arguments.
Even though b-ResNet can achieve the suboptimal approximation rate, it remains uncertain whether it can reach the optimal approximation rate.
As a closing remark, we acknowledge that while the Sobolev space is critical, it is a limited function class.

title: IBiT: Utilizing Inductive Biases to Create a More Data Efficient Attention Mechanism
name: ibit-inductive-bias-masks-for-vits
short_hypothesis: Introducing CNN-like inductive biases (translational equivariance and locality) into Vision Transformers through learnable masks applied to attention layers will allow them to learn more effectively on smaller datasets without requiring knowledge distillation. The parameter cost of these masks can be made efficient using a low-rank approximation.
related_work: Vision Transformers (ViTs) are powerful but data-hungry, lacking the inductive biases of CNNs. Prior work like DeiT introduced these biases via knowledge distillation from a strong teacher model, which itself requires large amounts of data to train. Other methods like ConViT use learned relative positional encodings to model CNN-like properties, but this can require specific architectural constraints (e.g., number of heads) and modify the base Transformer architecture. The proposed method aims to introduce inductive biases directly via learnable masks without relying on a teacher model or significantly altering the standard self-attention structure.
abstract: In recent years, Transformer-based architectures have become the dominant method for Computer Vision applications. While Transformers are explainable and scale well with dataset size, they lack the inductive biases of Convolutional Neural Networks. While these biases may be learned on large datasets, we propose that introducing these inductive biases through learned masks will allow Vision Transformers to learn on much smaller datasets without Knowledge Distillation. These Transformers, which we call Inductively Biased Image Transformers (IBiT), are intended to be more accurate on small datasets, while retaining the explainability of Transformers.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Inductively Biased Image Transformer (IBiT) against other data-efficient Transformer models.
experiments.Method: The IBiT model, which has the same architecture as DeiT but with Multi-Head Self Attention layers replaced by Learned Masked Self Attention, will be trained on the ImageNet-1k dataset for 300 epochs. The CutMix data augmentation technique will be removed. The model will be compared against DeiT and ConViT baselines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the dataset sample efficiency of the proposed model.
experiments.Method: The IBiT model will be compared against DeiT and ConViT when trained on varying percentages of the ImageNet dataset.

experiments.Description: To qualitatively assess whether the proposed model retains the explainability of standard Transformers.
experiments.Method: The Rollout Attention technique will be used to generate attention maps from the IBiT model to visualize which pixels the model uses for classification.

experiments.Description: To ablate the contributions of the proposed Learnable Mask Self Attention layer and the removal of the CutMix augmentation.
experiments.Method: The experiment will compare three configurations: (A) IBiT with learnable masks and no CutMix, (B) a model without learnable masks but with CutMix, and (C) a DeiT-like model without learnable masks and with CutMix.

risk_factors: This motivates the idea that learned masks may not even be needed in later layers, though we leave further architectural modifications to future work.

title: Robustness May be More Brittle than We Think under Different Degrees of Distribution Shifts
name: brittle-robustness-under-varying-shifts
short_hypothesis: Evaluating models across a continuous spectrum of distribution shift severities, rather than at fixed points, will reveal that model robustness is brittle and inconsistent. A model's performance under a mild shift may not predict its performance under a slightly stronger shift. Furthermore, large-scale pre-trained models like CLIP may be surprisingly sensitive to even minute distribution shifts on novel downstream tasks.
related_work: Most Out-of-Distribution (OOD) benchmarks like WILDS and DomainBed incorporate multiple datasets to cover diverse *types* of distribution shifts. However, they often overlook the *degree* of shift, typically evaluating on a fixed severity for each shift type. This can lead to biased conclusions about a model's OOD performance. Similarly, while foundation models like CLIP have shown remarkable robustness, it is unclear how their robustness transfers to downstream tasks and how they behave as the degree of a downstream distribution shift increases.
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization is a complicated problem due to the idiosyncrasies of possible distribution shifts between training and test domains. Most benchmarks employ diverse datasets to address this issue; however, the degree of the distribution shift between the training domains and the test domains of each dataset remains largely fixed. This may lead to biased conclusions that either underestimate or overestimate the actual OOD performance of a model. This study proposes to delve into a more nuanced evaluation setting that covers a broad range of shift degrees to investigate if model robustness is brittle and inconsistent under different degrees of distribution shifts. Additionally, the study will examine whether large-scale pre-trained models, such as CLIP, are sensitive to even minute distribution shifts of novel downstream tasks, which could indicate that pre-training may have minimal or even adverse effects on generalization in certain OOD scenarios.

experiments.Description: To investigate if model robustness extrapolates from milder to stronger distribution shifts by training models on mildly shifted data and evaluating them across a full spectrum of shift severities.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on image classification tasks using the NOISYMNIST, ROTATEDMNIST, and LOWLIGHTCIFAR10 datasets. The model architectures will be a simple 4-layer CNN and a ResNet-50, trained from scratch. These will be compared against a baseline of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) and over 20 domain generalization (DG) algorithms, including IRM, VREx, GroupDRO, CORAL, and DANN.

experiments.Description: To investigate the reverse question: whether robustness to strong distribution shifts guarantees robustness to milder shifts. This involves training models on strongly shifted data and evaluating them across all milder degrees of shift.
experiments.Method: ERM models (Simple CNN, ResNet-50) will be trained on the NOISYMNIST and ROTATEDMNIST datasets using various combinations of training domains, such as clean data plus strongly shifted data (e.g., D0 and D10). These will be compared to models trained only on clean data or on clean data plus mildly shifted data.

experiments.Description: To investigate how pre-training influences the OOD generalization behavior of models on downstream tasks under multiple shift degrees, and to test if large-scale pre-trained models are sensitive to novel downstream shifts.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use NOISYMNIST, ROTATEDMNIST, NOISYIMAGENET15, and LR-IMAGENET15 datasets. Pre-trained models including ImageNet pre-trained ResNet-50, ViT-B/32, and their CLIP-trained counterparts will be adapted to the downstream tasks via linear probing. These will be compared against randomly initialized ResNet-50 and ViT-B/32 models trained from scratch on the same tasks.

risk_factors: Although we focus on image classification problems and use accuracy as the metric for measuring model performance, we believe that the conclusions drawn from our experiment results also hold for more general problems.

title: InfoScan: Information-Efficient Visual Scanning via Resource-Adaptive Walks
name: infoscan-adaptive-visual-scanning
short_hypothesis: A novel Information-aware Scanning mechanism (InfoScan), tailored for state-space visual backbones, can dynamically allocate computational resources to the most salient regions of an image. By assessing patch informativeness using entropy and local structure, optimizing for detail and context, and learning an adaptive scanning policy via reinforcement learning, it is hypothesized that this approach will yield a superior efficiency-accuracy trade-off compared to methods with fixed scanning patterns.
related_work: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have high computational costs that scale quadratically with the number of input tokens, making them expensive for high-resolution images. Recent Mamba-based models alleviate this with structured scanning patterns (e.g., raster, Hilbert curves), but these traversal orders are predefined and content-agnostic. They treat all image patches identically, assuming a uniform information distribution, which limits adaptive feature aggregation and leads to inefficient computation. The research gap is the lack of a content-aware, dynamic scanning mechanism that can prioritize informative regions to improve efficiency and performance.
abstract: High-resolution visual representation learning remains challenging due to the quadratic complexity of Vision Transformers and the limitations of existing efficient approaches, where fixed scanning patterns in recent Mamba-based models hinder content-adaptive perception. To address these limitations, a novel Information-aware Scanning mechanism (InfoScan) tailored for state-space visual backbones is proposed, which dynamically allocates computational resources to the most salient regions of an image. Specifically, InfoScan rigorously assesses the informativeness of image patches by integrating entropy with local structural analyses, formulates a joint optimization objective balancing fine-grained detail preservation and broader contextual coherence, and learns an adaptive scanning policy via reinforcement learning. Built upon the innovative Visual Information State Space (VISS) block, InfoScan is designed to establish a new family of models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of InfoScan on image classification and compare it with mainstream baseline models in terms of accuracy and efficiency.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the ImageNet-1K dataset using 224x224 resolution. It will be compared against CNN (e.g., ResNet), Transformer (e.g., DeiT-S, Swin-T), and SSM (e.g., VMamba-S) baselines. Training will use the AdamW optimizer with cosine learning rate decay.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of InfoScan as a backbone for object detection tasks.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the MSCOCO2017 dataset using the Mask R-CNN framework. InfoScan will be compared as a backbone against other models like Swin Transformer, ConvNeXt, and VMamba.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of InfoScan on semantic segmentation across different domains.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the ADE20K (natural scenes) and BraTS-2021 (medical imaging) datasets, using UperNet as the segmentation head at 512x512 resolution. Baselines for comparison will include ResNet, DeiT, and Vim backbones.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of each component of the proposed scanning strategy, including the path scanning module, patch planning module, and reward function components.
experiments.Method: Ablation experiments will be performed on the ImageNet-1K, ADE-20K, and BraTS-2021 datasets. The study will compare a baseline (Vision Mamba) against versions with the patch selection and path planning modules enabled. A separate ablation will analyze the contribution of the reward function's components (adaptive revisitation, exploration bonus, neighborhood information gain).

experiments.Description: To conduct zero-shot cross-dataset generalization experiments to test whether models trained on one segmentation dataset can generalize to a significantly different one without fine-tuning.
experiments.Method: Models (InfoScan-T, ResNet-50, DeiT-T, Vim-T) will be trained on either the ADE-20K or BraTS-2021 dataset and then evaluated on the other dataset without any fine-tuning. All models will use the UPerNet segmentation head.

risk_factors: First, the current implementation assumes a uniform patch size and grid structure, which may not optimally capture multi-scale semantics in high-resolution images. Future work could explore adaptive patching or hierarchical scanning strategies.

Second, the information scoring module, though lightweight, introduces additional latency during inference. end-to-end deployment in real-time systems requires further optimization of the scoring and policy inference pipeline.
Third, the reward function contains hyperparameters (e.g., α_high, λ) that currently require mild tuning for extreme domain shifts (e.g., natural to medical).
Finally, all experiments focus on 2D images; extension to 3D Videos would require re-designing the action space and scanning policy, which we leave for future work.

title: The Impact of Post-training on Data Contamination
name: post-training-contamination-effects
short_hypothesis: Contamination that appears dormant after pre-training may be amplified, exploited, or attenuated by post-training procedures like Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). The type of post-training method and the model's scale will likely influence whether contamination leads to simple memorization or a more generalized capability.
related_work: Prior studies have revealed that data contamination, the overlap between benchmark examples and pre-training corpora, can inflate LLM performance metrics. However, most analyses focus exclusively on the pre-training stage. State-of-the-art LLMs are almost always subjected to post-training procedures like SFT or RLHF, which can materially reshape a model's internal representations. There is some evidence that SFT is more prone to memorization while RL can introduce generalization, but no prior work has jointly studied the interaction of contamination with different post-training methods (SFT and RL) across various model scales, creating a gap in understanding the full lifecycle of contamination.
abstract: We present a controlled study of how dataset contamination interacts with the post-training stages now standard in large language model training pipelines. Starting from clean checkpoints of Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B) and Gemma-3 (1B/4B), we inject five copies of GSM8K and MBPP test items into the first 2B tokens of an otherwise 25B token extended pre-training dataset. We then compare the contaminated and clean models both immediately after pre-training and again after two popular post-training methods: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) with group relative policy optimization (GRPO). The applied post-training steps do not have any contamination. This study aims to understand how contamination is affected by post-training and model scale.

experiments.Description: To investigate how post-training (SFT and GRPO) affects performance on a math reasoning task (GSM8K) when the test set is injected into the pre-training data, and to assess generalization on a related, uncontaminated benchmark (GSMPlus).
experiments.Method: Starting from clean Qwen2.5 (0.5B, 1.5B) and Gemma-3 (1B, 4B) checkpoints, we perform extended pre-training on a 25B token dataset. For the 'contaminated' run, five copies of the GSM8K test set are injected into the first 2B tokens. A parallel 'clean' run is conducted without injection. After this extended pre-training, both clean and contaminated models are separately post-trained using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on the GSM8K training set and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Performance will be compared on the GSM8K and GSMPlus benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To investigate how post-training (SFT and GRPO) affects performance on a code generation task (MBPP) when the test set is injected into the pre-training data, and to assess generalization on a related, uncontaminated benchmark (HumanEval).
experiments.Method: Starting from clean Qwen2.5 (0.5B, 1.5B) and Gemma-3 (1B, 4B) checkpoints, we perform extended pre-training on a 25B token dataset. For the 'contaminated' run, five copies of the MBPP test set are injected into the first 2B tokens. A parallel 'clean' run is conducted without injection. After this extended pre-training, both clean and contaminated models are separately post-trained using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Performance will be compared on the MBPP and HumanEval benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of model scale on how contamination interacts with different post-training recipes and its effect on generalization.
experiments.Method: The performance differences between contaminated and clean models of varying sizes (0.5B, 1B, 1.5B, 4B) will be compared across three training recipes: Base (extended pre-training only), SFT, and GRPO. The analysis will track the 'contamination-gap difference' between the contaminated benchmark (GSM8K) and the uncontaminated benchmark (GSMPlus) as model scale increases for each recipe.

risk_factors: Our study purposefully isolates a 5-copy, late-injection contamination scenario. Real-world leakage can be multi-pass, paraphrased, or distributed throughout pre-training, and its effects may differ.

Moreover, we restrict model sizes to 4B parameters and focus on two open-weights families. That said, larger proprietary models, different architectures, or alternative RLHF algorithms could behave differently.
Finally, our RL setting uses synthetic rule-based rewards; human-annotated preference signals might have different effects.

We do not have access to the original pre-training corpora for the base checkpoints (Qwen2.5 and Gemma-3), so we cannot certify that the “clean” models are entirely free of overlap with GSM8K/MBPP test items; any pre-existing leakage would bias both branches of our comparison.

Readers should therefore interpret our estimates as potentially, partial incremental effect of added leakage under post-training, rather than an absolute “contamination-free vs. contaminated” contrast.

title: Reward Translation via Reward Machine in Semi-Alignable MDPs
name: nrt-reward-translation-semi-alignable-mdps
short_hypothesis: The proposed Neural Reward Translation (NRT) framework, which leverages Reward Machines (RMs) to distill abstract alignments, can solve the cross-domain reward transfer problem within a newly defined class of 'semi-alignable MDPs'. This will enable the reuse of rewards to enhance training efficiency in new domains, even when a direct state-action mapping does not exist.
related_work: Current methods for cross-domain transfer in reinforcement learning, such as those for imitation learning, are limited because they require 'alignable MDPs' where a direct, often bijective, mapping between state-action spaces exists. These methods struggle with more general tasks where domains have different embodiments, dynamics, or viewpoints without such a strict alignment. This paper introduces 'semi-alignable MDPs' to formalize a coarser-grained, abstract alignment between tasks. While Reward Machines (RMs) have been used for various purposes in RL, this work proposes to use them specifically to uncover the latent task structure and transfer reward signals between these semi-alignable domains.
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning often requires significant engineering effort to design dense rewards. While reusing rewards across similar tasks can improve efficiency, current methods are restricted to domains with strict state-space alignments. To overcome this, it is crucial to identify coarser-grained, latent structural similarities between domains. This paper introduces 'semi-alignable Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)' to formalize these coarse-grained resemblances. Subsequently, the 'Neural Reward Translation (NRT)' framework is proposed, which employs reward machines to solve the cross-domain reward transfer problem within semi-alignable MDPs, aiming to facilitate more versatile reward reuse for reinforcement learning across diverse domains.

experiments.Description: To examine the effectiveness of the NRT framework under isomorphic reward machines by transferring state reward functions from NChain(n=5) to NChain(n=9).
experiments.Method: The experiment involves transferring rewards from a trained NChain(n=5) agent to a NChain(n=9) agent. The base RL algorithm is DQN. The proposed method (DQN+NRT) will be compared against a DQN baseline and an ablation, DQN+RM (using a reward machine but without the transferred reward function).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of steps required by the agent to reach the target']

experiments.Description: To investigate the efficacy of the NRT framework for homomorphic reward machines by transferring state reward functions from the Cartpole task to the HalfCheetah task, where the tasks have a modified MDP relationship (Mx ≥ My) and sparse rewards.
experiments.Method: The experiment involves transferring rewards from a trained Cartpole agent to a HalfCheetah agent. Both tasks are modified to have sparse rewards. The base RL algorithm is DDPG. The proposed method will be compared against a DDPG baseline and two ablations: DDPG+RM (without transferred reward) and DDPG+Reward (accessing transferred rewards without RM states).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of steps required by the agent to reach the target']

experiments.Description: To probe the NRT framework's performance under homomorphic reward machines by transferring state reward functions from the HalfCheetah task to the Atari-Freeway task, maintaining an MDP relationship of My ≥ Mx.
experiments.Method: The experiment involves transferring rewards from a trained HalfCheetah agent to an Atari-Freeway agent. The reward function transfer will be handled by a piece-wise function (Equation 7) due to the nature of the mapping. The NRT method will be compared against a baseline RL algorithm.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curve showing task value over training steps']

risk_factors: certain constraints were identified, particularly with tasks lacking effective injection or bijection, where NRT might inadvertently lead to local optima entrapment.
constructing suitable reward machines and discerning relationships in more complex tasks remains challenging.

title: MEM-$\alpha$: LEARNING MEMORY CONSTRUCTION VIA REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
name: mem-alpha-rl-memory
short_hypothesis: A reinforcement learning framework, Mem-a, can train LLM agents to effectively manage complex, multi-component memory systems by optimizing for downstream question-answering accuracy. This approach will enable agents to learn optimal memory construction strategies (what to store, how to structure it, and when to update) through interaction and feedback, overcoming the limitations of relying on pre-defined instructions.
related_work: Current memory-augmented agents either encode information into latent space, which has bounded capacity and requires model access, or use external memory with pre-defined tools and instructions (e.g., MemGPT, MIRIX). The latter approach's effectiveness is limited by the base model's instruction-following ability, especially with complex memory systems. While prior work has explored RL for memory construction (e.g., MEM1, MemAgent), these efforts have used relatively simple memory structures and focused on less complex tasks. This work addresses the gap by proposing an RL framework designed to train agents to operate a substantially more capable, multi-component memory architecture and generalize to much longer contexts.
abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are constrained by limited context windows, necessitating external memory systems for long-term information understanding. Current memory-augmented agents typically depend on pre-defined instructions and tools for memory updates. However, language models may lack the ability to determine which information to store, how to structure it, and when to update it—especially as memory systems become more complex. This results in suboptimal memory construction and information loss. To this end, we propose Mem-a, a reinforcement learning framework that trains agents to effectively manage complex memory systems through interaction and feedback. We also construct a specialized training dataset spanning diverse multi-turn interaction patterns paired with comprehensive evaluation questions designed to teach effective memory management. During training, agents process sequential information chunks, learn to extract, store, and update the memory system. The reward signal derives from downstream question-answering accuracy over the full interaction history, directly optimizing for memory construction. To illustrate the effectiveness of our training framework, we design a memory architecture comprising core, episodic, and semantic components, equipped with multiple tools for memory operations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Mem-a framework on a comprehensive set of memory tasks, including accurate retrieval, test-time learning, and long-range understanding.
experiments.Method: The Mem-a model (with a Qwen3-4B backbone) will be evaluated on the MemoryAgentBench benchmark. This includes tasks for Accurate Retrieval (Single-Doc, Multi-Doc, LME(S*)), Test-Time Learning (TREC-C, NLU, TREC-F, Clinic, Banking77), and Long-Range Understanding (InfBench). The performance will be compared against several baselines: Long-Context (Qwen3-32B), RAG-Top2 (BM25 retrieval), MemAgent, and MEM1.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task-specific metrics (F1/Accuracy)', 'Memory usage in thousands of tokens']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of Mem-a on the validation datasets that match its training distribution.
experiments.Method: The Mem-a model will be evaluated on validation datasets for Accurate Retrieval (SQUAD, HotpotQA, PerLTQA), Test-Time Learning (NLU, TREC-C, PubMed), and Long-Range Understanding (BookSum). Performance will be compared against Long-Context, RAG-Top2, MemAgent, and MEM1 baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task-specific metrics (F1/Accuracy)', 'Memory usage in thousands of tokens']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to demonstrate that performance improvements stem from the reinforcement learning approach itself, rather than solely from the memory architecture.
experiments.Method: Three configurations will be compared on the validation datasets: (1) the full RL-tuned Mem-a model, (2) the base Qwen3-4B model using the Mem-a memory framework without RL tuning, and (3) the gpt-4.1-mini model using the Mem-a memory framework without RL tuning.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task-specific metrics (F1/Accuracy)', 'Memory usage in thousands of tokens']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the reward function to examine the impact of the compression weight (β) and memory content quality weight (γ) hyperparameters.
experiments.Method: The Mem-a model will be trained and evaluated with different values for β and γ on the validation datasets and the MemoryAgentBench test dataset to observe their effect on performance and memory efficiency.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task-specific metrics (F1/Accuracy)', 'Memory usage in thousands of tokens']

risk_factors: Our current memory architecture could benefit from integration with more sophisticated systems like MIRIX, which may provide additional structural advantages for complex reasoning tasks.

Furthermore, extending Mem-a from simulated environments to real-world applications would require connecting the reinforcement learning framework with actual databases and production systems, introducing challenges around latency, scalability, and safety that warrant careful investigation.

title: Evaluating Unsupervised Denoising Requires Unsupervised Metrics
name: unsupervised-denoising-metrics
short_hypothesis: Two novel metrics, the unsupervised mean squared error (uMSE) and the unsupervised peak signal-to-noise ratio (uPSNR), can be computed exclusively from noisy data to evaluate unsupervised denoising methods. These metrics are proposed to be asymptotically consistent estimators of the supervised MSE and PSNR.
related_work: Unsupervised deep-learning denoising methods have shown impressive performance, but their quantitative evaluation is almost completely dominated by benchmarks with simulated noise, as obtaining ground-truth clean images for real-world data is often impossible. This forces practitioners to rely on visual inspection, which prevents systematic hyperparameter optimization. While Stein's unbiased risk estimator (SURE) can estimate MSE without clean data, it has limitations, such as requiring a closed-form expression for the noise likelihood and being computationally expensive. To the best of the authors' knowledge, no unsupervised metrics are currently available to evaluate denoising methods using only noisy data.
abstract: Unsupervised denoising is a crucial challenge in real-world imaging applications. Unsupervised deep-learning methods have demonstrated impressive performance on benchmarks based on synthetic noise. However, no metrics are available to evaluate these methods in an unsupervised fashion. This is highly problematic for the many practical applications where ground-truth clean images are not available. In this work, we propose two novel metrics: the unsupervised mean squared error (uMSE) and the unsupervised peak signal-to-noise ratio (uPSNR), which are computed using only noisy data. We provide a theoretical analysis of these metrics, showing that they are asymptotically consistent estimators of the supervised MSE and PSNR.

experiments.Description: To conduct a controlled evaluation of the proposed metrics by studying their properties in a scenario where ground-truth clean images are known.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a dataset of natural images (from Martin et al., 2001; Zhang et al., 2017b; Franzen, 1993) corrupted with additive Gaussian noise, and a dataset of simulated electron-microscopy images (from Vincent et al., 2021) corrupted with Poisson noise. For these datasets, the supervised MSE and PSNR will be computed using the ground-truth clean image. These will be compared to the proposed uMSE and uPSNR, which will be computed using noisy references corresponding to the same clean image, and also using references obtained from a single noisy image via spatial subsampling. The metrics will be applied to evaluate several denoising methods: Bilateral filter, DenseNet, DnCNN, UNet, and BlindSpot.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed metrics on a real-world dataset of videos in raw format, contaminated with real noise where no ground-truth is available.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a dataset of videos in raw format from Yue et al. (2020), which contains static objects captured at five different ISO levels. The proposed unsupervised metrics (uMSE and uPSNR), computed using three noisy frames from each video segment, will be compared with MSE and PSNR estimates obtained by averaging ten noisy frames. These metrics will be used to evaluate four different denoisers: a wavelet-based method, temporal averaging, an image-based CNN denoiser (UDVD), and a video-based CNN denoiser (UDVD).

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the application of the proposed metrics for quantitative evaluation of denoisers on real-world transmission electron microscopy (TEM) data in the absence of ground-truth clean images.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a TEM dataset of platinum nanoparticles. An unsupervised Neighbor2Neighbor convolutional neural network will be trained on a portion of the data. Its performance will be compared to a Gaussian-smoothing baseline on two test sets (moderate SNR and low SNR). The evaluation will be performed using the proposed uPSNR metric, computed from a single noisy image via spatial subsampling to handle noise correlation.

risk_factors: These results are limited to denoising under the assumption of independent noise.
How to address the bias introduced by spatial subsampling and achieve an unbiased approximation to the MSE from a single noisy image.
How to account for noise distributions and artifacts which are not pixel-wise independent (see e.g. Prakash et al. (2021)).
How to obtain unsupervised approximations of perceptual metrics such as SSIM Wang et al. (2004).
How to perform unsupervised evaluation for general inverse problems, and related applications such as realistic image synthesis Zwicker et al. (2015).

title: Midtraining Bridges Pretraining and Posttraining Distributions
name: midtraining-bridges-distributions
short_hypothesis: Midtraining, an intermediate training phase using specialized data, acts as a distributional bridge between general pretraining and specialized post-training data distributions. This approach is hypothesized to be most effective for domains syntactically distant from the pretraining data (like math and code), improving downstream performance and reducing catastrophic forgetting compared to continued pretraining or direct fine-tuning.
related_work: Many large language models use a "midtraining" phase, but there is little scientific understanding or systematic study of why it is effective. This practice is related to curriculum learning, but operates at a distributional level rather than on individual examples. It also differs from "continued pretraining," which typically shifts entirely to domain-specific data, whereas midtraining proposes maintaining a mixture of general and specialized data. The research gap is the lack of controlled experiments to understand when and why midtraining works.
abstract: Recently, many language models have been pretrained with a "midtraining" phase, in which higher quality, often instruction-formatted data, is mixed in at the end of pretraining. Despite the popularity of this practice, there is little scientific understanding of this phase of model training or why it is effective. In this work, we propose to conduct the first systematic investigation of midtraining through controlled experiments with language models pretrained from scratch and fine-tuned on supervised finetuning datasets in different domains. The investigation will explore the effectiveness of midtraining across different domains, compare it to continued pretraining, and ablate factors like the timing and mixture weight of the midtraining data, with the goal of establishing midtraining as a domain adaptation technique.

experiments.Description: To identify which downstream tasks and domains benefit most from midtraining and to assess its domain-specific nature.
experiments.Method: Pretrain Pythia models (70M-410M) on C4 web data. Then, conduct a midtraining phase using one of five different data mixtures: Starcoder (code), MATH, FLAN (instructions), KnowledgeQA, and DCLM (high-quality web). After midtraining, the models will be supervised fine-tuned (SFT) on four downstream datasets: GSM8k (math), SciQ (science QA), CodeSearchNet-Python (code), and LIMA (instruction following). A baseline model will be one that undergoes continued pretraining on C4 instead of a specialized midtraining mix.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Validation loss on the target SFT domain (adaptation effectiveness)', 'Cross-entropy loss on held-out C4 data (catastrophic forgetting)']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that effective midtraining data acts as a 'distributional bridge' between the pretraining and target fine-tuning data distributions.
experiments.Method: Measure the token-distributional similarity between the pretraining data (C4), each midtraining mixture, and each target SFT dataset. This 'proximity advantage' (how much closer the midtraining data is to the SFT data compared to C4) will be calculated. The plan is to analyze the relationship between this proximity advantage and downstream performance improvements.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["A token-based similarity metric (combining cosine, Jaccard, and Jensen-Shannon similarity) to calculate 'proximity advantage'", 'Correlation between proximity advantage and relative improvement in SFT validation loss']

experiments.Description: To compare the effectiveness of midtraining (mixing general and specialized data) against continued pretraining (switching entirely to specialized data).
experiments.Method: For code and math domains, compare a model with a default midtraining mix (e.g., 20% Starcoder) to a model that undergoes continued pretraining on 100% specialized data (100% Starcoder). Both approaches will be compared against a pretrain-only baseline. Models will then be fine-tuned on their respective target tasks (Pycode for code, GSM8K for math).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Validation loss on the in-domain SFT task', 'C4 validation loss for forgetting']

experiments.Description: To investigate the optimal timing and mixture weight for introducing midtraining data.
experiments.Method: Using the Starcoder midtraining mixture as a case study, conduct two sets of ablations. First, fix the mixture weight (20%) and vary the starting time of the midtraining phase (from 10% to 80% through pretraining). Second, fix the starting time at two different points (63B and 105B tokens) and vary the mixture weight of Starcoder data (from 10% to 80%). Models will be evaluated after fine-tuning on Pycode.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['SFT validation loss on Pycode (in-domain performance)', 'C4 validation loss (forgetting)']

experiments.Description: To investigate how midtraining changes the model's internal representations and whether it leads to smoother adaptation during fine-tuning.
experiments.Method: Use linear Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to measure the layer-wise similarity between four model states: base pretrained, midtrained (Starcoder), base fine-tuned, and midtrained fine-tuned. Activations will be extracted using probe datasets (C4 and APPS code dataset). The expectation is to observe smaller representational changes during fine-tuning for models that underwent midtraining.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['CKA similarity matrices between layer activations of different model checkpoints']

risk_factors: Furthermore, timing of data introduction appears to have a stronger effect than mixture weight of that specialized data, though more work is needed to clarify this effect.
A natural next step is to test the findings at much larger scales and with more diverse domains such as medicine, music, or other specific fields.

Another direction is understanding how midtraining, and pretraining data distributions more broadly, interact with reinforcement learning based posttraining, and whether there are any differences between this and supervised finetuning based posttraining when it comes to adaptation.
Due to the size of pretraining data sources and checkpoints, these have not yet been uploaded to the cloud, but will be provided after acceptance.

title: Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Collaboration Networks for Software Development
name: evomac-textual-backprop
short_hypothesis: A multi-agent collaboration (MAC) network for software development can be made to self-evolve and adapt to specific tasks at test time by using a novel "textual backpropagation" algorithm. This process involves obtaining text-based feedback by verifying the network's code output against a target proxy (like unit tests) in an objective environment (like a compiler), and then using this feedback to analyze errors and update the agent prompts and network structure.
related_work: Current LLM-driven multi-agent collaboration (MAC) systems for software development rely on static, human-designed workflows. This approach has two key limitations: their performance is confined by the initial human design, which cannot account for the diversity of all tasks, and they lack the flexibility to adapt to new or evolving requirements without significant manual intervention. Furthermore, existing software development benchmarks are often limited to the function-level, or if they are at the software-level, they rely on expensive human evaluation or inaccurate similarity-based metrics, lacking a method for automatic and precise evaluation of requirement correctness.
abstract: LLM-driven multi-agent collaboration (MAC) systems have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automatic software development at the function level. However, their heavy reliance on human design limits their adaptability to the diverse demands of real-world software development. To address this limitation, we introduce EvoMAC, a novel self-evolving paradigm for MAC networks. Inspired by traditional neural network training, EvoMAC obtains text-based environmental feedback by verifying the MAC network's output against a target proxy and leverages a novel textual backpropagation to update the network. To extend coding capabilities beyond function-level tasks to more challenging software-level development, we further propose rSDE-Bench, a requirement-oriented software development benchmark, which features complex and diverse software requirements along with automatic evaluation of requirement correctness.

experiments.Description: To validate the reliability of the proposed rSDE-Bench benchmark by assessing how well its automatic evaluation metric aligns with human judgments.
experiments.Method: The performance of four methods (GPT-40-Mini, MetaGPT, ChatDev, and EvoMAC) will be evaluated on the rSDE-Bench dataset. The results from the proposed automatic 'accuracy' metric will be compared against a golden standard of human evaluation conducted by expert code engineers, as well as against existing indirect metrics like 'consistency' and 'quality' from the SRDD benchmark.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Correlation coefficient between the proposed 'accuracy' metric and human evaluation scores.", "Correlation coefficient between existing metrics ('consistency', 'quality') and human evaluation scores."]

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of EvoMAC against state-of-the-art single-agent and multi-agent baselines on both software-level and function-level coding tasks.
experiments.Method: EvoMAC will be compared against single-agent baselines (GPT-40-Mini, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Gemini-1.5-flash) and multi-agent SOTA methods (MetaGPT, Autogen, Mapcoder, Agentverse, ChatDev). All multi-agent systems, including EvoMAC, will be powered by the GPT-40-Mini model for a fair comparison. The evaluation will be conducted on the proposed rSDE-Bench and the standard HumanEval benchmark.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effectiveness of the self-evolving process and the contribution of individual components within the EvoMAC framework through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: EvoMAC's performance will be evaluated across multiple evolving iterations using two different driving LLMs (GPT-40-Mini and Claude-3.5-Sonnet). An ablation study will be conducted on rSDE-Bench by testing seven variants of EvoMAC, comparing the effects of: using single vs. multi-agent teams for coding and testing, enabling/disabling the evolution process, and using an objective environment tool vs. an LLM-driven critique agent for feedback.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on rSDE-Bench and HumanEval across evolving iterations.', 'Performance changes between different ablation variants.']

risk_factors: In the future, we plan to introduce a reward model to enhance the self-evolving paradigm's ability to learn from feedback and extend the rSDE-Bench to more software types.

title: M$^3$E: Continual Vision-and-Language Navigation via Mixture of Macro and Micro Experts
name: m3e-continual-vln
short_hypothesis: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in continual Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) hinges on decoupling global scene reasoning from local perceptual alignment. A hierarchical Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with a dual-router architecture can achieve this by separating navigation into two levels of reasoning: a macro-level router for strategy based on global environment features and a micro-level router for perception based on local instruction-vision alignment. This allows an agent to adapt to new domains while preserving specialized capabilities, without needing to replay past data.
related_work: Most Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents are trained on static datasets and exhibit poor generalization and catastrophic forgetting when adapted to new environments. Existing continual learning (CL) methods for embodied agents primarily rely on rehearsal buffers, which replay past trajectories to mitigate forgetting. This approach increases storage and computational overhead and can raise privacy concerns. While replay-free MoE frameworks exist for other tasks like VQA, they have not been extended to VLN, which presents unique challenges like sequential planning under partial observability. The proposed research aims to bridge this gap by introducing a replay-free, MoE-based framework specifically designed for continual VLN that separates global scene reasoning from local token-level grounding.
abstract: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents have shown strong capabilities in following natural language instructions. However, they often struggle to generalize across environments due to catastrophic forgetting, which limits their practical use in real-world settings where agents must continually adapt to new domains. We argue that overcoming forgetting across environments hinges on decoupling global scene reasoning from local perceptual alignment, allowing the agent to adapt to new domains while preserving specialized capabilities. To this end, we propose M³E, the Mixture of Macro and Micro Experts, an environment-aware hierarchical MoE framework for continual VLN. Our method introduces a dual-router architecture that separates navigation into two levels of reasoning. A macro-level, scene-aware router selects strategy experts based on global environmental features (e.g., office vs. residential), while a micro-level, instance-aware router activates perception experts based on local instruction-vision alignment for step-wise decision making. To preserve knowledge across domains, we adopt a dynamic momentum update strategy that identifies expert utility in new environments and selectively updates or freezes their parameters.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed M³E method in a domain-incremental setting on the R2R dataset, assessing its ability to adapt to new scenes and retain knowledge of prior ones without revisiting past data.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the R2R dataset under a scene-partitioned continual stream protocol. A base agent is pretrained on the 'train' split. The 'val-unseen' scenes are partitioned into disjoint domains, each defining a continual learning task for sequential fine-tuning. The M³E model, built on a NaviLLM backbone with MoE-LORA, will be compared against several baselines: naive Finetune, regularization-based methods (L2, EWC), and rehearsal-based methods (ER, PerR, ESR, Dual-SR).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success Rate (SR)', 'Success weighted by Path Length (SPL)', 'Navigation Error (NE)', 'Oracle Success Rate (OSR)', 'Backward Transfer (BWT)', 'Forward Transfer (FWT)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the M³E method on the more challenging, goal-oriented REVERIE dataset using the same domain-incremental protocol to test its effectiveness on object-centric navigation tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the REVERIE dataset under a scene-partitioned continual stream protocol. The M³E model will be compared against a naive Finetune baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success Rate (SR)', 'Success weighted by Path Length (SPL)', 'Backward Transfer (BWT)', 'Forward Transfer (FWT)']

experiments.Description: To probe how much knowledge about previously seen environments is forgotten and how overall generalization changes when adapting to new domains in a bulk training setting, rather than task-by-task.
experiments.Method: Officially released checkpoints for HAMT, NaviLLM, and the proposed M³E model will be loaded and then training will be continued on the entire 'val-unseen' split of the R2R and REVERIE datasets. Performance will be evaluated on the 'val-seen' and 'test-unseen' splits both before and after this bulk training.

risk_factors: First, while our experiments are conducted in discrete simulators to adhere to standard VLN protocols, the proposed macro-micro decomposition is inherently compatible with continuous control.

Bridging the Sim-to-Real gap requires further addressing challenges such as visual domain gaps (e.g., lighting, motion blur) and real-world SLAM estimation errors, though our robustness analysis (Appendix A.3) suggests resilience to map noise.
Second, the fixed expert configuration may limit flexibility under extreme open-ended scenarios.
Future work includes dynamic expert allocation, privacy-aware hybrid replay, Sim-to-Real transfer and extension to physical robotic platforms.

title: Permutation Equivariant Neural Networks for Antisymmetric Tensors
name: Permutation-Equivariant-Nets-for-Antisymmetric-Tensors
short_hypothesis: A neural network architecture built upon a complete characterisation of all linear permutation equivariant functions between antisymmetric power spaces of R^n, implemented via a memory-efficient approach termed 'IO patterns', will outperform standard MLPs and general permutation equivariant networks on tasks where the target function depends on the antisymmetric structure of the input.
related_work: Existing permutation equivariant architectures are designed for general or symmetric tensors and do not guarantee the preservation of the antisymmetric structure, limiting their effectiveness on such data. While some work has explored SO(3) equivariance for antisymmetric tensors, permutation equivariance remains largely unexplored.
