This work aims to address this gap by providing a complete characterisation of permutation equivariant linear maps for antisymmetric tensors.
abstract: Antisymmetric tensors, which change sign under index swaps, appear naturally in physics, yet learning from them remains largely unexplored. We provide a complete characterisation of all linear permutation equivariant functions between antisymmetric power spaces of R^n. To make this characterisation practical, we introduce a memory-efficient implementation that eliminates the need to create and store large weight matrices. Our approach is intended to be efficient in learning functions that depend on the antisymmetric structure of the input and to incorporate this structure as an inductive bias.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model on a synthetic S8-equivariant task involving mapping antisymmetric tensors to vectors, where the target function depends on the input's antisymmetric structure.
experiments.Method: A synthetic dataset of 10,000 training and 2,000 test antisymmetric tensors in (R^8)^2 is generated. The task is to learn the function f(T)_i = σ(Σ_{j:j≠i} T_{i,j}). The proposed model, 'AntiSymmPermEquiv', is constructed with S8-equivariant linear layers designed for antisymmetric tensors. It will be compared against two baselines: 'PermEquiv', a standard S8-equivariant model for general tensors, and a standard 'MLP'.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model on an S4-invariant task of learning the absolute value of the Pfaffian of 4x4 antisymmetric matrices.
experiments.Method: Synthetic datasets of 30, 300, 3000, and 30000 antisymmetric tensors in (R^4)^2 are generated for training, with a test set of 1000 tensors. The proposed model, 'AntiSymmPermEquiv', is constructed with S4-equivariant and S4-invariant layers for antisymmetric tensors. It will be compared against two baselines: 'PermEquiv', a standard S4-invariant model for general tensors, and a standard 'MLP'.

risk_factors: This work is primarily theoretical, providing a complete characterisation of linear permutation equivariant functions between antisymmetric tensors. While its immediate practical impact may be limited, we believe that it lays a principled foundation for future developments in neural network architectures and learning algorithms that are capable of operating on data that can be represented by antisymmetric tensors, with potential applications in real-world domains where these structures naturally arise.

title: NeoBabel: An Inclusive Multilingual Open Tower for Visual Generation
name: multilingual-t2i-generation-neobabel
short_hypothesis: A novel multilingual image generation framework, NEOBABEL, can be developed to provide a scalable solution for direct text-to-image synthesis across multiple languages (English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, Persian) without requiring intermediate translation. By building upon a pretrained multilingual large language model and introducing a unified multimodal embedding space and modality-aware attention, the model is intended to achieve language-agnostic understanding, maintaining performance parity with or exceeding English-only models while being more efficient.
related_work: Existing text-to-image generation systems are predominantly English-centric, creating barriers for non-English speakers. The common workaround is a 'translation-first' strategy, where non-English prompts are translated to English before generation. This approach introduces significant problems, including computational overhead which increases inference time, and semantic drift, where culturally specific meanings and linguistic subtleties are lost in translation. This results in generated images that fail to capture the user's intended meaning. There is a need for a direct, native multilingual text-to-image generation model that avoids these pitfalls.
abstract: Text-to-image generation advancements have been predominantly English-centric, creating barriers for non-English speakers and perpetuating digital inequities. While existing systems rely on translation pipelines, these introduce semantic drift, computational overhead, and cultural misalignment. We introduce NEOBABEL, a novel multilingual image generation framework that sets a new Pareto frontier in performance, efficiency and inclusivity, supporting six languages: English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, and Persian. The model is trained using a combination of large-scale multilingual pretraining and high-resolution instruction tuning. To evaluate its capabilities, we plan to expand two English-only benchmarks to multilingual equivalents: m-GenEval and m-DPG.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's multilingual image generation performance on compositional prompts against both generative and unified models.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the m-GenEval benchmark, a multilingual extension of GenEval, across six languages (English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, Persian). The task involves assessing prompt-to-image alignment across six compositional dimensions: single object, two objects, counting, colors, position, and color attribute. Baselines for comparison are intended to include generative models (LlamaGen, PixArt-alpha, SDXL, SD3) and unified models (Chameleon, LWM, TokenFlow, EMU3, Show-o, Janus-Pro, BLIP3-o).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on general-purpose multilingual generation with open-ended, diverse prompts that test broader semantic understanding.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the m-DPG benchmark, a multilingual extension of DPG-Bench, across six languages (English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, Persian). Baselines for comparison are intended to include Show-o, Janus Pro, and BLIP3-o.

experiments.Description: To qualitatively assess the model's ability to generate semantically aligned and visually coherent images from multilingual prompts across diverse categories.
experiments.Method: The model will be used to generate images from prompts in six languages covering diverse categories, including compositional scenes, abstract concepts, and multilingual instructions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection for semantic alignment, visual coherence, and consistency across languages']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's zero-shot capability for text-guided image inpainting and extrapolation using multilingual prompts.
experiments.Method: The model will be tasked with modifying or extending input images based on prompts provided in various languages, without any task-specific fine-tuning.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection for semantic faithfulness and visual consistency with adjacent content']

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of the progressive pretraining and instruction tuning strategies on the model's multilingual performance.
experiments.Method: Model checkpoints from different training stages (Pixel Dependency Learning, Scaling Alignment, Refined Pretraining, Initial Instruction Alignment, Instruction Refinement) will be evaluated on the m-GenEval and m-DPG benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's robustness to code-switching, where prompts combine multiple languages within the same input.
experiments.Method: The model will be tested on specially designed cross-lingual prompts created by splitting a base prompt and translating segments into different languages. The evaluation will use prompts from the m-DPG benchmark.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Code-Switching Similarity (CSS) score, using EVA-CLIP and DINOv2 embeddings']

risk_factors: First, the model currently supports six languages; extending to broader linguistic coverage would require tokenizer adaptation and additional training.
Second, although NEOBABEL adopts a unified architecture, it does not yet support vision-language tasks such as visual question answering, due to the absence of task-specific fine-tuning.

title: Next-ToBE: Probabilistic Next Token-Bag Exploitation for Activating Anticipatory Capacity in LLMs
name: next-tobe
short_hypothesis: Replacing the standard one-hot target vector in next-token prediction with a flexible, soft target distribution over a future 'token-bag' will activate and enhance the intrinsic anticipatory capacity of LLMs. This soft target, with weights dynamically determined by temporal-semantic relevance and the model's own predictive tendencies, is expected to enrich supervision, inject forward-looking pressure, and ultimately improve reasoning performance without requiring architectural changes.
related_work: Standard auto-regressive next-token prediction (NTP) trains LLMs to predict only the single next token, which can suppress the model's inherent capacity to anticipate long-range future tokens. The rigid one-hot objective limits semantic flexibility and can lead to over-confidence. Alternatives like Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) use multiple prediction heads to foresee several future tokens, but this introduces significant computational and memory overhead and still relies on one-hot fitting for each head. Other approaches use register tokens or auxiliary losses, but also add complexity. The research gap is the need for a method that can enhance an LLM's anticipatory capacity and reasoning without the high cost of MTP or major architectural changes, by better utilizing the information available within the standard training paradigm.
abstract: Auto-regressive large language models (LLMs) exhibit a non-trivial capacity to "anticipate" long-range future tokens despite being trained to predict only one token at a time. However, how to systematically profile, enhance, and leverage this capacity to improve LLM reasoning performance remains unclear. This paper proposes Next Token-Bag Exploitation (Next-ToBE) to tackle this challenge. Next-ToBE aims to quantify and enhance LLM’s anticipatory capacity, which is often suppressed by the rigid one-hot objective in next-token prediction. To address this, the proposed method replaces the one-hot target vector with a soft target distribution spanning a bag of future tokens. The immediate next token retains the highest importance, while more distant "look-ahead tokens" are included to enrich supervision, with their importance dynamically determined by temporal and semantic relevance patterns. The fitting process is also designed to emphasize the model’s intrinsic anticipatory tendency to preserve the pre-trained model's fidelity and improve training stability.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of Next-ToBE in improving mathematical reasoning performance when fine-tuning pre-trained LLMs.
experiments.Method: Models (Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) will be fine-tuned using LoRA on the NuminaMath-CoT dataset. Performance will be compared against Next-Token Prediction (NTP), Medusa (MTP), and MuToR baselines on the MATH, MinervaMath, Olympiad Bench, AIME24, and AMC23 benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, where the final answer must be enclosed in a specific format (e.g., \\boxed{})']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of Next-ToBE in improving code generation performance when fine-tuning pre-trained LLMs.
experiments.Method: Models (Qwen-2.5-1.5/7B, Llama-3.1-8B-instruct) will be fine-tuned using LoRA on the CodeAlpaca-20k dataset. Performance will be compared against NTP, Medusa (MTP), and MuToR baselines on the HumanEval and MBPP benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of Next-ToBE in improving commonsense reasoning performance when fine-tuning pre-trained LLMs.
experiments.Method: Models (Qwen-2.5-1.5/7B, Llama-3.1-8B-instruct) will be fine-tuned using LoRA on the Commonsense-15k dataset. Performance will be compared against NTP, Medusa (MTP), and MuToR baselines on the PIQA, Hellsawage, ARC-C, ARC-E, and SIQA benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, evaluated using official codebases for each benchmark']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether Next-ToBE can cultivate anticipatory capacity from scratch in a pre-training setting and translate it into improved downstream performance.
experiments.Method: A GPT-2 style model (124M) will be trained from scratch on the WikiText-103 dataset using three different objectives: Next-ToBE, NTP, and MTP. The models will then be evaluated on downstream tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Perplexity (PPL) on the WikiText-103 test set', 'Future-token Hit Ratio (FtHR) on the WikiText-103 test set', 'Accuracy on the HellaSwag QA benchmark']

risk_factors: Although LLMs can pre-capture the look-ahead tokens by giving them non-zero probabilities in the current prediction, their actual values can be low, leading to heavily negative log-likelihood. This could dominate the loss and force the learning process to try very hard to raise these small probabilities, which may induce unstable training.
Next-ToBE incurs a modest overhead relative to standard NTP in terms of training time and peak memory usage.

title: HT-Sparse: Training-Free Query-Guided Head–Token Sparsification for Long-Video Multimodal Inference
name: ht-sparse-long-video-inference
short_hypothesis: At inference time and without weight updates, it is possible to construct a joint head-token sparsification that (i) selects informative heads in a query-conditioned manner; (ii) selects salient visual tokens to reduce input cardinality; and (iii) in selected layers, routes different head subsets to different token granularities so that top heads attend to all tokens while secondary heads attend to selected tokens. This approach is intended to avoid semantic loss while reducing compute and KV-cache.
related_work: Existing approaches for efficient long-video inference have several limitations. Fixed or hand-crafted sparse attention patterns are not content-adaptive and can degrade accuracy. Heuristic token dropping methods can be brittle for long videos where informative regions are sparse and entangled with distractors. Methods that operate at a single granularity, such as only pruning heads or only selecting tokens, are sub-optimal as they fail to exploit the complementary nature of head and token redundancy. Finally, many existing sparsity mechanisms require parameter updates or task-specific retraining, which limits their deployability in production settings due to data governance, latency-to-market, and distribution shift risks. This creates a need for a training-free, input-adaptive mechanism that can reduce computation and memory while preserving performance.
abstract: Long-video multimodal inference is limited by the quadratic cost of dense attention, cumulative KV-cache growth during decoding, and cross-modal interference, while retraining sparsity-aware variants is often impractical. We present HT-Sparse, a training-free, query-guided hierarchical sparsification that performs joint head–token computation to reduce both latency and memory without parameter updates. The method comprises two components executed adaptively across layers: (i) query-conditioned head sparsification, which ranks attention heads via analytically stable saliency statistics to retain the most informative subspaces for the current query; (ii) cross-modal token sparsification, which selects salient visual tokens by query–vision attention, enabling efficient computation and persistent KV-cache savings. We further introduce joint head–token routing in selected layers: top-ranked heads attend to the full visual token set, whereas secondary heads operate on the reduced (selected) set, preserving semantics while amortizing compute and cache. The approach is model-agnostic and plug-in deployable, offering a flexible route to scalable long-video reasoning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the task quality and inference efficiency of the proposed HT-Sparse method and its ablated variants on long-video understanding benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The method will be instantiated on two 7B-class multimodal LLMs: Qwen2.5-VL-7B and LLaVA-Video-7B. The evaluation will be conducted on four long-video benchmarks: VideoMME, MLVU, LongVB, and LVBench. The following inference-time variants will be compared: 'Dense' (standard dense attention), 'Head-only' (query-conditioned head sparsification only), 'Token-only' (cross-modal token sparsification only), and 'HT-Sparse' (joint head-token sparsification with joint routing).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on QA-style datasets', 'Peak GPU memory', 'KV-cache footprint (for prefill and per-token decode)', 'End-to-end latency', 'Prefill FLOPs', 'Decode FLOPs']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to disentangle the contribution of the joint head-token routing mechanism and the layer policy.
experiments.Method: The contribution of joint routing will be analyzed by comparing three settings: a sequential pipeline with no full-coverage heads, a sequential pipeline with at least one full-coverage head, and the proposed joint routing. The layer policy will be evaluated by applying joint routing to different combinations of early, mid, and late layer blocks (E, M, L, E+M, M+L, E+L, E+M+L).

risk_factors: (1) Head scoring relies on analytically stable but heuristic saliency statistics; failure cases may arise under extreme domain shifts.
(2) Selection knobs (HF, Hs, L˜v, ηc, τ) require modest tuning to balance fidelity and efficiency across models and datasets.
(3) Our evaluations focus on 7B-class VLMs and long-video QA/localization; broader coverage (larger models, instruction-following, retrieval-heavy tasks) remains future work.
(4) Joint routing adds light control-flow overhead; kernel-level co-design could further reduce wall-clock cost.

title: Observe-Then-Think: Learning to Elicit Multimodal Understanding by Decoupling Perception and Reasoning
name: Observe-Then-Think
short_hypothesis: Explicitly decoupling perception and reasoning into a structured 'observe-then-think' process, implemented via a two-stage post-training framework (SFT and RL), will elicit stronger perceptual abilities and improve multimodal reasoning in Vision-Language Models. The SFT stage will teach the model a structured output format, and the RL stage, using a Perception-Guided Consistency Optimization (PGCO) algorithm with specialized rewards, will jointly incentivize perceptual accuracy and reasoning consistency.
related_work: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown impressive capabilities but still struggle with complex, multi-step reasoning tasks. This is partly due to an imbalance between perceptual alignment and reasoning depth. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training, particularly methods like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has improved text-based reasoning, its application to VLMs is hindered because limited perception remains a key bottleneck. The central research question is whether RL can be used not only to sharpen reasoning but also to elicit stronger perceptual abilities in VLMs, thereby enabling more comprehensive multimodal reasoning. This work proposes to bridge this perception-reasoning gap by explicitly separating the two processes, inspired by the division of labor in the human brain's visual and prefrontal cortices.
abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved impressive results in fusing visual and textual inputs, yet they often stumble on tasks demanding complex, multimodal reasoning. This imbalance arises from the inherent separation between perception—accurately interpreting sensory data, and reasoning—conducting multi-step, symbolic inference. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel framework for multimodal reasoning, OTT (Observe-Then-Think), which includes a two-stage post-training process: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL). During SFT, the model learns to decouple perceptual understanding from logical inference, mastering structured output formats and ensuring logical consistency. In the RL stage, our Perception-Guided Consistency Optimization (PGCO) algorithm, inspired by human cognition, enhances visual understanding through perception rewards and employs consistency rewards to align perception and reasoning steps, ensuring the final answer accuracy, eliminating logical contradictions without external tool support.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall effectiveness and generality of the proposed OTT method against state-of-the-art closed and open-source models across a wide range of visual reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed OTT-7B model, based on Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct, will be evaluated against baselines including GPT-4o, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, InternVL2-8B, MiniCPM-V2.6-8B, Mulberry-7B, R1-VL-7B, and the base Qwen2-VL-7B model. The evaluation will be conducted on six benchmarks: MathVista, WeMath, BLINK, V*Bench, VisuLogic, and MME.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Benchmark-specific accuracy scores.', 'For MathVista and WeMath, a powerful judge model (Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct) will be used for evaluation.', 'For MME, a sum score will be used.']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the importance of the 'Observe' component within the reasoning framework.
experiments.Method: The performance of a base model (Qwen2-VL-7B) will be compared with a vanilla GRPO implementation and a GRPO implementation that incorporates the 'Observe' strategy. The evaluation will be performed on the MathVista and MME datasets.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed two-stage OTT method against alternative training strategies, specifically vanilla GRPO and SFT alone.
experiments.Method: The base model (Qwen2-VL-7B) will be trained using three different strategies: vanilla GRPO, SFT only, and the full OTT method. The comparison will be made on the V*Bench and VisuLogic datasets.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the synergistic effects of the perception and consistency reward components within the PGCO algorithm.
experiments.Method: The OTT framework will be evaluated with different reward configurations: using only the perception reward, using only the consistency reward, and using both rewards combined. The evaluation will be performed on the WeMath and MathVista datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the scalability of the OTT framework across different model sizes.
experiments.Method: The OTT framework will be applied to two base models of different scales, Qwen2-VL-2B and Qwen2-VL-7B. Their performance will be compared against their respective base models on the BLINK and MathVista benchmarks. This will be tested using both a 20k and a 260k SFT dataset.

experiments.Description: To assess the impact of varying SFT and RL data scales on the performance of the OTT framework.
experiments.Method: Using Qwen2-VL-7B as the base model, experiments will be run by: 1) varying the SFT data size (20k vs. 260k) while keeping the RL data size fixed, and 2) varying the RL data size (5k vs. 20k) while keeping the SFT data size fixed. The evaluation will be performed on the BLINK and MME datasets.

risk_factors: Although vision-language models (VLMs) have made great strides in combining visual and linguistic data, they still struggle with complex reasoning tasks requiring deep abstract understanding.

The main challenge is integrating perception, which handles sensory inputs, with reasoning, which involves symbolic and multi-step inference, and the lack of high-quality multimodal reasoning datasets further limits their real-world performance in areas like scientific analysis.
However, it also raises concerns about potential misuse, such as the creation of misleading content, necessitating careful and responsible deployment.

title: Fine-Tuning of Continuous-Time Diffusion Models as Entropy-Regularized Control
name: EntropyRegularizedDiffusionFinetuning
short_hypothesis: Framing the finetuning problem of diffusion models as entropy-regularized control against the pretrained model will allow for the optimization of entropy-enhanced rewards with neural SDEs, mitigating reward collapse.
related_work: Existing approaches to finetune diffusion models in a goal-directed way by maximizing a reward function can lead to reduced sample diversity, significant deviations from the training data distribution, and poor sample quality due to the exploitation of an imperfect reward function. These challenges are collectively termed 'reward collapse'.
abstract: Diffusion models excel at capturing complex data distributions, but fine-tuning them for a specific goal by maximizing a reward function can lead to issues like reduced sample diversity and poor quality, a problem known as 'reward collapse'. This often occurs when exploiting an imperfect, learned reward function. To address this, we frame the finetuning problem as entropy-regularized control against the pretrained diffusion model, i.e., directly optimizing entropy-enhanced rewards with neural SDEs.

title: GenCorres: Consistent Shape Matching via Coupled Implicit-Explicit Shape Generative Models
name: GenCorres-JSM-via-Coupled-Generators
short_hypothesis: A mesh generator can be learned to solve unsupervised joint shape matching (JSM) for an unorganized collection of deformable shapes. By coupling an implicit generator (for robust initialization) with an explicit mesh generator and introducing novel regularization losses that constrain deformations between adjacent synthetic shapes to preserve local geometric structure and cycle-consistency, it is possible to unify pairwise and consistent matching, leverage a large synthetic dataset, and obtain a concise encoding for correspondences.
related_work: Prior work in shape matching includes pairwise methods, which struggle with dissimilar shapes, and joint shape matching (JSM) methods, which enforce cycle-consistency but often decouple pairwise and joint matching and can be sensitive to initialization. Generative models have been used for correspondence, but mesh-based generators often require pre-existing correspondences, while point-based ones are permutation-invariant and may not yield meaningful correspondences. Matching implicit surfaces is also challenging due to limited constraints. This work proposes to bridge these gaps by learning coupled implicit-explicit generative models from unorganized shapes, using novel geometric and cycle-consistency regularizations to establish high-quality, consistent correspondences without supervision.
abstract: This paper introduces GenCorres, a novel unsupervised joint shape matching (JSM) approach. Our key idea is to learn a mesh generator to fit an unorganized deformable shape collection while constraining deformations between adjacent synthetic shapes to preserve geometric structures such as local rigidity and local conformality. GenCorres presents three appealing advantages over existing JSM techniques. First, GenCorres performs JSM among a synthetic shape collection whose size is much bigger than the input shapes and fully leverages the data-driven power of JSM. Second, GenCorres unifies consistent shape matching and pairwise matching (i.e., by enforcing deformation priors between adjacent synthetic shapes). Third, the generator provides a concise encoding of consistent shape correspondences. However, learning a mesh generator from an unorganized shape collection is challenging, requiring a good initialization. GenCorres addresses this issue by learning an implicit generator from the input shapes, which provides intermediate shapes between two arbitrary shapes. We introduce a novel approach for computing correspondences between adjacent implicit surfaces, which we use to regularize the implicit generator. Synthetic shapes of the implicit generator then guide initial fittings (i.e., via template-based deformation) for learning the mesh generator.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the shape generation quality of the proposed GenCorres model.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on the Human (DFAUST, SMAL) and Animal shape collections. It will be compared against several state-of-the-art shape generation baselines that do not require ground-truth correspondences, including implicit generators (DeepSDF, SALD) and point-based generators (LGF, DPM).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of GenCorres on the task of Joint Shape Matching (JSM).
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the DFAUST and SMAL datasets. GenCorres will be compared against state-of-the-art JSM approaches (CZO, MIM) and a top-performing pairwise matching approach (NeuroMorph). The evaluation will consist of computing the correspondence error from a template shape to the rest of the shapes in the collection.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of GenCorres on a standard pairwise shape matching benchmark.
experiments.Method: The model will be applied to the FAUST dataset, augmented with shapes from the DFAUST dataset to form a larger collection. The performance will be compared against template-based methods (3D-CODED, IFS), axiomatic methods (BCICP, ZO, S-Shells), and spectral learning methods (GeoFM, AFmap, ULRSSM, D-Shells, NM).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to justify the importance of the different components of the GenCorres framework.
experiments.Method: The correspondence quality will be evaluated on the DFAUST and SMAL datasets by systematically altering the model. The planned ablations are: (1) removing the cycle-consistency regularization, (2) removing the geometric deformation regularization, (3) replacing ACAP regularization with ARAP regularization (GenCorres-NoACAP), and (4) using only the implicit generator without the explicit mesh generator (GenCorres-Imp).

risk_factors: One limitation of GenCorres is that it requires a reasonably large input shape collection to learn the shape generator and does not work with few input shapes.
How to incorporate intrinsic features into our pipeline is left for future research.
So far, the regularization terms are based on discretizing implicit surfaces into meshes. An interesting question is how to define them without mesh discretization.

title: Diffusion SigFormer for Interference Time-series Signal Recognition
name: diffusion-sigformer
short_hypothesis: A novel interference signal recognition transformer, Diffusion SigFormer, which combines a diffusion signal denoising module with a transformer-convolution hybrid (SigFormer), can effectively recognize and classify interference time-series signals.
related_work: The various interferences in the actual environment make electromagnetic signal recognition challenging, creating a need for methods with robust anti-interference capabilities.
abstract: The various interferences in the actual environment make electromagnetic signal recognition a challenging but important task. To address this, a novel interference signal recognition transformer, named Diffusion SigFormer, is proposed. The method first uses a diffusion signal denoising module to denoise the input interference signal. Then, a model named SigFormer, which leverages 1-D Patch Embedding and combines a transformer with convolution, is designed to extract features from and classify the denoised signal.

experiments.Description: To verify the anti-interference ability and recognition performance of the proposed Diffusion SigFormer method.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be evaluated on the RML2016.10a, RML2016.10b, and BT datasets.

title: Modeling Time Series as Text Sequence A Frequency-vectorization Transformer for Time Series Forecasting
name: freq-tst-tokenization
short_hypothesis: By transforming a time series into the frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), its constituent frequency components can be treated as discrete tokens. These frequency units can then be vectorized using a shared, fixed dictionary and processed by a standard Transformer encoder for forecasting, effectively modeling the time series as a text sequence.
related_work: Transformer-based models excel at sequence modeling for text and DNA but struggle with time series data because individual numerical data points lack inherent semantics for tokenization. Previous methods have used the frequency domain to extract features or for auxiliary computation, but they ultimately transform the data back to the time domain before the main modeling step. No prior work has utilized the frequency domain to directly tokenize a continuous time series for processing by a Transformer, treating it analogously to a sequence of words.
abstract: Transformer-based models have achieved great success in modeling sequence data but fail to understand time series sequences because individual numerical data points cannot be easily tokenized. To address this challenge, we propose a frequency-vectorization time series forecasting method. We propose to utilize the frequency spectrum as a common dictionary for tokenizing time series, which converts a single time series into a sequence of frequency units with weights. This vectorized frequency token sequence can then be modeled by transformer layers for prediction tasks. To align the frequency and time domains, we also introduce two pretraining tasks: a time series reconstruction task and a maximum position prediction task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the forecasting performance of the proposed FreqTST model against state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets.
experiments.Method: The FreqTST model will be trained in a supervised manner on four datasets: Weather, Electricity, ETTm2, and ETTh2. The look-back window is fixed at 96, and performance will be evaluated for forecasting lengths of 96, 192, 336, and 720. The model will be compared against a set of baselines including Informer, Autoformer, FEDformer, FiLM, Dlinear, and PatchTST.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed pretraining tasks (Time Series Reconstruction and Maximum Position Prediction).
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted on the Weather and Electricity datasets. The performance of four model variants will be compared: 1) FreqTST (supervised only), 2) FreqTSTOnlyTSR (pretraining with only the TSR task), 3) FreqTSTOnlyMPP (pretraining with only the MPP task), and 4) FreqTSTpretraining (pretraining with both tasks).

experiments.Description: To verify the long-term forecasting performance of FreqTST.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Weather, Electricity, and ETTh2 datasets with extended forecasting lengths of 960, 1120, 1280, and 1440. It will be compared against long-term forecasting baselines including Autoformer, FEDformer, DLinear, and PatchTSTpretraining.

experiments.Description: To verify the zero-shot transfer learning ability of the FreqTST model.
experiments.Method: The FreqTST and FreqTSTpretraining models will be pretrained on the large-scale Electricity dataset and then tested directly on the small Etth2 dataset without any fine-tuning. Their performance will be compared to PatchTST and PatchTSTpretraining models under the same zero-shot setting, as well as to fully supervised Autoformer and FEDformer models trained on Etth2.

risk_factors: The frequency domain representation is insensitive to amplitude variations.
The downstream forecasting task is strongly associated with time domain information, so only pretraining frequency tokens may limit the forecasting performance.

title: Towards Second-Order Optimization in Learned Image Compression: Faster, Better, and More Deployable
name: soap-for-lic
short_hypothesis: Replacing standard first-order optimizers like Adam with a second-order quasi-Newton optimizer, SOAP, will act as a seamless drop-in replacement that dramatically improves training efficiency and final rate-distortion performance for Learned Image Compression (LIC) models. This is because SOAP's Newton preconditioning will inherently resolve the intra-step and inter-step gradient conflicts that are intrinsic to the rate-distortion objective, leading to faster, more stable convergence and producing models with fewer outliers, which enhances deployability.
related_work: Training Learned Image Compression (LIC) models typically uses first-order optimizers like Adam, but this approach suffers from slow convergence and suboptimal performance due to gradient conflicts between the rate and distortion objectives. Previous work has tried to accelerate training via methods like auxiliary transforms (AuxT), parameter decomposition (CMD-LIC), or explicit gradient re-weighting (Balanced R-D). However, these strategies often introduce additional architectural complexity, rely on fragile hyperparameter tuning, or require non-trivial modifications to the training pipeline. This creates a need for a method that can accelerate training and mitigate gradient conflicts without these added overheads, ideally as a simple drop-in replacement for the standard optimizer.
abstract: Training learned image compression (LIC) models entails navigating a challenging optimization landscape defined by the fundamental trade-off between rate and distortion. Standard first-order optimizers, such as SGD and Adam, struggle with gradient conflicts arising from competing objectives, leading to slow convergence and suboptimal rate–distortion performance. In this work, we propose that a simple switch to a second-order quasi-Newton optimizer, SOAP, will dramatically improve both training efficiency and final performance across diverse LIC architectures. Our theoretical and empirical analyses are designed to reveal that SOAP’s Newton preconditioning inherently resolves the intra-step and inter-step update conflicts intrinsic to the R–D objective, facilitating faster, more stable convergence. Beyond acceleration, we aim to uncover a critical deployability benefit: that SOAP-trained models will exhibit significantly fewer activation and latent outliers, which would improve entropy modeling and substantially enhance robustness to post-training quantization.

experiments.Description: To empirically demonstrate the benefits of second-order optimization (SOAP) on training acceleration and final rate-distortion (R-D) performance compared to a first-order method (Adam) and other specialized training strategies across several representative LIC architectures.
experiments.Method:
Models: ELIC, TCM, LALIC, and DCAE.
Training Data: COCO 2017 dataset.
Evaluation Datasets: Kodak, Tecnick, and CLIC 2022.
Baselines: The primary baseline is the Adam optimizer. Comparisons will also be made against other training strategies including AuxT, CMD-LIC, and Balanced-RD.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Steps-to-Adam: The ratio of training steps required to reach a target validation loss, normalized by the number required by Adam.', 'Time-to-Adam: The ratio of training time to reach a target validation loss, relative to Adam.', 'BD-Rate after Convergence: The average bitrate savings at matched image quality between a model and its Adam-trained counterpart after full convergence.']

experiments.Description: To validate the theoretical hypothesis that SOAP's performance gains stem from its ability to mitigate gradient conflicts by aligning update vectors both within a single step (intra-step) and across consecutive steps (inter-step).
experiments.Method: The ELIC model will be trained with both Adam and SOAP, initialized from a pretrained model to observe behavior near a local minimum. The cosine similarity of update vectors will be tracked during training.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Intra-step score (S_intra): Cosine similarity between the rate and distortion update vectors within a single step.', 'Inter-step score (S_inter): Cosine similarity of the total update vector across consecutive steps.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that SOAP-trained models produce fewer latent and activation outliers, and to evaluate whether this translates to enhanced robustness under post-training quantization (PTQ).
experiments.Method: Models (ELIC, TCM, LALIC, DCAE) trained with Adam and SOAP will be evaluated. Outlier statistics will be measured on the Kodak dataset. PTQ robustness will be assessed using W8A8 (int8 weights and activations) quantization with the AdaRound method. Latent scaled deviation maps for the ELIC model will also be visualized.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Kurt(X): A measure of the tailedness of channel energies.', 'MaxMed(X): A measure of per-sample extreme values relative to typical magnitudes.', 'ΔBD-Rate (%): The change in BD-Rate after applying W8A8 post-training quantization, to measure robustness.']

risk_factors: The argument is layerwise and ignores inter-layer curvature; nonetheless, in practice, the rotated-space diagonalization substantially improves conditioning compared to first-order methods.
Finite-sample EMAs, infrequent preconditioner updates, and regularization (+δI) introduce additional approximation error.

The Adam preconditioner tracks (diagonal) second moments of gradients (Fisher-like), not the exact Hessian diagonal; the identification in equation 15 is a local approximation strongest when diag(HGN) ≈ diag(H) near the optimum.

title: Harnessing spectral representations for subgraph alignment
name: spectral-maps-for-subgraph-alignment
short_hypothesis: A spectral representation for maps between graphs, known as functional maps, can provide a compact, robust, and efficient framework for subgraph alignment. This representation is hypothesized to exhibit a special, sparse structure in its coefficients that captures the partiality of the subgraph relationship, even in the presence of topological changes and in the absence of exact isomorphism.
related_work: Prior work on graph alignment primarily focuses on node-to-node correspondence, using methods like IsoRank or embedding-based matching. These approaches can be computationally infeasible for large graphs and often target specific applications. The functional maps framework, originally from geometry processing, offers an alternative by operating in a spectral domain. While some works have applied it to graphs, they have been limited to specific cases like Euclidean graphs or noisy complete graphs. This paper addresses the gap by proposing to use functional maps for the more general and challenging problem of subgraph alignment on arbitrary graphs, including those with significant partiality and topological perturbations, and aims to characterize the structure of these maps in this setting.
abstract: With the rise and advent of graph learning techniques, graph data has become ubiquitous. However, less effort is being spent on problems involving maps between graphs, such as signal transfer, graph isomorphism and subgraph correspondence. With this paper, we anticipate the need for a convenient framework to deal with such problems, and focus in particular on the challenging subgraph alignment scenario. We claim that the representation of a map plays a central role on how these problems should be modeled. Taking the hint from recent work in geometry processing, we propose the adoption of a spectral representation for maps that is compact, easy to compute, robust to topological changes, easy to plug into existing pipelines, and is especially effective for subgraph alignment problems.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of the functional map representation to simultaneous partiality and topological changes (rewiring) in the subgraph.
experiments.Method: For six graphs (Cat, CORA, QM9, Minnesota, Citeseer, Karate), a subgraph will be computed. Then, small incremental topological changes (removing and adding random edges) will be applied, with increments of 3% of the total number of edges. The variation in the functional map matrix C will be analyzed and compared to the effect of adding Gaussian noise.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of the functional map matrix C', "Frobenius norm of the difference between the original and perturbed functional maps (||C - C'||_F)"]

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the functional map representation for transferring node signals (positional encodings) from a graph to its subgraphs.
experiments.Method: On datasets including PPIO, CiteSeer, PPI13, AmazonPhoto, FraudAmazon, and AmazonComputer, Random Walk Positional Encodings will be computed on the full graphs. These signals will then be transferred to subgraphs using the functional map formula. The performance will be analyzed as the number of eigenfunctions used in the map representation is increased.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) between the transferred signal and the ground-truth transferred signal']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of using a spectral representation for the task of inferring node-to-node correspondence between a graph and its subgraph, comparing it to established graph matching baselines.
experiments.Method: On datasets including Cat, Minnesota, Cora, and Douban, two functional map-based matching algorithms (FMw and FMw + ZM) will be applied. These will be compared against IsoRank, FINAL, REGAL, and PALE. All methods will use 50 landmark matches as input. The performance will also be evaluated using a ground-truth (GT) functional map to isolate the representation's quality from the matching algorithm's performance.

risk_factors: while in this paper we showed extensive evidence that the spectral map representation bears a special structure depending on the type of partiality, currently we have not taken full advantage of this structure.

When the task at hand requires seeking for the subgraph alignment, i.e. whenever the map is unknown, it may be possible to design stronger regularizers to induce sparsity in the matrix representation of the map.

However, there is no theoretical counterpart to explain the map structure between G1 and its subgraphs, due to the complete absence of metric information about the underlying surface: the eigenfunctions are computed purely from the graph connectivity.

title: Robust LLM safeguarding via refusal feature adversarial training
name: Refusal-Feature-Adversarial-Training
short_hypothesis: A novel algorithm, Refusal Feature Adversarial Training (ReFAT), can efficiently perform LLM adversarial training by simulating the effect of input-level attacks via Refusal Feature Ablation (RFA). This is based on the hypothesis that adversarial attacks share a universal mechanism of ablating a 'refusal feature' in the residual stream, and that RFA can approximate the worst-case perturbation of offsetting model safety.
related_work: Large language models (LLMs) are vulnerable to various adversarial attacks, including gradient-based searches (GCG), automated modifications (PAIR), and genetic algorithms (AutoDAN). Existing defensive methods like adversarial training (AT) have proven effective but are often computationally prohibitive, requiring thousands of model evaluations to simulate a single attack. Another line of work, representation engineering (RepE), has identified and manipulated linear features in activation space, such as the 'refusal feature', to control model behavior. The research gap lies in the high computational cost of robust AT methods, which prevents their widespread adoption. This work aims to bridge AT and RepE by proposing an efficient AT method that leverages insights about the refusal feature to simulate attacks without expensive search procedures.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that can elicit harmful responses. Defending against such attacks remains challenging due to the opacity of jailbreaking mechanisms and the high computational cost of training LLMs robustly. We demonstrate that adversarial attacks share a universal mechanism for circumventing LLM safeguards that works by ablating a dimension in the residual stream embedding space called the refusal feature. We further show that the operation of refusal feature ablation (RFA) approximates the worst-case perturbation of offsetting model safety. Based on these findings, we propose Refusal Feature Adversarial Training (ReFAT), a novel algorithm that efficiently performs LLM adversarial training by simulating the effect of input-level attacks via RFA.

experiments.Description: To assess if ReFAT enhances LLM robustness against a wide range of adversarial attacks compared to baseline defense methods.
experiments.Method: Models (Llama-3-8B, Mistral-7B, Gemma-7B) will be fine-tuned using ReFAT. The training data consists of 5000 harmful requests from Zou et al. (2024), 5000 harmless examples from UltraChat, and 150 examples from XSTest. The robustness of these models will be evaluated on harmful requests from HarmBench and AdvBench datasets using four attack methods: GCG, PAIR, AutoDAN, and HumanJailbreaks, as well as an RFA attack. The performance will be compared against several baselines: standard refusal training (RT), Robust Refusal Dynamic Defense (R2D2), Continuous Adversarial Training (CAT), and Latent Adversarial Training (LAT).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Attack Success Rate (ASR), as computed by three LLM-as-a-judge models: the official HarmBench classifier, Llama-Guard-2, and the Gemma-2B version of the StrongReject safety classifier.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if ReFAT preserves the general capabilities and utility of the LLMs, avoiding significant performance degradation on standard benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The same ReFAT-trained models and baselines from the robustness experiment (Llama-3-8B, Mistral-7B, Gemma-7B) will be evaluated on standard LLM benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Performance scores on MMLU and MT-Bench.

experiments.Description: To assess whether ReFAT causes the models to become overly cautious and refuse benign requests.
experiments.Method: The ReFAT-trained models and baselines will be evaluated on a held-out set of 100 test examples from the XSTest dataset, which includes benign requests that may appear risky.

risk_factors: Future work should address this limitation of the current ReFAT method by employing a semantically and linguistically more diverse set of instructions to compute refusal features.
One concern is that increased robustness might lead to overconfidence in the safety of LLMs, highlighting the need for ongoing red teaming efforts.
Another potential negative impact is the possibility that adversarial training could be misused to suppress content the model operator deems undesirable, regardless of its harmfulness.

Our analysis of ReFAT’s failure modes reveals that LLMs may remain vulnerable to certain types of attacks (e.g., multilingual attacks), underscoring the importance of developing more rigorous and reliable evaluation protocols.

title: Expressivity of Neural Networks with Random Weights and Learned Biases
name: universal-approximation-with-learned-biases
short_hypothesis: Feedforward and recurrent neural networks with fixed, randomly initialized weights are universal function approximators when only their bias parameters are learned. Specifically, with high probability, wide FNNs can approximate any continuous function, and wide RNNs can approximate finite-time trajectories from smooth dynamical systems, solely by tuning biases.
related_work: Universal approximation theorems traditionally rely on tuning both weights and biases. Subsequent work has shown that tuning smaller parameter subsets, like only the output weights (in random feature models) or using masks (Lottery Ticket Hypothesis), can still yield expressive networks. However, it remains an open question whether universal approximation holds when only biases are learned, a scenario motivated by efficient fine-tuning methods in AI and models of neuromodulation in the brain.
abstract: Landmark universal function approximation results for neural networks with trained weights and biases provided the impetus for their use. Recent work has extended these results to networks in which a smaller subset of weights are tuned, leaving other parameters random. However, it remains an open question whether universal approximation holds when only biases are learned, despite evidence from neuroscience and AI that biases significantly shape neural responses. This paper proposes to answer this question by providing theoretical and numerical evidence for the hypothesis that feedforward neural networks with fixed random weights can approximate any continuous function on compact sets. An analogous result for the approximation of dynamical systems with recurrent neural networks is also proposed.

experiments.Description: To validate the theoretical claim that bias-learning FNNs are universal approximators by testing their performance on a standard image classification task and evaluating how performance scales with the number of trainable parameters (i.e., hidden units).
experiments.Method: A single-hidden-layer FNN will be trained on the Fashion MNIST classification task. The experiment will compare three set-ups: a fully-trained network, a bias-learned network with uniformly distributed random weights, and a bias-learned network with Gaussian random weights. The number of trainable parameters will be varied to observe scaling.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that a single network with fixed random weights can learn multiple tasks by optimizing task-specific bias vectors.
experiments.Method: A single-hidden-layer FNN with a fixed random weight matrix will be trained on 7 different image classification tasks: MNIST, KMNIST, Fashion MNIST, Ethiopic-MNIST, Vai-MNIST, Osmanya-MNIST, and Kannada-MNIST. For each task, a separate bias vector will be learned. The performance will be compared to a fully-trained network of the same architecture trained on the same tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Validation accuracy per task', 'Task Variance (TV) of hidden units', 'Spearman correlation between TV and bias vectors']

experiments.Description: To investigate the relationship between bias learning and mask learning, based on the theoretical intuition that bias learning works by effectively 'shutting off' units, similar to masking.
experiments.Method: On the MNIST dataset, two learning paradigms will be compared using the same randomly initialized weights in a single-hidden-layer ReLU network: (1) bias learning, where only biases are trained, and (2) mask learning, where binary masks are approximated using 'soft' sigmoid masks and learned. The solutions found by each method will be compared.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test accuracy', "Probability of unit overlap ('ON' units)", 'Histograms of unit variances']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the expressivity of bias-learning in RNNs for generating autonomous dynamical systems, both linear and nonlinear.
experiments.Method: An RNN with fixed random Gaussian weights and trainable biases will be trained to generate two autonomous systems: a simple cosine function and a nonlinear Van der Pol oscillator. The mechanism will be investigated by comparing the eigenvalue spectrum of the initial random recurrent weight matrix with the learned effective connectivity (Jacobian). The sensitivity to the initial weight distribution statistics (gain) will also be tested.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual comparison of generated vs. target trajectories', 'Eigenvalue spectra of the Jacobian', 'Training performance (loss)']

experiments.Description: To test the capability of bias-learning RNNs in a non-autonomous setting by predicting future states of a partially observed chaotic dynamical system.
experiments.Method: An RNN with random frozen weights and trained biases will be tasked with predicting a future time-step of a single dimension of the Lorenz attractor, given the current state as input. Performance will be compared against a fully-trained RNN.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Validation R²', 'Prediction accuracy in a self-sustained, autoregressive mode']

experiments.Description: To test whether bias learning in an RNN can solve a classic motor control problem, requiring the generation of diverse open-loop control signals from a single set of learned parameters.
experiments.Method: An RNN with random weights and a single set of learned biases will be trained to perform a center-out reaching task. Linear readouts from the RNN activity will control a simulated point-mass arm to reach 6 peripheral targets. The network's ability to generalize to new, unseen targets will also be evaluated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training loss', 'Trajectory accuracy to trained and novel targets', 'Velocity profiles of the arm movements']

risk_factors: First, the convergence results for dynamical systems were only for finite-time trajectories.
Second, a potential confounding factor in our comparison of bias and mask learning is that the latter approach used a learning schedule in the steepness parameter for the soft masks.

Third, a better grasp of bias learning scaling—how layer width or parameter count increases as a function of performance—is needed. Our theory does not shed light here as it significantly overestimates how a bias-learned network should scale (Remark 2 on Lemma 3 in Appendix).
Fourth, while our results appeared to generalize beyond one-layer networks (Fig. E.2E), a more detailed study of bias-learned deep FNNs is left to future work.

title: Locality-aware Parallel Decoding for Efficient Autoregressive Image Generation
name: locality-aware-parallel-decoding
short_hypothesis: By introducing a flexible parallelized autoregressive modeling architecture and a locality-aware generation order schedule, it is possible to significantly increase the parallelization of autoregressive image generation, thereby reducing latency without compromising generation quality. This is achieved by using learnable position query tokens to guide generation and by scheduling token groups to maximize contextual support while minimizing intra-group dependencies.
related_work: Traditional autoregressive image generation relies on next-patch prediction, a sequential and memory-bound process leading to high latency. Previous attempts to parallelize this by shifting to multi-patch prediction have shown limited success. Other approaches like next-scale prediction are incompatible with the universal flat token representation used by many foundation models. Non-autoregressive models can predict multiple patches but are less efficient as they require full bidirectional attention and cannot leverage KV caching. The research gap lies in achieving high parallelization and flexible generation ordering within the efficient autoregressive framework.
abstract: We present Locality-aware Parallel Decoding (LPD) to accelerate autoregressive image generation. Traditional autoregressive image generation relies on next-patch prediction, a memory-bound process that leads to high latency. Existing works have tried to parallelize next-patch prediction by shifting to multi-patch prediction to accelerate the process, but only achieved limited parallelization. To achieve high parallelization while maintaining generation quality, we introduce two key techniques: (1) Flexible Parallelized Autoregressive Modeling, a novel architecture that enables arbitrary generation ordering and degrees of parallelization. It uses learnable position query tokens to guide generation at target positions while ensuring mutual visibility among concurrently generated tokens for consistent parallel decoding. (2) Locality-aware Generation Ordering, a novel schedule that forms groups to minimize intra-group dependencies and maximize contextual support, enhancing generation quality.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed LPD method on class-conditional image generation at 256x256 resolution against a broad set of generative baselines.
experiments.Method: The LPD models (LPD-L, LPD-XL, LPD-XXL) are trained on the ImageNet 256x256 dataset for class-conditional generation. For fair comparison, a raster-order counterpart is also created. The method is compared against baselines from several categories: Diffusion models (ADM-G, LDM-4, DiT-XL/2), Masked models (MaskGIT, MAR), Variational Autoencoder models (VAR), standard Autoregressive models (LlamaGen, RAR), and other Parallelized Autoregressive models (PAR, RandAR, ARPG, NAR).

experiments.Description: To assess the scalability of the LPD method to higher resolution class-conditional image generation on a 512x512 dataset.
experiments.Method: LPD models are fine-tuned on the ImageNet 512x512 dataset from the 256-resolution checkpoints. Performance is compared against a raster-order counterpart and other high-resolution generative models including Diffusion (DiT-XL/2), Masked (MAGVIT-v2, MAR-L), VAR, and Parallelized AR (ARPG-XL) models.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the generalizability of the LPD method to high-resolution text-to-image generation.
experiments.Method: The LPD models are extended for text-to-image generation by incorporating a Gemma-2-2B text encoder and trained on a large-scale synthetic dataset. The evaluation is performed on the GenEval benchmark for 1024x1024 text-to-image generation, comparing against a raster-order counterpart.

experiments.Description: To perform ablation studies to isolate the contributions of the two main components of LPD: the flexible parallelized autoregressive modeling architecture and the locality-aware generation order schedule.
experiments.Method: Using the LPD-XL model on ImageNet 256x256, several ablations are planned. First, to test the modeling architecture, LPD is compared with ARPG and RandAR using a random generation order. Second, to test the generation schedule, the proposed locality-aware schedule is compared against random and Halton order schedules. Third, the two guiding principles of the locality-aware schedule (high proximity to context and low proximity among concurrent tokens) are ablated individually.

risk_factors: Our method introduces position query tokens to enable flexible generation. These tokens add extra queries and thereby increase FLOPs.

title: Reducing Atomic Clashes in Geometric Diffusion Models for 3D Structure-Based Drug Design
name: ProximalSamplingForAtomicClashes
short_hypothesis: By integrating a non-convex physical constraint into the Langevin Dynamics sampling process of a pre-trained Geometric Diffusion Model (GDM) and using proximal regularization techniques, it is possible to force generated molecular coordinates to obey physical distance constraints, thereby reducing atomic clashes without modifying the model's training process.
related_work: Geometric Diffusion Models (GDM) are state-of-the-art for 3D structure-based drug design. However, their data-driven nature can lead them to neglect physical principles, producing molecules with atomic clashes (atoms too close to the protein pocket). Prior work attempted to address this by using the gradient of clash constraints, but this approach can be problematic for non-smooth constraints. There is a need for a method that can robustly enforce physical constraints during the sampling phase of GDMs without requiring retraining.
abstract: In Three-dimensional Structure-Based Drug Design (3D SBDD), Geometric Diffusion Models (GDM) are state-of-the-art but can produce molecules that violate physical principles, exhibiting atomic clashes with protein structures. This is due to the inherent data-driven nature of GDMs, which can neglect critical inter-molecular interactions. To address this, we introduce a novel constrained sampling process. By integrating a non-convex constraint within the Langevin Dynamics (LD) of GDM and utilizing proximal regularization techniques, we propose to force molecular coordinates to obey imposed physical constraints. This method requires no modifications to the training process of GDMs.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed constrained sampling method in reducing atomic clashes compared to existing generative models.
experiments.Method: The proposed constrained sampling formula will be applied to the TargetDiff model. Its performance will be compared against several baseline models: PMDM, DiffSBDD, and Decompdiff. All models are to be evaluated on a pre-divided test set of 100 protein samples from the CrossDocked2020 dataset.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the key parameters of the proposed constrained sampling process to understand their impact on generation quality and clash reduction.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be evaluated by varying its adjustable parameters, including the number of nearest protein atoms considered (k), the constraint weight (λ), and the time steps during which the constraint is applied.

experiments.Description: To assess whether the proposed constrained sampling method negatively affects the chemical and drug-like properties of the generated molecules.
experiments.Method: Molecules will be generated using the proposed method with different constraint settings. The chemical properties of these molecules will be analyzed and compared to those generated without constraints.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['SA (Synthesizability)', 'QED (Drug-likeness)', 'LogP', 'Lipinski', 'Vina score', 'High affinity', 'Jensen-Shannon divergence of bond distributions']

experiments.Description: To test the generalizability of the proposed constrained sampling method by applying it to a different diffusion model.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be applied to the DiffSBDD model and evaluated on the test set.

risk_factors: For future work, we believe that focus on a deeper understanding of the modeling of the diffusion model Spatial relationship between ligands and pockets. This may further improve model stability and efficiency in structure-based drug design.

title: DiTTo-TTS: Diffusion Transformers for Scalable Text-to-Speech without Domain-Specific Factors
name: ditto-tts-diffusion-transformer-for-scalable-tts
short_hypothesis: A Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) for Text-to-Speech (TTS) can achieve state-of-the-art performance without relying on domain-specific factors like phonemes and durations. This can be accomplished by using a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture, modeling variable speech length with a dedicated speech length predictor, and ensuring semantic alignment between text and speech latent representations through joint training or fine-tuning of the audio codec.
related_work: Large-scale latent diffusion models (LDMs) have shown success in various generative tasks but their application in text-to-speech (TTS) has been limited by a reliance on domain-specific factors like phonemes and durations. This complicates data preparation and hinders scalability. While recent pioneering works have explored removing these factors by using pre-trained encoders and cross-attention for alignment, their performance remains suboptimal. This raises the question of whether an LDM can achieve state-of-the-art TTS performance at scale without these domain-specific factors, and what architectural and training components are necessary for such success.
abstract: Large-scale latent diffusion models (LDMs) excel in content generation across various modalities, but their reliance on phonemes and durations in text-to-speech (TTS) limits scalability and access from other fields. While recent studies show potential in removing these domain-specific factors, performance remains suboptimal. In this work, we introduce DiTTo-TTS, a Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based TTS model, to investigate whether LDM-based TTS can achieve state-of-the-art performance without domain-specific factors. The proposed approach involves investigating the DiT architecture, variable-length modeling via a speech length predictor, and conditions like semantic alignment in speech latent representations. The model and training data are intended to be scaled to investigate performance improvements.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed DiTTo-TTS model against state-of-the-art TTS models on English-only zero-shot speech synthesis tasks.
experiments.Method: The DiTTo-en model, trained on 55K hours of English data, will be evaluated on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset for `continuation` and `cross-sentence` tasks. Performance will be compared against autoregressive models (VALL-E, SPEAR-TTS, CLaM-TTS), non-autoregressive models (YourTTS, Voicebox), and other diffusion-based models (Simple-TTS). Human evaluations will also be conducted.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Word Error Rate (WER)', 'Character Error Rate (CER)', 'Speaker Similarity (SIM-o, SIM-r)', 'Similarity MOS (SMOS)', 'Comparative MOS (CMOS)', 'Inference Time', '#Param.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the multilingual capabilities of the proposed model on a continuation task across nine languages.
experiments.Method: The DiTTo-multi model, trained on an 82K-hour multilingual dataset, will be evaluated on test sets for English, Korean, German, Dutch, French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, and Polish. Performance will be compared against the CLaM-multi baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Word Error Rate (WER)', 'Character Error Rate (CER)', 'Speaker Similarity (SIM-o, SIM-r)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the most suitable backbone architecture for a diffusion-based TTS model without domain-specific factors by comparing the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) against U-Net variants.
experiments.Method: Three architectural variants will be compared on an English cross-sentence task: a U-Net based on U-Audio Transformer, a Flat-U-Net without down/up-sampling, and the proposed DiTTo-mls architecture.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effectiveness of modeling variable speech lengths compared to fixed-length modeling approaches common in prior work.
experiments.Method: Four different speech length modeling approaches will be compared: `Fixed-length` (with partial padding in loss), `Fixed-length-full` (with full padding in loss), `SLP-CE` (proposed speech length predictor with cross-entropy loss), and `SLP-Regression` (predictor with a regression objective).

experiments.Description: To validate the hypothesis that aligning text and speech embeddings is crucial for effective cross-attention and overall TTS performance.
experiments.Method: DiTTo-mls variants will be trained using different text encoders (ByT5, SpeechT5) and neural audio codecs (the original Mel-VAE vs. a fine-tuned Mel-VAE++ aligned with text). The combinations will be evaluated on the English cross-sentence task to determine the impact of joint text-speech modeling.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Word Error Rate (WER)', 'Character Error Rate (CER)', 'Speaker Similarity (SIM-o, SIM-r)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the scalability of the DiTTo-TTS model with respect to both model size and dataset size.
experiments.Method: Four model sizes (Small, Base, Large, XLarge) will be trained on a 55K-hour English dataset. Additionally, experiments will be conducted by training models on data subsets of increasing sizes (0.5K, 5.5K, 10.5K, and 50.5K hours) to observe performance trends.

experiments.Description: To determine the most suitable latent representation for DiTTo-TTS by comparing different neural audio codecs.
experiments.Method: DiTTo-mls variants will be trained using different audio codecs as the diffusion target: Mel-VAE, EnCodec, and DAC. Their performance will be compared on the English cross-sentence task.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Word Error Rate (WER)', 'Speaker Similarity (SIM-o, SIM-r)', 'Codec PESQ', 'Codec ViSQOL']

risk_factors: We observe that models trained on smaller datasets like 0.5K show lower speech accuracy compared to phoneme-level duration-based models such as P-Flow (Kim et al., 2024b). This is one of the limitations of our model.

Although these results do not allow for a direct comparison with Voicebox's corresponding setting due to overfitting, we believe that incorporating phoneme-level durations during training, as done in Voicebox, ensures greater robustness when working with small datasets.

Its ability to synthesize any voice—including those of real individuals—with minimal data input presents potential risks, particularly in terms of misuse for malicious purposes such as voice spoofing, impersonation, or deepfake audio generation.

title: Latent Diffusion with LLMs for Reasoning
name: latent-diffusion-for-reasoning
short_hypothesis: Combining latent diffusion models (LDMs) with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture provides a scalable way to address the fundamental shortcomings of autoregressive models for complex reasoning. This is achieved by performing iterative reasoning in a semantically rich latent space, which is not handicapped by the sequential order of tokens and allows for non-sequential, self-correcting reasoning steps.
related_work: Autoregressive large language models (LLMs) are the de-facto standard for natural language generation but struggle with complex reasoning. Their limitations stem from their autoregressive nature, where compute is constant per token and generation is strictly sequential, preventing them from solving easier subproblems first. Prior approaches like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, longer context, and recurrent models have attempted to address these issues but are not general solutions. Discrete text diffusion models (e.g., Diffusion-of-Thought) have shown promise for self-correction but lack the training efficiency of traditional LLMs. This work proposes to merge the benefits of both worlds by using a latent diffusion model on top of representations from a pretrained encoder-decoder LLM.
abstract: Despite the widespread adoption of large language models with hundreds of billions of parameters, these models still struggle on complex reasoning benchmarks. In this paper, we argue that the autoregressive nature of current language models are not suited for reasoning due to fundamental limitations, and that reasoning requires slow accumulation of knowledge through time. We show that combining latent diffusion models with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture provides a scalable way to address some of the fundamental shortcomings posed by autoregressive models. Diffusion models can arrive at predictions through many forward passes in latent space, and their reasoning is not handicapped by the order of the tokens in the dataset. We propose that latent diffusion language models is a feasible approach towards scalable language models that have general complex reasoning abilities.

experiments.Description: To analyze the performance of the proposed hybrid architecture on downstream arithmetic reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed Latent Diffusion model is to be evaluated on custom-created problem sets for single digit addition (3-5 numbers), double digit addition, and single digit three-operation tasks. For single digit addition, Chain-of-Thought reasoning chains are provided as the target. The performance will be compared against fine-tuned BART and various sizes of GPT-3 (400M, 13B, 175B).

experiments.Description: To study the benefits of the proposed latent diffusion augmented LLM on a mock spatial reasoning problem that requires non-trivial reasoning chains to compute the first token.
experiments.Method: A custom spatial reasoning task is created where the model must perform a series of rotations based on input numbers. The proposed Latent Diffusion model will be tested on this task and its performance will be compared against a fine-tuned BART baseline.

experiments.Description: To ablate different architectural variants, diffusion sequence lengths, and model layers to determine the best architecture for scaling and to study the effect of different input text conditioning methods.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies are planned on the Common Crawl (C4) dataset. These include: 1) Comparing different input text conditioning architectures (In-Context, Cross-Attention, AdaLN-Zero). 2) Varying the number of model layers (6, 12, 24). 3) Varying the diffusion sequence length (16, 32, 64). 4) Comparing the use of different pretrained encoder-decoder backbones (BART-base vs. BART-large).

risk_factors: A notable limitation of diffusion is that it is relatively inefficient to train compared to conventional language models.
One hypothesis is that there is a combinatorial explosion as more tokens are diffused at once, hence the gradients are not as well-behaved (noisy gradient landscapes).
However, due to its inefficiencies and other potential obstacles, it remains uncertain how far we can practically scale such architectures with current hardware and algorithms.

We've found that generation length decreases with longer training times from preliminary experiments which led to uncomparable BertScores since longer sequences have higher BertScores on average and that shorter sequences have lower Perplexity on average.
Since experiments are trained with different hyperparameters for different learned generation lengths, metrics cannot be compared between different experiments.

title: Unified Neural Network Scaling Laws and Scale-time Equivalence
name: scale-time-equivalence
short_hypothesis: Scaling the size of a neural network is functionally equivalent to increasing its training time by a proportional factor. This 'scale-time equivalence' can be combined with a linear model analysis of double descent to create a unified scaling law that explains phenomena like parameter-wise double descent as an effect of early noise acquisition by models that train effectively faster.
related_work: Existing neural network scaling laws characterize how test error varies with network size and data volume, often as a power law, but they are applicable only in limited regimes and do not incorporate training time or phenomena like double descent. Conversely, theories of double descent explain non-monotonic error curves with respect to model size or data volume, but often fail to explain its behavior with respect to training time or why it is not always observed empirically. This work seeks to unify these two lines of research into a single framework that accounts for model size, training time, and data volume.
abstract: Existing neural network scaling laws, which characterize how test error varies with network size and data volume, are often applicable only in limited regimes and do not incorporate phenomena such as double descent. This work presents a novel theoretical characterization of how model size, training time, and data volume interact. We propose a theoretical and empirical equivalence between scaling the size of a neural network and increasing its training time proportionally ('scale-time equivalence'). This concept challenges the practice of training large models for short durations and suggests smaller models trained longer could be as effective. We combine scale-time equivalence with a linear model analysis of double descent to obtain a unified theoretical scaling law.

experiments.Description: To empirically validate the proposed 'scale-time equivalence' by examining the trade-off between model scale and the training time required for a neural network to generalize.
