However, it remains uncertain how these findings translate to video games, which often have substantial visual differences from real-world images. This study aims to bridge this gap by systematically comparing a broad spectrum of modern pre-trained and end-to-end trained visual encoders for imitation learning in complex video games.
abstract: Video games have served as useful benchmarks for the decision making community, but training agents in modern games has been prohibitively expensive. Recent progress in large vision models has the potential to amortize some of these costs. However, it is currently unclear which of these models have learnt representations that retain information critical for sequential decision making. Towards enabling wider participation in the research of gameplaying agents in modern games, we present a systematic study of imitation learning with publicly available visual encoders compared to the typical, task-specific, end-to-end training approach in Minecraft, Minecraft Dungeons and Counter-Strike: Global Offensive.

experiments.Description: To systematically compare the effectiveness of various visual encoders for imitation learning in a modern video game. This involves comparing a wide range of end-to-end trained encoders against a diverse set of frozen, pre-trained encoders.
experiments.Method:
Behavior cloning (BC) agents are trained on the 'Arch Haven' level in Minecraft Dungeons using a dataset of human gameplay. All agents use an identical policy network (a two-layered LSTM) but differ in their visual encoder. 
End-to-end encoders tested include: Impala ResNet, Custom ResNets (for 128x128 and 256x256 images), ViT Tiny, and Custom ViTs (for 128x128 and 256x256 images), trained with and without image augmentation.
Pre-trained encoders tested include: CLIP (ResNet50, ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14), DINOv2 (ViT-S/14, ViT-B/14, ViT-L/14), FocalNet (Large, XLarge, Huge), and the Stable Diffusion 2.1 VAE encoder.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The primary metric is the progression percentage through the level, computed over 20 online rollouts per trained agent. Gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) will also be used to visually inspect what information the encoders capture.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the same set of visual encoders on a different game and task (Minecraft 'Treechop') to assess the generality of the findings.
experiments.Method: BC agents are trained for the 'Treechop' task in the MineRL environment using a filtered 40-minute subset of the OpenAI VPT dataset. The same set of 12 end-to-end and 10 pre-trained visual encoders are used with the same policy architecture as in the first experiment.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The primary metric is the success rate of chopping at least one tree within one minute, evaluated over 100 episodes. Grad-CAM will be used for visual inspection.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that pre-trained encoders are more advantageous in low-data regimes by comparing the performance of the best-performing end-to-end and pre-trained encoders on reduced training datasets.
experiments.Method: The best-performing end-to-end (ResNet and ViT on 128x128 images with augmentation) and pre-trained (DINOv2 ViT-S/14, ViT-B/14) encoders are selected. They are then re-trained on smaller subsets of the original datasets. For Minecraft Dungeons, datasets of 50% (~4 hours) and 25% (~2 hours) are used. For Minecraft, a dataset of 10% (~3.5 minutes) is used.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Progression percentage in Minecraft Dungeons and success rate in Minecraft.

experiments.Description: To investigate how the best-performing visual encoders compare in a video game (CS:GO) with more realistic visuals, which are more akin to the training data of most pre-trained encoders.
experiments.Method: BC agents are trained on the 'Clean aim train' task in CS:GO using a 45-minute expert dataset. The best-performing end-to-end encoders (ResNet 128 +Aug, ViT 128 +Aug) and a pre-trained encoder (DINOv2 ViT-S/14) are evaluated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The average kills-per-minute (KPM) over three rollouts of five minutes each.

risk_factors: In order to maintain focus and feasibility in our study, we concentrated on a specific set of visual encoders, enabling a range of comparisons between different architectures and pre-trained model paradigms.

Our study could be complemented by exploring additional comparison points, such as diverse supervised-trained pre-trained encoder architectures and additional scenarios within the examined or other video games.

Although our study focused on settings with available training data for the evaluation task, future work could explore the potential benefits of pre-trained visual encoders when agents need to generalise across diverse levels or maps with variable visuals.
We leave further investigation into the efficacy of pre-trained visual encoders in CS:GO and the observed failure of DINOv2 for future work.

title: UrbanPlanBench: A Comprehensive Assessment of Urban Planning Abilities in Large Language Models
name: urbanplanbench-eval
short_hypothesis: A comprehensive benchmark, UrbanPlanBench, based on real-world urban planner qualification exams, can quantitatively assess the proficiency of LLMs in urban planning. Furthermore, a large-scale supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset, UrbanPlanText, created from textbooks and past exams, can be used to enhance the domain-specific knowledge and reasoning abilities of these models.
related_work: While general-purpose benchmarks like SuperGLUE, BIG-BENCH, and C-Eval exist, along with domain-specific ones like LawBench and BizBench, there is a notable absence of dedicated benchmarks for urban planning. This gap restricts the ability to quantify the extent to which LLMs acquire specialized knowledge in this field. Similarly, there are no off-the-shelf resources for fine-tuning LLMs specifically for urban planning, creating a need for both a tailored evaluation suite and a domain-specific fine-tuning dataset.
abstract: Urban planning is a professional discipline that shapes our daily surroundings, which demands multifaceted domain knowledge and relies heavily on human expertise. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) holds promise for revolutionizing such a field by leveraging their pre-trained world knowledge. However, the extent to which these models can assist human practitioners remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we introduce a comprehensive benchmark, UrbanPlanBench, tailored to evaluate the efficacy of LLMs in urban planning, which encompasses fundamental principles, professional knowledge, and management and regulations, aligning closely with the qualifications expected of human planners. Besides the benchmark, we present the largest-ever supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset, UrbanPlanText, for LLMs in urban planning, comprising over 30,000 instruction pairs sourced from urban planning exams and textbooks. The proposed benchmark, dataset, and associated evaluation and fine-tuning toolsets aim to catalyze the integration of LLMs into practical urban computing.

experiments.Description: To assess the acquisition of urban planning skills by a diverse array of advanced LLMs using a new comprehensive benchmark, and to compare their performance against a human professional standard.
experiments.Method: A diverse set of open-source and commercial LLMs (including LLaMA series, Gemma series, ChatGLM series, Baichuan2, Qwen series, Yi, and ChatGPT 3.5/4o) will be evaluated on the UrbanPlanBench. The benchmark consists of multiple-choice questions (both single-correct and multiple-correct) across three subjects: S1 (Fundamental principles), S2 (Professional knowledge), and S3 (Management and regulations). Performance will be compared against random guessing and the certification bar for human planners.

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of model size on performance on the UrbanPlanBench benchmark, examining potential scaling laws.
experiments.Method: Qwen1.5 models of varying parameter scales (ranging from 0.8B to 32B) will be evaluated on the UrbanPlanBench. Their performance on both single-correct (MCQ-S) and multiple-correct (MCQ-M) questions will be analyzed in relation to model size.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of prompting techniques, specifically Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT), for enhancing LLM competence in urban planning tasks.
experiments.Method: Using GPT-4o-mini as the base model, its performance on UrbanPlanBench will be evaluated with and without prompting techniques. RAG experiments will use a knowledge base of urban planning textbooks and past exams. CoT experiments will include both few-shot and zero-shot variants.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with a domain-specific dataset on the urban planning capabilities of LLMs.
experiments.Method: Several LLMs (LLaMA3, ChatGLM3, Baichuan2, Qwen1.5, Yi) will be fine-tuned on the newly created UrbanPlanText dataset using the LoRA method. The performance of these fine-tuned models will then be evaluated on the UrbanPlanBench and compared against their pre-SFT performance.

risk_factors: Moving forward, our future work includes expanding both UrbanPlanBench and UrbanPlanText to incorporate multi-linguistic urban planning materials, thereby enabling broader use cases of the benchmark and dataset.

Additionally, we aim to extend UrbanPlanBench into a multi-modal benchmark, integrating both imagery of urban plans and their corresponding descriptive text, further enriching the evaluation capabilities of LLMs in urban planning contexts.

title: PARL: A Unified Framework for Policy Alignment in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
name: parl-bilevel-rl-alignment
short_hypothesis: Formulating policy alignment in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) as a bilevel optimization problem will address the distribution shift issues in current RLHF formulations. This is achieved by explicitly parameterizing the distribution of the upper-level alignment objective (reward design) by the lower-level optimal variable (the optimal policy for that reward), leading to a more reliable evaluation objective and improved policy alignment.
related_work: Current approaches for policy alignment in RL, such as PEBBLE and SURF, use heuristic iterative procedures. However, they do not focus on the entanglement between the alignment objective and the trajectory-collecting policy. This gap leads to a misaligned objective for evaluation and sub-optimal performance, as the data distribution for the alignment objective depends on the agent's policy, which is often ignored. This work proposes a bilevel optimization formulation to explicitly model this dependency, which is a new class of stochastic bilevel problem where the upper-level stochasticity depends on the lower-level variable.
abstract: We present a novel unified bilevel optimization-based framework, PARL, formulated to address the critical issue of policy alignment in reinforcement learning using utility or preference-based feedback. We identify a major gap within current algorithmic designs for solving policy alignment due to a lack of precise characterization of the dependence of the alignment objective on the data generated by policy trajectories. Our framework addresses these concerns by explicitly parameterizing the distribution of the upper alignment objective (reward design) by the lower optimal variable (optimal policy for the designed reward). From an optimization perspective, our formulation leads to a new class of stochastic bilevel problems where the stochasticity at the upper objective depends upon the lower-level variable. This work presents the first formulation of the RLHF as a bilevel optimization problem which generalizes existing RLHF formulations and addresses distribution shift issues. To demonstrate the efficacy of our formulation, we devise an algorithm named A-PARL to solve the PARL problem.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed A-PARL algorithm against state-of-the-art baselines on large-scale continuous control locomotion tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Walker task from the DM Control suite. The proposed A-PARL algorithm will be compared against PEBBLE, PEBBLE+SURF, and an Oracle (using the true reward). Human feedback will be simulated based on the ground-truth reward function.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed A-PARL algorithm against state-of-the-art baselines on large-scale continuous control manipulation tasks from Meta-World.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the DoorOpen and ButtonPress tasks from the Meta-World benchmark. The proposed A-PARL algorithm will be compared against PEBBLE, PEBBLE+SURF, and an Oracle (using the true reward). Human feedback will be simulated based on the ground-truth reward function.

experiments.Description: To qualitatively test the alignment behavior of the learned policy by validating the generated trajectories against potential reward hacking or spurious learning.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Door Open task. The behavior of the agent trained with the proposed A-PARL algorithm will be visually compared to the behavior of an agent trained with the baseline PEBBLE algorithm at an early stage of learning.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of the learned behavior to validate if the agent learns the desired task (e.g., opening the door) correctly.']

risk_factors: For this work, we sidestep this challenge by assuming access to the oracles to (14)-(17). This helps us to focus more on the policy and reward entanglement in our bilevel formulation.
We can extend the analysis to stochastic settings utilizing the standard techniques in stochastic optimization literature.

It is not extremely fair to compare PEBBLE+SURF with ours due to this obvious benefit [of using additional unsupervised data and augmentations], but still, we observe that our algorithm performs better than PEBBLE+SURF with controlled augmentations.
A rigorous and thorough evaluation of multiple other environments remains a scope for future study.

title: Proto-Value Networks: Scaling Representation Learning with Auxiliary Tasks
name: Proto-Value Networks
short_hypothesis: A new family of auxiliary tasks, based on the successor measure and implemented with random network indicators, can be used to learn rich state representations from offline data. The quality of these representations is expected to improve when the number of tasks and the size of the agent's network are simultaneously increased.
related_work: Prior work has used auxiliary tasks to improve representations in deep RL, but typically in support of a main reward-driven objective rather than for representation learning itself. The scaling properties of representation learning from auxiliary tasks—i.e., the effect of using more tasks or increasing network capacity—remain poorly understood. Other methods for representation learning in RL include contrastive learning and behavioral similarity metrics, but these are often complex or heavily tied to the environment's reward structure. This work aims to decouple representation learning from the main task reward and systematically study the scaling properties of auxiliary tasks.
abstract: Auxiliary tasks improve the representations learned by deep reinforcement learning agents. Analytically, their effect is reasonably well-understood; in practice, however, their primary use remains in support of a main learning objective, rather than as a method for learning representations. This is perhaps surprising given that many auxiliary tasks are defined procedurally, and hence can be treated as an essentially infinite source of information about the environment. Based on this observation, we study the effectiveness of auxiliary tasks for learning rich representations, focusing on the setting where the number of tasks and the size of the agent’s network are simultaneously increased. For this purpose, we derive a new family of auxiliary tasks based on the successor measure. These tasks are easy to implement and have appealing theoretical properties. Combined with a suitable off-policy learning rule, the result is a representation learning algorithm that can be understood as extending Mahadevan & Maggioni (2007)’s proto-value functions to deep reinforcement learning – accordingly, we call the resulting object proto-value networks.

experiments.Description: To study the effect of network representational capacity on the number of auxiliary tasks that can be learned effectively.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Impala-CNN network architecture and varies its width by a multiplier in {1, 2, 4, 8}. For each width, the number of auxiliary tasks is swept from {0, 10, ..., 100}. The representation encoder is pre-trained on offline data from 5 Atari games (ASTERIX, BEAM RIDER, PONG, QBERT, and SPACE INVADERS) using the proposed Proto-Value Network (PVN) method. After pre-training, the encoder is frozen and a linear head is trained online to perform the main RL task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of the learned representation from Proto-Value Networks on a broad suite of tasks and compare it against other representation learning baselines.
experiments.Method: An 8x width Impala-CNN is pre-trained with 100 auxiliary tasks on offline data from all 46 games in the RL Unplugged benchmark. The learned encoder is then frozen, and a linear head is trained online. This approach is compared against several pre-training baselines: Random Initialization, Random Cumulants (RCs), Self-Predictive Representations (SPR), and Behavior Cloning (BC).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Median Human Normalized Score', 'IQM Human Normalized Score', 'Mean Human Normalized Score', 'Optimality Gap']

experiments.Description: To ablate the components of the Proto-Value Network method, specifically the choice of indicator function and the use of a random target policy.
experiments.Method: Two ablations are planned. First, the proposed Random Network Indicators (RNIs) will be replaced with universal hash functions to create the auxiliary tasks. Second, the TD-target calculation, which averages over next-state actions (for a random policy), will be replaced with a maximization over next-state actions (for an optimal policy). These variants will be compared to the full PVN method.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Median Human Normalized Score', 'IQM Human Normalized Score', 'Mean Human Normalized Score', 'Optimality Gap']

risk_factors: It is surprising that with even a relatively large network with tens of millions of parameters, such as Impala (8×), the network only supports a handful of tasks.
It is not clear why training with more tasks leads to worse performance, especially for smaller Impala architectures.
One drawback of using universal hash functions to define sets of interest is that they may assign different values to perceptually near-identical states (a single pixel difference suffices).

title: Align and Filter: Improving Performance in Asynchronous On-Policy RL
name: vaco-for-policy-lag
short_hypothesis: A novel algorithm, Total Variation-based Advantage-aligned Constrained policy Optimization (VACO), can mitigate policy lag in asynchronous on-policy RL. This is achieved by using two main ideas: 1) 'advantage realignment' to estimate the advantage function of the learning policy from off-policy data, addressing the initial 'backward policy lag', and 2) 'TV divergence-based filtering' to control the divergence of the learning policy from the data distribution, addressing the accumulating 'forward policy lag'.
related_work: Distributed reinforcement learning accelerates training by collecting data and updating policies simultaneously. However, this introduces 'policy lag'—a mismatch between the data-generating (behavior) policy and the policy being updated (learning policy)—which violates a key assumption of on-policy algorithms like PPO. On-policy methods make conservative updates to stay close to the data distribution, but this can hinder performance, especially with frequent updates. This paper categorizes policy lag into 'backward lag' (from initial policy mismatch) and 'forward lag' (from subsequent gradient updates) and proposes a method to address both sources, which current methods do not explicitly distinguish and handle.
abstract: Distributed training and increasing the gradient update frequency are practical strategies to accelerate learning and improve performance, but both exacerbate a central challenge: policy lag, which is the mismatch between the behavior policy generating data and the learning policy being updated. Policy lag can hinder the scaling of on-policy learning algorithms to larger problems. In this paper, we identify the sources of policy lag caused by distributed learning and high update frequency. We use the findings to propose total Variation-based Advantage aligned Constrained policy Optimization (VACO) as a practical approach to mitigate policy lag. We will empirically validate our method in classic robotics RL tasks and a modern RL for LLM math reasoning task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method's robustness to backward policy lag in a controlled, simulated asynchronous environment.
experiments.Method: VACO will be implemented on top of PPO in the CleanRL codebase. The experiment will use a simulated asynchronous setup with a policy buffer to create a mixture of policies, representing varying degrees of asynchronicity. This will be tested on MuJoCo robotics tasks. Baselines for comparison will include PPO-Clip (vanilla PPO), PPO with a KL penalty, Simple Policy Optimization (SPO), and IMPALA.

experiments.Description: To investigate the proposed method's robustness to forward policy lag when finetuning Large Language Models (LLMs) with reinforcement learning for math reasoning.
experiments.Method: VACO will be applied to the GRPO algorithm for finetuning a Qwen 2.5 0.5B model on the GSM8k (Grade School Math) dataset using a verifiable reward (RLVR) setup. Forward policy lag will be induced by training for N steps on N minibatches of data generated from the same initial policy. The baseline will be the standard GRPO algorithm using PPO-clipping.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Evaluation performance on GSM8k', 'Frequency of filtering/clipping events']

risk_factors: While various asynchronous RL setups have been proposed in the past, obtaining reproducible results is a challenging task due to the underlying non-determinism on the hardware level (Huang et al., 2023).

title: Grad-TopoCAM: EEG Brain Region Visual Interpretability via Gradient-Based Topographic Class Activation Map
name: grad-topocam
short_hypothesis: A universal interpretability and visualization method, Grad-TopoCAM (Gradient-Based Topographic Class Activation Map), can directly map EEG features from any target layer of a deep learning model to brain regions for EEG decoding. This approach is proposed to be adaptable, not require model architecture modifications, and enhance universality and flexibility compared to existing indirect or model-specific methods.
related_work: Current interpretability techniques for EEG decoding often rely on indirect approaches, such as transforming EEG signals into 2D images and then applying computer vision methods like Grad-CAM. These methods can struggle to reveal the specific brain regions influencing model decisions. Other studies have designed model-specific algorithms for visualization, but these approaches generally lack generalizability and are not easily adaptable across different network architectures or layers. Consequently, there is a lack of a universal interpretability method that can directly map model-decision features to corresponding brain region activity.
abstract: The visualization and interpretability of electroencephalogram (EEG) decoding significantly contribute to brain-computer interfaces (BCI) and cognitive neuroscience. Although some existing research has attempted to map EEG features to specific brain regions, these approaches fail to fully utilize raw signals and lack extensibility to other Deep Learning (DL) models. In this work, Grad-TopoCAM (Gradient-Based Topographic Class Activation Map) is proposed, which enhances interpretability in DL models for EEG decoding adaptively. Grad-TopoCAM calculates the gradient of feature maps for the target class at the target layer. The weights of the feature maps are obtained through global average pooling of the gradients. The class activation map is generated by performing a linear combination of weights and feature maps, which is subsequently mapped to different brain regions.

experiments.Description: To validate the Grad-TopoCAM method by generating visualizations of salient brain regions from various deep learning models trained on different EEG decoding tasks.
experiments.Method: Grad-TopoCAM will be applied to eight different DL models (ShallowConvNet, DeepConvNet, EEGNet, RACNN, EEG-ChannelNet, Conformer, LMDA-Net, D-FaST) trained on four public datasets: BCI Competition IV Dataset 2a (motor imagery), Nieto et al. 2022 (inner speech), Dataset III (silent reading of Chinese words), and Dataset IV (silent reading of English words). The visualizations will be generated from the model with the highest accuracy for each subject.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification Accuracy', 'Qualitative analysis of generated brain topography maps']

experiments.Description: To conduct a layer-wise analysis of a deep learning model to observe the dynamic changes in identified brain regions across different network layers.
experiments.Method: The proposed Grad-TopoCAM method will be applied to different convolutional layers of the EEGNet model trained on the motor imagery task (Dataset I). This will be done to visualize how the focus on brain regions evolves as the network deepens.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative analysis of layer-wise brain topography maps']

experiments.Description: To assess if Grad-TopoCAM can be used to facilitate channel selection, and to evaluate the effect of this selection on model efficiency and classification performance.
experiments.Method: Grad-TopoCAM visualizations will be used to determine the contribution of different brain regions (channels) to the classification task. Channels will be ranked by significance, and a subset consisting of the top half will be selected. The performance and efficiency of various models (e.g., EEGNet, LMDA-Net, ShallowConvNet) will be compared when using the full set of channels versus the selected subset.

risk_factors: The current implementation primarily focuses on enhancing interpretability within supervised learning frameworks. As such, its effectiveness in unsupervised or semi-supervised contexts remains unexplored.

title: Sqrt(d) Dimension Dependence of Langevin Monte Carlo
name: lmc-sqrt-d-dependence
short_hypothesis: The mixing time of unadjusted Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) for sampling from log-smooth and log-strongly-convex distributions has a dimension dependence of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d})$, an improvement over the existing $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ bounds. This can be achieved under an additional growth condition on the third-order derivative of the potential, without requiring momentum as in kinetic Langevin methods.
related_work: The best known mixing time bound for unadjusted Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) in 2-Wasserstein distance is $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/\epsilon)$. In contrast, methods like Metropolis-Adjusted LMC (MALA) and discretized kinetic Langevin dynamics have been shown to achieve a better $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d})$ dependence. It is an open question whether unadjusted LMC can also achieve this improved dimension dependence, as it was previously thought that the momentum term in kinetic methods was necessary for this improvement.
abstract: This article considers the popular MCMC method of unadjusted Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) and provides a non-asymptotic analysis of its sampling error in 2-Wasserstein distance. The motivation is to improve upon the best previously known $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/\epsilon)$ mixing time bound. The proposed approach is based on a refinement of mean-square analysis, which is used to automate the analysis of a large class of sampling algorithms based on discretizations of contractive SDEs. Using this framework, we aim to establish an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d}/\epsilon)$ mixing time bound for LMC under common log-smooth and log-strongly-convex conditions, plus a growth condition on the 3rd-order derivative of the potential.

experiments.Description: To numerically study the dependence of LMC's sampling error on the problem dimension d.
experiments.Method: Run LMC with a fixed step size (h=0.1) for 100 iterations on two target potential functions, $f_1(x) = \frac{1}{2}||x||^2 + \log(\sum_{i=1}^{d}e^{x_i})$ and $f_2(x) = \frac{1}{2}||x||^2 - \frac{1}{2d^{1/2}}\sum_{i=1}^{d}\cos(d^{1/4}x_i)$. The experiment will be run for a range of dimensions $d \in \{1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000\}$. The ground truth mean is to be approximated by running LMC with a very small step size for a long time.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Euclidean norm of the mean error, $||\\mathbb{E}\\overline{x}_{k}-\\mathbb{E}_{\\mu}x||$, as a surrogate for the 2-Wasserstein distance.']

experiments.Description: To numerically study the dependence of LMC's sampling error on the algorithm's step size h.
experiments.Method: Run LMC with a fixed dimension (d=10) on the same two target potential functions, $f_1(x)$ and $f_2(x)$. The experiment will be run for a range of step sizes $h \in \{0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0\}$. The algorithm will run until a fixed time T=20.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Euclidean norm of the mean error, $||\\mathbb{E}\\overline{x}_{k}-\\mathbb{E}_{\\mu}x||$, as a surrogate for the 2-Wasserstein distance.']

risk_factors: Note that the above optimality results only partially, but not completely, close the gap between the upper and lower bounds of LMC over the entire family of log-smooth and log-strongly-convex target measures, because of one limitation of our result — A(ssumption)2 is, despite of its close relation to the Hessian Lipschitz condition frequently used in the literature, still an extra condition.

We tend to believe that A2 may not be essential, but rather an artifact of our proof technique. However, at this moment we cannot eliminate the possibility that the best scaling one can get out of Assumption 1 only (no A2) is worse than $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d}/\epsilon)$
We also note KLMC has better condition number dependence than our current LMC result, although the κ dependence in our bound may not be tight.

title: Confidence Elicitation: A New Attack Vector for Large Language Models
name: confidence-elicitation-attack
short_hypothesis: In a black-box setting where only the final output of a Large Language Model is accessible, it is possible to elicit the model's confidence in its own predictions. This elicited confidence can then be used as a feedback signal, similar to soft-labels in grey-box attacks, to guide an adversarial attack. Specifically, by iteratively perturbing the input to minimize the model's elicited confidence in the correct label, the likelihood of achieving a misclassification can be increased.
related_work: Adversarial attacks are categorized as white-box (full access), grey-box (e.g., access to logits/probabilities), and black-box (access to final prediction only). With the rise of closed-source, API-based LLMs, the grey-box or 'soft-label' scenario has become unrealistic. Current black-box 'hard-label' attacks are limited by the lack of rich feedback for optimizing perturbations, often requiring a large number of model queries. This research addresses the gap left by the absence of probability-based guidance in modern black-box threat models for LLMs.
abstract: A fundamental issue in deep learning has been adversarial robustness. Currently, large language models (LLMs) suffer from adversarial attacks, but threat models have changed. With the introduction of closed-source models, no information is available apart from the generated output, meaning current black-box attacks can only utilize the final prediction. In this work, we investigate the potential of attack guidance, akin to using output probabilities, while having only black-box access in a classification setting. This is proposed to be achieved through the ability to elicit confidence from the model. The hypothesis is that the elicited confidence is calibrated and that by minimizing it, one can increase the likelihood of misclassification.

experiments.Description: To validate the core assumption that elicited confidence from LLMs is calibrated and not hallucinated, which is a prerequisite for using it as a feedback signal for attacks.
experiments.Method: The calibration of verbal confidence elicitation will be assessed for LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct and Mistral-7B-Instruct-V0.3 models on zero-shot classification tasks across the SST-2, AG-News, and StrategyQA datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Expected Calibration Error (ECE)', 'Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC)', 'Reliability diagrams']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Confidence Elicitation Attack (CEAttack) and compare its performance against existing hard-label black-box attack methods on word-substitution tasks.
experiments.Method: The attack will be performed on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 models for classification tasks on the SST-2, AG-News, and StrategyQA datasets. The proposed CEAttack will be compared against baseline methods: 'Self-Fool Word Sub', TextHoaxer, and SSPAttack.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Clean accuracy (CA)', 'Accuracy under attack (AUA)', 'Attack success rate (ASR)', 'Semantic similarity (SemSim)', 'Perplexity', 'All Att Queries Avg', 'Succ Att Queries Avg', 'Total Attack Time']

experiments.Description: To test the applicability and performance of confidence elicitation attacks on a large-scale, closed-source API model.
experiments.Method: The CEAttack and an enhanced variant (CEAttack++) will be tested against the GPT-4o API model on the SST-2, AG-News, and StrategyQA datasets. The performance will be compared to the SSPAttack baseline.

risk_factors: Ultimately, we concluded that confidence elicitation may be at odds with robustness.
Our results suggest the potential for confidence elicitation to enhance jailbreaks.

title: RefactorBench: Evaluating Stateful Reasoning in Language Agents Through Code
name: refactorbench-for-stateful-agents
short_hypothesis: Current language model agents, framed as Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs), struggle with long-horizon, multi-file code refactoring tasks because their understanding of the environment's state weakens over many steps. Introducing a "state-aware" interface that explicitly provides agents with a summary of their past actions and the resulting state changes will improve their reasoning capabilities and task success rate.
related_work: Prior work on evaluating software engineering agents has relied on benchmarks like SWE-bench, which focuses on resolving GitHub issues, or function-level code generation benchmarks. These benchmarks do not specifically isolate the challenges of multi-file code refactoring, which requires stateful reasoning across a sequence of edits. While some research has augmented agents with external memory to handle long-term dependencies, this work focuses on enabling concurrent reasoning and state-aware behaviors by providing agents with explicit state representations, a concept explored in embodied control but less so for real-world language agents interacting with code.
abstract: To better understand the unique limitations of language model (LM) agents in autonomous, feedback-driven systems, we introduce RefactorBench, a benchmark consisting of 100 large handcrafted multi-file refactoring tasks in popular open-source repositories. Solving tasks within RefactorBench requires thorough exploration of dependencies across multiple files and strong adherence to relevant instructions. Every task is defined by 3 natural language instructions of varying specificity. We propose adapting a baseline agent to condition on representations of state to improve performance on these tasks. We further propose extending this state-aware approach to encompass entire digital environments. RefactorBench aims to support the study of LM agents by providing a set of real-world, multi-hop tasks within the realm of code.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of baseline language model agents on the newly proposed multi-file code refactoring benchmark and analyze their failure modes.
experiments.Method: A baseline of the SWE-agent with gpt-4 and claude-3.5-sonnet will be run on all tasks in the RefactorBench benchmark. RefactorBench consists of 100 handcrafted multi-file refactoring tasks in 9 popular Python repositories. Each task is defined by three instruction sets with varying levels of detail: 'lazy', 'base', and 'descriptive'. A proficient human developer with a 5-minute time limit per task will also be evaluated as a baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task resolution rate (percentage of completely successful task instances)', 'Success is determined by passing all associated Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) based unit tests for a given task.', 'Manual review of trajectories to identify failure modes.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that a language model's ability to track state degrades as the number of actions taken increases.
experiments.Method: A synthetic experiment will be conducted where a language model (GPT-4 Turbo) is given an initial state consisting of 15 categories of preferences. The model will then be prompted with an increasing number of updates (actions) to these preferences and asked to output the final, updated list of preferences after each sequence of actions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy of the final constructed state (the list of preferences) at each step, measured over 125 randomly initialized runs.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether augmenting a language model agent with a state-aware interface improves its performance on RefactorBench.
experiments.Method: The SWE-agent, using gpt-4o, will be modified to include a 'state-aware interface'. This interface provides the agent with a cached and updated summary of all previous edit actions before every function call. This state-aware agent will be evaluated on the RefactorBench tasks across all three instruction sets (lazy, base, descriptive) and its performance will be compared to the baseline SWE-agent.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task resolution rate', 'Subtask completion rate (based on passing individual AST tests)']

risk_factors: RefactorBench's task instances are all in Python, and we hope to expand the benchmark to various languages that are statically and dynamically typed, allowing for evaluations on more styles of refactors.
We also focus on highly used open-source Python repositories, and language models may have a better understanding of the repositories due to their prevalence in training data.

RefactorBench also has a limited amount of task instances due to the intensive process to create a singular end-to-end task and the necessity for quick evaluations (evaluations still takes hours with RefactorBench).
We also find, similar to previous works, that agent runs are not deterministic and can solve differing tasks in different runs.

RefactorBench is a step forward in evaluating LM agents in robust manners through complex task construction, but like all benchmarks, is still plagued by the possible issues of over-fit data distributions (i.e. only refactors).

title: Learning for Highly Faithful Explainability
name: faithfulness-guided-amortized-explainer
short_hypothesis: A faithfulness-guided amortized explainer, named DeepFaith, can be trained to produce more faithful explanations by jointly optimizing a novel, model-agnostic self-supervised faithfulness objective and a pattern consistency loss against high-quality supervisory signals. These signals are to be generated by aggregating, deduplicating, and filtering explanations from multiple prior methods based on faithfulness metrics. A dynamic weighting strategy will be used to balance the two objectives during training.
related_work: The Learning to Explain paradigm aims to train efficient neural network explainers. Existing methods fall into two categories. Self-supervised optimization-based methods train an explainer by having it interact with the target model, but their objectives often rely on restrictive assumptions about the model or task. Prior-explanation-driven methods train an explainer to imitate explanations generated by existing XAI algorithms, but they struggle to guarantee the quality of these supervisory signals and their performance is inherently bounded by the quality of the prior methods. Relying exclusively on either approach can lead to poor convergence or limited explanation quality.
abstract: Learning to Explain is a forward-looking paradigm recently proposed in the field of explainable AI, which envisions training explainers capable of producing high-quality explanations for target models efficiently. However, this paradigm faces three critical challenges: 1) self-supervised objectives rely on assumptions about the target model or task, restricting their generalizability; 2) methods driven by prior explanations struggle to guarantee the quality of the supervisory signals; and 3) depending exclusively on either approach leads to poor convergence or limited explanation quality. To address these challenges, we propose a faithfulness-guided amortized explainer that 1) theoretically derives a self-supervised objective free from assumptions about the target model or task, 2) practically generates high-quality supervisory signals by deduplicating and filtering prior explanations, and 3) jointly optimizes both objectives via a dynamic weighting strategy. The goal is to enable the amortized explainer to produce more faithful explanations for complex, high-dimensional models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the faithfulness of the proposed DeepFaith explainer against a comprehensive set of baseline post-hoc explanation methods across various data modalities and target model architectures.
experiments.Method: The DeepFaith explainer will be evaluated on 12 explanation tasks. Image tasks use ImageNet and UCSD OCT Retina datasets with ResNet50, EfficientNetb0, and DeiT models. Text tasks use IMDb and AGNews datasets with LSTM and Transformer models. Tabular tasks use NHANES Age Prediction (NAP) and Wholesale Customers Data (WCD) with MLP models. Baselines for image tasks include Occlusion, LIME, Kernel SHAP, Saliency, Input × Gradient, Guided Backprop, Grad-CAM, Score-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Integrated Grads, Expected Grads, DeepLIFT, DeepLIFT SHAP, and LRP. Baselines for text and tabular tasks include Integrated Grads, Gradient SHAP, DeepLIFT, Saliency, Occlusion, Feature Ablation, LIME, and Kernel SHAP.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Faithfulness Correlation (FC)', 'Faithfulness Estimate (FE)', 'Monotonicity Correlation (MC)', 'Infidelity (INF)', 'Deletion Score (DEL)', 'Insertion Score (INS)', 'Negative Perturbation (NEG)', 'Positive Perturbation (POS)', 'Region Perturbation (RP)', 'Iterative Removal of Features (IROF)']

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of DeepFaith against other Learning to Explain methods that are also applicable across multiple modalities.
experiments.Method: The comparison will be conducted on four explanation tasks: NAP+MLP, AGNews+LSTM, OCT+ResNet, and ImageNet+DeiT. The baseline methods for comparison are VerT, L2X, CXPlain, and FastSHAP.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Faithfulness Correlation (FC)', 'Faithfulness Estimate (FE)', 'Monotonicity Correlation (MC)', 'Region Perturbation (RP)', 'Insertion Score (INS)', 'Deletion Score (DEL)', 'Negative Perturbation (NEG)', 'Positive Perturbation (POS)', 'Iterative Removal of Features (IROF)', 'Infidelity (INF)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to evaluate the individual contributions of the pattern consistency loss (LPC), the local consistency loss (LLC), and the deduplicating and filtering steps for generating supervision signals.
experiments.Method: The explainer will be trained on four representative explanation tasks under five different configurations: using the full loss function (LOBJ), using only LPC, using only LLC, using both losses with only deduplication, and using both losses with only filtering.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Faithfulness Correlation (FC)', 'Faithfulness Estimate (FE)', 'Monotonicity Correlation (MC)', 'Region Perturbation (RP)', 'Insertion Score (INS)', 'Deletion Score (DEL)', 'Negative Perturbation (NEG)', 'Positive Perturbation (POS)', 'Iterative Removal of Features (IROF)', 'Infidelity (INF)']

risk_factors: Jointly optimizing both objectives is not straightforward. This is because the local and global optima of the two objectives differ, leading to inconsistent gradient directions: optimizing one can easily degrade the other.

Studying the sensitivity of these hyperparameters is essential for understanding whether DeepFaith can generalize stably under different parameter settings, thereby reducing reliance on manual tuning.

title: Attend or Perish: Benchmarking Attention on Algorithmic Reasoning
name: Attention Inconsistency Causes Extrapolation Failure
short_hypothesis: The catastrophic failure of Transformers to extrapolate to sequence lengths beyond their training regime is not a fundamental limitation in their ability to learn general rules. Instead, this failure is caused by inconsistent attention patterns learned by individual attention heads, which fail to remain stable as sequence length increases, thereby disrupting the execution of the learned algorithm at novel lengths. We hypothesize that these failures can be diagnosed by inspecting attention patterns and repaired by minimal, targeted interventions on the Key (WK) and Query (WQ) projection matrices.
related_work: Prior work has developed benchmarks to evaluate algorithmic reasoning and length generalization (e.g., CRLS-Text, BIG-Bench, Flip-Flop Language Modeling) and proposed architectural changes or specialized positional embeddings (e.g., TRA, PRISM, Abacus) to improve it. However, these methods do not fully explain why standard Transformers fail to learn generalizable attention patterns from data alone. While some studies have explored data-centric solutions, few have directly diagnosed the root cause of extrapolation failures within the attention mechanism itself. This work proposes to move beyond black-box evaluations to investigate the model's internal computations, specifically hypothesizing that the self-attention mechanism is the source of the problem.
abstract: While Transformer models can learn algorithmic tasks and generalize reliably to unseen, in-distribution data, they often fail catastrophically when required to extrapolate to sequence lengths beyond their training regime. This paper investigates the root cause of this critical failure in length generalization. Using AttentionSpan—a benchmark of algorithmic tasks such as addition and multiplication, specifically designed to enable interpretability and facilitate inspection of internal model computations—we plan to analyze the model’s behavior on length extrapolation. We propose that this failure is attributable to inconsistent attention patterns, where information retrieval strategies learned by individual attention heads fail to remain stable as sequence length increases. This inconsistency is expected to disrupt the execution of the algorithm at novel lengths. We will test whether fine-tuning just a single column of the Key and Query projection matrices in all attention heads on longer sequences is sufficient to improve performance, suggesting that such failures could be addressed with minimal adjustments to attention weights.

experiments.Description: Establish a baseline for length generalization failure by training and evaluating Transformer models on a benchmark of algorithmic tasks, separating performance on in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) sequence lengths.
experiments.Method: Models (Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, gemma-3-1b-it) will be fine-tuned on the AttentionSpan benchmark, which includes tasks like String Reversal, Long Addition, Long Multiplication, Flip-Flop Language Modeling (FFLM), and Value Assignment. Training will use only ID data (e.g., sequences of length 1-10), and evaluation will be performed on both ID and OOD test sets (e.g., sequences of length 11-50).

experiments.Description: Diagnose the cause of generalization failure by analyzing whether incorrect OOD predictions are associated with inconsistent attention patterns, defined as deviations from an ideal 'reference attention pattern'.
experiments.Method: For tasks with clear algorithmic steps (e.g., String Reversal, Value Assignment), we will define a 'reference attention pattern' (a boolean mask of critical input tokens for each output token). Using attention rollout, we will aggregate attention scores and compute an 'Attn Score' metric, quantifying the proportion of attention on reference tokens. This score will be compared between correctly and incorrectly predicted tokens to identify systematic attention errors.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Attn Score', 'Mean Attention Entropy', 'Visual inspection of attention maps']

experiments.Description: Attempt to causally repair OOD performance by directly intervening on attention scores during inference to reinforce the ideal reference attention pattern.
experiments.Method: First, identify the attention heads most responsible for implementing the reference pattern based on their attention to reference tokens on ID data. During OOD inference, for these selected heads, a constant value (δ) will be added to the pre-softmax attention scores of the reference tokens. This intervention will be tested on tasks like String Reversal and Value Assignment.

experiments.Description: For complex tasks without a single ground-truth attention pattern, test whether a trainable intervention that learns to correct attention scores can restore OOD performance.
experiments.Method: We will freeze the base model and introduce a small, trainable component. Two architectures for this component will be explored: 1) a static trainable bias matrix added to the pre-softmax logits, and 2) an auxiliary MHA module whose output logits are added as a bias. This component will be trained exclusively on OOD samples.

experiments.Description: Determine the minimal sufficient intervention required to fix extrapolation failures by testing if fine-tuning only a single dimension of the key and query projection matrices is enough to restore OOD performance.
experiments.Method: All model parameters will be frozen except for a single, chosen dimension in the WQ and WK matrices of each attention head. The model will then be fine-tuned on OOD data. A control experiment will be run to confirm this minimal fine-tuning is a 'tweak' and not powerful enough to learn a task from scratch.

experiments.Description: Investigate the role of Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) in length extrapolation failures to determine if the issue lies with the positional encoding scheme itself or the learned attention parameters.
experiments.Method: A series of ablation experiments will be conducted on a model fine-tuned for the String Reversal task. The primary experiment will involve removing RoPE entirely during inference and observing the impact on performance.

risk_factors: While this intervention by a reference attention pattern is effective for simple tasks like String Reversal and Value Assignment, it does not extend to more complex algorithms such as multi-digit addition or multiplication.
Despite the success of these interventions on tasks with clear reference patterns, they do not generalize to more complex algorithms like multi-digit addition or multiplication.

[The static trainable bias approach] is effective for tasks with input-independent patterns, such as the diagonal pattern in String Reversal, but fails on tasks that require content-based lookups like Value Assignment which requires to find the correct output symbol by association with the specific input symbol.

title: Using reinforcement learning to solve vehicle routing problems with dynamic customers
name: rl-for-dynamic-vrp
short_hypothesis: A reinforcement learning framework, using a GNN encoder-decoder architecture with a recurrent component to track dynamic arrivals, can learn a fast and effective constructive heuristic for solving the partially dynamic travelling repairman problem (PDTRP) and its variants with time windows and customer demands.
related_work: While reinforcement learning with sophisticated deep learning models like GNNs and attention has been successful for static vehicle routing problems (VRPs), existing approaches for dynamic VRPs (DVRPs) often use simpler models or approximate dynamic programming. There is a gap in applying these modern deep RL architectures to learn construction heuristics for DVRPs, which are naturally suited for the step-by-step decision-making process of RL.
abstract: This paper proposes using reinforcement learning to learn effective and fast constructive heuristics for solving the partially dynamic travelling repairman problem (PDTRP) and its variants with customer demands and time windows. The proposed method uses a GNN encoder-decoder network that can handle variable problem sizes and introduces a recurrent neural network component to the decoder to track the arrival of dynamic customers. The goal is to investigate how well features from static VRP literature translate to this dynamic domain, how the model generalizes to problems of different sizes, and to compare its performance against baselines on various problem distributions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed RL-based construction heuristic against baselines on several variants of the partially dynamic vehicle routing problem (PDTRP).
experiments.Method: The proposed GNN encoder-decoder model will be trained using a policy gradient algorithm. It will be tested on three problem types: standard PDTRP, PDTRP with Time Windows (PDTRPTW), and Capacitated PDTRP with Time Windows (PDCTRPTW). Each problem type will be evaluated on three instance distributions: Uniform-uniform (U.U.), Skewed spatial-temporal (S.T.), and Real world (R.W.). Baselines for comparison will include simple heuristics (Nearest Neighbour, First Come First Served), a re-solving approach with the OR-Tools meta-heuristic solver, and a re-solving approach with the RL method from Joshi et al. (2020).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective cost', 'Percentage gap to a static offline baseline', 'Time taken to solve an instance']

experiments.Description: To investigate the generalization capabilities of the trained model across different problem sizes and data distributions.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on instances of a specific size range (e.g., 20-50 or 20-100 nodes) and then tested on instances of specific sizes (e.g., N=50, N=100) to assess size generalization. Additionally, models trained on one data distribution (e.g., Uniform-uniform) will be tested on other distributions (e.g., Skewed spatial-temporal and Real world) to assess cross-distribution performance.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to determine which architectural components, features, and training methods contribute most to performance in the dynamic problem setting.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be performed on the PDTRP and PDCTRPTW problems. This will involve comparing different training methods (e.g., base model vs. POMO), different edge feature representations (adjacency vs. distance), and the inclusion of specific features for handling dynamism, such as customer arrival times and a recurrent (LSTM) hidden state in the decoder context.

risk_factors: Due to the complexity of VRPs, it is not possible to employ an exact solver even on the offline instances. This can lead to rare situations where this baseline is outperformed.

title: Non-Clashing Teaching in Graphs: Algorithms, Complexity, and Bounds
name: nctd-closed-neighborhoods
short_hypothesis: By studying (positive) non-clashing teaching for the concept class of closed neighborhoods in graphs, it is possible to obtain improved algorithmic results, including Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT) algorithms for more general parameters than previously known, derive stronger computational lower bounds, and establish new combinatorial upper bounds for wider classes of graphs.
related_work: Prior work introduced non-clashing teaching and established it as an efficient, collusion-avoidant model. Subsequent research thoroughly studied positive non-clashing teaching for the concept class of 'balls in graphs', providing a nearly complete picture of its computational complexity. This revealed the problem is generally intractable (NP-hard and W[1]-hard), with tractability limited to restricted graph classes. The research gap lies in the analysis of the more general concept class of 'closed neighborhoods', for which a comprehensive complexity analysis, particularly regarding FPT algorithms for the general (non-positive) case and tighter complexity bounds, is missing.
abstract: This work considers (positive) non-clashing teaching for closed neighborhoods in graphs. This concept class is not only extensively studied in various related contexts, but it also exhibits broad generality, as any finite binary concept class can be equivalently represented by a set of closed neighborhoods in a graph. In comparison to the works on balls in graphs, the aim is to provide improved algorithmic results, notably including FPT algorithms for more general classes of parameters, and to complement these results by deriving stronger lower bounds. Lastly, the goal is to obtain combinatorial upper bounds for wider classes of graphs.

experiments.Description: To establish improved algorithmic upper and lower complexity bounds for non-clashing teaching on closed neighborhoods (N-NCTD and N-NCTD+).
experiments.Method: The investigation is theoretical. For N-NCTD, the plan is to prove a lower bound via a reduction from 3-SAT, aiming to show that no algorithm running in 2^o(f(k)·|V(G)|) time exists for any computable function f, unless the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) fails. For N-NCTD+, the plan is to provide a brute-force algorithm and prove a matching lower bound, also based on ETH, to establish tight complexity.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Computational complexity class (e.g., NP-hardness)', 'Algorithmic time complexity bounds (e.g., proving the existence or non-existence of algorithms with specific exponential running times under ETH)']

experiments.Description: To develop fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) algorithms for N-NCTD and N-NCTD+ parameterized by structural graph properties.
experiments.Method: The plan is to design and analyze two new algorithms. First, an FPT algorithm for N-NCTD+ parameterized by the treedepth of the graph, which will be proven correct via a reduction rule that prunes a treedepth decomposition. Second, an FPT algorithm for N-NCTD parameterized by the vertex cover number of the graph, which will be established via a kernelization algorithm that applies two reduction rules.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Fixed-parameter tractability (FPT) with respect to treedepth', 'Fixed-parameter tractability (FPT) with respect to vertex cover number']

experiments.Description: To derive novel combinatorial upper bounds on the non-clashing teaching dimension for closed neighborhoods within specific large graph classes.
experiments.Method: The investigation is theoretical. For planar graphs, the plan is to construct teaching maps to prove that NCTD+(B) ≤ 7 and NCTD(B) ≤ 5. For unit square graphs, the plan is to use geometric arguments about minimum enclosing rectangles to prove that NCTD+(B) ≤ 4.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Constant upper bound on the positive non-clashing teaching dimension (NCTD+)', 'Constant upper bound on the non-clashing teaching dimension (NCTD)']

risk_factors: Several open directions remain for future work, such as exploring the complexity with respect to treewidth or planarity for N-NCTD+ and N-NCTD, as well as vertex integrity and treedepth for N-NCTD.
However, like NON-CLASH (Ganian et al., 2025), we expect that they are W[1]-hard parameterized by the treewidth.

It is also important to determine whether Theorem 13 is tight, since the balls of a planar graph has been mentioned (Chalopin et al., 2024) as a potential concept class C negatively answering the question of (Kirkpatrick et al., 2019; Fallat et al., 2023), i.e., such that NCTD(C) > VCD(C).

title: Hyperspherical Latents Improve Continuous-Token Autoregressive Generation
name: hyperspherical-latents-for-ar-generation
short_hypothesis: By constraining all inputs and outputs of a continuous-token autoregressive model to lie on a fixed-radius hypersphere using a hyperspherical VAE (S-VAE), the model can be made scale-invariant. This will remove the primary cause of variance collapse—scale heterogeneity and drift—and thereby stabilize AR decoding, leading to improved image generation quality.
related_work: Continuous-token autoregressive (AR) models for image generation often trail other generative approaches like diffusion or masked-generation models. This performance gap is attributed to variance pathologies during AR decoding, where heterogeneous variances in VAE latents are amplified by exposure bias and classifier-free guidance, leading to instability and variance collapse. Prior solutions, such as inflating the KL term or fixing latent variance, help but do not address the root cause of scale heterogeneity. This work proposes a more principled solution by enforcing scale-invariance directly in the latent space, hypothesizing this will be more effective than prior methods or post-hoc normalization of Gaussian latents.
abstract: Autoregressive (AR) models are promising for image generation, yet continuous-token AR variants often trail latent diffusion and masked-generation models. The core issue is heterogeneous variance in VAE latents, which is amplified during AR decoding, especially under classifier-free guidance (CFG), and can cause variance collapse. We propose SphereAR to address this issue. Its core design is to constrain all AR inputs and outputs—including after CFG—to lie on a fixed-radius hypersphere (constant l2 norm), leveraging hyperspherical VAEs. Our theoretical analysis suggests that this hyperspherical constraint removes the scale component (the primary cause of variance collapse), thereby stabilizing AR decoding.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed SphereAR model for class-conditional image generation against state-of-the-art baselines across different model scales.
experiments.Method: SphereAR models of varying sizes (SphereAR-B, SphereAR-L, SphereAR-H) will be trained for class-conditional image generation on the ImageNet-1K dataset at 256x256 resolution. Performance will be compared against a range of baseline models, including discrete-token AR (VQGAN, LlamaGen, RandAR), continuous-token AR (GIVT, LatentLM), masked-generation (MAR, MaskGIT), next-scale (VAR), and diffusion models (LDM, DiT, SiT).

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to validate the design choice of using a hyperspherical VAE posterior over standard Gaussian-based VAE posteriors.
experiments.Method: Several VAE variants will be trained and paired with the same autoregressive transformer backbone. The variants to be compared are: (1) S-VAE with a Power Spherical posterior, (2) a standard diagonal-Gaussian VAE (β-VAE) with varying KL weights, (3) a fixed-variance VAE (σ-VAE), and (4) a diagonal-Gaussian VAE with post-hoc l2 normalization applied to its latents. All models will be evaluated on ImageNet generation.

experiments.Description: To isolate the specific contributions of the hyperspherical posterior and the application of normalization at different stages of the model pipeline (VAE decoder vs. AR model).
experiments.Method: A series of models will be trained and evaluated: (1) a baseline with a Gaussian posterior and no normalization, (2) a model with normalization applied only to the VAE decoder's input, (3) a model with normalization applied to both the VAE decoder input and the AR model's inputs/outputs, and (4) the full SphereAR model with a spherical posterior and full normalization.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SphereAR on higher-resolution image generation.
experiments.Method: The SphereAR-L model will be trained and evaluated for class-conditional image generation on the ImageNet dataset at 512x512 resolution. Its performance will be compared to prior models capable of generating at this resolution, such as DiT-XL/2 and MAR-L.

risk_factors: Similar to existing methods, SphereAR can produce results with noticeable artifacts, especially in complex classes.

These primarily include artifacts in human faces and, most prominently, a failure to render legible text or other high-frequency details (e.g., street signs, bottle labels), which are often generated as semantically meaningless patterns.

Due to computational constraints, our current evaluation is focused on the ImageNet benchmark. Therefore, assessing the scalability of SphereAR and its robustness in more diverse, large-scale data regimes remains a critical direction for future investigation.

title: LST-Bench:A Benchmark for long sequence time-series forecasting Task
name: LST-Bench
short_hypothesis: A comprehensive benchmark, LST-Bench, is needed to systematically evaluate and compare the performance of a wide range of modern deep learning models (Transformer-based, MLP-based, CNN-based, and RNN-based) for the long sequence time-series forecasting (LSTF) task, addressing the current lack of standardized evaluation.
related_work: Recent years have seen a surge in deep learning models for long-term time series forecasting, including variants of Transformer, CNN, RNN, and MLP architectures. However, recent studies have questioned the practical effectiveness of complex Transformer-based models compared to simpler MLP-based ones. Despite this rapid development, there is a notable scarcity of comprehensive and up-to-date benchmarks. Existing benchmarks are often dated and do not include modern deep learning methodologies, creating a gap in the ability to fairly and rigorously compare the performance of new models.
abstract: This paper introduces LST-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark designed for evaluating long sequence time-series forecasting(LSTF) models. This benchmark has been developed in response to recent advancements in deep learning methods in the field of LSTF tasks. LST-Bench includes Transformer-based, MLP-based, CNN-based, and RNN-based models, evaluating the performance of 11 major forecasting models on a set of commonly used 7 datasets and 7 new datasets that we have introduced. We plan to conduct a thorough analysis of the experimental results, including the overall prediction performance of models and their generalization across different prediction lengths and datasets. Our LST-Bench is intended to serve as a valuable resource for advancing research in the field of time series forecasting.

experiments.Description: To comprehensively evaluate widely used long sequence time-series forecasting models by assessing their overall prediction performance, generalization across different datasets and prediction lengths, prediction consistency, execution speed, and training efficiency.
experiments.Method: The performance of 11 major forecasting models (including Transformer-based, MLP-based, CNN-based, and RNN-based architectures) will be evaluated. The evaluation will be conducted on a set of 14 datasets, comprising 7 commonly used public datasets (Weather, Traffic, Electricity, and 4 ETT datasets) and 7 newly introduced datasets called NEW. Experiments will be run at four different prediction lengths (96, 192, 336, and 720 time steps) with a unified input sequence length of 336. To ensure fairness, all models will be trained and tested on a single Nvidia V100 32GB GPU, using the original authors' open-source code and default hyperparameters where possible.

risk_factors: We believe it is because the current metrics fail to adequately reflect the actual requirements of time series forecasting tasks or are influenced by certain data characteristics.

Addressing this issue requires a reevaluation or redefinition of evaluation metrics applicable to time series forecasting to more accurately measure model performance, model design, and improvement on that.

title: LICORICE: Label-Efficient Concept-Based Interpretable Reinforcement Learning
name: licorice-label-efficient-concept-rl
short_hypothesis: A novel training scheme, LICORICE, which combines iterative training, data decorrelation, and disagreement-based active learning, will enable reinforcement learning agents to efficiently learn an interpretable, concept-based policy by querying for only a small, actively selected set of concept labels, thus overcoming the impractical annotation requirements of prior work.
related_work: Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) improve interpretability by forcing decisions through a layer of human-understandable concepts. While applied to supervised learning, their use in reinforcement learning (RL) is limited by the impractical assumption that concept annotations are readily available for the vast number of states encountered during training. This necessitates a method for learning concept-based policies with a limited annotation budget.
abstract: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have predominantly leveraged neural network policies for decision-making, yet these models often lack interpretability. Concept bottleneck models offer an interpretable alternative by integrating human-understandable concepts into policies. However, prior work assumes that concept annotations are readily available during training. For RL, this requirement poses a significant limitation: it necessitates continuous real-time concept annotation. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a novel training scheme, LICORICE, that enables RL agents to efficiently learn a concept-based policy by only querying annotators to label a small set of data. Our algorithm involves three main contributions: interleaving concept learning and RL training, using an ensemble to actively select informative data points for labeling, and decorrelating the concept data. We also propose to explore the use of VLMs as automated concept annotators. Our work aims to significantly reduce the annotation burden for interpretable RL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if LICORICE can achieve high environment reward and high concept accuracy under a limited annotation budget, and to compare its label efficiency against other methods (RQ1 & RQ3).
experiments.Method: The proposed LICORICE algorithm will be evaluated on five environments with image input: PixelCartPole, DoorKey, DynamicObstacles, Boxing, and Pong. It will be compared against three budget-constrained baselines (Sequential-Q, Disagreement-Q, Random-Q) and one budget-unconstrained baseline (CPM). All algorithms use PPO as the RL backbone.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Environment reward', 'Concept error (MSE for regression; 1 - accuracy for classification)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effectiveness of using Vision Language Models (VLMs) as automated concept annotators within the LICORICE framework (RQ2).
experiments.Method: The LICORICE algorithm will be run with concept labels provided by a VLM (GPT-4o) instead of a perfect human annotator. This variant, LICORICE+GPT-40, will be evaluated on the five environments: PixelCartPole, DoorKey, DynamicObstacles, Boxing, and Pong.

experiments.Description: To validate that policies trained with LICORICE support test-time concept interventions, a key feature of interpretable concept-based models (RQ4).
experiments.Method: A trained LICORICE policy will be evaluated using a noisy concept model. Then, individual concepts will be sequentially corrected to their ground-truth values, and the impact on policy performance will be measured. This will be performed on PixelCartPole and other discrete-concept environments.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Change in environment reward as more concepts are intervened upon']

experiments.Description: To assess the contribution of each of the three main components of LICORICE (iterative training, data decorrelation, and disagreement-based active learning) to its overall performance through an ablation study.
experiments.Method: Three variants of LICORICE will be created, each removing one component: LICORICE-IT (removes iterative training), LICORICE-DE (removes data decorrelation), and LICORICE-AC (removes active learning). These variants will be compared against the full LICORICE model on the environments.

risk_factors: One significant challenge is the difficulty VLMs face with certain types of concepts, especially continuous variables.

Furthermore, although VLMs can be successfully used for automatic labeling of some concepts, there are still hallucination issues (Achiam et al., 2023) and other failure cases, such as providing inaccurate counts.
Finally, designing a concept-based representation for RL remains an open challenge.

title: Counterintuitive RL: The Hidden Value of Acting Bad
name: maxmin-td-learning
short_hypothesis: A theoretically-founded improvement to temporal difference learning can be achieved by taking actions that minimize the state-action value function, especially early in training. This counterintuitive strategy is proposed to increase the information gain from environment interactions by generating experiences with higher temporal difference errors, thereby accelerating learning at zero additional computational cost.
related_work: Experience collection in deep reinforcement learning is an open problem. While provably optimal techniques exist for tabular or bandit settings (e.g., count-based methods), they do not transfer well to high-dimensional state spaces and often introduce significant computational complexity. Consequently, practitioners still rely on simple, naive techniques like e-greedy. This paper aims to bridge the gap by introducing a theoretically motivated, computationally efficient, and simple experience collection strategy for deep RL.
abstract: Learning to make sequential decisions solely from interacting with an environment without any supervision has been achieved by the initial installation of deep neural networks as function approximators to represent and learn a value function in high-dimensional MDPs. Reinforcement learning policies face exponentially growing state spaces in experience collection in high dimensional MDPs resulting in a dichotomy between computational complexity and policy success. In our paper we focus on the agent’s interaction with the environment in a high-dimensional MDP during the learning phase and we introduce a theoretically-founded novel method based on experiences obtained through extremum actions. Our analysis and method provides a theoretical basis for effective, accelerated and efficient experience collection, and further comes with zero additional computational cost while leading to significant acceleration of training in deep reinforcement learning.

experiments.Description: To provide intuition for the proposed method in a simple, controlled environment, an experiment is planned on a toy problem called the chain MDP.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on a chain MDP with n=10 states. The proposed MaxMin TD Learning method will be compared with the e-greedy action selection method and the upper confidence bound (UCB) method within a tabular Q-learning framework.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curves showing Average Cumulative Return vs. Environment Interactions.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the sample efficiency of the proposed method in a low-data regime across a wide range of tasks.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) 100K benchmark. The proposed MaxMin TD Learning exploration strategy will be integrated into DDQN and QRDQN algorithms. It will be compared against two baselines: the standard e-greedy exploration strategy and NoisyNetworks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Human normalized median scores', 'Human normalized 20th percentile scores', 'Human normalized 80th percentile scores']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed method in a standard, large-data regime with extended training.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) with 200 million frames of training (50 million environment interactions). The proposed MaxMin TD Learning algorithm will be compared against the original version of the DDQN algorithm with e-greedy training on a selection of games (e.g., StarGunner, Bowling, Surround, BankHeist, JamesBond, Amidar, Gravitar, Tennis).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curves showing game scores vs. environment interactions.']

experiments.Description: To empirically investigate the theoretical claim that taking the minimum Q-value action results in transitions with higher temporal difference (TD).
experiments.Method: During training on the ALE 100K benchmark and the chain MDP, the temporal difference will be recorded for batches of transitions. The TD obtained by the MaxMin TD Learning method will be compared to the TD obtained by e-greedy-based learning and other baselines like NoisyNetworks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Temporal difference values', 'Normalized TD Gain, defined as 1 + (TD_method - TD_e-greedy) / |TD_e-greedy|']

title: A Dual-Metric Approach for Model Selection in self-supervised learning for histopathology
name: dual-metric-model-selection-histopathology
short_hypothesis: The appropriate approach for model selection in self-supervised learning for histopathology is a combination of out-of-distribution benchmark performance (task-dependent metrics) and representation quality estimation approaches (task-agnostic metrics).
related_work: Recent efforts to quantify the quality of self-supervised learning (SSL) representations have focused on rank-based metrics like RankMe, which have shown correlation with downstream performance on linear probing tasks in natural images. However, their effectiveness for non-linear tasks crucial to histopathology, such as instance segmentation and slide-level classification, is unknown.
