Current practice in histopathology often relies on evaluating performance on benchmark tasks, but it is unclear how this correlates with performance on other downstream tasks or when to terminate training for optimal generalization.
abstract: Selecting appropriate models during self-supervised training of vision transformers in histopathology is challenging. Recent efforts to quantify the quality of self-supervised learning representations through rank estimation approaches have shown promise in natural image classification tasks. However, their effectiveness in histopathology, particularly for non-linear tasks such as instance segmentation and classification from whole slide images, remains unexplored. This study proposes an approach for model selection in histopathology by combining task-specific metrics (such as accuracy) and task agnostic metrics (such as rank estimation). The proposed approach also allows for obtaining a model based on the type of downstream task. Towards this end, three types of model selection based on the downstream task performance are to be evaluated: classification-best, segmentation-best, and a best all-round one.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed dual-metric model selection procedure by identifying the best-performing checkpoints for different downstream task types (classification, segmentation, all-round) from self-supervised training runs.
experiments.Method: Nine distinct Vision Transformer (ViT) models (ViT-S, ViT-B, and SMoE variants) are trained using the dinov1 self-supervised framework on a dataset of LUAD patches. During training, checkpoints are saved periodically. For each checkpoint, task-agnostic quality metrics (RankMe, LiDAR, a-ReQ) are computed on the pre-training test set. Simultaneously, task-specific performance is evaluated on a set of out-of-distribution benchmark tasks: classification (BACH, MHIST, CRC) and instance segmentation (PanNuke, MoNuSeg). The proposed selection algorithm (Algorithm 1) will then be used to identify three optimal checkpoints: 'classification-best' (using only classification benchmarks), 'segmentation-best' (using only segmentation benchmarks), and 'all-round' (using all benchmarks).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Weighted F1-score for classification tasks.', 'Aggregated Jaccard Index (AJI) for segmentation tasks.', 'RankMe, LiDAR, and a-ReQ for representation quality.']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of the selected checkpoints on held-out downstream tasks that were not used in the selection process.
experiments.Method: The three checkpoints selected for each model ('classification-best', 'segmentation-best', 'all-round') will be evaluated on two new, held-out tasks. The first is a tile-level LUAD subtype classification task. The second is a slide-level EGFR binary classification task using a gated multi-head attention (GMA) MIL aggregator. The encoder weights are kept frozen during this evaluation.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of the trained small-scale models, selected via the proposed method, against large-scale, state-of-the-art foundation models from the literature.
experiments.Method: The best-performing checkpoints identified for the trained models will be benchmarked against external foundation models (Virchow, Virchow2, UNI) on the same set of out-of-distribution tile-level tasks (BACH, MHIST, CRC, PanNuke, MoNuSeg).

risk_factors: A key limitation of this work is that exploring the generalization of each checkpoint is expensive.

This is due to the broad range of benchmark tasks, both in terms of scope and field of view, typically encountered in histopathology and the limited ability of current representation quality estimation approaches to estimate instance segmentation performance.

Rank estimation approaches may only predict linear probing performance, while typical histopathology classification tasks involve slide-level aggregation of patch-level features, introducing a non-linearity that cannot be directly modeled by an embedding rank.

While this limitation was ameliorated in this work by using out-of-distribution benchmark tasks in conjunction with the representation rank, further work is necessary in developing more comprehensive representation quality metrics.

title: Generative Inbetweening: Adapting Image-to-Video Models for Keyframe Interpolation
name: generative-inbetweening-dual-diffusion
short_hypothesis: A pre-trained image-to-video diffusion model can be adapted for keyframe interpolation by creating a 'backward' model through a lightweight fine-tuning process that leverages rotated temporal self-attention maps. A dual-directional sampling process can then fuse predictions from the original 'forward' model and the new 'backward' model to generate a coherent video between two keyframes.
related_work: Existing methods for video frame interpolation, such as flow-based or transformer-based approaches, are often designed for closely spaced frames and struggle with large motions. While some diffusion models have been trained from scratch for keyframe interpolation, this is computationally expensive. An alternative is to adapt pre-trained image-to-video models. However, a naive approach of fusing a forward generation from the start frame with a reversed forward generation from the end frame can lead to oscillating, incoherent motion. This work proposes a method to create a true backward-motion model to enable consistent fusion.
abstract: We present a method for generating video sequences with coherent motion between a pair of input keyframes. We adapt a pretrained large-scale image-to-video diffusion model (originally trained to generate videos moving forward in time from a single input image) for keyframe interpolation, i.e., to produce a video between two input frames. We accomplish this adaptation through a lightweight fine-tuning technique that produces a version of the model that instead predicts videos moving backwards in time from a single input image. This model (along with the original forward-moving model) is subsequently used in a dual-directional diffusion sampling process that combines the overlapping model estimates starting from each of the two keyframes.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's ability to generate coherent in-between videos for dynamic scenes against existing interpolation and generation techniques on the Pexels dataset.
experiments.Method: The proposed method, using a fine-tuned Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) model, will be compared against FILM (a traditional frame interpolation method), TRF (an alternative SVD adaptation method), and DynamiCrafter (a large-scale image-to-video model trained for keyframing). The evaluation will be conducted on a curated set of keyframe pairs from the Pexels dataset.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's ability to generate coherent in-between videos for articulated motions against existing interpolation and generation techniques on the Davis dataset.
experiments.Method: The proposed method, using a fine-tuned Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) model, will be compared against FILM, TRF, and DynamiCrafter. The evaluation will be conducted on a curated set of 117 input pairs from the Davis dataset.

experiments.Description: To perform an ablation study to justify the key design decisions of the proposed method, specifically the lightweight fine-tuning and the use of rotated attention maps.
experiments.Method: The full proposed method will be compared against two variants: 1) 'Ours w/o RA', which fine-tunes all temporal self-attention parameters without using the 180-degree rotated temporal self-attention map as input, and 2) 'Ours w/o FT', which uses the rotated attention maps but omits the fine-tuning of the value and output projection matrices for backward motion.

risk_factors: Our method is limited by the motion quality of the underlying base model, Stable Video Diffusion (SVD), as discussed in Sec. 5.4.

Another limitation is that SVD has strong motion priors derived from the input image, tending to generate only specific motions for a given input. As a result, the actual motion required to connect the input key frames may not be represented within SVD's motion space, making it challenging to synthesize plausible intermediate videos.

When the input pairs are captured at such distant intervals that they have sparse correspondences, as shown in Fig. 6, where only a small portion of cars appear in both input frames, it becomes difficult for our method to fuse the forward and backward motions.
SVD itself struggles to generate natural running movements in between.

title: On the Surprising Efficacy of Online Self-Improvement for Embodied Multimodal Foundation Models
name: online-self-improvement-for-embodied-mfas
short_hypothesis: A two-stage fine-tuning approach for Multimodal Foundation Agents (MFAs) in robotics, consisting of an initial Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) stage followed by an Online Self-Improvement (RL) stage, will be more sample-efficient and produce more performant policies than SFT alone. The second stage can leverage the model's own predictions of 'steps-to-go' to derive an effective reward function, eliminating the need for manual reward engineering.
related_work: The application of foundation models to low-level robot control has been largely limited to behavioral cloning (e.g., RT-2). While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great success in fine-tuning LLMs, its application to robotics is hindered by challenges like sample efficiency and manual reward engineering. This work aims to bridge this gap by designing a two-stage framework inspired by LLM post-training processes, specifically for robotic MFAs, to enable sample-efficient online improvement without ground-truth rewards.
abstract: Foundation models trained on web-scale data have revolutionized robotics, but their application to low-level control remains largely limited to behavioral cloning. Drawing inspiration from the sample efficiency and success of reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning in large language models (LLMs), we propose a two-stage approach suited to robotics. The first stage, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), fine-tunes pre-trained foundation models using goal-conditioned behavioral cloning and “steps-to-go” prediction objectives. In the second stage, this foundation enables the extraction of a well-shaped reward function and a success detector, eliminating the need for manual reward engineering and real-world instrumentation, and allowing robots to practice autonomously with minimal human supervision.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if the proposed self-improvement procedure improves performance beyond the supervised learning stage and is more sample-efficient than supervised learning alone in a simulated environment.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the simulated LanguageTable domain. The PaLI MFA will be trained on subsets (10%, 20%, 80%) of the human-generated trajectory dataset. Stage 1 involves supervised fine-tuning with behavioral cloning and 'steps-to-go' prediction objectives. Stage 2 involves online self-improvement (RL) on the 'Block2Block' tasks. The performance of the two-stage model will be compared against the Stage 1 policy baseline (equivalent to RT-2).

experiments.Description: To test if the self-improvement procedure is reliable and reproducible enough to be employed for real-world robotics.
experiments.Method: The two-stage framework will be applied to the real-world LanguageTable domain, using 20% and 80% of the real-world dataset for Stage 1. Stage 2 fine-tuning will be performed on the 'Block2Block' subset of tasks, with one human operator monitoring 3-4 robot stations simultaneously.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed fine-tuning framework on a second, more complex robot embodiment, the bimanual Aloha manipulation platform.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a simulated Aloha platform for a bimanual insertion task. Imitation datasets of 5K, 10K, and 15K trajectories will be used. The two-stage process will be applied to the 5K and 10K datasets and compared against supervised learning baselines (Stage 1 only) trained on 10K and 15K episodes.

experiments.Description: To ablate the contribution of the web-scale pre-training of the foundation model to the effectiveness of Stage 2 self-improvement.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be run on the simulated LanguageTable domain. The Stage 2 policy will be initialized from a PaLI Stage 1 checkpoint, but the reward model will be derived from different model variations: 1) a fully pretrained PaLI model, 2) a 'Frankenstein' model (pretrained ViT and language Transformer without joint co-training), and 3) a 'Scratch' model (randomly initialized architecture).

experiments.Description: To investigate the framework's ability to enable domain transfer, specifically Real2Sim transfer.
experiments.Method: Stage 1 models will be trained using 80% of the real-world LanguageTable dataset. Then, Stage 2 self-improvement will be performed in the simulated LanguageTable environment. This will also be compared to using a 'Frankenstein' reward model to highlight the role of pre-training in domain transfer.

experiments.Description: To test the generalization ability of the self-improvement approach to learn novel skills not present in the initial imitation dataset.
experiments.Method: Starting from a real-world LanguageTable policy already fine-tuned for 'Block2Block' tasks, further Stage 2 fine-tuning will be performed on the 'BananaTable' task. This task replaces the blocks with a prosthetic banana and requires pushing it, a visually and behaviorally distinct skill not seen by the policy or reward model before.

risk_factors: Our approach uses on-policy REINFORCE for simplicity which does not reuse any collected data in Stage 2. Off-policy methods have the potential to even more substantially improve Stage 2 sample-efficiency.

Training large models requires significant compute budgets. Understanding whether our framework is amenable to parameter-efficient tuning methods (e.g. LoRA (Hu et al., 2021)) is critical towards enabling broad access to Stage 2 fine-tuning.

What are the failure cases of our reward formulation? Anecdotally, we have seen that if we continue training Stage 2 for past the peak performance, the success rate of the policies can begin to degrade. Is this due to gaps between our reward formulation and intended task outcome?

title: SimpleVLA-RL: Scaling VLA Training via Reinforcement Learning
name: simplevla-rl
short_hypothesis: Inspired by LLM breakthroughs where RL with outcome rewards enhances step-by-step reasoning, it is hypothesized that an outcome-driven RL paradigm can improve the long-horizon, step-by-step action planning of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. This can be achieved with an efficient RL framework, SimpleVLA-RL, which builds on veRL and introduces VLA-specific trajectory sampling, scalable parallelization, multi-environment rendering, optimized loss computation, and exploration-enhancing strategies to scale VLA training beyond limited static datasets.
related_work: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, typically trained via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), are constrained by the scarcity and high cost of large-scale, high-quality robotic trajectory data, which leads to poor generalization on out-of-distribution tasks. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a path to learn through environmental interaction, traditional robotics RL often requires hand-crafted, dense reward functions for each task, limiting its scalability and generalizability. Recent work in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) has shown that simple, sparse outcome-based rewards can significantly enhance step-by-step reasoning. This creates an opportunity to apply a similar outcome-driven RL approach to VLAs to overcome the data dependency of SFT and the reward engineering burden of traditional RL.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for robotic manipulation. Despite substantial progress enabled by large-scale pre-training and supervised fine-tuning (SFT), these models face two fundamental challenges: (i) the scarcity and high cost of large-scale robotic trajectories required for SFT scaling, and (ii) limited generalization to tasks under distribution shift. To overcome these limitations, we explore reinforcement learning (RL) as a pathway to scaling VLA training beyond limited datasets. Inspired by LLM breakthroughs where RL with outcome rewards enhances step-by-step reasoning, we ask: Can outcome-driven RL improve long-horizon step-by-step action planning of VLA? In this work, we introduce SimpleVLA-RL, an efficient RL framework tailored for VLA models. Building upon veRL, we introduce VLA-specific trajectory sampling, scalable parallelization, multi-environment rendering, and optimized loss computation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SimpleVLA-RL against SFT-only and other advanced VLA baselines on a range of simulated manipulation benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The SimpleVLA-RL framework will be applied to the OpenVLA-OFT model backbone after an initial SFT stage. Its performance will be compared against a suite of baseline models including UniVLA, RDT-1B, π₀, πfast, Nora, OpenVLA, Octo, DP, and DP3, which are trained with SFT only. The evaluation will be conducted on the LIBERO, RoboTwin1.0, and RoboTwin2.0 benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To investigate whether SimpleVLA-RL can enhance VLA model performance in data-scarce scenarios with extremely limited demonstration data.
experiments.Method: An OpenVLA-OFT model will be fine-tuned on the LIBERO benchmark under two data conditions: 'One-Trajectory SFT' (using only one demonstration per task) and 'Full-Trajectory SFT' (using all 500 demonstrations per task). SimpleVLA-RL will then be applied to the models from both SFT settings. The performance will be compared against the SFT-only models from each condition.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average Success Rate (SR) across LIBERO task suites']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization ability of SimpleVLA-RL on out-of-distribution tasks compared to a standard SFT approach.
experiments.Method: For each of the LIBERO task suites (Spatial, Object, Goal), nine tasks will be selected as 'seen' for training, with one task reserved as 'unseen' for evaluation. A base model will be prepared using the 'One-Trajectory SFT' setting. This base model will then be further trained using two methods: (1) SFT on demonstrations from the 9 seen tasks, and (2) SimpleVLA-RL on scenarios from the 9 seen tasks. The performance of both resulting models will be evaluated on the held-out 'unseen' task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the real-world effectiveness and sim-to-real transfer of policies trained with SimpleVLA-RL.
experiments.Method: An SFT model and an RL model (trained with SimpleVLA-RL) will be developed entirely using simulation data from four RoboTwin2.0 tasks (Stack Bowls, Handover Block, Pick Bottle, Click Bell). These policies will then be deployed on AgileX Piper robotic arms to perform the tasks in the real world. The performance will be compared against the SFT-only model and an RDT baseline model.

risk_factors: RL fails completely when the base model lacks initial task capability (0% success rate).
when initial success rates are too low (< 5%), RL improvements remain negligible, while stronger initial models achieve substantially better final performance after RL training.
The results indicate that a minimal level of task competence is essential for effective RL. Below this threshold, exploration is ineffective and RL fails to produce meaningful gains.

title: Towards Photonic Band Diagram Generation with Transformer-Latent Diffusion Models
name: transformer-latent-diffusion-for-photonic-band-diagrams
short_hypothesis: A latent diffusion model, conditioned by a transformer encoder that processes 3D photonic crystal structures as sequences of 2D slices, can generate their corresponding photonic band diagrams (BDs). The transformer's self-attention mechanism is hypothesized to be well-suited for capturing the complex, long-range physical interactions (like interference and scattering) inherent in photonics, providing meaningful contextual embeddings to guide the diffusion process. This approach is intended to be a scalable and generalizable surrogate model, avoiding the high computational cost of traditional numerical simulations.
related_work: Computing photonic band diagrams (BDs) requires solving Maxwell's equations, which is numerically expensive and can be intractable in optimization loops for inverse design. Prior deep learning (DL) works have proposed surrogate models using architectures like dense networks, VAEs, U-Nets, CNNs, and GANs. However, these methods often have limitations: they may use basic DL techniques not adapted for complex photonic phenomena, focus only on 2D structures without a clear path to 3D scalability, or are not designed to generate full BDs. While diffusion models have been used in other scientific domains and once in photonics for field distribution from text, no prior work has proposed a diffusion-based approach for generating full BDs from arbitrary 3D geometric structures. The proposed method aims to fill this gap by using a transformer-based latent diffusion model, which is designed to be more scalable and better at capturing the complex physics compared to previous approaches.
abstract: Computing photonic band diagrams (BDs), a key tool for investigating light propagation in inhomogeneous structured materials, is numerically expensive as it requires solving Maxwell's equations. This cost is a significant challenge, especially for inverse design optimization. To address this, we introduce an approach for BD generation based on diffusion models, with the intent to generalize and scale to arbitrary three-dimensional structures. This study proposes coupling a transformer encoder, which extracts contextual embeddings from the input structure, with a latent diffusion model to generate the corresponding BD. We also aim to provide insights into why transformers and diffusion models are well suited to capture the complex interference and scattering phenomena inherent to photonics, bridging modern deep learning with complex photonic design problems.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the alignment capability of the Material-to-Context (M2C) and Band Diagram (BD) encoders. The goal is to train the encoders to map a photonic structure and its corresponding BD to a shared latent space and measure how well they can identify correct pairs.
experiments.Method: The M2C and BD encoders are jointly trained on two custom datasets ('small' and 'large') of 3D Photonic Crystal (PhC) structures and their corresponding BDs generated via RCWA simulations. The training uses a contrastive method based on Momentum Contrast (MoCo) to align the global contextual embeddings from both encoders. The BD encoder/decoder is a VAE with a Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder, and its training also includes a reconstruction loss and KL regularization. The M2C encoder is a transformer that processes the 3D structure as a sequence of 2D slices.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of a latent diffusion model in generating photonic band diagrams (BDs) when conditioned on structural embeddings from the pre-trained M2C encoder.
experiments.Method: A U-Net based latent diffusion model is trained to generate the latent representation of a BD, conditioned on the M2C encoder's output. The model is trained on the 'small' and 'large' datasets of PhCs. The diffusion process is defined with a cosine noise scheduler over 1,000 steps. Different training strategies will be explored, including freezing versus fine-tuning the M2C encoder and the effect of adding a binary cross-entropy reconstruction loss to the standard noise prediction loss. For inference, Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) will be used to generate BDs in 50 steps.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative visual inspection of generated BDs against ground truth', 'Dice coefficient', 'Mean Average Precision (mAP)', 'Computation time comparison against RCWA simulations']

risk_factors: While our dataset covers only a limited set of structures, the method is designed to be generalizable and scalable to more arbitrary 3D configurations.
BD generation becomes more challenging as the number of modes (bands) increases.
We hypothesize that further improving the M2C encoder could help mitigate this limitation.
In particular, the fidelity of the generated BDs compared to real simulation is inevitably reduced, especially when increasing the number of layers.
Despite the fact that BD generation does not yet reach the physical fidelity of real simulations, Table 3 highlights how fast it can be.

Because photonic band diagrams consist of very thin and sometimes sparse pixel bands, these overlap-based metrics are difficult to interpret in absolute terms compared to more traditional computer vision tasks.

title: Mean Flow Policy with Instantaneous Velocity Constraint for One-step Action Generation
name: mean-velocity-policy-with-ivc
short_hypothesis: A new generative policy, the mean velocity policy (MVP), which models the mean velocity field instead of the instantaneous velocity, can achieve one-step action generation, unifying the expressiveness of generative policies with high efficiency. Furthermore, introducing an instantaneous velocity constraint (IVC) during training will serve as a crucial boundary condition, improving learning accuracy and enhancing policy expressiveness.
related_work: Existing generative policies in reinforcement learning, such as those based on diffusion models and flow matching, are highly expressive but suffer from a key limitation: their dependence on iterative, multi-step refinement to generate actions from noise. This computational dependency introduces significant overhead, hindering training speed (especially for online RL) and creating high inference latency, which is a major impediment for real-time control systems. The research gap is the need for a policy that unifies the expressiveness of generative models with the efficiency of one-step action generation.
abstract: Learning expressive and efficient policy functions is a promising direction in reinforcement learning (RL). While flow-based policies have proven effective in modeling complex action distributions, they still face a trade-off between expressiveness and computational burden, which is typically controlled by the number of flow steps. This work proposes a mean velocity policy (MVP), a new generative policy function that models the mean velocity field to achieve the fastest one-step action generation. To ensure its high expressiveness, an instantaneous velocity constraint (IVC) is introduced on the mean velocity field during training. The hypothesis is that this design explicitly serves as a crucial boundary condition, thereby improving learning accuracy and enhancing policy expressiveness.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the Mean Velocity Policy (MVP) against strong offline-to-online RL baselines on a variety of challenging robotic manipulation tasks.
experiments.Method: The MVP model will be tested on 9 sparse-reward robotic manipulation tasks. This includes 3 tasks from the Robomimic benchmark (Lift, Can, Square) using multi-human datasets, and 6 tasks from OGBench (cube-double-task 2/3/4 and cube-triple-task 2/3/4) using default play-style datasets. The performance will be compared against three baselines: FQL (flow Q learning), BFN (best-of-N), and QC (Q-chunking).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the instantaneous velocity constraint (IVC) to validate its role and sensitivity to its coefficient (λ).
experiments.Method: The full MVP model (λ=1.0) will be compared against variants with a reduced constraint (λ=0.5) and without the constraint (λ=0.0). The evaluation will be performed on challenging tasks such as Cube-triple-task3 and Cube-triple-task4.

experiments.Description: To compare MVP against one-step variants of the baseline methods to determine if a naive one-step configuration is sufficient for solving complex tasks.
experiments.Method: MVP will be compared against one-step versions of the baselines: FQL-Onestep, BFN-Onestep, and QC-Onestep. The experiment will be run on the Cube-triple-task3 and Cube-triple-task4 tasks.

experiments.Description: To analyze the training and inference time-efficiency of MVP compared to baseline methods.
experiments.Method: Training speed will be compared across all 9 benchmark tasks. Inference time will be evaluated on a CPU-only environment (AMD Ryzen Threadripper 3960X 24-Core Processor) with JAX's Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation disabled to simulate a realistic deployment scenario. The comparison will be against FQL, BFN, and QC.

risk_factors: Regarding limitation, a primary one is the additional GPU memory consumption during training, which stems from the Jacobian-Vector Product (JVP) operation.

title: DuoAttention: Efficient Long-Context LLM Inference with Retrieval and Streaming Heads
name: duo-attention
short_hypothesis: Attention heads in LLMs can be categorized into two types: 'Retrieval Heads' that are critical for long-context processing and require full attention, and 'Streaming Heads' that focus on recent tokens and attention sinks. By applying a full KV cache only to retrieval heads and a lightweight, constant-length KV cache to streaming heads, it is possible to significantly reduce memory and latency for long-context inference without compromising the model's long-context abilities.
related_work: Existing approaches to improve long-context LLM efficiency fall into several categories. Model architecture modifications like Multi-Query Attention (MQA) and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) reduce KV cache size but require pre-training and don't lower computational costs. Approximate attention methods like Sparse Transformer, H2O, TOVA, and StreamingLLM reduce complexity but can degrade long-context performance. System-level optimizations like FlashAttention and vLLM improve computational efficiency but do not reduce the KV cache size itself. Recent works have identified the concept of retrieval heads, but they either do not compress the KV cache, do not optimize decoding, or rely on less accurate attention profiling for head identification. The proposed method, DuoAttention, aims to fill this gap by using a lightweight, optimization-based procedure to identify and differentiate head types, enabling compression for both pre-filling and decoding stages to improve efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
abstract: Deploying long-context large language models (LLMs) is essential but poses significant computational and memory challenges. Caching all Key and Value (KV) states across all attention heads consumes substantial memory. Existing KV cache pruning methods either damage the long-context capabilities of LLMs or offer only limited efficiency improvements. This paper identifies that only a fraction of attention heads, a.k.a, Retrieval Heads, are critical for processing long contexts and require full attention across all tokens. In contrast, all other heads, which primarily focus on recent tokens and attention sinks—referred to as Streaming Heads—do not require full attention. Based on this insight, we introduce DuoAttention, a framework that only applies a full KV cache to retrieval heads while using a light-weight, constant-length KV cache for streaming heads. This is intended to reduce both LLM's decoding and pre-filling memory and latency without compromising its long-context abilities. DuoAttention uses a lightweight, optimization-based algorithm with synthetic data to identify retrieval heads accurately.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to accurately identify and retrieve relevant information from lengthy contexts using a 'needle in a haystack' style pressure test.
experiments.Method: The method will be evaluated on the Needle-in-a-Haystack (NIAH) benchmark. The models used are Llama-2-7B-32K-Instruct and Llama-3-8B-Instruct-Gradient-1048k. The performance of DuoAttention will be compared against baselines including H2O, TOVA, StreamingLLM, and FastGen.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Retrieval accuracy across various sequence depths and context lengths.']

experiments.Description: To assess the model's long-context understanding capabilities more thoroughly across a comprehensive suite of datasets encompassing multiple tasks and natural texts.
experiments.Method: The method will be evaluated on the LongBench benchmark. The models used are Llama-2-7B-32K-Instruct and Llama-3-8B-Instruct-Gradient-1048k. The performance of DuoAttention will be compared against baselines including H2O, TOVA, and StreamingLLM.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task-specific accuracy scores across the various datasets within LongBench.']

experiments.Description: To ensure that the proposed method does not compromise the model's performance on short-context tasks, assessing its knowledge, coding abilities, and helpfulness.
experiments.Method: The method will be evaluated on three short-context benchmarks: MMLU, MBPP, and MT-Bench. The models used include Llama-2-7B, Llama-3-8B, and Llama-3-70B-Instruct. DuoAttention will be compared against all baselines under the same KV cache budget.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['One-shot accuracy on MMLU', 'Zero-shot score on MBPP', 'Zero-shot score on MT-Bench']

experiments.Description: To measure the efficiency improvements in terms of decoding latency, pre-filling latency, and memory usage.
experiments.Method: The decoding and pre-filling performance of DuoAttention will be evaluated on Llama-2-7B and Llama-3-8B models on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU. The evaluation will be conducted across varying context sizes and pre-filling chunk sizes, comparing against a full attention baseline.

experiments.Description: To validate the key design choices of the DuoAttention framework, including the head identification method and the configuration of sink and recent tokens.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies will be conducted using the Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 model on passkey retrieval and MMLU datasets. The studies will compare: 1) the proposed optimization-based head identification against attention profiling, 2) using synthetic data versus natural language modeling for optimization, 3) the necessity of combining sink and recent attention, and 4) the effect of different deployment configurations for sink and recent token counts.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of combining DuoAttention with KV quantization techniques.
experiments.Method: An experiment will be run combining DuoAttention with INT4 KV Pre-Rope quantization on the Llama-3-8B-Instruct-Gradient-1048k model. This will be compared to a baseline of the original model with only INT4 KV quantization.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Overall accuracy score on the NIAH benchmark.']

title: Interpreting CLIP's Image Representation via Text-Based Decomposition
name: clip-text-decomposition
short_hypothesis: The image representation of a CLIP-ViT model can be decomposed into a sum of text-interpretable contributions from individual attention heads and image patches. This decomposition allows for identifying specialized semantic roles for attention heads (e.g., shape, color, location), which can then be leveraged to improve downstream tasks like debiasing and zero-shot segmentation.
related_work: Prior work in vision model explainability either produces heatmaps to highlight important input regions, which fails to explain non-spatial attributes, or interprets intermediate neurons visually. A few methods use text to interpret intermediate representations, but they do not exploit the specific decompositional structure of ViT's residual connections and attention mechanism within CLIP's joint vision-language space. This paper proposes to leverage these architectural properties for a more fine-grained, text-based decomposition and interpretation.
abstract: We investigate the CLIP image encoder by analyzing how individual model components affect the final representation. We propose to decompose the image representation as a sum across individual image patches, model layers, and attention heads, and use CLIP's text representation to interpret the summands. For interpreting the attention heads, we will characterize each head's role by automatically finding text representations that span its output space, which should reveal property-specific roles (e.g. location or shape). For interpreting the image patches, we expect to uncover an emergent spatial localization within CLIP. We plan to use this understanding to remove spurious features from CLIP and to create a strong zero-shot image segmenter.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the direct contribution of different model layers (MLPs and MSAs) to the final image representation and identify the most influential ones.
experiments.Method: Using OpenCLIP ViT-H-14, L-14, and B-16 models, we will perform mean-ablation. The direct effects of all MLPs will be ablated simultaneously. Separately, the direct effects of MSA layers will be cumulatively ablated from the earliest layer forward. The evaluation will be on the ImageNet validation set.

experiments.Description: To develop and validate an algorithm, TEXTSPAN, that automatically finds a basis of text descriptions for each attention head's output space, thereby revealing its semantic role.
experiments.Method: TEXTSPAN will be applied to the late attention heads of a CLIP ViT-L model. To validate that the discovered text bases capture the head's function, we will project each head's output onto its basis and measure the impact on classification. This will be compared against baselines using one-word descriptions or random vectors as the basis pool.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that the identified head roles can be used to mitigate spurious correlations in a downstream classification task.
experiments.Method: On the Waterbirds dataset, we will first use TEXTSPAN's output to manually annotate heads with 'geolocation' or 'image-location' roles. Then, we will mean-ablate the direct contributions of these identified heads and evaluate the model's performance. This will be compared to a baseline of ablating an equal number of random heads.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that head-specific representations can be used for property-specific image retrieval.
experiments.Method: For a given input image and a specific attention head identified as capturing a property (e.g., color, object count), we will compute the similarity between its head-specific representation and that of all other images in a dataset. We will then retrieve the nearest neighbors.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if decomposing the model's output across image tokens can produce a high-quality zero-shot image segmenter.
experiments.Method: The model's output will be decomposed into a sum of contributions from each image token. For a given text prompt, a heatmap will be generated by computing the similarity of each token's contribution to the text embedding. This heatmap will be binarized to create a segmentation mask. The approach will be evaluated on the ImageNet-segmentation dataset and compared against other explainability methods like LRP, partial-LRP, rollout, raw attention, GradCAM, and Chefer et al. (2021).

risk_factors: We analyzed only the direct effects of model components on the representation.
The outputs of TEXTSPAN show that not every head captures a single image property.
First, some heads may not correspond to coherent properties.
Second, the initial descriptions pool does not include descriptions of any image property.
Third, some heads may collaborate and have a coherent role only when their outputs are addressed together.

title: AirPhyNet: Harnessing Physics-Guided Neural Networks for Air Quality Prediction
name: physics-guided-air-quality-prediction
short_hypothesis: A hybrid deep learning model, AirPhyNet, that leverages the physics principles of air particle movement (diffusion and advection) by representing them as differential equation networks within a graph-based architecture, will generate more accurate and interpretable air quality forecasts compared to purely data-driven or physics-based models, especially in scenarios with sparse data.
related_work: Air quality prediction is typically addressed by two paradigms. Physics-based models, like Chemical Transport Models (CTMs), simulate atmospheric dynamics using governing equations but are computationally expensive and difficult to calibrate for real-world conditions. Data-driven approaches, particularly deep learning models like Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks (STGNNs) and attention-based models, can learn complex relationships from historical data but require extensive training data, lack physical grounding, and suffer from poor interpretability and generalizability. While some studies have incorporated physical constraints into deep learning, this paper proposes the first physics-guided hybrid deep learning framework specifically for outdoor air quality prediction by integrating dispersion processes into a GNN-based differential equation network.
abstract: Traditional data-driven models for air quality prediction have shown promise, but their long-term accuracy can be limited, especially with sparse or incomplete data. They also often rely on black-box deep learning structures that lack a solid physical foundation, leading to reduced transparency and interpretability. To address these limitations, this paper presents a novel approach named Physics guided Neural Network for Air Quality Prediction (AirPhyNet). Specifically, the method leverages two well-established physics principles of air particle movement (diffusion and advection) by representing them as differential equation networks. It then utilizes a graph structure to integrate this physics knowledge into a neural network architecture and exploits latent representations to capture spatio-temporal relationships within the air quality data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall prediction performance of AirPhyNet against baseline models for forecasting PM2.5 concentrations at different future time horizons (24h, 48h, 72h).
experiments.Method: The model is tasked with forecasting PM2.5 concentrations for the next 72 hours using the past 72 hours of PM2.5, wind speed, and wind direction data from the Beijing and Shenzhen datasets. AirPhyNet is compared against three categories of baselines: 1) Classical Methods (Historical Average, Vector Auto-Regression), 2) Spatio-temporal Deep Learning Methods (DCRNN, STGCN, GMAN, GTS, PM2.5-GNN, AirFormer), and 3) Differential equation network based methods (Latent-ODE, ODE-LSTM).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance in predicting sudden changes in air quality, defined as instances where PM2.5 concentration exceeds a certain threshold and exhibits a significant change in the following hours.
experiments.Method: Using the Beijing and Shenzhen datasets, the performance of AirPhyNet is compared against deep learning baselines on a subset of the data corresponding to defined 'sudden change' events.

experiments.Description: To assess the generalizability and performance of the model in a sparse data scenario by training it on a smaller portion of the available data.
experiments.Method: The models are evaluated on the Beijing and Shenzhen datasets using a reduced training set (3:1:6 train:val:test split) to simulate data scarcity. AirPhyNet's performance is compared against all baselines under these conditions.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to illustrate the effect of incorporating different physical knowledge components (advection and diffusion) into the model.
experiments.Method: The full AirPhyNet model is compared against two variants on the Beijing dataset: an 'AdvOnly' model that only considers the advection process and a 'DiffOnly' model that only considers the diffusion process.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the efficacy of the RNN-based encoder for capturing temporal dependencies.
experiments.Method: The standard AirPhyNet model, which uses a GRU encoder, is compared with a variant named 'TCNEnc' that replaces the GRU with a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) based encoder.

experiments.Description: To conduct a qualitative case study to demonstrate the model's interpretability with respect to the physical processes of diffusion and advection.
experiments.Method: The study plans to visualize the predicted PM2.5 concentrations alongside wind direction data to check for consistency with advection. It also plans to visualize the diffusion of particles from a high-concentration station to its neighbors to verify that the model captures the physical diffusion process.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative visualization of predicted concentration heatmaps, wind vectors, and diffusion patterns.']

risk_factors: Our focus solely on PM2.5 concentration, while significant, may not account for the complete spectrum of pollutants that contribute to overall air pollution.
However, the applicability of our approach to highly chemical-reacting pollutants remains uncertain as such complex chemical behaviours are not captured by the model.

Additionally, our deep learning architecture emphasizes the incorporation of physical processes, limiting its suitability for pollutants heavily influenced by chemical reactions.This could potentially hinder the model's accuracy in scenarios dominated by chemical reactions rather than physical transport.

title: Oscillatory State-Space Models
name: LinOSS
short_hypothesis: We propose to construct state-space models based on stable discretizations of forced linear second-order ordinary differential equations (ODEs) modelling oscillators. This approach is motivated by the observation that forced harmonic oscillators can produce stable dynamics while ensuring expressive representations, potentially requiring less restrictive parameterizations than existing state-space models.
related_work: State-space models (SSMs) like S4, S5, and Mamba have become powerful for long sequence modeling. These models often rely on specific structural constraints on their state matrices (e.g., HiPPO, diagonal) to ensure stability and learn long-range dependencies, which can potentially limit their expressive power. This work proposes a different approach inspired by oscillatory dynamics in biological and physical systems. Unlike prior work on nonlinear oscillatory RNNs (e.g., CORNNs), this paper introduces the concept of harmonic oscillators into a linear state-space model framework, aiming to achieve stability with less restrictive parameterizations (only requiring a non-negative diagonal state matrix).
abstract: We propose Linear Oscillatory State-Space models (LinOSS) for efficiently learning on long sequences. Inspired by cortical dynamics of biological neural networks, we base our proposed LinOSS model on a system of forced harmonic oscillators. A stable discretization, integrated over time using fast associative parallel scans, yields the proposed state-space model. We prove that LinOSS produces stable dynamics only requiring a nonnegative diagonal state matrix, in contrast to many previous state-space models relying heavily on restrictive parameterizations. Moreover, we rigorously show that LinOSS is universal, i.e., it can approximate any continuous and causal operator mapping between time-varying functions to a desired accuracy. In addition, we show that an implicit-explicit discretization of LinOSS perfectly conserves the symmetry of time reversibility of the underlying dynamics. Together, these properties are intended to enable efficient modeling of long-range interactions, while ensuring stable and accurate long-horizon forecasting.

experiments.Description: To test the model's performance on learning long-range interactions on a benchmark of challenging time-series classification tasks.
experiments.Method: The LinOSS models (LinOSS-IM and LinOSS-IMEX) will be evaluated on six datasets from the UEA Multivariate Time Series Classification Archive (UEA-MTSCA) with the longest sequences: EigenWorms, SelfRegulationSCP1, SelfRegulationSCP2, EthanolConcentration, Heartbeat, and MotorImagery. The training procedure and hyperparameter search grid will follow the protocol from Walker et al. (2024) for a fair comparison against baselines including NRDE, NCDE, Log-NCDE, LRU, S5, S6, and Mamba.

experiments.Description: To test the performance of LinOSS on very long-range interactions with extremely long sequences.
experiments.Method: The LinOSS models will be evaluated on the PPG-DaLiA dataset for a multivariate time series regression task (heart rate prediction), using sequences of length 49,920. The same hyperparameter tuning protocol as competing models will be used. Baselines include NRDE, NCDE, Log-NCDE, LRU, S5, S6, and Mamba.

experiments.Description: To test the ability of LinOSS to serve as a general sequence-to-sequence model on a long-horizon forecasting task, without incorporating task-specific inductive biases.
experiments.Method: LinOSS will be configured as a masked sequence-to-sequence model to perform weather prediction on a task from Zhou et al. (2021), predicting 720 future timesteps based on 720 past timesteps. The performance will be compared against Transformer-based baselines (Informer, LogTrans, Reformer), LSTMs (LSTMa, LSTnet), and other state-space models (S4).

experiments.Description: To empirically analyze how design choices such as state matrix parameterization, initialization, and the discretization timestep affect the performance of LinOSS, and to analyze the roles of dissipation and conservation in the two model variants.
experiments.Method: A series of ablation studies will be conducted. This includes: 1) Comparing ReLU vs. squared parameterizations and uniform vs. normal initializations for the state matrix A on the UEA datasets. 2) Evaluating performance sensitivity to the timestep `Δt` on the SelfRegulationSCP1, Heartbeat, and MotorImagery datasets. 3) Comparing LinOSS-IM (dissipative) and LinOSS-IMEX (conservative) on a simulated energy-conserving simple harmonic motion prediction task.

risk_factors: A key issue in the context of learning chaotic systems with recurrent models is the inevitability of exploding gradients during training... it can be seen that the Lipschitz constant of the readout MLP... would blow up, thereby enabling exploding gradients. However, it is crucial to emphasize that this issue persists with all recurrent models and is not unique to LinOSS or other state-space models.
We aim to explore the role of higher-order discretization schemes within the LinOSS framework in future research.

title: OAR: Training Quantization-Friendly Object Detectors via Outlier-Aware Restriction
name: Outlier-Aware-Restriction
short_hypothesis: Applying a combination of Resonant Shrinkage loss to suppress activation outliers, Output Adaptation to normalize task branch outputs, and Edge-Aware KURE loss to refine weight distributions during pre-training will produce object detectors that are more robust to low-bit quantization and exhibit reduced performance disparities between task branches.
related_work: Prior work in model quantization includes Quantization-Aware Training (QAT), Post-Training Quantization (PTQ), and various regularization techniques. However, existing regularization methods have primarily focused on adjusting the statistical distribution of weights, without addressing how to make activations more suitable for quantization. Furthermore, they do not specifically tackle the issue of statistical inconsistencies and sensitivity discrepancies observed across different task branches (e.g., classification and regression) in modern object detector heads, which can lead to imbalanced sub-task performance after quantization.
abstract: Model quantization is widely employed to reduce computational resource usage during inference, often in conjunction with specialized hardware system for acceleration. While modern object detectors perform well at compact bit-widths (e.g., 8-bit), further quantization to ultra-low bit-widths (e.g., 4 or 3 bits) remains challenging. We identify the presence of outliers in the statistical distribution of activations in pre-trained detectors as a key obstacle, as such outliers expand the dynamic range and increase quantization error. Moreover, we observe significant numerical discrepancies in activation outliers across the task branches of the detection head, potentially leading to imbalanced sub-task performance after quantization. To address these issues, we propose Resonant Shrinkage loss and Output Adaptation to suppress activation outliers during pre-training. Additionally, we introduce Edge-Aware KURE loss to enhance the robustness of detector weights under quantization. All components are applied during the pre-training phase, producing detectors that are more quantization-friendly without altering training hyperparameters, while also reducing quantization sensitivity disparities between task branches. Our training framework is compatible with existing state-of-the-art quantization methods.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the OAR pre-training framework on various object detectors when subjected to naive Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) at different bit-widths.
experiments.Method: Models (RetinaNet-ResNet18, RetinaNet-ResNet50, ATSS-ResNet50, YOLOX-tiny) will be trained on the MS COCO dataset with and without the proposed OAR framework. The resulting full-precision models will be compared, and then quantized using a naive PTQ method to 6-bit, 5-bit, and 4-bit precision for further comparison.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of OAR-trained models when combined with the QDrop post-training quantization method, particularly at extremely low bit-widths (4-bit, 3-bit, 2-bit).
experiments.Method: Models (RetinaNet-ResNet18, RetinaNet-ResNet50, ATSS-ResNet50, YOLOX-tiny) trained on MS COCO with and without OAR will be quantized using the QDrop algorithm to INT4, INT3, and INT2.

experiments.Description: To validate the performance of the OAR framework when used as a pre-training step for Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) under extreme 2-bit conditions.
experiments.Method: On the MS COCO dataset, the LSQ QAT method will be applied to baseline models (RetinaNet-ResNet18, RetinaNet-ResNet50, ATSS-ResNet50) and models pre-trained with OAR. The experiment will also evaluate combining OAR with the HQOD algorithm during the QAT phase.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed OAR framework against other quantization-aware pre-training regularization schemes.
experiments.Method: A RetinaNet-ResNet18 model will be trained on MS COCO using different regularization algorithms: a baseline, KURE, R_LINF, and the proposed OAR. The performance of the full-precision and 4-bit quantized models will be compared.

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of OAR on the quantization noise and performance difference between the classification and regression task branches of a detection head.
experiments.Method: Using a RetinaNet-ResNet18 model on MS COCO, the activations of the classification branch and regression branch will be individually quantized to 2-bits for both a baseline model and an OAR-trained model. The performance gap between the branches will be measured.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to understand the contribution of each component of the OAR framework: Resonant Shrinkage (RS) loss, Output Adaptation (OA), and Edge-Aware KURE (EA-KURE) loss.
experiments.Method: A RetinaNet-ResNet18 model will be trained on MS COCO with the full OAR framework. Then, each component (RS, OA, EA-KURE) will be progressively removed, and the performance of each resulting model will be evaluated in both full-precision and after 4-bit naive PTQ.

risk_factors: OAR does not uniformly improve statistical metrics across all layers; in particular, its performance in the neck structure is relatively suboptimal.

We attribute this to the inherent characteristics of the FPN neck, which fuses features across multiple levels via multi-input, multi-output processing, potentially compromising statistical stability due to improvements made in the backbone and head. This issue warrants further investigation.

title: WorldAlignment: Benchmarking Expert-Level Human Preference Alignment across Domains and Aspects
name: world-alignment-benchmark
short_hypothesis: A new multi-aspect human preference benchmark, WorldAlignment, which incorporates complex preference pairs across instruction following, mathematical reasoning, and code-related tasks, will enable a more rigorous and nuanced assessment of LLM alignment capabilities in realistic, expert-level scenarios compared to existing benchmarks that focus on simplistic, general-purpose tasks.
related_work: Recent methods leverage LLMs-as-a-judge (e.g., AlpacaEval 2.0, MT-Bench, WildBench) to assess alignment quality, which is more cost-effective and shows high agreement with human judgments. However, these existing benchmarks primarily use simplistic, instruction-following data from general user preferences. This makes them insufficient for evaluating complex, domain-specific scenarios and susceptible to spurious correlations like response length bias. They suffer from limited domain coverage, neglecting specialized knowledge areas such as mathematical reasoning or code generation, which are increasingly important for real-world applications. This creates a gap for a more comprehensive and challenging evaluation framework.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities, yet aligning their behavior with human preferences remains both challenging and essential. Human evaluation of model outputs is often costly and must account for diverse user preferences. To address this, recent methods leverage LLM-as-a-judge to assess alignment quality, which achieves higher agreement with human judgments while being more cost-effective. However, existing widely used benchmarks such as AlpacaEval 2.0 primarily rely on simplistic, instruction-following data pairs derived from general user preferences. These benchmarks are insufficient for evaluating complex, domain-specific, and nuanced scenarios, and many are outdated for current alignment evaluation. To overcome these limitations, we introduce WorldAlignment, an expert-level, multi-domain human preference benchmark designed for efficient and comprehensive evaluation of alignment capabilities. WorldAlignment provides more challenging, higher-quality, and diverse preference pairs across multiple domains, enabling more robust alignment assessment. Furthermore, WorldAlignment supports evaluation across various dimensions, including instruction-following, mathematical reasoning, and code-related tasks, providing a holistic view of alignment performance.

experiments.Description: To conduct a comprehensive assessment of state-of-the-art LLMs on the proposed WorldAlignment benchmark to establish its utility as a robust evaluation framework.
experiments.Method: A range of open-source and proprietary models (GPT5, GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1-Mini, Gemma-3-27B-IT, and others) will be evaluated on the WorldAlignment benchmark, which consists of three tasks: instruction following, mathematical reasoning, and code generation. Responses from GPT-4o will be used as the baseline reference. A dual-judge system, using GPT-4o as the primary evaluator and GPT-4.1-Mini as a complementary assessor, will be employed to judge the model outputs.

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of different post-training optimization methods on model performance across the instruction, math, and code tasks in the WorldAlignment benchmark.
experiments.Method: The Gemma-2-9b-it and Llama-3-Instruct-8B model families will be evaluated. The performance of the base models will be compared against their variants fine-tuned with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Simple Preference Optimization (SimPO).

experiments.Description: To evaluate and compare the domain-specific performance of several mini-model series on the top five domains from the instruction-following portion of the WorldAlignment benchmark.
experiments.Method: The analysis will focus on the top five domains from the WorldAlignment(inst) benchmark: general knowledge, medicine, biology, history, and engineering. Three models will be compared: GPT-4.1-Mini-2025-04-14, GPT-40-Mini-2024-07-18, and 03-Mini-2025-01-31.

risk_factors: While our work focuses on evaluating LLM alignment across three core aspects and five specialized domains, the framework could be extended to additional domains such as scientific research, legal reasoning, and creative writing.
Expanding WorldAlignment to incorporate multilingual evaluation and cross-cultural preference alignment represents another promising direction.

Additionally, integrating dynamic difficulty adjustment and adaptive evaluation strategies could further enhance the framework’s ability to provide fine-grained assessment across varying complexity levels within each domain.

title: Online approximate factorization of a kernel matrix by a Hebbian neural network
name: kernel-similarity-matching-hebbian-networks
short_hypothesis: An online algorithm for kernel similarity matching can be derived by directly learning nonlinear features. This can be achieved by deriving and minimizing an upper bound for the sum of squared errors between output and input kernel similarities, which leads to online, correlation-based learning rules implementable in a 1-layer recurrent neural network.
related_work: Recent works have developed neural networks with online, correlation-based Hebbian learning rules to perform linear similarity matching, where output dot products approximate input dot products (y_s·y_t ≈ x_s·x_t). To handle nonlinear similarities, some approaches apply these linear algorithms to features generated by a fixed, random nonlinear mapping, such as random Fourier features. The research gap is the lack of a method that directly learns the nonlinear features for kernel similarity matching using online, biologically plausible (Hebbian) learning rules, instead of relying on a fixed random projection.
abstract: Recent works have derived neural networks with online correlation-based learning rules to perform kernel similarity matching by applying linear similarity matching algorithms to nonlinear features generated with random Fourier methods. In this paper, we attempt to perform kernel similarity matching by directly learning the nonlinear features. Our algorithm proceeds by deriving and then minimizing an upper bound for the sum of squared errors between output and input kernel similarities. The construction of our upper bound leads to online correlation-based learning rules which can be implemented with a 1-layer recurrent neural network. In addition to generating high-dimensional linearly separable representations, we propose that our upper bound naturally yields representations which are sparse and selective for specific input patterns.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the kernel approximation quality of the proposed method on a synthetic dataset and compare it against various approximation methods.
experiments.Method: Train the proposed network using a Gaussian kernel on the half moons dataset. The number of output neurons will be varied (n ∈ {2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64}). The performance will be compared to several baselines: the optimal eigenvector-based solution (kernel PCA), Nyström approximation with uniformly sampled landmarks, Nyström with KMeans landmarks, Nyström using the features learned by our method as landmarks, and the random Fourier feature method.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the kernel approximation quality of the proposed method on a real-world image dataset using both linear and nonlinear kernels.
experiments.Method: Train the proposed network on the MNIST dataset using a 'power-cosine' kernel of the form f(u,v) = ||u|| ||v|| (û · v̂)^α. Experiments will be run for a linear kernel (α=1) and a nonlinear kernel (α=3). The performance will be compared against kernel PCA and Nyström methods (with uniformly sampled, KMeans, and our learned landmarks).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the utility of the learned representations for downstream tasks, specifically clustering and linear separability.
experiments.Method: For the half moons dataset, run KMeans clustering on the learned 16D representations and visualize the top two principal components. For the MNIST dataset, train a linear classifier on the learned 800D representations (for α=1,2,3,4) using a varying number of labels per class (k ∈ {1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300, 1000, 3000}). The performance will be compared to running the same tasks on the original input data (x).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of principal components and KMeans clustering results', 'Train and test set accuracy of the linear classifier']

experiments.Description: To analyze the properties of the learned representations, specifically their sparsity.
experiments.Method: Train networks on MNIST with n=800 neurons and varying kernel nonlinearities (α={1,2,3,4}). The distribution of neuron responses (y) over all input patterns will be plotted and analyzed.

risk_factors: A key obstacle faced by users of this algorithm is the stochastic gradient descent ascent procedure.
Empirically the convergence our algorithm is quite sensitive to the learning rate choices.
This method does not provide the same sorts of theoretically guarantees or empirically observed robustness of sampling based methods.

title: GNNX-BENCH: Unravelling the Utility of Perturbation-based GNN Explainers through In-depth Benchmarking
name: gnn-explainer-benchmarking
short_hypothesis: A systematic benchmarking study of perturbation-based factual and counterfactual GNN explainers across diverse and previously uninvestigated facets—such as stability, feasibility, and a broader range of methods—will reveal their strengths, limitations, and technical deficiencies, thereby identifying open research challenges.
related_work: Numerous GNN explainability methods have been proposed, but empirical evaluations lack diversity. Existing benchmarking studies have primarily focused on a subset of factual explainers, leaving counterfactual reasoners largely uninvestigated. Consequently, critical aspects like the stability of explanations to noise and architectural variations, the feasibility of counterfactual recourses under domain constraints, and the stochasticity of explainer models have not been formally or systematically studied. This work aims to fill this gap by providing a comprehensive benchmarking study that includes both factual and counterfactual methods and evaluates them on these underexplored dimensions.
abstract: Numerous explainability methods have been proposed to shed light on the inner workings of GNNs. Despite the inclusion of empirical evaluations in all the proposed algorithms, the interrogative aspects of these evaluations lack diversity. As a result, various facets of explainability pertaining to GNNs, such as a comparative analysis of counterfactual reasoners, their stability to variational factors such as different GNN architectures, noise, stochasticity in non-convex loss surfaces, and feasibility amidst domain constraints have yet to be formally investigated. Motivated by this need, we present a benchmarking study on perturbation-based explainability methods for GNNs, aiming to systematically evaluate and compare a wide range of explainability techniques.

experiments.Description: Conduct a comparative analysis of perturbation-based GNN explainers to evaluate their performance and identify Pareto-optimal methods for both factual and counterfactual reasoning.
experiments.Method: Evaluate seven factual explainers (GNNExplainer, PGExplainer, TAGExplainer, GEM, SubgraphX, CF2, RCExplainer) and four counterfactual explainers (CF-GNNExplainer, CF2, RCExplainer, CLEAR). The evaluation will be performed on graph classification tasks using datasets like MUTAGENICITY, PROTEINS, IMDB-B, AIDS, MUTAG, NCI1, GRAPH-SST2, DD, REDDIT-B, and OGBG-MOLHIV, and on node classification tasks using TREE-CYCLES, TREE-GRID, and BA-SHAPES. The base GNN model is a GCN.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the stability of GNN explainers to ensure their reliability and trustworthiness when faced with minor variations in the evaluation framework.
experiments.Method: Assess stability from three perspectives: 1) Perturbations in topological space by injecting a small number of edge deletions/additions. 2) Model parameter stochasticity by applying explainers with different random seeds. 3) Model architecture variations by evaluating explanations across different GNNs (GCN, GAT, GIN, GraphSAGE). Baselines include GNNExplainer, PGExplainer, TAGExplainer, GEM, SubgraphX, CF2, and RCExplainer.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Jaccard similarity between the original explanation and the explanation obtained after introducing a variation.']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the quality of factual explanations through the lenses of necessity and reproducibility.
experiments.Method: Necessity is measured by removing the explanation subgraph and observing if the prediction flips. Reproducibility is measured in two ways: Reproducibility+ evaluates if a GNN retrained only on explanation graphs can retain original predictions, and Reproducibility- evaluates if a GNN retrained on the residual graph (original minus explanation) can still predict the class label.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the feasibility of counterfactual explanations by assessing their ability to generate recourses that adhere to specific domain constraints.
experiments.Method: The analysis will be conducted on molecular graph datasets. It will involve studying the distribution of molecules that are connected in the original dataset and comparing it to the distribution in the generated counterfactual recourses to see if they correspond to valid molecules.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['p-value of the deviation in the distribution of connected components between original and counterfactual graphs.']

risk_factors: The current generation of counterfactual explainers often fails to provide feasible recourses due to violations of topological constraints encoded by domain-specific considerations.

Current counterfactual explainers predominantly concentrate on identifying the shortest edit path that nudges the graph toward the decision boundary. This design inherently neglects the feasibility of the proposed edits.
Since SubgraphX employs tree search, its scalability is limited when dealing with large graphs.
Working on only subset of graphs may introduce potential biases or noises in the results.
The existing body of literature on counterfactual reasoning with GNNs has not adequately addressed this aspect [feasibility], a gap we address in our benchmarking study.

title: Feature Averaging: An Implicit Bias of Gradient Descent Leading to Non-Robustness in Neural Networks
name: feature-averaging-bias
short_hypothesis: Neural networks trained with gradient descent exhibit an implicit bias called "Feature Averaging," where they learn to rely on an average of discriminative features rather than individual ones, leading to non-robustness. This vulnerability can be mitigated by training with more granular, feature-level supervision.
related_work: Prior work has studied the implicit bias of gradient descent, often linking it to good generalization through properties like margin maximization or connections to Neural Tangent Kernels (NTK). More recent studies have shown this bias can be a "double-edged sword," leading to non-robust solutions even when robust ones exist. These works conjectured that weights converge to an average of feature centers. This paper aims to provide a rigorous, finite-time theoretical analysis of this phenomenon, termed "feature averaging," as a more intuitive explanation for non-robustness, and to propose and prove that fine-grained supervision can overcome this bias to learn robust features.
abstract: In this work, we investigate a particular implicit bias in gradient descent training, which we term “Feature Averaging,” and argue that it is one of the principal factors contributing to the non-robustness of deep neural networks. We show that, even when multiple discriminative features are present in the input data, neural networks trained by gradient descent tend to rely on an average of these features for classification, rather than distinguishing and leveraging each feature individually. Specifically, we provide a detailed theoretical analysis of the training dynamics of two-layer ReLU networks on a binary classification task where the data distribution consists of multiple clusters with mutually orthogonal centers. We plan to prove that gradient descent biases the network towards feature averaging, making it vulnerable to certain input perturbations. On the positive side, we will demonstrate that this vulnerability can be mitigated through more granular supervision. We propose to prove that a two-layer ReLU network can achieve optimal robustness when trained to classify individual features rather than merely the original binary classes.

experiments.Description: To validate the theoretical claims that standard gradient descent leads to feature averaging, while fine-grained supervision leads to feature decoupling.
experiments.Method: Two settings will be compared: training with 2-class labels versus 10-class labels. This will be tested on a synthetic multi-cluster dataset (k=10 clusters) as described in the theory, and on CIFAR-10. For CIFAR-10, a ResNet18 model pre-trained on CIFAR-10 will be used as a feature extractor, and a two-layer ReLU network will be trained on the penultimate layer's representations. The 2-class task on CIFAR-10 is created by merging the first five classes into one and the last five into another.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cosine similarity between the learned weight vectors of the neurons and the feature vectors (cluster centers), visualized as a correlation matrix.']

experiments.Description: To verify the hypothesis that models trained with fine-grained supervision (10-class labels) are more adversarially robust than models trained with only coarse supervision (2-class labels).
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on three datasets: a synthetic dataset, MNIST (parity classification), and CIFAR-10 (binary classification as described previously). For each dataset, two models will be trained: one with 2-class labels and another with 10-class labels. For MNIST and CIFAR-10, ResNet18 models will be trained from scratch. The output of the 10-class model will be converted to a binary prediction by summing the relevant logits to ensure a fair comparison.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Robust test accuracy under l2 PGD attacks with varying perturbation radii.']

risk_factors: We make specific choices of hyper-parameters for the sake of calculations, and we emphasize that these may not be the tightest possible choices.
For simplicity, our work focuses on the setting with clusters exactly orthogonal to each other.
Our analysis of the GD dynamics requires small initialization, whereas their results depend on starting from a solution that already correctly classifies the training set.
In their setting, the second layer of the network is also trainable, but we fix the second layer for simplicity.

title: Parameterized Hardness of Zonotope Containment and Neural Network Verification
name: parameterized-hardness-relu-verification
short_hypothesis: Several fundamental problems in neural network verification, including positivity, surjectivity, maximum approximation, and Lipschitz constant computation for shallow ReLU networks, as well as the related zonotope containment problem, are W[1]-hard when parameterized by the input dimension `d`. This implies they are not fixed-parameter tractable and that existing brute-force enumeration algorithms are essentially optimal under the Exponential Time Hypothesis.
related_work: Network verification, Lipschitz constant estimation, and zonotope containment are known to be (co)NP-hard, with intractability linked to the input dimension `d`. This raises the question of whether these problems become tractable for low-dimensional input spaces, i.e., if they are fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) with respect to `d`. While some related problems like injectivity checking are known to be FPT, the parameterized complexity status of network verification (specifically positivity) and Lipschitz constant computation was posed as an open problem. This work aims to resolve these open questions.
abstract: Neural networks with ReLU activations are a widely used model in machine learning, making it important to have a profound understanding of the properties of the functions they compute. Recently, there has been increasing interest in the (parameterized) computational complexity of determining these properties. In this work, we plan to close several gaps and resolve an open problem regarding the parameterized complexity of various problems related to network verification. In particular, we will prove that deciding positivity (and thus surjectivity) of a function f: R^d -> R computed by a 2-layer ReLU network is W[1]-hard when parameterized by d. This result would also imply that zonotope (non-)containment is W[1]-hard with respect to d. Moreover, we intend to show that (a) approximating the maximum within any multiplicative factor in 2-layer ReLU networks, (b) computing the Lp-Lipschitz constant for p in (0, infinity] in 2-layer networks, and (c) approximating the Lp-Lipschitz constant in 3-layer networks are all NP-hard and W[1]-hard with respect to d.

experiments.Description: To determine the parameterized complexity of network verification problems, specifically positivity and surjectivity for 2-layer ReLU networks.
experiments.Method: The proposed method is a parameterized reduction from the MULTICOLORED CLIQUE problem. For a given graph G, a 2-layer ReLU network f: R^k -> R will be constructed in polynomial time. The network's input dimension `k` will be tied to the clique size parameter of the MULTICOLORED CLIQUE instance. The construction is designed such that the network has a positive output (or is surjective) if and only if the graph G contains a k-colored clique.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['W[1]-hardness with respect to input dimension d', 'Running time lower bound under the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH)']

experiments.Description: To determine the parameterized complexity of the Zonotope Containment problem.
experiments.Method: This investigation will leverage the established duality between 2-layer ReLU networks and zonotopes. The hardness result for 2-LAYER RELU POSITIVITY is hypothesized to be directly transferable to ZONOTOPE NON-CONTAINMENT, proving its hardness without a new reduction.
