experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['W[1]-hardness with respect to dimension d', 'Running time lower bound under the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH)']

experiments.Description: To determine the parameterized complexity of computing or approximating the Lp-Lipschitz constant for shallow ReLU networks.
experiments.Method: The core reduction from MULTICOLORED CLIQUE will be adapted. For 2-layer networks, the constructed function will be modified to show that computing its Lp-Lipschitz constant for p in (0, infinity] is hard. For 3-layer networks, an additional hidden layer will be added to the 2-layer construction to demonstrate that even approximating the Lp-Lipschitz constant is hard.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['NP-hardness', 'W[1]-hardness with respect to input dimension d', 'Running time lower bound under the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the complexity of deciding if a 3-layer ReLU network computes a non-zero function.
experiments.Method: An additional hidden layer with a single ReLU neuron will be added to the network constructed for the 2-LAYER RELU POSITIVITY problem. The goal is to show that this new 3-layer network has a non-zero output if and only if the original 2-layer network had a positive output, thereby transferring the hardness result.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['W[1]-hardness with respect to input dimension d', 'Running time lower bound under the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH)']

risk_factors: Our paper is clearly of purely theoretical nature.
Hence, our results are naturally worst-case results.

Although the algorithms we give are essentially optimal in terms of running time (assuming the ETH), it might be possible to achieve a better running time by reducing the constant hidden in the exponent.
Moreover, additional assumptions on the network structure might render the problems tractable (as in the case of input convex networks for the L1-Lipschitz constant).
A full literature review (e.g., for the broad field of network verification) is beyond the scope of this paper.

title: Thompson Sampling via Fine-Tuning of LLMs
name: TOSFIT-LLM-Thompson-Sampling
short_hypothesis: A scalable alternative to Bayesian optimization can be developed based on Thompson sampling, which eliminates the need for acquisition function maximization. This approach, Thompson Sampling via Fine-Tuning (TOSFIT), directly parameterizes the probability of maximality (PoM) with a prompt-conditioned large language model. By leveraging the prior knowledge embedded in the LLM and incrementally adapting it toward the posterior using the Variational Bayesian Optimistic Sampling (VBOS) objective, this method can efficiently perform optimization in large, unstructured discrete spaces.
related_work: Bayesian optimization in large, unstructured discrete spaces is computationally challenging because maximizing the acquisition function is intractable without gradients. Standard Thompson sampling, which requires finding the maximum of a function drawn from the posterior, faces this same issue. While methods exist to make acquisition function maximization tractable in high-dimensional Euclidean spaces, they are not applicable to unstructured discrete domains. This work proposes to circumvent acquisition function maximization entirely by parameterizing the sampling policy (the probability of maximality) with a generative LLM. This builds on Variational Bayesian Optimistic Sampling (VBOS), but instead of optimizing from a uniform policy, it initializes with a strong prior from a pre-trained LLM and gradually adapts it to the posterior.
abstract: Bayesian optimization in large unstructured discrete spaces is often hindered by the computational cost of maximizing acquisition functions due to the absence of gradients. We propose a scalable alternative based on Thompson sampling that eliminates the need for acquisition function maximization by directly parameterizing the probability that a candidate yields the maximum reward. Our approach, Thompson Sampling via Fine-Tuning (TOSFIT), leverages the prior knowledge embedded in prompt-conditioned large language models, and incrementally adapts them toward the posterior.

experiments.Description: To test the algorithm's ability to optimize text based on semantic alignment in a natural language task.
experiments.Method: The task is FAQ Response Refinement, where a Qwen3-1.7B/Qwen3-8B model is prompted to write an FAQ response. The reward is the alignment to an unknown ground-truth response, judged by a Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B model. The method will be compared against baselines including UNGUIDED GENERATION, POST-GENERATION TS, ACTOR CRITIC, SOFT ACTOR CRITIC, EVOLUTIONARY SEARCH (LLM and Character), and FIBO.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['best-seen reward (maximum reward observed up to a given step)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method on the challenge of designing thermally stable proteins, a task with a vast discrete search space of amino acid sequences.
experiments.Method: The task is Protein Search, where the goal is to identify amino acid sequences with high thermal stability. Sequences are generated using ProtGPT2. The reward is the negative thermal instability index. A Gaussian Process with a linear kernel over mean token embeddings is used as the reward model. The method will be compared against baselines including UNGUIDED GENERATION, POST-GENERATION TS, ACTOR CRITIC, SOFT ACTOR CRITIC, EVOLUTIONARY SEARCH (LLM and Character), and FIBO.

experiments.Description: To assess the method's performance in designing quantum circuits that prepare low-energy quantum states, navigating a discrete space of valid quantum programs.
experiments.Method: The task is Quantum Circuit Design, where a Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B model is used to generate Qiskit circuits. The reward is the negative energy of the prepared state under an unknown Hamiltonian. A Gaussian Process with a linear kernel over features including a code validity bit and two-qubit Pauli observables is used. The method will be compared against baselines including UNGUIDED GENERATION, POST-GENERATION TS, ACTOR CRITIC, SOFT ACTOR CRITIC, EVOLUTIONARY SEARCH (LLM and Character), and FIBO.

risk_factors: To ensure controlled comparisons, we evaluate TOSFIT under fixed feature maps—either derived from pre-trained embeddings or manually designed.

A promising direction for future work is to learn deep, task-adaptive embeddings jointly with the Gaussian process, as in Ranković & Schwaller (2025), or replace the GP with more expressive reward models such as Bayesian neural networks or ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017).
Moreover, to reduce the computational and memory overhead introduced by fine-tuning, one could restrict updates to the last few layers of the generator.
Finally, alternative strategies for optimizing the VBOS objective V(π)—such as in-context conditioning—may offer a lightweight alternative to weight adaptation.

title: ExpeAttack: Efficient and Diverse LLM Jailbreaking via Two-Stage Exploration with Experience Replay
name: expe-attack
short_hypothesis: A dynamic, two-stage framework, ExpeAttack, that leverages experience replay can enhance the efficiency and diversity of LLM jailbreaking prompts. The method first generates diverse seed prompts using a 'Pattern Factory' and then iteratively refines them using short- and long-term memory pools with an insight-based memory compression mechanism to distill successful attack patterns into transferable meta-instructions.
related_work: Current automated jailbreaking methods are categorized as white-box or black-box. White-box attacks (e.g., GCG) require model parameters, leading to high computational overhead and limited interpretability. Black-box methods (e.g., GPTFuzzer, Autodan, PAIR, TAP) do not require parameter access but have their own shortcomings. They often rely heavily on static, human-written templates, which limits diversity. Their prompt refinement processes can be inefficient, using randomized search or only reflecting on the current iteration's results without leveraging historical successes. Furthermore, most methods do not consider resource consumption (API costs, token length). This creates a need for a more efficient, diverse, and economically viable black-box jailbreaking method that learns from past experiences.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are susceptible to jailbreaking attacks, where adversarial prompts bypass safety mechanisms to generate harmful outputs. Traditional jailbreaking methods often rely on static templates or inefficient processes, resulting in limited diversity and effectiveness. This paper introduces ExpeAttack, a novel framework designed to enhance the efficiency and diversity of jailbreaking prompts through a dynamic, experience-driven approach. ExpeAttack operates in two stages: seed generation and iterative refinement. In the seed generation phase, a Pattern Factory is employed to create diverse initial prompts by integrating various attack strategies. The refinement phase utilizes a combination of short-term and long-term memory pools, along with an insight-based memory compression mechanism, to distill successful attack patterns into transferable meta-instructions. This process is intended to enable efficient and interpretable refinement of attack samples.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed jailbreaking method against state-of-the-art baselines on various open-source and closed-source LLMs.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on 100 harmful instructions sampled from the AdvBench dataset. The target models for the attack will include Vicuna-7B, Llama-2-7B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4.0, and Claude. The attacker model will be GPT-3.5 and the evaluator model will be GPT-4. The proposed method, ExpeAttack, will be compared against GCG (white-box), GPTFuzzer (black-box), and PAIR (black-box) baselines.

experiments.Description: To assess the transferability of jailbreak prompts generated by ExpeAttack across different target models.
experiments.Method: Jailbreak prompts will be generated using ExpeAttack on source models: Vicuna-7B, GPT-3.5, and GPT-4.0. The effectiveness of these generated prompts will then be tested against a set of target models: Vicuna-7B, Llama-7B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4.0, and Claude.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the diversity of generated jailbreak prompts using a novel fine-tuning-based protocol, comparing against a baseline method.
experiments.Method: Generated prompts from ExpeAttack and the PAIR baseline will each be split into a fine-tuning subset and an evaluation subset. A target model will be fine-tuned on the fine-tuning subset from each method. The robustness of each fine-tuned model will then be evaluated against the evaluation subsets from both methods, as well as a human-written evaluation set.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Attack Success Rate (ASR) on the evaluation sets after fine-tuning']

risk_factors: Future work will focus on improving the robustness and adaptability of our framework to emerging LLMs and enhancing the generalization of attack patterns.

title: High variance score function estimates help diffusion models generalize
name: dsm-variance-generalization
short_hypothesis: The generalization ability of diffusion models arises in part from the denoising score matching (DSM) objective, whose optimization typically produces high variance estimates of the score function, especially at early diffusion times. This high variance is not a bug, but a feature that aids generalization.
related_work: It is unclear how diffusion-based generative models generalize. Existing explanations are insufficient: noise in the sampling process and numerical integration inaccuracies improve sample quality but are not the primary drivers of generalization. Perfectly optimizing the score matching objective on a finite dataset would only reproduce training examples. While neural network inductive biases play a role, they are unlikely to be the sole cause. This paper investigates an alternative source of generalization: the inherent high variance of score estimates from the DSM objective.
abstract: How do diffusion-based generative models generalize beyond their training set? In particular, do they perform something similar to kernel density estimation? If so, what is the kernel, and which aspects of training and sampling determine its form? We argue that a key contributor to generalization is the fact that the denoising score matching objective usually used to train diffusion models tends to obtain high variance score function estimates at early times. We investigate this claim by mathematically studying score estimation for (unconditional) diffusion models using estimators that are linear in a set of feature maps. The goal is to show that this high variance is mathematically equivalent to adding a noise term to the probability flow ODE, and that in some cases, reverse diffusion is equivalent to convolving the training distribution with a data-dependent kernel function.

experiments.Description: To mathematically analyze the effect of training a linear score estimator with the Denoising Score Matching (DSM) objective in an asymptotic limit. The goal is to derive the form of the 'typically' learned distribution and contrast it with training using a naive score matching objective.
experiments.Method: The analysis considers a score estimator that is linear in a set of feature maps. Its parameters are optimized according to the DSM objective using N independent samples. The reverse diffusion process is modeled by Euler-integrating the probability flow ODE. The analysis is performed in the limit where N → ∞ and the time step Δt → 0, with NΔt held constant. This setup is contrasted with two alternative scenarios: 1) optimizing parameters using the naive score matching objective, and 2) using a time sampling distribution that does not undersample small times.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Derivation of the backward-time SDE for the expected learned distribution E[q(x0|xT, θ)]', "Derivation of the covariance of the emergent noise term (the 'V kernel')"]

experiments.Description: To investigate whether the generalization effect from high-variance score estimates applies to neural networks trained with gradient descent in the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) regime.
experiments.Method: The analysis considers a score estimator parameterized by a gradient-descent-trained neural network in the NTK regime. This setup is framed as a special case of the linear estimator analyzed in the main theorem, where the feature maps are related to the gradient of the network output with respect to its parameters.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Verification that the conclusions of the main theorem (asymptotic generalization via an added noise term) hold for this model class.']

experiments.Description: To analyze the form of the learned distribution when the training data is an empirical distribution (a mixture of M delta functions centered on the training data points).
experiments.Method: The theoretical framework is applied to a training distribution p(x0) defined as a mixture of M delta functions. The analysis will derive the resulting expected distribution E[q(x0|θ)] and the specific form of the V kernel for this data distribution.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Analytical form of the expected learned distribution E[q(x0|θ)]', 'Analytical form of the V kernel, V(y)']

experiments.Description: To build intuition about the properties of the derived 'V kernel' by examining its behavior in simplified, analytically tractable settings.
experiments.Method: The V kernel's effect is analyzed in three special cases: (1) when the score is estimated using linear features, (2) when the estimator uses features that are orthogonal with respect to the data, and (3) for a problem with features defined for a Gaussian mixture model, analogous to the Fisher information matrix.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Effect on the mean and variance of the learned distribution', 'Form of the characteristic function of the learned distribution', "Relationship between the kernel's noise injection and feature properties (e.g., feature variance, data density)"]

risk_factors: Our calculation assumes an estimator that is linear in its features
we only consider unconditional models
no attention mechanism is included
and we do not consider learning dynamics

title: GILT: An LLM-Free, Tuning-Free Graph Foundational Model for In-Context Learning
name: gilt-in-context-learning-for-graphs
short_hypothesis: A framework called Graph In-context Learning Transformer (GILT) can reframe few-shot graph tasks, including node, edge, and graph classification, into a unified, token-based in-context learning problem. This approach, which uses a Transformer backbone, is designed to be LLM-free and tuning-free, enabling it to handle heterogeneous graphs with generic numerical features and adapt to new tasks dynamically from context without requiring parameter updates.
related_work: Current Graph Foundational Models (GFMs) struggle with the heterogeneity of graph data. One paradigm leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to unify data in a semantic space, but this makes them dependent on textual features and unsuitable for graphs with numerical or structural data. A second paradigm pre-trains a structure-based model and adapts it using per-graph tuning (e.g., graph prompting), but this creates a significant efficiency bottleneck. While some pioneering works have explored tuning-free adaptation (ICL), there is a need for a more general and powerful framework. GILT aims to bridge this gap by being both LLM-free and tuning-free, operating directly on numerical features via in-context learning.
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are powerful tools for precessing relational data but often struggle to generalize to unseen graphs, giving rise to the development of Graph Foundational Models (GFMs). However, current GFMs are challenged by the extreme heterogeneity of graph data, where each graph can possess a unique feature space, label set, and topology. To address this, two main paradigms have emerged. The first leverages Large Language Models (LLMs), but is fundamentally text-dependent, thus struggles to handle the numerical features in vast graphs. The second pre-trains a structure-based model, but the adaptation to new tasks typically requires a costly, per-graph tuning stage, creating a critical efficiency bottleneck. In this work, we move beyond these limitations and introduce Graph In-context Learning Transformer (GILT), a framework built on an LLM-free and tuning-free architecture. GILT introduces a novel token-based framework for in-context learning (ICL) on graphs, reframing classification tasks spanning node, edge and graph levels in a unified framework. This mechanism is the key to handling heterogeneity, as it is designed to operate on generic numerical features. Further, its ability to understand class semantics dynamically from the context enables tuning-free adaptation.

experiments.Description: To assess the few-shot performance of GILT on the task of node classification against contemporary GFMs and fully supervised models.
experiments.Method: GILT will be evaluated in an N-way, K-shot protocol on the Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, and WikiCS datasets. It will be compared against few-shot competitors (GCOPE, RiemannGFM, OFA, GraphAny), LLM-based zero-shot baselines (GraphGPT, LLaga, ZeroG, UniGraph, GOFA), and fully supervised reference standards (GCN, GAT).

experiments.Description: To assess the few-shot performance of GILT on the task of link prediction against standard supervised models and specialized methods.
experiments.Method: GILT will be evaluated in a strict 5-shot setting on the Planetoid datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and the ogbl-collab benchmark. Baselines will include standard fully supervised models (GCN, GraphSAGE), specialized subgraph methods (SEAL, MaskGAE), and the TEA-GLM framework.

experiments.Description: To assess the few-shot performance of GILT on the task of graph classification against other few-shot GFM baselines.
experiments.Method: GILT will be evaluated in a 5-shot setting on the OGB benchmarks ogbg-molhiv and ogbg-molpcba. Baselines will include OFA and GFT.

experiments.Description: To analyze the inference efficiency of GILT's tuning-free paradigm compared to tuning-based and generative LLM models.
experiments.Method: The total inference time for a single task on the Cora dataset will be measured for GILT and compared against Tuning-Free (GraphAny), Tuning-Based (RiemannGFM, GCOPE), and Generative LLM (OFA, ZeroG, GOFA) models.

risk_factors: While our current PCA-based approach is robust, a learnable dimension unifier could offer greater expressive power.
Further avenues include scaling the model with larger pre-training corpora and investigating advanced objectives like contrastive learning to enhance representation quality.

title: Local Control Networks (LCNs): Optimizing Flexibility in Neural Network Data Pattern Capture
name: local-control-networks
short_hypothesis: Employing a uniform, fixed activation function across all nodes in a neural network is suboptimal. Using distinct, learnable activation functions at each node, specifically by leveraging B-spline functions, will increase the network's flexibility, adaptability, and representational capacity, enabling it to model complex data patterns more effectively.
related_work: Traditional Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLPs) use a fixed activation function (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) for all neurons, which can limit flexibility and lead to issues like 'dead neurons' or vanishing gradients. More recent architectures like Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have introduced learnable activation functions on the edges of the network, offering increased flexibility but facing limitations in application and complexity. The proposed Local Control Networks (LCNs) aim to bridge this gap by enabling each neuron to have its own unique, learnable B-spline activation function within a standard network structure, offering a higher degree of flexibility and local adaptability without the architectural complexity of KANs.
abstract: The widespread use of multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) often relies on a fixed activation function (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) for all nodes within the hidden layers. While effective in many scenarios, this uniformity may limit the network's ability to capture complex data patterns. We argue that employing the same activation function at every node is suboptimal and propose leveraging different activation functions at each node to increase flexibility and adaptability. To achieve this, we introduce Local Control Networks (LCNs), which leverage B-spline functions to enable distinct activation curves at each node.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed LCN model on a variety of basic machine learning tasks and compare it against standard baselines.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Bank Marketing, Bean Classification, Spam Detection, and Telescope datasets. The proposed LCN model will be compared against conventional Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) with fixed activations and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs). To ensure a fair comparison, the total number of parameters will be standardized across all models, and LCN and KAN models will undergo hyperparameter tuning via grid search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scalability of LCNs on computer vision tasks involving high-dimensional input spaces.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST (FMNIST) datasets for image classification. The LCN model's performance will be compared to that of MLPs and KANs. Model configurations will be standardized by adjusting layers and neurons to have a comparable number of total parameters, with hyperparameters for LCN and KAN being tuned via grid search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of LCNs on symbolic regression tasks, which involve approximating mathematical functions.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on synthetic datasets designed for symbolic regression. The LCN model will be benchmarked against MLPs and KANs. The total number of parameters will be standardized across models for a fair comparison.

risk_factors: The marginal improvements of LCNs over MLPs and KANs reflect the simplicity of the datasets used, which follow the standards in (Yu et al., 2024).
On these basic tasks, MLPs already achieve strong performance, leaving limited room for improvement for LCNs and KANs.
However, the flexibility and localized activations of LCNs are expected to show greater advantages on more complex datasets with higher-dimensional inputs or intricate patterns.

title: Automating High-Quality Concept Banks: Leveraging LLMs and Multimodal Evaluation Metrics
name: automated-concept-bank-generation-and-evaluation
short_hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs) can be leveraged to automatically generate high-quality concept banks for concept bottleneck models, eliminating the need for manual annotation. Furthermore, a proposed training-free, multimodal evaluation metric, which exploits the pretrained knowledge of models like CLIP, can effectively quantify the quality of these generated concepts, and will be more effective than unimodal text-based similarity metrics.
related_work: Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) improve interpretability but traditionally rely on manual concept annotation, which is resource-intensive, subjective, and not scalable. Attempts to automate concept generation have included learning concepts from data, bootstrapping from a small seed set, or using LLMs. However, evaluating the quality of these generated concepts typically requires running a full end-to-end CBM pipeline, which is computationally expensive and provides only an indirect measure of concept quality. There is a need for an unsupervised, efficient technique to directly generate and evaluate concept banks at an early stage, before they are fed into a CBM.
abstract: Interpretability in recent deep learning models has become an epicenter of research. Concept bottleneck models have emerged as a promising approach for achieving transparency by leveraging a set of human-understandable concepts as an intermediate representation. However, manual concept annotation is discouraged due to the time and effort involved. Our work explores the potential of large language models (LLMs) for generating high-quality concept banks and proposes a multimodal evaluation metric to assess the quality of generated concepts. We investigate three key research questions: the ability of LLMs to generate concept banks comparable to existing knowledge bases like ConceptNet, the sufficiency of unimodal text-based semantic similarity for evaluating concept-class label associations, and the effectiveness of multimodal information in quantifying concept generation quality. Being able to automatically generate and evaluate high-quality concepts will enable researchers to quickly adapt and iterate to a newer dataset with little to no effort before they can feed that into concept bottleneck models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of a unimodal text-based similarity model for assessing the association between generated concepts and class labels (RQ2).
experiments.Method: Three sets of concepts will be generated for the CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and CUB-200 datasets: 1) LLM-generated concepts using LLaMa3-70B and Qwen2-72B, 2) ConceptNet-based concepts, and 3) Randomly generated concepts. The semantic similarity between these concepts and the ground-truth class labels will be computed using BERTScore.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy based on matching the top-k concepts against the ground truth class labels.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate a proposed concept-driven class label prediction scheme that relies on multimodal features to assess concept quality, addressing whether LLMs can generate superior concept banks (RQ1) and whether multimodal information is more effective for evaluation than unimodal methods (RQ3).
experiments.Method: For the CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and CUB-200 datasets, images and the three sets of generated concepts (LLM-generated, ConceptNet-based, Random) will be mapped to a shared embedding space using OpenAI's CLIP model (ViT-B/32). Class labels will be predicted based on the computed image-concept similarity scores. The performance of the LLM-generated concepts will be compared against the other two concept sets and a baseline method, CMS (Semenov et al., 2024).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Class label prediction accuracy based on top-k concepts.']

risk_factors: The proposed LLM-based concept generation approach may not outperform knowledge-base methods like ConceptNet on all datasets, particularly for complex datasets requiring specific domain knowledge.
The effectiveness of this approach may be limited by the quality and relevance of the utilized knowledge graphs.
It necessitates the creation of a predefined library of initial concepts, which may involve concept pruning, requiring human intervention via annotation.
This approach relies on preexisting knowledge graphs, such as ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), to identify relationships between classes and related concepts.

title: Scalable Option Learning in High-Throughput Environments
name: scalable-option-learning
short_hypothesis: By making specific system design choices—a single neural network with multiple heads, an environment wrapper to track policies, and parallelized masking for advantage computation—it is possible to create a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) algorithm, Scalable Option Learning (SOL), that can be scaled to train on billions of samples in high-throughput environments, overcoming the scalability limitations of existing HRL methods.
related_work: Existing hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) methods, such as those based on options, feudal RL, and manager-worker architectures, have shown promise but have been limited to relatively small data regimes (millions of samples). In contrast, flat RL agents are routinely trained on billions of samples. This discrepancy is due to several challenges that prevent the straightforward scaling of hierarchical agents, such as the difficulty in parallelizing computations for sequences of different policies within a batch of trajectories. This work aims to bridge this gap by developing an HRL algorithm designed for large-scale, high-throughput training.
abstract: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (RL) has the potential to enable effective decision-making over long timescales. Existing approaches, while promising, have yet to realize the benefits of large-scale training. In this work, we identify and solve several key challenges in scaling online hierarchical RL to high-throughput environments. We propose Scalable Option Learning (SOL), a highly scalable hierarchical RL algorithm. To demonstrate SOL's performance and scalability, we plan to train hierarchical agents using 30 billion frames of experience on the complex game of NetHack. We will also validate SOL on MiniHack and Mujoco environments, showcasing its general applicability.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SOL on complex, long-horizon tasks that require difficult credit assignment and coordination of different behaviors, compared to flat and other hierarchical baselines.
experiments.Method: The experiments will be conducted on the NetHackScore environment, and two custom MiniHack environments: ZombieHorde and TreasureDash. The proposed method, SOL, will be compared against several baselines: a flat asynchronous PPO agent (APPO) trained with only task reward, a flat APPO agent trained with a combination of task and option rewards, a scalable implementation of HiPPO (SOL-HiPPO), and in the NetHack experiments, the Motif baseline which uses an LLM to synthesize rewards.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Game score on NetHackScore and TreasureDash', 'Kill counts on ZombieHorde']

experiments.Description: To test the generality of the SOL algorithm on continuous control tasks, particularly in a challenging maze with a local optimum that is difficult to escape.
experiments.Method: The experiments will use the Gymnasium Robotics PointMaze environments, specifically the U-Maze and a custom-designed G-maze. The proposed SOL agent will be compared against SOL-HiPPO, APPO (with and without option rewards), and Multiple Option Critic (MOC).

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that SOL is compatible with methods for automatic reward synthesis and can be used to experiment with them at scale, without requiring hand-coded intrinsic rewards.
experiments.Method: A variant of the proposed method, SOL+DIAYN, will be evaluated on the MiniHack ZombieHorde and TreasureDash environments. In this variant, the option rewards are learned using the DIAYN skill discovery method. This will be compared against the standard SOL with hardcoded rewards and a flat APPO baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Kill counts on ZombieHorde', 'Game score on TreasureDash']

risk_factors: while we provide evidence that SOL is compatible with both hardcoded and learned option rewards, the question of how best to learn option rewards remains an open area of research, and more work is needed to find truly general methods which are successful across a wide range of environments.

our system is designed for computational efficiency, not sample efficiency. It focuses on achieving superior asymptotic performance in the large sample regime, rather than making optimal use of limited samples. Therefore, it is currently limited to settings where samples are easy to gather and compute is the bottleneck, such as video games, digital agents or sim-to-real transfer.

title: HyperHuman: Hyper-Realistic Human Generation with Latent Structural Diffusion
name: latent-structural-diffusion-for-humans
short_hypothesis: Simultaneously denoising RGB images, depth maps, and surface-normal maps within a unified latent diffusion model, conditioned on text and pose, will enable the generation of more coherent and hyper-realistic human images by explicitly capturing the multi-level structural correlations between appearance and geometry.
related_work: Existing large-scale text-to-image models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E 2 struggle to generate humans with coherent anatomy and natural poses because they lack inherent structural awareness. Recent controllable methods like ControlNet and T2I-Adapter introduce structural guidance but can suffer from inconsistencies between the control signal and the generated image. Other specialized models like HumanSD are confined to specific artistic styles. The research gap lies in the lack of a model that jointly captures the multi-level correlations between human appearance (RGB) and different types of structural information (pose, depth, surface-normal) in a unified framework.
abstract: Despite significant advances in large-scale text-to-image models, achieving hyper-realistic human image generation remains a desirable yet unsolved task. Existing models like Stable Diffusion and DALL·E 2 tend to generate human images with incoherent parts or unnatural poses. To tackle these challenges, our key insight is that human image is inherently structural over multiple granularities, from the coarse-level body skeleton to the fine-grained spatial geometry. Therefore, capturing such correlations between the explicit appearance and latent structure in one model is essential to generate coherent and natural human images. To this end, we propose a unified framework, HyperHuman, that generates in-the-wild human images of high realism and diverse layouts. Specifically, 1) we first build a large-scale human-centric dataset, named HumanVerse, which consists of 340M images with comprehensive annotations like human pose, depth, and surface normal. 2) Next, we propose a Latent Structural Diffusion Model that simultaneously denoises the depth and surface normal along with the synthesized RGB image. Our model enforces the joint learning of image appearance, spatial relationship, and geometry in a unified network. 3) Finally, to further boost the visual quality, we propose a Structure-Guided Refiner to compose the predicted conditions for more detailed generation of higher resolution.

experiments.Description: To quantitatively evaluate the proposed model in a zero-shot setting against state-of-the-art general and controllable text-to-image models on a human-centric benchmark.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on the newly curated HumanVerse dataset and evaluated on a human subset of the MS-COCO 2014 validation set. The evaluation will use the RGB output of the first-stage Latent Structural Diffusion Model. Baselines include general T2I models (SD 1.5, SD 2.0, SD 2.1, SDXL, DeepFloyd-IF) and controllable methods (ControlNet, T2I-Adapter, HumanSD).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Image Quality (FID, KID, FID_CLIP)', 'Text-Image Alignment (CLIP similarity)', 'Pose Accuracy (Average Precision - AP, Average Recall - AR, AP_clean, AR_clean)']

experiments.Description: To qualitatively compare the high-resolution generation capabilities of the full two-stage model and to conduct a user preference study against baselines.
experiments.Method: The full HyperHuman model, including the Structure-Guided Refiner, will be used to generate high-resolution (1024x1024) images. These will be visually compared to outputs from baselines (SD 2.1, SDXL, IF, ControlNet, T2I-Adapter, HumanSD) using their officially provided best configurations. A side-by-side user study will be conducted to gauge user preference.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of realism, quality, diversity, and controllability', 'User preference ratio']

experiments.Description: To perform ablation studies on the key components of the Latent Structural Diffusion Model and the Structure-Guided Refiner to validate their contributions.
experiments.Method: Ablations will be performed on the first-stage model to test the impact of: 1) Denoising only RGB vs. jointly denoising RGB+depth and/or RGB+normal. 2) The number of replicated layers in the structural expert branches. 3) The proposed noise schedule vs. a default schedule and different timesteps. Further ablations on the second-stage refiner will test the impact of each structural condition (pose, depth, normal) and the random dropout scheme.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['FID', 'FID_CLIP', 'CLIP score', 'L2-error of depth (Ld) and normal (Ln) predictions']

risk_factors: However, due to the limited performance of existing pose/depth/normal estimators for in-the-wild humans, we find it sometimes fails to generate subtle details like finger and eyes.
Besides, the current pipeline still requires body skeleton as input, where deep priors like LLMs can be explored to achieve text-to-pose generation in future work.

title: EasyTune: Efficient Step-Aware Fine-Tuning for Diffusion-Based Motion Generation
name: easytune-step-aware-finetuning
short_hypothesis: Fine-tuning a diffusion model at each individual denoising step, rather than over the entire trajectory, will decouple the recursive gradient dependence, enabling more memory-efficient, dense, and fine-grained optimization. Furthermore, a self-refinement preference learning mechanism can dynamically construct preference pairs from a retrieval model's failures to train a high-quality reward model without human annotation.
related_work: Existing differentiable reward methods for aligning diffusion models (e.g., DRaFT, DRTune) suffer from inefficiency, high memory usage, and coarse-grained optimization. This is attributed to backpropagating gradients through the entire multi-step denoising trajectory, which creates a recursive dependence that causes vanishing gradients for early steps and requires storing a large computation graph. Additionally, methods for training reward models are hampered by the scarcity of high-quality, large-scale preference data for motion generation.
abstract: Aligning motion generative models with downstream objectives is challenging. While using differentiable rewards to align diffusion models is promising, existing methods suffer from inefficient, coarse-grained optimization and high memory consumption, which is caused by the recursive dependence between steps in the denoising trajectory. This work proposes EasyTune, a method to fine-tune diffusion models at each denoising step rather than over the entire trajectory, decoupling the recursive dependence for more efficient optimization. To address the scarcity of preference data for reward model training, a Self-refinement Preference Learning (SPL) mechanism is also introduced to dynamically identify preference pairs for learning.

experiments.Description: To assess the fine-tuning effectiveness and efficiency of EasyTune compared to state-of-the-art differentiable reward-based methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the HumanML3D dataset. The MLD model will be fine-tuned using EasyTune and benchmarked against baselines including DRaFT, AlignProp, and DRTune.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['R-Precision@k', 'Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Multi-Modal Distance (MM Dist)', 'Diversity', 'peak memory usage']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of EasyTune when applied to base models for the end-to-end text-to-motion generation task, comparing against state-of-the-art generation models.
experiments.Method: The MLD and MLD++ models will be fine-tuned with EasyTune. Performance will be evaluated on the HumanML3D and KIT-ML datasets. Baselines will include a wide range of text-to-motion models such as TM2T, T2M, MDM, ReMoDiffuse, ParCo, and SoPo.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization capability of the EasyTune framework across various pre-trained text-to-motion diffusion models.
experiments.Method: EasyTune will be applied to a set of different pre-trained diffusion-based architectures, including MLD, MLD++, MLCM1S, and MDM50S, to assess its performance improvement on each.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Self-refinement Preference Learning (SPL) mechanism for training a reward model for the text-motion retrieval task.
experiments.Method: The SPL mechanism will be used to fine-tune a pre-trained retrieval model (ReAlign). Its performance will be evaluated on the HumanML3D and KIT-ML datasets and compared against the original ReAlign model and other state-of-the-art retrieval methods like LaMP and TMR.

risk_factors: In this work, we focus on improving semantic alignment in text-to-motion generation.
Due to limited preference data, SPL relies on retrieval-based mining, which can introduce noisy or ambiguous pairs and provides limited physical grounding in reward design.
Building a unified reward model that explicitly captures both physical plausibility and semantic alignment remains an important direction for future work.

title: Adaptive Compression of the Latent Space in Variational Autoencoders
name: adaptive-latent-compression-vae
short_hypothesis: A novel, automated approach, ALD-VAE (Adaptive Latent Dimensionality), can determine the optimal latent space size in VAEs during a single training process. This is achieved by starting with a high-dimensional latent space, gradually decreasing its size by removing neurons, and observing performance metrics (reconstruction loss, FID, Silhouette score) to find a stopping point. This method is hypothesized to approximate the optimal dimensionality, avoid manual tuning, and achieve performance comparable to a VAE trained on the optimal latent size from scratch.
related_work: Traditionally, the optimal latent space size for a VAE is determined through time-consuming and unreliable trial-and-error or grid search. While some works have focused on tuning hyperparameters 'on the fly', they have limitations. For instance, one method for adversarial auto-encoders requires an additional trainable mask layer and a discriminator network, which is not present in standard VAEs. Another approach uses a two-stage training process where a model with a large latent space is first trained and then a second VAE is finetuned, but this method does not focus on the clustering capability of the latent space. The proposed work aims to provide an adaptive method applicable to any VAE that finds the optimal dimensionality by observing both reconstruction and clustering capabilities during a single training, without requiring extra computational costs.
abstract: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) are powerful generative models, but one of their known challenges is sensitivity to hyperparameters, such as the latent space size. This paper presents a simple extension of VAEs for automatically determining the optimal latent space size during the training process. The proposed method involves gradually decreasing the latent size through neuron removal and observing the model performance.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed ALD-VAE approach in finding the optimal latent space dimensionality compared to a standard grid search.
experiments.Method: The proposed ALD-VAE model, which gradually compresses its latent space, will be compared against a baseline of standard VAE models trained with various different fixed latent dimensionalities. The comparison will be performed on four image datasets: SPRITES, MNIST, FashionMNIST, and EuroSAT. For each dataset, specific VAE architectures are used (e.g., 3D CNNs for SPRITES, fully-connected layers for MNIST/FashionMNIST, 2D CNNs for EuroSAT). The ALD-VAE models will be initialized with a high latent dimensionality (e.g., 100, 80, 64).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Reconstruction loss', 'Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for reconstructed images', 'Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for generated images', 'Silhouette score']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of the ALD-VAE method to different initial latent dimensionalities and random seeds.
experiments.Method: The ALD-VAE model will be trained multiple times on the MNIST, FashionMNIST, EuroSAT, and SPRITES datasets. Robustness to initialization will be tested by starting the training with different initial latent dimensionalities (nz = 100, 80, and 64). Robustness to stochasticity will be tested by training the model on 5 different seeds for each dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Final converged latent dimensionality (nz)', 'Comparison of the curves for Reconstruction loss, FID, and Silhouette score across different runs.']

risk_factors: The optimal latent dimensionality obtained with ALD-VAE sometimes differs across different seeds.

The experimental evaluation was performed on image datasets, allowing for the straightforward calculation of the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID). However, it is important to note that for datasets of other types, using FID might not be appropriate or may need to be replaced with an alternative metric more suitable for the specific data.

title: Thinking Forward and Backward: Effective Backward Planning with Large Language Models
name: backward-planning-llm
short_hypothesis: A backward planning algorithm for LLMs that first flips the problem and then plans forward in the flipped problem will avoid backward bias, generate more diverse plans, and exploit problem asymmetries to improve planning success rates.
related_work: Most prior work in LLM planning has used models to reason in a forward direction from an initial state to a goal. However, many planning problems have an inherent asymmetry where planning backward from the goal is significantly easier, but this direction has not been fully explored for LLMs, which exhibit systematic biases leading to poor performance in direct backward planning.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have exhibited remarkable reasoning and planning capabilities. Most prior work in this area has used LLMs to reason through steps from an initial to a goal state or criterion, thereby effectively reasoning in a forward direction. Nonetheless, many planning problems exhibit an inherent asymmetry such that planning backward from the goal is significantly easier. We take inspiration from this observation and demonstrate that this bias holds for LLM planning as well. However, our experiments also reveal systematic biases which lead to poor planning in the backward direction. With this knowledge, we propose a backward planning algorithm for LLMs that first flips the problem and then plans forward in the flipped problem. This helps avoid the backward bias, generate more diverse candidate plans, and exploit asymmetries between the forward and backward directions in planning problems. We propose that combining planning in both directions with self-verification will improve overall planning success.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed backward planning algorithm and the combination of planning in both forward and backward directions with self-verification.
experiments.Method: The method will be tested in three planning domains using Large Language Models. The performance will be compared to standard forward-only planning.

title: Learning from One and Only One Shot
name: learning-topological-similarity-via-distortable-canvas
short_hypothesis: By computationally modeling innate human priors about object distortion via a 'distortable canvas', it is possible to learn a transformation-based topological similarity metric. This metric, when used with a simple classifier like nearest-neighbor, will enable learning from very few examples (1-10) without any pre-training, unlike standard few-shot learning methods.
related_work: Modern machine learning models are data-hungry and complex. Few-shot learning (FSL) addresses data scarcity but relies on pre-training on large, relevant source datasets, which can be difficult to select and may lead to negative transfer. Other approaches build transformations into the model (e.g., optimal-transport maps, data augmentation), but these often encode specific domain knowledge (like rotation or scaling) rather than considering all possible transformations. The proposed research aims to bridge this gap by creating a model that learns a general, transformation-based similarity from first principles, requiring no pre-training at all.
abstract: Humans can generalize from only a few examples and from little pre-training on similar tasks. Yet, machine learning (ML) typically requires large data to learn or pre-learn to transfer. Inspired by nativism, we directly model basic human-innate priors in abstract visual tasks e.g., character/doodle recognition. This yields a white-box model that learns transformation-based topological similarity by mimicking how humans naturally “distort" an object when first seeing it. Using the simple nearest-neighbor classifier in this similarity space, our model is designed for character recognition using only 1–10 examples per class and no pre-training. This differs from few-shot learning (FSL) using significant pre-training. Further, by mimicking k-means but in a non-Euclidean space, our model enables unsupervised learning and can generate human-interpretable archetypes as cluster “centroids".

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's classification performance in a 'tiny-data' regime on the MNIST dataset, training on only the first N images per class.
experiments.Method: The learned Dc- / Dv-distance is used with a nearest-neighbor classifier (Dc- / Dv-nearest-neighbor). The model is trained on the first N images per class from the MNIST training set, where N ranges from 1 to 20. Baselines for comparison include neural-network-based models (TextCaps, CNN+dropout, Capsule+Siamese) and classical ML models (SVM, Nearest-Neighbor, Decision Tree, Random Forest).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test set accuracy versus the number of training examples per class (N)', 'The minimum number of training examples per class needed to reach a 90% accuracy threshold']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's classification performance in a 'tiny-data' regime on the more challenging EMNIST-letters dataset.
experiments.Method: The learned Dc- / Dv-distance is used with a nearest-neighbor classifier. The model is trained on the first N images per class from the EMNIST-letters training set, where N ranges from 1 to 20. Baselines for comparison include TextCaps, SVM, Nearest-Neighbor, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and a standard Neural Network.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test set accuracy versus the number of training examples per class (N)', 'The minimum number of training examples per class needed to reach a 75% accuracy threshold']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model on the Omniglot one-shot classification challenge, which involves learning from a single example per class without pre-training.
experiments.Method: The Dc-nearest-neighbor model is run on the Omniglot one-shot task. It uses one training image and one test image per character in each 20-way classification task. The model is run without using any background set for pre-training or any stroke-movement information. Baselines include BPL, Humans, RCN, Siamese ConvNet, Simple ConvNet, Prototypical Net, and VHE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Error rate on the 20-way one-shot classification tasks']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's ability to perform unsupervised clustering and generate human-interpretable archetypes for a given class of images.
experiments.Method: A k-means-style clustering algorithm is adapted to use the learned topological similarity space. This is applied to a set of images of the digit '7' from MNIST and a set of 'giraffe' doodles from the Quick, Draw! dataset. The elbow method will be used to select a suitable number of clusters (k).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Within-cluster sum of distances (WCSD) versus the number of clusters (k)', 'Qualitative inspection of the generated cluster centroids (archetypes) for interpretability']

risk_factors: This paper focuses on the first type, handling abstract images only, by modeling humans’ distortion-based intuition.

Although our model has shown dominant classification performance in the tiny-data regime of the presented benchmarks, its dominance diminishes when training size increases. This is due to 1-NN being 100% biased towards the “nearest neighbor” and hence fragile against noisy, erroneous, and ambiguous training examples.
Being sensitive to ambiguities and outliers, however, is not a deficiency of our distortable canvas model, but a property of nearest-neighbor.
A related issue due to lacking validation data is about picking a proper model configuration.
By contrast, our distortable canvas model requires little to tune, other than the (G, pc)-solution path.

title: LEGATO: Large-scale End-to-end Generalizable Approach to Typeset OMR
name: legato-end-to-end-omr
short_hypothesis: An end-to-end Optical Music Recognition (OMR) model, built upon a pretrained vision encoder and a transformer decoder trained to generate ABC notation, will be capable of recognizing full-page and multi-page typeset music scores and generalizing across various score types. This approach is enabled by training on a newly constructed large-scale dataset of rendered music scores paired with their ABC representations.
related_work: Prior successful end-to-end OMR systems, such as SMT++, have focused exclusively on specific formats like piano, monophonic music, or single-system scores. More generalizable solutions are needed to handle the diversity of inputs, such as complex layouts with multiple systems, staves, and voices, and to support various output formats. To our knowledge, an image-to-ABC OMR model has not yet been trained, and there is no large-scale pretrained model capable of recognizing multi-page typeset scores.
abstract: We propose Legato, a new end-to-end model for optical music recognition (OMR), a task of converting music score images to machine-readable documents. Legato is the first large-scale pretrained OMR model capable of recognizing full-page or multi-page typeset music scores and the first to generate documents in ABC notation, a concise, human-readable format for symbolic music. Bringing together a pretrained vision encoder with an ABC decoder trained on a dataset of more than 214K images, our model is designed to exhibit the strong ability to generalize across various typeset scores.

experiments.Description: To establish the in-domain performance of the Legato models on the output format they were trained to produce.
experiments.Method: The Legato and Legatosmall models are evaluated on the test split of the PDMX-Synth dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['character error rate (CER)', 'symbol error rate (SER)', 'line error rate (LER)']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of Legato on unseen string quartet scores from a different distribution than the training data.
experiments.Method: Legato, Legatosmall, SMT++, and GPT-5 are evaluated on the OpenScore String Quartets dataset. The evaluation is performed on two subsets: 'Camera' (scanned real scores) and 'Rendered' (clean images generated from MusicXML).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of Legato on unseen Lieder (song) scores.
experiments.Method: Legato, Legatosmall, SMT++, and GPT-5 are evaluated on the OpenScore Lieder dataset. The evaluation is performed on both 'Camera' (scanned) and 'Rendered' images.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

experiments.Description: To conduct a realistic evaluation on piano-form camera scores from a source not used in training and produced with older typesetting technology.
experiments.Method: Legato, Legatosmall, SMT++, and GPT-5 are evaluated on a newly manually annotated dataset of full-page piano scores from IMSLP, ensuring no overlap with the training data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to quantify the improvements brought by different design considerations of Legato, such as output format, tokenizer, model architecture, and dataset size.
experiments.Method: The original SMT++ and Legato models are compared against several newly trained variants on the IMSLP Piano Scores dataset. These variants include SMT++ trained on ABC format, an enlarged SMT++large model, and SMT++large trained on the new PDMX-Synth dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

risk_factors: Finally, we focus on the recognition of musical notation rather than textual features of a musical score (e.g., lyrics), leaving the textual recognition to future work.
Training autoregressive models on long sequences is computationally expensive, and since such cases are rare, our approach sacrifices them for efficiency.
Finetuning this module [the vision encoder] specifically for scores is a promising direction for future work.
These inputs [multi-page] often result in long sequences that are truncated before reaching the decoder, limiting model performance.

The current OMR-NED implementation is optimized for **kern through syntax correction, while ABC v2.1 lacks parser support; thus, ABC outputs are converted to MusicXML for symbol extraction, which may introduce errors and disadvantage ABC.

title: Multi-timestep models for Model-based Reinforcement Learning
name: multi-timestep-objective-for-mbrl
short_hypothesis: Training a one-step dynamics model using a multi-timestep objective, which is a weighted sum of prediction losses at various future horizons, will improve its long-horizon prediction accuracy and reduce compounding errors compared to standard one-step training.
related_work: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) typically uses one-step dynamics models, which suffer from compounding errors over long prediction horizons. Prior work has addressed this with multi-step prediction models, such as recurrent state-space models or fixed-horizon architectures, but these can be computationally heavy or lack flexibility. Other methods like 'hallucinated replay' teach the model to recover from its own errors. This paper identifies a gap in directly aligning the training objective of a standard one-step model to optimize for multi-step prediction performance, which is proposed as a more direct way to mitigate long-horizon error.
abstract: In model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL), most algorithms rely on simulating trajectories from one-step dynamics models learned on data. A critical challenge of this approach is the compounding of one-step prediction errors as the length of the trajectory grows. This paper proposes to tackle this issue by using a multi-timestep objective to train one-step models. The proposed objective is a weighted sum of a loss function (e.g., negative log-likelihood) at various future horizons. The plan is to explore and test a range of weight profiles for this objective.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the predictive error of the proposed multi-timestep models in a supervised learning fashion on fixed datasets.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Cartpole swing-up environment, with datasets of varying characteristics: `medium`, `random`, `expert`, and a `Noisy Cartpole` variant (`expert_noisy`). One-step models will be trained using the proposed multi-timestep loss with different weighting schemes (Uniform, Decay, Learnable, Proportional) and compared against a vanilla one-step model baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['R2 score evaluated at different future horizons']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the multi-timestep models in a practical pure batch (offline) RL scenario, which can be viewed as a single-iteration of MBRL.
experiments.Method: Using the `medium`, `random`, `expert`, and `expert_noisy` datasets for the Cartpole swing-up environment, dynamics models will be pre-trained. A Soft-Actor Critic (SAC) agent will then be trained on imaginary rollouts generated by these models. The performance of agents trained with the proposed multi-timestep models will be compared to agents trained with a vanilla one-step model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed models in a full iterated batch MBRL setting, iterating between model learning, agent learning, and data collection.
experiments.Method: This experiment will be run on the Cartpole swing-up and Noisy Cartpole environments. The process will alternate between generating a system trace, training the dynamics model on all collected traces, and training a policy on the updated model. The performance of the best multi-timestep models will be compared against a vanilla one-step model baseline within this iterative loop.

risk_factors: First, the study focused solely on the Cartpole benchmark, a relatively low-dimensional environment that reduces the modelling complexity that may be encountered in other high-dimensional systems.

Second, since our proposed objective can be plugged into any single-step model-based algorithm, we believe it would be interesting to test its integration with some of the state-of-the-art MBRL algorithms in different setups/applications.

title: The Surprising Effectiveness of Equivariant Models in Domains with Latent Symmetry
name: Extrinsic Equivariance for Latent Symmetries
short_hypothesis: This paper tests the hypothesis that if a model is constrained to a symmetry class which is related to the true, but unknown, problem symmetry, then it may help learn a model that satisfies the true symmetry. Specifically, imposing equivariance constraints that do not exactly match the domain symmetry (termed 'extrinsic equivariance') can be helpful in learning the true symmetry in the environment and can improve performance over unconstrained models.
related_work: Previous work on equivariant neural networks typically assumes that the domain symmetry is perfectly known and can be explicitly modeled. In contrast, this work addresses domains with latent or partial symmetries where the action of the symmetry group on the input is not precisely known. While other methods learn symmetric representations, this work proposes to directly use an equivariant network as an encoder, even when the equivariance constraint is 'imprecise'. This extends prior work on equivariant reinforcement learning, which is mostly limited to fully equivariant domains, to tasks with latent symmetries.
abstract: Equivariant neural networks can improve sample efficiency by enforcing an inductive bias, but this typically assumes domain symmetry is fully described by explicit transformations. However, many real-life applications contain only latent or partial symmetries which cannot be easily described by simple transformations of the input. In these cases, it is necessary to learn symmetry in the environment instead of imposing it mathematically. This work proposes that imposing equivariance constraints that do not exactly match the domain symmetry can be helpful in learning the true symmetry. We differentiate between 'extrinsic' and 'incorrect' symmetry constraints, and propose that imposing extrinsic symmetry can improve performance.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effect of different types of mismatched model symmetries (extrinsic vs. incorrect) on the performance of an equivariant network in a supervised learning context.
experiments.Method: The task is a supervised learning problem to classify the relative rotation (from group C8) between two images of three ducks. The experiment will introduce various 'symmetry corruptions' such as grid, occlusion, light effect, squeeze, reflect, side, and invert-label. The primary model is an equivariant network (Equi) built with e2cnn. It will be compared against a conventional CNN model with a similar parameter count, and a CNN model augmented with C8 image rotations.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that enforcing extrinsic equivariance can improve sample efficiency in reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation tasks with latent symmetries.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses five robotic manipulation environments from the BulletArm benchmark (Block Pulling, Block Pushing, Block Picking, Drawer Opening, Block in Bowl) with skewed-perspective RGBD image inputs, creating latent O(2) symmetry. The proposed method is Equivariant SAC (enforcing D4 symmetry) with random crop augmentation (RAD). It will be compared against several baselines: CNN SAC + RAD, CNN SAC + DrQ, FERM, and SEN + RAD.

experiments.Description: To investigate how the performance of an extrinsically equivariant network is affected by increasing the degree of symmetry corruption, i.e., the gap between the model's image-space transform and the true object-space transform.
experiments.Method: Using robotic manipulation environments, the camera view angle will be varied from 90 degrees (top-down, perfect symmetry) to 15 degrees (highly skewed, large corruption). The performance of Equivariant SAC + RAD will be compared against CNN SAC + RAD across these different angles.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Discounted reward at the end of training']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate a scenario where enforcing an 'incorrect' equivariance constraint harms the performance of an equivariant RL agent compared to an unconstrained one.
experiments.Method: The robotic manipulation environments are modified to apply random reflections (vertical and horizontal) to the state image at each step. This is designed to turn the extrinsic D4 symmetry constraint of Equivariant SAC into an incorrect one. The performance of Equivariant SAC will be compared to a standard CNN-based SAC.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether enforcing extrinsic equivariance can improve training performance on standard continuous control benchmarks with inherent object-level symmetries but corrupted visual inputs.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a subset of tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite (cartpole, cup catch, pendulum, acrobot, reacher) which have object-level symmetry but visual corruptions (e.g., checkered floors, random backgrounds). An equivariant version of the DrQv2 algorithm will be created and compared against the original non-equivariant DrQv2. The model will enforce D1 or D2 group symmetry depending on the task.

risk_factors: A limitation of this work is that we mainly experiment in reinforcement learning and a simple supervised setting but not in other domains where equivariant learning is widely used.
Another limitation is that we focus on planar equivariant networks.

title: Adapting to Distribution Shift by Visual Domain Prompt Generation
name: Visual Domain Prompt Generation
short_hypothesis: A domain-specific visual prompt can be generated from a few unlabeled target domain examples to guide a frozen, pre-trained foundation model for effective test-time domain adaptation. This process can be formulated by using a learnable knowledge bank to encode transferable knowledge from source domains, a conditional prompt generator to condense this knowledge, and a guidance module to apply it to visual features, all trained end-to-end with meta-learning and a domain-aware contrastive loss.
related_work: Previous studies in few-shot test-time domain adaptation (FSTT-DA) either fail to utilize powerful foundation models (FMs) like CLIP, or they require finetuning the FM which can harm its generalization capabilities. Existing visual prompt tuning (VPT) methods often model source domains independently, use simple linear combinations for adaptation, or require access to the model's internal weights, making them unsuitable for black-box scenarios. Furthermore, these methods often separate the process of modeling source knowledge and learning to adapt into disjoint stages, which can compromise knowledge transfer to unseen domains.
abstract: In this paper, we aim to adapt a model at test-time using a few unlabeled data to address distribution shifts. To tackle the challenges of extracting domain knowledge from a limited amount of data, it is crucial to utilize correlated information from pre-trained backbones and source domains. Previous studies fail to utilize recent foundation models with strong out-of-distribution generalization. Additionally, domain-centric designs are not flavored in their works. Furthermore, they employ the process of modelling source domains and the process of learning to adapt independently into disjoint training stages. In this work, we propose an approach on top of the pre-computed features of the foundation model. Specifically, we build a knowledge bank to learn the transferable knowledge from source domains. Conditioned on few-shot target data, we introduce a domain prompt generator to condense the knowledge bank into a domain-specific prompt. The domain prompt then directs the visual features towards a particular domain via a guidance module. Moreover, we propose a domain-aware contrastive loss and employ meta-learning to facilitate domain knowledge extraction.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Visual Domain Prompt Generator (VDPG) on a diverse set of real-world distribution shift benchmarks for both classification and regression tasks.
experiments.Method: The method will be evaluated on the WILDS benchmarks: iWildCam, Camelyon17, FMoW, and PovertyMap. The model uses a frozen ViT-L/14 CLIP image encoder. Baselines for comparison will include ERM, CORAL, IRM, ARM-CML, ARM-BN, Meta-DMoE, MABN, zero-shot CLIP (ZS), and FLYP.

experiments.Description: To evaluate VDPG's effectiveness on a large-scale, leave-one-domain-out domain generalization benchmark.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the DomainNet dataset using the official leave-one-domain-out protocol. The model will use a frozen ViT-B/16 CLIP image encoder. The performance will be compared against CNN-based baselines (ERM, Mixup, CORAL, MTL, SegNet, ARM, Meta-DMoE, MABN) and CLIP-based baselines (DoPrompt, Zero-shot ZS, ERM, MIRO).

experiments.Description: To analyze the contribution of each proposed component, the training strategy, and the quality of the generated domain-specific information.
experiments.Method: A series of ablation studies will be performed on the iWildCam dataset. This includes: 1) Evaluating the effect of the episodic training scheme by comparing it to standard ERM training. 2) Analyzing the impact of the domain-aware contrastive loss (Ldac) and the knowledge bank correlation loss (Lcorr). 3) Assessing the importance of the generated prompt by replacing it with random vectors, zero vectors, or the knowledge bank itself. 4) Evaluating the necessity of the prompt generator (G) and the knowledge bank (KB) modules.

risk_factors: One limitation of our method is that the performance highly depends on the out-of-distribution generalization in the foundation models.
Although pre-trained on the web-scale dataset, it is not guaranteed that the foundation model covers all the data, especially those that are strictly protected due to privacy.
However, our method is built on top of the foundation models without knowing their architectures. It is flexible as long as the final feature can be obtained.

title: Flow-Based Alignment of Uni-Modal Vision and Text Encoders for Few-Shot Image Classification
name: FSF (Few-Shot-Flow)
short_hypothesis: By first applying a closed-form Orthogonal Procrustes map to align arbitrary uni-modal encoders and then training a lightweight, flow-matching prior to regularize adaptation, it is possible to effectively perform few-shot cross-modal classification. This approach is designed to combine the efficiency of linear alignment with the expressive capacity of non-linear flows, without relying on jointly pretrained models or large paired datasets.
related_work: Current few-shot classification (FSC) methods predominantly rely on adapters for large, jointly pretrained vision-language models like CLIP. This reliance is limiting as it prevents the use of potentially stronger or more domain-specialized uni-modal encoders. While some methods do leverage uni-modal encoders, they typically require large paired datasets and heavy adapter training, making them unsuitable for the few-shot setting. Simpler approaches like linear alignment maps are efficient but cannot capture the non-linear dependencies between modalities. This work proposes to bridge this gap by combining an efficient, closed-form linear alignment with a lightweight, non-linear flow-based model to regularize the alignment in the few-shot regime.
abstract: Few-shot classification with vision-language models remains challenging, particularly when relying on multi-modal encoders such as CLIP that are restricted to paired image-text data. We introduce FSF, a framework that leverages arbitrary uni-modal encoders—including vision or text models that were pretrained on broad or domain-specific corpora—and aligns them for cross-modal classification. FSF first applies a closed-form orthogonal Procrustes map to align image and text embeddings while preserving their geometry, and then trains a lightweight flow-matching prior that regularizes adaptation in the few-shot regime. At inference, images are classified by cosine similarity in the aligned feature space between query embeddings and mapped class prototypes.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the flexibility of FSF in adapting independently trained (uni-modal) text and vision encoders for few-shot classification, and to compare its performance against methods restricted to jointly trained models.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on 16-shot ImageNet classification. FSF will be tested with various combinations of independently trained encoders (e.g., CLIP text + DINOv2 vision, Qwen-8B text + DINOv2-B vision) and also with jointly trained CLIP encoders. Performance will be compared against a variety of baseline methods including ZS-CLIP, Tip-Adapter, CoOp, CLIP-Adapter, ProKeR, and LFA.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of FSF on a diverse set of few-shot classification benchmarks, covering generic, fine-grained, and other specialized image recognition tasks.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will follow the CoOp 11-dataset protocol, using {1, 2, 4, 8, 16} shots. The datasets include ImageNet, Caltech101, OxfordPets, StandfordCars, Flowers102, Food101, FGVCAircraft, SUN397, DTD, EuroSAT, and UCF101. FSF will be tested with both multi-modal (CLIP-RN50) and uni-modal (CLIP-RN50 text + DINOv2-S vision) encoder configurations. Baselines will include Zero-Shot, CoOp, CLIP-A, TIP-A(f), TaskRes(e), CLAP, and LFA.

experiments.Description: To assess the out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization capability of FSF. Models will be adapted on few-shot ImageNet and then evaluated on several ImageNet variant datasets without further tuning.
experiments.Method: The task is 16-shot OOD evaluation. Models adapted on ImageNet will be tested on ImageNetV2, ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-A, and ImageNet-R. FSF will be evaluated with both multi-modal (CLIP) and uni-modal (CLIP text + DINOv2 vision) encoders. Baselines will include Zero-Shot, CLIP-A, TIP-A, TaskRes(e), CLAP, and LFA.

experiments.Description: To test whether FSF can adapt a strong, domain-specific uni-modal vision encoder into an effective multi-modal classifier for a specialized, multi-label medical imaging task, without requiring a jointly pretrained medical VLM.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the VinDr-CXR chest X-ray benchmark for multi-label classification with {16, 32, 64, 128} shots. FSF will be used to align a medical-specific vision encoder (RAD-DINO) with various independent text encoders (e.g., BiomedCLIP, Qwen-8B, CLIP). Performance will be compared against a RAD-DINO linear probe baseline.

risk_factors: Our method still relies on the availability of strong uni-modal encoders, and the benefits of flow-based alignment may diminish if either side lacks semantic quality.
Moreover, although the flow prior is lightweight compared to full model tuning, it still introduces an extra training component beyond closed-form alignment.

title: Guiding Masked Representation Learning to Capture Spatio-Temporal Relationship of Electrocardiogram
name: st-mem
short_hypothesis: A self-supervised learning framework, ST-MEM, based on a masked auto-encoder with spatio-temporal patchifying, can learn general and enhanced ECG representations by explicitly capturing the spatio-temporal relationships within multi-lead ECG data. This is achieved by reconstructing masked portions of the signal while using lead-indicating modules and a lead-wise shared decoder to increase the task's complexity and guide the model to learn spatial features.
related_work: Self-supervised learning (SSL) for ECGs is typically approached with contrastive or generative methods.
