Contrastive learning relies on data augmentations, which can inadvertently alter the semantic meaning of ECG signals. Generative methods, like Masked Autoencoders (MAE), reconstruct parts of the input. However, naive applications of MAE to ECGs, which treat the data as simple temporal sequences (temporal patchifying) or stacked channels (spatial patchifying), fail to adequately capture the inherent spatio-temporal relationships between different leads. Some prior work used spatio-temporal patchifying but lacked specific modules to help the model distinguish between leads, making it challenging to learn the spatial relationships effectively.
abstract: Electrocardiograms (ECG) are widely used for screening various diseases, but adapting machine learning models is challenging due to limited labeled data. Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a known approach to overcome data scarcity, but a naive application to ECG data may yield suboptimal results by not considering the inherent spatial-temporal relationships. This paper introduces ST-MEM (Spatio-Temporal Masked Electrocardiogram Modeling), a method designed to learn spatio-temporal features by reconstructing masked 12-lead ECG data. The model is designed to be adaptable to various lead combinations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of the general ECG representation learned by the proposed SSL method against baselines on downstream arrhythmia and myocardial infarction classification tasks.
experiments.Method: The model will be pre-trained on a combination of the Chapman, Ningbo, and CODE-15 datasets. The learned representations will then be evaluated using both linear probing and fine-tuning on two unseen datasets: PTB-XL and CPSC2018. The proposed model, ST-MEM, will be compared against a supervised baseline and several SSL methods, including contrastive (MoCo v3, CMSC) and generative (MTAE, MLAE, MTAE+RLM) approaches. All models will use a ViT-B backbone.

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of the learned general ECG representation in a low-resource setting where labeled data is scarce.
experiments.Method: The pre-trained models will be fine-tuned on small, randomly sampled subsets (1% and 5%) of the training data from the PTB-XL and CPSC2018 datasets. The performance will be compared against other SSL baselines and a supervised model trained under the same low-data conditions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's robustness and adaptability to ECG data with a reduced number of leads, simulating scenarios such as data from smartwatches.
experiments.Method: The pre-trained models will be fine-tuned and evaluated on datasets with varying numbers of leads: 12-lead, 6-lead (limb leads only), and 1-lead (lead I only). The experiments will use the PTB-XL, CPSC2018, and the single-lead PhysioNet2017 datasets. Performance will be compared against the MTAE+RLM and MLAE baselines.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to investigate the importance of the proposed architectural components, specifically the lead indication modules.
experiments.Method: The experiment will involve training and evaluating versions of ST-MEM with different components removed. This includes a model with only spatio-temporal patchifying, a model with a lead-wise shared decoder added, and a model with SEP embeddings added, to isolate the contribution of each component. The evaluation will be a linear probe on the PTB-XL and CPSC2018 datasets.

risk_factors: One drawback of spatio-temporal patchifying is that it simplifies the reconstruction task, allowing the decoder to access unmasked embeddings from different leads with identical temporal information.
This ease of reconstruction can negatively impact the training of the encoder.

title: Beyond Data Silos : Leveraging Disparate Data Sources for ITE Estimation
name: causal-transformer-federated-ite
short_hypothesis: A novel framework based on causal transformers can enable collaborative learning of heterogeneous Individual Treatment Effect (ITE) estimators across disparate data sources, including in a federated setting, and can be utilized to predict the effects of novel and unseen treatments.
related_work: Conventional methods for Individual Treatment Effect (ITE) estimation require substantial amounts of costly training data for each intervention. They are not designed to handle disparate data sources with diverse patient populations, non-identical patient measurements (covariates), and different treatments, leading to data silos.
abstract: While ML systems for Individual Treatment Effect (ITE) estimation have advanced healthcare decision-making, conventional methods require substantial amounts of costly training data for each intervention under consideration. In this work, we present a novel framework, based on causal transformers, for collaborative learning of heterogeneous ITE estimators across disparate data sources. Our approach can be deployed across distributed institutions (such as hospitals) via Federated Learning, enabling training on a large and diverse dataset (without sharing sensitive health data), and the same framework can be applied locally when multiple heterogeneous data sources exist within a single institution, breaking down data silos. The proposed method is flexible to handle diverse patient populations and non-identical patient measurements (covariates) across different data sources, while allowing for the estimation of treatment effects of disparate treatments being administered across these sources. Moreover, this framework can be utilized to predict the effects of novel and unseen treatments by utilizing available treatment level information.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed framework's ability to estimate ITE by leveraging disparate data sources and compare its performance against existing methods.
experiments.Method: The proposed model is to be evaluated on real-world clinical trial datasets and widely-used research datasets. Its performance will be compared against existing ITE estimation baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Not specified in the provided text.

experiments.Description: To analyze the model's attention mechanisms to determine if they capture clinically meaningful patterns related to diseases and treatments.
experiments.Method: Analysis of the model's attention mechanisms will be conducted and validated by domain expertise.

title: MuseGNN: Forming Scalable, Convergent GNN Layers that Minimize a Sampling-Based Energy
name: musegnn
short_hypothesis: By incorporating offline subgraph sampling directly into the design of a graph-regularized energy function, it is possible to create a scalable and convergent Unfolded GNN (UGNN) architecture that retains the desirable properties of UGNNs (like interpretability) while being applicable to very large graphs.
related_work: A subclass of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), known as Unfolded GNNs (UGNNs), are designed such that their forward pass iteratively minimizes a graph-regularized energy function. This provides them with transparent inductive biases and interpretability. However, these models face significant scalability challenges, as convergence can require considerable depth, making them computationally expensive for large graphs. Existing scaling techniques for GNNs have not been systematically adapted and analyzed for UGNNs, and recent attempts to scale UGNNs (e.g., USP, LazyGNN) have limitations regarding convergence guarantees or performance on the largest benchmarks. This paper addresses the gap by proposing a method to explicitly build scalability into the UGNN's energy function itself.
abstract: Among the many variants of graph neural network (GNN) architectures, an important subclass involves layers designed such that the forward pass iteratively reduces a graph-regularized energy function. In this way, node embeddings produced at the output layer dually serve as both predictive features and energy function minimizers that inherit transparent, exploitable inductive biases and interpretability. However, scaling GNN architectures constructed in this way remains challenging, in part because the convergence of the forward pass may involve models with considerable depth. To tackle this limitation, we propose a sampling-based energy function and scalable GNN layers that iteratively reduce it, guided by convergence guarantees in certain settings. We also instantiate a full GNN architecture, MuseGNN, based on these designs.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scalability of MuseGNN on node classification tasks across a wide range of graph sizes and compare it against various scalable GNN baselines.
experiments.Method: The MuseGNN model will be evaluated on node classification. The model will use 3-layer MLPs for its base and output functions, with K=8 unfolded layers. Offline subgraph sampling will be performed using a variant of neighbor sampling (ShadowKHop). The evaluation will be conducted on datasets from the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) and Illinois Graph Benchmark (IGB), specifically: ogbn-arxiv, IGB-tiny, ogbn-products, ogbn-papers100M, MAG240M, and IGB-full. Baselines for comparison will include: GCN, SAGE, and GAT (with both Neighbor Sampling 'NS' and GNNAutoScale 'GAS' scaling methods); recent scalable models like MariusGNN, FreshGNN, and SGFormer; and other scalable UGNN approaches like the full-graph UGNN (FG), LazyGNN, and USP.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Node classification accuracy (%)', 'Training speed (epoch time in seconds)']

experiments.Description: To empirically demonstrate the convergence of the proposed sampling-based energy function during the forward pass of the MuseGNN model and to analyze the effect of the penalty factor γ.
experiments.Method: The MuseGNN model will be run on the ogbn-papers100M dataset. The experiment will involve varying the value of the penalty factor γ that controls the linkage between embeddings of the same node across different subgraphs.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A plot of the energy value over the K unfolded layers of the forward pass to observe convergence.']

risk_factors: While MuseGNN was primarily designed for scaling the most common UGNNs on homogeneous graphs, there do exist prior UGNNs models based on energy functions sensitive to heterogeneous graph structure.
But there is nothing to prevent us from extending the core techniques and analysis that undergird MuseGNN to scale such heterogeneous cases.

While we do not envision that there are any noteworthy risks introduced by our work, it is of course always possible that a large-scale model like MuseGNN could be deployed, either intentionally or unintentionally, in such a way as to cause societal harm. For example, a graph neural network designed to screen fraudulent credit applications could be biased against certain minority groups.

title: Episodic Memories Generation and Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
name: episodic-memory-benchmark-for-llms
short_hypothesis: A comprehensive framework, inspired by cognitive science, can be used to systematically model and evaluate the episodic memory capabilities of Large Language Models. This involves representing episodic events in a structured way (encapsulating temporal/spatial contexts, entities, and descriptions) and creating a synthetic benchmark to assess LLM performance on recall and reasoning tasks.
related_work: Existing benchmarks for Large Language Models primarily focus on simple retrieval ('needle-in-a-haystack') or reasoning over long contexts, but they have several limitations for evaluating episodic memory. These benchmarks often lack explicit temporal and spatial awareness, fail to track entity state changes, use artificial scenarios that allow for shortcut reasoning, suffer from data contamination, and are labor-intensive to create. This creates a need for a new benchmark that systematically incorporates key elements of episodic memory, such as spatio-temporal context, entity state tracking, and varied cue-based recall, while being contamination-free and balancing complexity with realism.
abstract: Episodic memory – the ability to recall specific events grounded in time and space – is a cornerstone of human cognition. Despite their remarkable capabilities, Large Language Models (LLMs) lack a robust mechanism for episodic memory. We argue that integrating episodic memory capabilities into LLMs is essential for advancing AI towards human-like cognition, increasing their potential to reason consistently and ground their output in real-world episodic events, hence avoiding confabulations. To address this challenge, we introduce a comprehensive framework to model and evaluate LLM episodic memory capabilities. Drawing inspiration from cognitive science, we develop a structured approach to represent episodic events, encapsulating temporal and spatial contexts, involved entities, and detailed descriptions. We synthesize a unique episodic memory benchmark, free from contamination, and release open source code and datasets to assess LLM performance across various recall and episodic reasoning tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the episodic memory capabilities of various state-of-the-art LLMs using a newly generated benchmark. The evaluation will test performance on recall, reasoning, entity state tracking, and chronological analysis tasks under different memory strategies.
experiments.Method: A synthetic benchmark will be generated, consisting of narrative documents ('short book' of ~10k tokens and 'long book' of ~100k tokens) containing controlled episodic events. A corresponding set of question-answer pairs will be created using templates to probe for recall of specific event details based on various cue compositions (time, space, entity, content). The evaluation will be performed on several LLMs: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude 3 Haiku, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3.1 405B, and o1-mini. Three memory strategies will be tested as baselines: (1) In-Context Memory, where the full document is prepended to the question; (2) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), where relevant paragraphs are retrieved from a vector database and provided as context; and (3) Parametric Memory via Fine-Tuning, where models are fine-tuned on single-event question-answer pairs.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['F1-score, using an LLM-as-a-judge to identify true positives against the ground truth.', "Kendall's τ coefficient for assessing the correctness of ordering in chronological recall tasks.", "F1-score on questions with zero matching events to assess the model's ability to avoid confabulation."]

risk_factors: Temporal representation. Our benchmark relies on explicit temporal markers, which may not fully capture the nuanced ways time is expressed in natural language (e.g. “yesterday”, “last week”, or “after the party”).
Event independence. The independent generation of chapters, while facilitating control, does not capture the interconnected and causal nature of real-world events.

Limited domain scope. Our benchmark primarily involves human-like protagonists within fictional contexts. Extending the framework to include diverse domains (e.g., software projects, virtual environments) would test models’ ability to generalize episodic memory capabilities.

Training limitations. The observed performance limitations suggest that current fine-tuning methodologies may not be optimally suited for episodic memory tasks, underscoring the need for developing new strategies for the broad scientific community.

title: Teacher Ascent: Robust and Efficient Machine Unlearning via Knowledge Distillation and Continual Learning
name: teacher-ascent
short_hypothesis: A novel unlearning method, Teacher Ascent (TA), based on knowledge distillation and continual learning, can effectively forget target data while preventing catastrophic forgetting. This is achieved by maximizing a bounded KL-divergence on the forget set and using a regularization term inspired by Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), which uses the Fisher Information Matrix to protect parameters essential for generalization. This approach is hypothesized to be more robust to hyperparameter choices and more efficient than retraining, while mimicking the functional behavior of a retrained model.
related_work: Established approximate unlearning methods like SCRUB+R and SSD have emerged as alternatives to costly model retraining. However, their practical application is limited by hyperparameter sensitivity. SCRUB+R relies on maximizing an unbounded KL-divergence, which can lead to catastrophic forgetting if not carefully tuned, and requires a complex rewind procedure. SSD is highly dependent on a predefined threshold for parameter intervention, where an incorrect choice leads to either model degradation or ineffective unlearning. This creates a need for a more stable and robust unlearning method that does not require precise hyperparameter tuning against a retrained model, which is often unavailable in practice.
abstract: Removing specific knowledge from a trained machine learning model is an open problem of increasing importance. While powerful Machine Unlearning methods have emerged as effective alternatives to retraining, their practical application is often hindered by narrow functional ranges for hyperparameters, which typically require access to a retrained model for effective tuning. Established unlearning methods like SCRUB+R and SSD require precise specification of their hyperparameters to achieve unlearning whilst preventing catastrophic forgetting. We address this challenge by proposing Teacher Ascent (TA), a novel unlearning method that is based on knowledge distillation and continual learning. Inspired by Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), TA forgets target data while protecting parameters essential for generalization by using the Fisher Information Matrix.

experiments.Description: To investigate the convergence properties of Teacher Ascent (TA) and highlight the claimed instability of the SCRUB+R baseline.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on a subsampled MNIST dataset. The task is to unlearn samples from three different forget sets, which are constructed from local neighborhoods of a t-SNE embedding space. The performance of TA will be compared against SCRUB+R by analyzing model accuracies as a function of the number of unlearning rounds.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the retain set', 'Accuracy on the forget set', 'Accuracy on the validation set']

experiments.Description: To evaluate Teacher Ascent (TA) against other established machine unlearning methods across a variety of unlearning tasks and datasets.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be run on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Pins Face Recognition datasets using a vision transformer (ViT) model. The performance of TA will be benchmarked against a retrained model, SCRUB+R, SSD, and LoTUS. The evaluation will cover three unlearning scenarios: 1) Forgetting an entire class (on all datasets), 2) Forgetting a random subset of a class (on CIFAR-10), and 3) Forgetting corrupted/mislabeled data (on CIFAR-10).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Model Utility (accuracy on the test set)', 'Unlearning Efficacy (accuracy on the forget set)', 'Privacy (exposure to Membership Inference Attacks - MIA)']

experiments.Description: To introduce and apply a more realistic evaluation protocol for hyperparameter selection, which aims to highlight the hyperparameter sensitivity of different unlearning methods.
experiments.Method: Instead of tuning hyperparameters on the target task, this protocol involves selecting hyperparameters by optimizing performance on a separate but semantically related proxy unlearning task. For example, when the downstream task is to forget the 'ship' class in CIFAR-10, the proxy task for hyperparameter search will be to forget the 'airplane' class. The best hyperparameters found on the proxy task using Optuna's TPE will then be applied directly to the downstream task without modification.

risk_factors: However, for larger datasets we suspect that setting k > 1 may be necessary to retain generalizability.

While TA and SCRUB+R show promise and lessen the gap in MIA probability to a retrained model, the remaining difference indicates that perfectly replicating the privacy profile of a retrained model is a challenging task and warrants further investigation.

It could be interesting to further investigate how well the various unlearn methods perform as the difference between the downstream forget set and the one used for hyperparameter search increases. We defer this to future research.

title: Long Horizon Episodic Decision Making for Cognitively Inspired Robots
name: Cognitive-Memory-Transformer
short_hypothesis: Mimicking human memory mechanisms by modifying a Transformer-XL architecture with 'Automatic Chunking' to attend to relevant memory segments, 'ForgetSpan' to remove memories that do not contribute to learning, and 'Similarity based forgetting' to avoid storing repetitive observations will improve the memory efficiency and performance of agents in long-horizon, multi-modal robotic tasks.
related_work: Standard Transformer architectures have shown great results in multi-modal robotic tasks but are limited in their ability to scale to large memory sizes and learn long-horizon tasks efficiently. This is because their computational requirements scale non-linearly with memory length. This research is inspired by human cognition, which stores past observations in a derived form, including only important information and forgetting the rest, to address the efficiency limitations of transformers in long-horizon scenarios.
abstract: The Human decision-making process works by recollecting past sequences of observations and using them to decide the best possible action in the present. These past sequences of observations are stored in a derived form which only includes important information the brain thinks might be useful in the future, while forgetting the rest. Transformers have shown great results in multi-modal robotic navigation and human-robot collaboration tasks but lack the ability to scale to large memory sizes and learn long horizon tasks efficiently as the computational requirements needed to run these models scale non-linearly with memory length. Our model tries to mimic the human brain and improve the memory efficiency of transformers by using a modified TransformerXL architecture which uses Automatic Chunking that chunks the past memories and only attends to the relevant chunks in the transformer block. On top of this, we use ForgetSpan which is a technique to remove memories that do not contribute to learning. We also theorize the technique of Similarity based forgetting where the current observations are compared with the elements in the memory and only the new observations are stored, similar to how humans do not store repetitive memories. We plan to test our model in various visual and audio-visual tasks that demand long horizon recollection, audio-visual instruction deciphering and robotic navigation.

experiments.Description: To test the agent's ability to remember information from the start of an episode and use it effectively to reach a goal in a 2D navigation environment.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Minigrid memory task. The models to be evaluated are a baseline Gated Transformer-XL and variations incorporating Automatic Chunking, ForgetSpan, and SimilarityWeight.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average rewards across episodes', 'Average training time per episode']

experiments.Description: To test the agent's long-horizon recollection and multi-modal instruction deciphering ability by having it follow an audio command to navigate to a specific visual target.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Audio-Visual Instructions Task from the Unity MLAgents toolkit. The models to be evaluated are a baseline Gated Transformer-XL, and variations with Automatic Chunking and ForgetSpan (using different ramp lengths). A generalization test is also planned by training the model and then testing it in a scenario where the target object positions are changed.

experiments.Description: To test the agent's ability to recall information after a variable-length distractor phase, which is a more dynamic scenario for testing memory retention.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Visual Corridor Task from the Unity MLAgents toolkit. The models to be evaluated are Automatic Chunking with and without the ForgetSpan mechanism. A generalization test is also planned where the length of the distractor corridor is doubled during testing.

risk_factors: The computational cost increase is significant as we have to calculate similarity of a new observation with the whole memory buffer every timestep.
Increasing the Ramp length to 64 led to worsening performance. This is probably caused by the gradient used to train the ForgetSpan is more gradual leading to slower learning of the ForgetSpan layer.

title: Revisiting Graph Adversarial Attack and Defense From a Data Distribution Perspective
name: graph-attack-distribution-shift
short_hypothesis: The destructive power of gradient-based adversarial attacks on GNNs stems from their ability to effectively increase the distribution shift between training and testing nodes. This data distribution perspective can be used to explain various attack phenomena and to design more effective attack and defense strategies, such as a fast heuristic attack and a self-training based defense.
related_work: Recent studies have demonstrated that GNNs are vulnerable to adversarial structural perturbations, and most attack methods are gradient-based. However, a general framework to explain the effectiveness of these gradient-based methods is lacking. While some works have analyzed properties like increased heterophily or spectral changes, they have not explored the problem from a data distribution perspective, which considers the differences between the training and testing sets induced by the attack.
abstract: The reasons for the destructive nature of gradient-based adversarial attacks on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in the semi-supervised node classification (SSNC) task are rarely explored. This work discovers that adversarial edges are not uniformly distributed, with a majority of perturbations generated around the training nodes in poisoning attacks. This phenomenon motivates an explanation for the effectiveness of gradient-based attacks from a data distribution perspective. The paper proposes to revisit poisoning and evasion attacks in SSNC through this new lens, empirically and theoretically discussing attack tendencies. Based on this analysis, the work provides practical tips for attack and defense, and designs a fast attack method and a self-training defense method intended to outperform existing methods and scale to large graphs.

experiments.Description: To quantify the distribution of adversarial edges generated by different attack methods and analyze how this distribution changes with varying data splits.
experiments.Method: Apply poisoning attacks (MetaAttack, PGD, FGSM) to GNNs on Cora, Citeseer, and Polblogs datasets. The training set size will be varied from 10% to 90%.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Intention Score (IS) to measure perturbation density in Train-Train, Train-Test, and Test-Test graph partitions', 'Adversarial accuracy of the GCN model']

experiments.Description: To validate the hypothesis that attacking the smaller part of the graph (e.g., the smaller of the training or testing set) is a more effective strategy for increasing distribution shift.
experiments.Method: Improve heuristic methods DICE and Random by modifying them to focus perturbations on the smaller graph partition (DICEimp, Ranimp). These will be tested on Cora, Citeseer, and ogbn-arxiv datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Adversarial accuracy of GCN vs. perturbation rate']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scalability of the proposed fast heuristic attack algorithm, which is designed based on the distribution shift perspective.
experiments.Method: The proposed heuristic attack will be compared against gradient-based methods (MetaAttack, PGD) and a heuristic baseline (DICE) on Citeseer and ogbn-arxiv datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed self-training defense method (STRG), which is designed to improve robustness by reducing the distribution shift between the training and testing sets.
experiments.Method: STRG will be compared against a standard GCN and other robust GNN methods (GNNGuard, ProGNN, SimpGCN, Elastic, STABLE) on Cora and Citeseer datasets under poisoning attacks from MetaAttack.

experiments.Description: To validate the hypothesis that defending the most vulnerable part of the graph (i.e., the training nodes under poisoning attack) is a more effective defense strategy.
experiments.Method: Graph Structure Learning (GSL) methods Jaccard and GNNGuard will be modified to only refine the structure of the training set. Their performance will be compared to their vanilla versions on Cora and Citeseer under MetaAttack.

experiments.Description: To empirically support the theoretical claim that inserting heterophilous edges causes a larger distribution shift than deleting homophilous edges.
experiments.Method: The MetaAttack method will be applied to a dense synthetic graph where the possibilities to insert and delete edges are balanced.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Ratio of insertions to deletions in the generated perturbations']

risk_factors: The two proposed algorithms are not our focus in this work, and the key to their success is the thinkings behind them but not a technical novelty.

title: Contextual Fusion For Adversarial Robustness
name: contextual-fusion-adversarial-robustness
short_hypothesis: Fusing features from two parallel data streams—one focused on object-centric foreground information (from an Imagenet-pretrained model) and another on scene-centric background context (from a Places365-pretrained model)—can improve adversarial robustness. This approach is based on the idea that adversarial attacks may have divergent effects on these two feature spaces, and the unaffected stream can compensate to maintain classification performance without adversarial retraining.
related_work: Current deep learning networks are susceptible to adversarial attacks. Existing defense methods, like adversarial training, often do not generalize across different attack types and can compromise classification accuracy on clean data. While multimodal fusion has been explored for tasks like scene and action recognition by combining features from different sources (e.g., Places and Imagenet), its application as a defense mechanism against adversarial attacks is not well-established. This work proposes to use a biologically-inspired, multi-stream fusion approach to improve adversarial robustness as a complementary strategy to existing defenses.
abstract: Mammalian brains handle complex reasoning tasks by integrating information from specialized regions, which allows for improved robustness. In contrast, deep neural networks typically process a single information stream and are susceptible to adversarial perturbations. While many defense methods exist, they often do not generalize and can negatively affect performance on clean data. We propose a fusion model using a combination of background and foreground features extracted in parallel from Places-CNN and Imagenet-CNN. We plan to test the benefits of this fusion approach on preserving adversarial robustness for both human-perceivable (e.g., Gaussian blur) and network-perceivable (e.g., gradient-based) attacks. We propose that this biologically inspired approach provides a new way to improve adversarial robustness that can be complementary to current state-of-the-art approaches.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of the contextual fusion model against human-perceivable perturbations, specifically Gaussian blur applied only to the foreground object.
experiments.Method: The model consists of a foreground classifier (ResNet18 pretrained on Imagenet), a background classifier (ResNet18 pretrained on Places365), and a joint classifier that fuses features from both using late fusion. The experiment will be run on a pruned version of the MS COCO dataset. Gaussian blur of varying intensity (sigma) will be applied to the foreground object in the test images. The performance of the three classifiers will be compared. The experiment will also be conducted on subsets of MS COCO with 'Similar' and 'Dissimilar' contexts to test the effect of context variability.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy as a function of the blur intensity (sigma)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the contextual fusion model as a defense against network-perceivable, gradient-based adversarial attacks (FGSM).
experiments.Method: Adversarial examples will be generated using the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) with varying attack strengths (epsilon), crafted specifically to attack the foreground classifier. The performance of the foreground, background, and joint classifiers will be evaluated on these adversarial examples. This experiment will be conducted on both a pruned MS COCO dataset and the CIFAR-10 dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy as a function of the attack strength (epsilon)']

experiments.Description: To investigate if regularizing the targeted (foreground) stream in the joint model can further enhance performance when the nature of the adversarial attack is known.
experiments.Method: An L2 penalty, controlled by a hyperparameter alpha, will be added to the loss function to regularize the weights of the foreground network within the joint classifier. This is intended to bias the joint classifier's decision towards the more reliable background stream. The performance of this regularized joint network will be evaluated on FGSM-attacked images from the MS COCO dataset and compared against a standard adversarial retraining defense baseline (Madry et al., 2017).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy as a function of attack strength (epsilon) for different levels of regularization (alpha)']

risk_factors: The degree of success for this method partially depends on the amount of variability in the background data.
In this work, we used a simple method of fusion - a single fully connected layer integrating the outputs of the object-centric and scene-centric networks.

title: Learning Latent Structural Causal Models
name: latent-scm-bayesian-inference
short_hypothesis: A tractable approximate inference method, BIOLS, can perform joint Bayesian inference over the causal variables, structure, and parameters of a latent Structural Causal Model (SCM) from low-level, high-dimensional data and known interventions. This is achieved by using variational inference to model the joint posterior over the latent SCM, specifically for linear Gaussian models.
related_work: Prior work in causal discovery typically assumes that causal variables are directly observed. In machine learning, however, one often deals with high-dimensional data (e.g., images) where the causal variables, their structure, and the mechanisms are all latent. While some methods address causal representation learning or structure learning with some latent variables, they often rely on supervision, known structures, or specific identifiability conditions. This paper addresses the problem of "latent causal discovery," where the entire Structural Causal Model (SCM) is latent and must be recovered from low-level observational and interventional data in an unsupervised manner.
abstract: In machine learning tasks, one often operates on low-level data like image pixels or high-dimensional vectors. In such settings, the entire Structural Causal Model (SCM) – structure, parameters, and high-level causal variables – is latent and needs to be learnt from low-level data. We treat this problem as Bayesian inference of the latent SCM, given low-level data. We present BIOLS, a tractable approximate inference method which performs joint inference over the causal variables, structure and parameters of the latent SCM from known interventions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method's ability to recover the latent SCM from high-dimensional synthetic vector data generated with different projection functions.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on synthetically generated data. Latent SCMs are created using random Erdős–Rényi (ER) DAGs. The resulting latent causal variables are projected to a 100-dimensional vector space using three different generative functions: an SO(n) projection, a linear projection, and a nonlinear projection (3-layer neural network). The model's performance will be compared against VAE, GraphVAE, ILCM, and ILCM-GT baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Expected Structural Hamming Distance (E-SHD)', 'Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method's ability to perform latent causal discovery from image pixels using a benchmark dataset.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the chemistry environment benchmark dataset (Ke et al., 2021), which generates 50x50 pixel images from underlying causal variables. The task is to recover the latent SCM from these images given known interventions. The model will be compared against VAE and GraphVAE baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Expected Structural Hamming Distance (E-SHD)', 'Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC)']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the model's ability to generalize by generating images from unseen interventional distributions.
experiments.Method: Using the model trained on the chemistry dataset, images will be sampled conditional on unseen interventional distributions. These generated images will be qualitatively compared to the ground truth images corresponding to the same unseen interventions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual comparison of generated images against ground truth samples.']

risk_factors: Extensions of the proposed method to learn nonlinear, non-Gaussian latent SCMs from unknown interventions would also open doors to general algorithms that can learn causal representations.

title: When Reasoning Meets Compression: Understanding the Effects of LLMs Compression on Large Reasoning Models
name: lrm-compression-effects-and-interpretation
short_hypothesis: The importance shift of weights should be minimal in an ideal compression scenario, as a compressed Large Reasoning Model (LRM) should remain as close as possible to its original, more reasoning-capable counterpart. Analyzing the change in relative importance of weights after compression can identify which reasoning capabilities are diminished and locate critical weights for reasoning.
related_work: Existing studies on compressing Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) are insufficient. They either fail to comprehensively compare the three main compression methods (quantization, distillation, pruning) or lack in-depth interpretation of how compression affects model internals. While some works benchmark quantization or pruning, they often use general-purpose LLM benchmarks rather than reasoning-intensive tasks. Distillation studies often do not evaluate on diverse reasoning tasks or consider the effect on parametric knowledge. A significant research gap is the lack of interpretability, which is necessary to understand how compression methods affect LRMs, reveal bottlenecks, and guide future research.
abstract: Compression methods, including quantization, distillation, and pruning, improve the computational efficiency of large reasoning models (LRMs). However, existing studies either fail to sufficiently compare all three compression methods on LRMs or lack in-depth interpretation analysis. In this paper, we investigate how the reasoning capabilities of LRMs are compromised during compression, through performance benchmarking and mechanistic interpretation. To uncover the effects of compression on reasoning performance, we propose to benchmark quantized, distilled, and pruned DeepSeek-R1 models on four reasoning datasets (AIME 2024, FOLIO, Temporal Sequences, and MuSiQue). To precisely locate compression effects on model weights, we plan to adapt difference of means and attribution patching techniques, focusing on the activation of every linear component in compressed LRMs, to interpret fine-grained causal relationships between weights and various reasoning capabilities. This fine-grained interpretation is intended to address a fundamental question of compression: which weights are the most important for reasoning?

experiments.Description: To benchmark the performance of various compressed versions of the DeepSeek-R1 model against the original model to understand how different compression strategies affect reasoning capabilities.
experiments.Method: The DeepSeek-R1 model and its distilled variants (R1-Distill-Llama, R1-Distill-Qwen) will be compressed using multiple techniques. These include dynamic quantization (Unsloth at 2.51, 1.73, 1.58-bit), post-training quantization (AWQ, GPTQ, GPTAQ, ANY4/3 at 4-bit and 3-bit), and pruning (SparseGPT, AlphaPruning at various sparsities). These models will be evaluated on a suite of reasoning tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on AIME 2024, FOLIO, and Temporal Sequences.', 'Exact Match (EM) and F1 score on MuSiQue.']

experiments.Description: To mechanistically interpret how compression affects model weights by quantifying the contribution of each linear module to specific reasoning capabilities and tracking how this importance shifts after compression.
experiments.Method: Using the original and compressed LRMs, we will adapt 'difference of means' and 'attribution patching' techniques. This will be used to compute an importance score for each linear module with respect to four core reasoning behaviors: backtracking, uncertainty estimation, example testing, and adding knowledge. We will then calculate the 'importance shift' (change in relative importance) from the original model to its compressed variant.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Importance scores (I_ml) for each module.', 'Relative importance shift (change of RI_ml) visualized via heatmaps.']

experiments.Description: To validate the importance scores by selectively quantizing components identified as most important and measuring the resulting performance degradation.
experiments.Method: For a distilled LRM (R1 distilled Llama-8B), we will apply 3-bit round-to-nearest quantization to a single component at a time. The components chosen will be those identified as most important (e.g., the up_projection in the final layer) and other components for comparison (e.g., other modules in the same layer or other layers in the same module type).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy drop on AIME 2024, FOLIO, and Temporal Sequences benchmarks.']

experiments.Description: To validate the hypothesis that current quantization methods overly compress certain modules by designing a selective protection mechanism and evaluating its effect on performance.
experiments.Method: We will apply 3-bit AWQ quantization to an LRM. In a separate run, we will apply the same quantization but protect the MLP modules in the final layer by keeping them at their original 16-bit precision. The performance of this mixed-precision model will be compared to the standard 3-bit quantized model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, EM, and F1 scores on the AIME 2024, FOLIO, Temporal Sequences, and MuSiQue benchmarks.']

risk_factors: We focus on analyzing the effect of compression methods on performance and thus do not consider inference speedup. The reason is that these methods run on different inference platforms, so it is hard to control the consistency of inference optimization across various platforms.

title: VIT-MOQ: REVISITING MOMENTUM QUEUES FOR RESOURCE-EFFICIENT VISION TRANSFORMERS AND DOMAIN GENERALIZATION IN SELF- SUPERVISED LEARNING
name: vit-moq
short_hypothesis: Symmetric encoder architectures are essential for stable momentum queue training in Vision Transformers. By reintroducing a momentum queue with a symmetric encoder setup (identical projection-only architectures for query and key encoders) and removing the prediction head, it is possible to resolve the incompatibility that previously prevented successful queue-transformer integration, leading to a compute-efficient and robust self-supervised learning method.
related_work: Current state-of-the-art self-supervised learning (SSL) methods like DINO and MAE achieve high performance but require substantial computational resources, such as large batch sizes (e.g., 4096) and multi-GPU clusters. This limits their accessibility. Early contrastive methods like MoCo successfully used a momentum queue to decouple batch size from the number of negative samples, but this approach was largely abandoned for Vision Transformers (ViTs) in favor of large-batch, in-batch sampling. The prevailing assumption was that momentum queues were obsolete for transformer architectures. Furthermore, domain generalization for SSL, especially in low-compute settings, remains an underexplored area. There is a need for a reliable, resource-efficient SSL method for ViTs that can be trained on modest hardware and learns transferable, domain-agnostic representations.
abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has achieved remarkable success in computer vision, but current state-of-the-art methods require substantial computational resources with large batch sizes and multi-GPU clusters. We present ViT-MoQ, a compute-efficient contrastive SSL method that reintroduces momentum queues to Vision Transformer architectures. Our key insight is that symmetric encoder architectures are essential for queue-based learning in ViTs, contrary to the asymmetric designs prevalent in recent SSL methods. ViT-MoQ is designed to achieve competitive performance while requiring only a single consumer GPU, considerably reducing compute requirements. This work challenges the assumption that momentum queues are obsolete in the transformer era and aims to demonstrate that architectural compatibility, not inherent limitations, was the barrier to their adoption. The goal is to make high-quality self-supervised learning accessible on modest hardware while learning more transferable, domain-agnostic representations.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the compute efficiency and downstream performance of ViT-MoQ on a large-scale dataset.
experiments.Method: The ViT-MoQ model, using a ViT-S/16 backbone, will be pre-trained on the ImageNet-1k dataset on a single RTX 4090 GPU. Performance will be evaluated using the linear probing protocol, where the backbone is frozen and a linear classifier is trained on top. The results will be compared against MoCo-v1, MoCo-v2, and MoCo-v3 baselines.

experiments.Description: Analyze the domain generalization capabilities of the learned features using a single-source protocol.
experiments.Method: The model will be pre-trained on the DomainNet-Real dataset. Then, the frozen backbone will be evaluated on other unseen domains from the DomainNet collection (Clipart, Infographic, Quickdraw, Painting, Sketch) to test its transferability. Performance will be compared against MoCo-v2 and MoCo-v3.

experiments.Description: Conduct stability analysis and ablation studies on a medium-sized, practical dataset.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on ImageNet-100 multiple times with the same parameter set to evaluate training stability and reproducibility. This setup is chosen to represent a more realistic setting for researchers with limited compute.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean and standard deviation of final downstream accuracy across multiple runs']

experiments.Description: Determine the optimal negative sampling diversity by evaluating a range of queue sizes.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on ImageNet-100 with systematically varied queue sizes, ranging from 4,096 to 65,536 samples.

experiments.Description: Ablation study to test the core hypothesis that a symmetric encoder architecture is necessary for stable training.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on ImageNet-100 under two configurations: one with the proposed symmetric encoder (no prediction head) and one with an asymmetric encoder (with a prediction head on the query encoder).

risk_factors: In line with the authors of MoCo-v3, we also observed instability during training.

While it was not possible to have multiple runs over different sets of parameters due to computational and time restrictions, we offer these runs as evidence that our method is stable and reproducible with mean = 69.742 and std = 0.1890
Here, due to differences in evaluation protocols, the comparison is fair only between certain groups.
While not being able to completely reproduce state-of-the-art downstream performance this way, a clear gain in compute efficiency is evident from plots 2 and 3, which was the main focus of our study.

title: Discrete Diffusion VLA: Bringing Discrete Diffusion to Action Decoding in Vision-Language-Action Policies
name: discrete-diffusion-vla
short_hypothesis: A unified transformer policy that models discretized action chunks with discrete diffusion can overcome the limitations of autoregressive or separate-head VLA models. This approach, which uses an adaptive 'easy-first, hard-later' decoding order and secondary re-masking for error correction, is hypothesized to preserve pretrained vision-language priors, support parallel decoding, break the autoregressive bottleneck, and reduce the number of function evaluations, while remaining natively compatible with the discrete token interface of VLMs.
related_work: Prevailing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models either generate actions autoregressively (AR) in a fixed order (e.g., OpenVLA) or use separate action decoders like MLPs or continuous diffusion heads (e.g., π, SmolVLA). AR approaches suffer from a sequential bottleneck, while separate decoders create fragmented information pathways and are decoupled from the VLM backbone, hindering scalability and unified training. This research aims to address this gap by unifying vision, language, and action generation within a single transformer using discrete diffusion, drawing inspiration from its success in language and image generation.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models adapt large vision-language backbones to map images and instructions into robot actions. However, prevailing VLAs either generate actions autoregressively in a fixed left-to-right order or attach separate MLP or diffusion heads outside the backbone, leading to fragmented information pathways and specialized training requirements that hinder a unified, scalable architecture. We present Discrete Diffusion VLA, a unified-transformer policy that models discretized action chunks with discrete diffusion. The design retains diffusion's progressive refinement paradigm while remaining natively compatible with the discrete token interface of VLMs. Our method achieves an adaptive decoding order that resolves easy action elements before harder ones and uses secondary re-masking to revisit uncertain predictions across refinement rounds, which improves consistency and enables robust error correction. This unified decoder preserves pretrained vision-language priors, supports parallel decoding, breaks the autoregressive bottleneck, and reduces the number of function evaluations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall task performance of Discrete Diffusion VLA on a variety of manipulation tasks.
experiments.Method: The model is fine-tuned on the LIBERO benchmark, which includes four suites: LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, LIBERO-Goal, and LIBERO-Long. The proposed model is compared against a range of baselines, including autoregressive (AR) token decoders (OpenVLA, Octo-Base, π+FAST, OpenVLA-OFT (Discrete)), separate MLP decoders, and continuous diffusion/flow-matching action heads (Diffusion Policy, MDT, π, OpenVLA-OFT (Cont.-Diffusion), GR00T-N1).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance and robustness on diverse scene variations using a Google Robot.
experiments.Method: The model is evaluated on the SimplerEnv-Fractal benchmark. It is compared against baselines including RT-1-X, RT-2-X, Octo-Base, OpenVLA, RoboVLM, π, π-FAST, and OpenVLA-OFT.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's real-to-sim performance on a WidowX Robot.
experiments.Method: The model is evaluated on the SimplerEnv-Bridge benchmark, fine-tuned on BridgeData-V2. It is compared against baselines such as RT-1-X, Octo-Base, OpenVLA, RoboVLM, π, and π-FAST.

experiments.Description: To ablate the components of the proposed decoding strategy and analyze its effect on performance.
experiments.Method: On the LIBERO-Goal suite, different decoding strategies are compared: one-shot parallel decoding, random order, confidence-gap selection, max-confidence selection, and the proposed max-confidence with secondary re-masking. The effect of choice temperature during sampling is also studied.

experiments.Description: To analyze the inference efficiency of the proposed method compared to other decoding paradigms.
experiments.Method: The number of function evaluations (NFEs), end-to-end latency, and throughput (action chunks per second) are measured and compared against autoregressive (AR) and one-shot parallel decoding baselines on a single NVIDIA H800 GPU.

experiments.Description: To validate the claim that the unified architecture preserves pretrained vision-language priors under out-of-distribution (OOD) perturbations.
experiments.Method: The model is evaluated on the LIBERO-Goal and LIBERO-Spatial benchmarks under two OOD conditions: (1) Language Augmentation, where task instructions are paraphrased, and (2) Vision Augmentation, where object appearances (materials, colors, sizes) are altered. Performance is compared to parallel decoding (OpenVLA-OFT Discrete) and continuous diffusion (OpenVLA-OFT Cont-Diffusion) baselines.

title: Exploring Redundancy and Shared Representations for Transformer Models Optimization
name: transformer-redundancy-and-compression-strategies
short_hypothesis: Structural and weight redundancies in Transformer architectures can be exploited for model compression through three novel techniques: 1) MASS, which enforces weight aggregation and sharing across blocks; 2) GlobaL Fact, which uses a shared global low-rank factor and layer-specific local factors; and 3) ABACO, which progressively transfers knowledge to low-rank adapters.
related_work: Prior work in model compression includes pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and low-rank factorization. Simple weight-sharing strategies have often failed to preserve accuracy, suggesting that redundancy is more subtle than a simple duplication of parameters. The degree of functional overlap across layers in Transformers, and its implications for structured compression, remains insufficiently understood.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) deliver state-of-the-art performance but at the cost of extreme computational and energy demands, raising the question of how much of their capacity is truly necessary. This paper explores structural and weight redundancies in Transformer-based architectures, aiming to identify inefficiencies and leverage them through targeted compression techniques. A central focus is assessing whether different modules perform overlapping functions and whether model matrices exhibit an inherently low-rank structure. To further explore these aspects, three novel compression methods are introduced: MASS, which enforces weight aggregation and sharing, along with two factorization-based techniques, GlobaL Fact and ABACO. This paper aims to contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the efficiency of LLMs.

experiments.Description: To investigate the degree of functional redundancy in Transformer models by assessing the performance impact of swapping modules between different layers.
experiments.Method: For Llama 3.1 (8B) and Gemma 2 (2B) models, systematically exchange feed-forward network (FFNN) and self-attention modules between different Transformer blocks (e.g., replace the module from block `i` with the one from block `i+k`). The performance of the modified model will be evaluated against a baseline where the original module is ablated instead of replaced. The evaluation will be conducted on language modeling benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the Matrix Aggregation and Sharing Strategy (MASS) for model compression.
experiments.Method: Apply MASS to Llama 3.1 (8B). Layers will be partitioned into groups of structurally compatible matrices (e.g., all 'query' matrices). Within each group, weights and biases will be merged into a single shared layer via simple averaging. The compressed model will then be fine-tuned on the OpenWebText dataset. The evaluation will compare the performance of the compressed model against the original, uncompressed model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of parameters / Compression Ratio', 'Performance on HellaSwag (before and after fine-tuning)', 'Performance on GSM8K (before and after fine-tuning)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the Global and Local Factorization (GlobaL Fact) compression method, which factorizes weights into a shared global matrix and layer-specific local matrices.
experiments.Method: Apply GlobaL Fact to the weight matrices of Llama 3.1 (8B). Selected weight matrices will be approximated as the product of a shared global matrix (Gs) and a layer-specific local matrix (Lj). The global matrix will be initialized using SVD. The compressed model will be fine-tuned on OpenWebText. The approach will be compared against a standard Truncated SVD baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of parameters / Compression Ratio', 'Performance on HellaSwag (before and after fine-tuning)', 'Performance on GSM8K (before and after fine-tuning)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the Adapter-Based Approximation and Compression Optimization (ABACO) method, which progressively transfers knowledge from full-rank matrices to low-rank adapters.
experiments.Method: Apply ABACO to Gemma 2 (2B). The method integrates LoRA adapters while fine-tuning on OpenWebText. During training, the contribution of the original pre-trained weights is progressively reduced via an exponential decay schedule, transferring knowledge to the trainable low-rank adapters. The original weights are later discarded or approximated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance on HellaSwag (after fine-tuning)', 'Performance on GSM8K (after fine-tuning)', 'Training and validation loss curves']

risk_factors: simple weight-sharing strategies often fail to preserve accuracy, indicating that redundancy may be more subtle than anticipated.
replacing multiple layers with a shared representation inevitably reduces layer-specific expressivity.
Exploring more sophisticated grouping criteria is left for future work.
Alternative strategies (e.g., weighted averaging or clustering-based aggregation) may better capture shared features and are left for future research

title: Clearer Frames, Anytime: Resolving Velocity Ambiguity in Video Frame Interpolation
name: distance-indexing-vfi
short_hypothesis: Instead of implicitly learning a complicated time-to-location mapping via a scalar timestep, explicitly providing a Video Frame Interpolation (VFI) network with a 'distance index'—a map indicating how far an object has traveled—will resolve speed ambiguity and lead to sharper interpolated frames. Furthermore, an iterative reference-based estimation strategy, which breaks long-range predictions into shorter, easier steps, will resolve the remaining directional ambiguity.
related_work: Existing learning-based Video Frame Interpolation (VFI) methods, for both fixed-time (t=0.5) and arbitrary-time (t ∈ [0, 1]) paradigms, use a scalar timestep 't' to specify the target frame. This approach suffers from 'velocity ambiguity,' where a single input set (start frame, end frame, t) can correspond to multiple valid ground-truth frames depending on the object's unknown acceleration and trajectory. This one-to-many mapping during training causes models to learn an average of the possibilities, resulting in blurry and imprecise interpolated frames. This paper aims to explicitly resolve this ambiguity rather than relying on the model to learn it implicitly.
abstract: Existing video frame interpolation (VFI) methods blindly predict where each object is at a specific timestep t ('time indexing'), which struggles to predict precise object movements, often resulting in blurry frames as the method averages out possibilities. Instead of forcing the network to learn this complicated time-to-location mapping implicitly, we provide the network with an explicit hint on how far the object has traveled between start and end frames, a novel approach termed 'distance indexing'. This method offers a clearer learning goal for models, reducing the uncertainty tied to object speeds. To solve the remaining directional ambiguity in long-range motion, especially halfway between frames, we propose an iterative reference-based estimation strategy that breaks down a long-range prediction into several short-range steps.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed 'distance indexing' and 'iterative reference-based estimation' strategies as plug-and-play modules on existing arbitrary time VFI models.
experiments.Method: The strategies will be integrated into four state-of-the-art VFI models: RIFE, IFRNet, AMT, and EMA-VFI. All models will be trained on the Vimeo90K septuplet dataset. The experiment will compare the performance of the baseline model using time indexing ([T]) against enhanced versions incorporating distance indexing ([D]), iterative reference-based estimation ([T, R]), and a combination of both ([D, R]).

experiments.Description: To validate the perceptual quality improvement of the proposed strategies through a human evaluation study.
experiments.Method: A user study with 30 anonymous participants will be conducted. Participants will be tasked with ranking the interpolation quality of frames produced by the four model variants: the baseline ([T]), distance indexing ([D]), iterative reference-based estimation ([T,R]), and the combined approach ([D,R]).

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of the proposed strategies on different benchmarks and for higher interpolation factors.
experiments.Method: The RIFE model, enhanced with the proposed strategies, will be evaluated on the Adobe240 and X4K1000FPS datasets for ×8 and ×16 interpolation tasks. Inference will be performed using uniform distance maps.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the novel capability of using distance indexing for per-object temporal manipulation, or 'manipulated interpolation of anything'.
experiments.Method: Object masks will be produced for a starting frame using the Segment Anything Model (SAM). Users can then customize the distance curve for each object mask to control its interpolation pattern (e.g., making one person move backward in time). The VFI model will then generate an interpolated sequence based on these specified, spatially-varying distance maps.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed distance indexing approach against traditional time indexing in a fixed-time (t=0.5) training setting.
experiments.Method: The RIFE model will be trained on the Vimeo90K triplet dataset using both traditional time indexing and the proposed distance indexing approach.

risk_factors: Firstly, the current system only allows for manipulations along a fixed trajectory. It does not possess the capability to model the distribution of possible trajectories.

In our current approach, we use a uniform distance map which assumes every object moves at constant speed along their trajectories. In practice, different objects may have different acceleration and therefore this constant speed assumption may lead to sub-optimal frame interpolation.

The overall effectiveness and accuracy of the model are intrinsically tied to the precision of optical flow estimation and the quality of segmentation masks (optional). Any inaccuracies or shortcomings in these preliminary steps can cascade and potentially impact the final interpolation results.

title: HANRAG: Heuristic Accurate Noise-resistant Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multi-hop Question Answering
name: hanrag
short_hypothesis: A novel heuristic-based framework, HANRAG, driven by a powerful master agent called a 'Revelator', can efficiently tackle multi-hop queries by routing them to appropriate processing chains, decomposing them into sub-queries, and filtering noise from retrieved documents. This will enhance the system's adaptability and noise resistance, making it more capable of handling diverse query types compared to standard RAG methods.
related_work: Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods face challenges with multi-hop queries. Some approaches are inefficient due to an over-reliance on iterative retrieval, especially for 'compound queries' which have independent sub-questions. Other methods struggle with 'irrational querying', where using the original complex query for multiple retrieval rounds fails to retrieve content relevant to specific sub-queries. This leads to 'noise accumulation', where irrelevant information from one retrieval step negatively impacts subsequent steps, degrading overall performance.
abstract: The Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approach enhances question-answering systems and dialogue generation tasks by integrating information retrieval (IR) technologies with large language models (LLMs). This strategy, which retrieves information from external knowledge bases to bolster the response capabilities of generative models, has achieved certain successes. However, current RAG methods still face numerous challenges when dealing with multi-hop queries. For instance, some approaches overly rely on iterative retrieval, wasting too many retrieval steps on compound queries. Additionally, using the original complex query for retrieval may fail to capture content relevant to specific sub-queries, resulting in noisy retrieved content. If the noise is not managed, it can lead to the problem of noise accumulation. To address these issues, we introduce HANRAG, a novel heuristic-based framework designed to efficiently tackle problems of varying complexity. Driven by a powerful revelator, HANRAG routes queries, decomposes them into sub-queries, and filters noise from retrieved documents. This enhances the system's adaptability and noise resistance, making it highly capable of handling diverse queries.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of HANRAG on single-hop question answering tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed HANRAG framework will be compared against several baselines: a method without retrieval (LLM only), a single-step BM25 retrieval method, IRCOT, Adaptive Retrieval, self-RAG, and Adaptive-RAG. The evaluation will be conducted on the SQUAD v1.1, Natural Questions, and TriviaQA datasets. A fair-comparison version, HANRAG-Fair, will also be tested using FLAN-T5-XL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of HANRAG on complex multi-hop question answering tasks.
experiments.Method: HANRAG will be compared against baselines including no retrieval, single-step retrieval, IRCOT, Adaptive Retrieval, self-RAG, and Adaptive-RAG. The evaluation will be performed on the HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of HANRAG on a newly constructed multi-hop compound query benchmark, focusing on its ability to decompose and answer parallel sub-queries.
experiments.Method: The proposed HANRAG framework will be compared with Adaptive-RAG on a custom-built multi-hop compound query dataset.

risk_factors: the gains remain limited, mainly due to the generative model's challenges in following instructions precisely and its insufficient ability to locate answers within a large scope ('needle-in-a-haystack' scenarios).

title: Communication-efficient Algorithms Under Generalized Smoothness Assumptions
name: normalized-ef-generalized-smoothness
short_hypothesis: Incorporating normalization into distributed error feedback algorithms (specifically, EF21 and EF21-SGDM) will guarantee convergence for nonconvex optimization problems that satisfy generalized smoothness assumptions, a setting where traditional analyses for error feedback fail. This will allow for larger, problem-parameter-independent stepsizes.
related_work: Traditional analyses of communication-efficient error feedback algorithms, such as EF14 and EF21, rely on the L-smoothness assumption (Lipschitz continuous gradients). However, many modern machine learning problems, particularly in deep learning, do not satisfy this condition but instead exhibit generalized smoothness (e.g., (L0, L1)-smoothness). While some first-order methods like gradient clipping and normalization have been analyzed under generalized smoothness, they have mostly been studied in single-node settings or under restrictive assumptions for distributed settings (e.g., bounded data heterogeneity, bounded variance). The theoretical understanding of error feedback algorithms under these more realistic generalized smoothness assumptions remains largely unexplored.
abstract: Traditional analyses of error feedback algorithms rely on the smoothness assumption, which does not capture the properties of objective functions in many modern machine learning problems like deep neural networks. These problems have recently been shown to satisfy generalized smoothness assumptions, but the theoretical understanding of error feedback algorithms under these conditions is largely unexplored. Furthermore, existing analyses under generalized smoothness are often limited to single-node settings or make strong, unrealistic assumptions for distributed settings. This paper proposes distributed error feedback algorithms that utilize normalization to achieve a convergence rate for nonconvex problems under generalized smoothness. The proposed analysis is intended to apply to distributed settings without restrictive data heterogeneity conditions and to enable stepsize tuning that is independent of problem parameters. The work also aims to provide strong convergence guarantees for normalized error feedback algorithms in stochastic settings.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of normalized EF21 against the original EF21 for minimizing nonconvex polynomial functions that satisfy generalized smoothness.
experiments.Method: The experiment will involve minimizing a nonconvex polynomial function in a single-node setting. The proposed algorithm, Normalized EF21 (EF21-norm), will be compared against the baseline EF21. A top-k sparsifier will be used for compression. The impact of the stepsize parameter γ₀ and the number of iterations K on convergence will also be investigated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Gradient norm squared (||∇f(x)||^2) vs. iteration count', 'Number of iterations required to achieve a target accuracy']

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of normalized EF21 and EF21 on a logistic regression problem with a nonconvex regularizer, which satisfies generalized smoothness.
experiments.Method: The task is logistic regression with a nonconvex regularization term. The proposed Normalized EF21 (EF21-norm) will be compared against the EF21 baseline. The evaluation will be conducted on the 'Breast Cancer' and 'a1a' datasets from LIBSVM, as well as a synthetically generated dataset. A top-k sparsifier will be used for compression.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of normalized EF21 against EF21 for training a ResNet-20 model on the CIFAR-10 dataset, a task empirically shown to satisfy the (L0, L1)-smoothness condition.
experiments.Method: The experiment involves training a ResNet-20 model for image classification on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The dataset will be distributed among 5 clients. The proposed Normalized EF21 (EF21-norm) will be compared against the EF21 baseline. A top-k compressor will be used for communication.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Gradient norm vs. communicated bits', 'Training Loss vs. communicated bits', 'Train Accuracy vs. communicated bits', 'Test Accuracy vs. communicated bits']

risk_factors: Our work implies many promising research directions. One interesting direction is to extend our convergence results for normalized EF21 and normalized EF21-SGDM to accommodate decreasing or adaptive stepsize schedules, as the constant stepsizes required by our current analysis can become impractically small when the total number of iterations is large.

In particular, applying appropriate decreasing stepsizes to EF21-SGDM could overcome its current theoretical requirement of a sufficiently large mini-batch size for the stochastic gradient at initialization.

title: Greedy Distill: Efficient Video Generative Modeling with Linear Time Complexity
name: Greedy-Distill-Linear-Complexity
short_hypothesis: A novel distillation paradigm, GREEDY DISTILL, can reduce the computational complexity of video generation from O(n²) to linear. This is proposed to be achieved by a student model composed of two new components: a Streaming Diffusion Decoder (SDD) that generates frames conditioned only on the first and last frames to avoid redundant computation, and an Efficient Temporal Module (ETM) to capture global temporal information. Furthermore, applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning is hypothesized to mitigate the error accumulation inherent in streaming generation.
related_work: Video generation models based on Diffusion Transformers (DiT) suffer from O(n²) computational complexity due to bidirectional attention, making long-form video generation computationally expensive. Existing distillation methods primarily focus on reducing sampling steps, overlooking the complexity along the frame dimension. While some recent methods like CausVid employ asymmetric distillation, they require the teacher and student models to be nearly isomorphic. This work addresses the need for a distillation framework that can significantly reduce computational complexity along the frame dimension while allowing for substantial architectural differences between teacher and student models.
abstract: Due to bidirectional attention dependencies, video generation models generally suffer from O(n²) computational complexity. This work identifies a "local inter-frame information redundancy" phenomenon, indicating strong local temporal dependencies with marginal contributions from global attention. Based on this, a novel distillation training paradigm, GREEDY DISTILL, is introduced. It proposes a Streaming Diffusion Decoder (SDD) to generate the next frame using only the 0-th and last frames, avoiding redundant computational costs. Concurrently, an Efficient Temporal Module (ETM) is introduced to capture global temporal information. These two modules are designed to reduce the computational complexity from O(n²) to linear. Additionally, this work proposes to be the first to apply RL fine-tuning to address error accumulation during streaming generation.

experiments.Description: To conduct a comprehensive quantitative and qualitative comparison of the proposed Greedy Distill method against existing state-of-the-art video generation and distillation models.
experiments.Method: The teacher model is the bidirectional DiT architecture of Wan2.1. The student model will adopt the Greedy Distill architecture (ETM and SDD), initialized from Wan2.1 and fine-tuned using LoRA. Training will use prompts from OpenVidHD and video data sampled from the teacher model. The proposed model will be compared against a range of baselines including LTX-Video, Wan2.1, SkyReels-V2, CausVid, Self Forcing (chunk-wise and frame-wise), NOVA, and Pyramid Flow.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: VBench benchmark (Total Score, Quality Score, Semantic Score), Throughput (FPS), and Latency (s).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the perceptual quality of the generated videos via a human assessment study, comparing against baseline methods.
experiments.Method: A user study will be conducted with 60 participants. They will be presented with video samples of varying durations (short, medium, long) generated by Greedy Distill and baseline models (Wan2.1-1.3B, CausVid, Self Forcing, Pyramid Flow) and asked to select the preferred video.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Human preference scores based on three dimensions: Text Alignment, Motion Quality, and Visual Quality.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's ability to mitigate error accumulation in long-duration video generation.
experiments.Method: Long-video generation experiments (e.g., 18 seconds) will be conducted. The performance of Greedy Distill will be compared against other methods capable of long video generation, such as CausVid, Self-Forcing, and StreamingT2V.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: VBench metrics, with a particular focus on the Temporal Coherence metric, as well as Frame Quality and Semantic Alignment over time.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to investigate the necessity and contribution of the key components of the proposed framework, specifically the Efficient Temporal Module (ETM) and the Reinforcement Learning (RL) Fine-tuning stage.
experiments.Method: The experiment will compare different configurations of the distillation framework: 1) A baseline model with direct distillation (similar to CausVid/Self-Forcing), 2) a model with the ETM component added, and 3) the full model including both ETM and RL Fine-tuning.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Total Score, Quality Score, and Semantic Score from the VBench benchmark.

risk_factors: Generally, error accumulation problem is more broadly known as exposure bias, where a model is trained exclusively on ground-truth context but must rely on its own imperfect predictions at inference time, resulting in a distributional mismatch that compounds errors as generation progresses.
In experiments, we observe that after next-chunk fine-tuning, the model already demonstrates certain generative capabilities, but it still suffers from error accumulation.
In addition, qualitative results reveal that some frames remain insufficiently sharp after next-chunk fine-tuning.

title: Building Neural Networks that are Robust to Adversarial Examples Using Probabilistic Loss Function
name: probabilistic-loss-for-adversarial-robustness
short_hypothesis: Putting a non-zero probability mass on all classes, with the correct class carrying the highest weight, may reduce over-fitting and render a trained DNN less vulnerable to adversarial attacks by modifying the standard cross-entropy loss function.
