related_work: Prior defenses against adversarial examples include adversarial training (computationally expensive), defensive distillation and ensemble methods (overhead of multiple models), and input transformations. This work proposes a method that modifies the loss function directly, aiming to avoid the overhead of training additional models or augmenting the input data.
abstract: Adversarial examples are an Achilles heel of deep neural networks, robbing them of their functional performance in mission-critical applications. This work proposes a novel method of making deep neural networks robust to adversarial examples by modifying the loss function to a soft version of cross-entropy loss for classification problems. For regression problems, the data distribution is examined using Bayesian techniques (Gaussian Mixture Model) and the loss function is modified using a posterior probability distribution. The approach is justified using mathematical derivation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed probabilistic loss function in defending against adversarial attacks on the MNIST image classification task.
experiments.Method: A deep neural network based on the Inception CNN architecture will be trained on the MNIST dataset. One model will be trained using the standard cross-entropy loss function as a baseline. A second model will be trained using the proposed calibrated probabilistic loss function (calibrated via Algorithm 1). Both trained models will be subjected to FGSM and Carlini-Wagner L2 attacks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy on adversarial examples generated by FGSM and Carlini-Wagner L2 attacks.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the defense's effectiveness on a more complex, large-scale image classification task using the Imagenet dataset.
experiments.Method: A model will be trained on the Imagenet dataset using the proposed probabilistic loss function. Its robustness will be tested against the Carlini-Wagner attack.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy on adversarial examples generated by the Carlini-Wagner attack.']

risk_factors: For regression problems, we do not know the number of clusters. Therefore, this becomes a hyper-parameter.
Investigating the effectiveness of other non-Gaussian priors is an avenue for future research.

title: Jorge: Approximate Preconditioning for GPU-Efficient Second-Order Optimization
name: jorge-approximate-preconditioning
short_hypothesis: A second-order optimizer, Jorge, can be developed to achieve the rapid convergence of second-order methods with the computational efficiency of first-order methods by approximating the preconditioner computation to completely eliminate matrix inverse calculations. This approximation is based on a truncated binomial series expansion of the inverse fourth root of the Shampoo preconditioner. Furthermore, its hyperparameters can be deterministically bootstrapped from a well-tuned SGD baseline, minimizing tuning effort.
related_work: First-order optimization methods like SGD and Adam are computationally inexpensive but can have slower convergence than second-order methods. State-of-the-art second-order optimizers like Shampoo and K-FAC offer better convergence but are bottlenecked by expensive matrix inverse computations, which are inefficient on GPUs. Prior work has attempted to mitigate this cost through parallel implementations or approximations using the Sherman-Morrison identity. The proposed method, Jorge, aims to fill the gap by creating a GPU-efficient second-order optimizer that avoids matrix inversions entirely, making it competitive with first-order methods in wall-clock time per iteration.
abstract: Despite their better convergence properties compared to first-order optimizers, second-order optimizers for deep learning have been less popular due to their significant computational costs. The primary efficiency bottleneck in such optimizers is matrix inverse calculations in the preconditioning step, which are expensive to compute on GPUs. In this paper, we introduce Jorge, a second-order optimizer that promises the best of both worlds – rapid convergence benefits of second-order methods, and high computational efficiency typical of first-order methods. We address the primary computational bottleneck of computing matrix inverses by completely eliminating them using an approximation of the preconditioner computation. This makes Jorge extremely efficient on GPUs in terms of wall-clock time. Further, we describe an approach to determine Jorge's hyperparameters directly from a well-tuned SGD baseline, thereby significantly minimizing tuning efforts.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the sample efficiency, wall-clock time, and generalization of Jorge against state-of-the-art first-order and second-order optimizers across several deep learning benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will be conducted on three tasks: 1) Image Classification with ResNet-50 on ImageNet (using both 256 and 1024 batch sizes), 2) Object Detection with Mask-RCNN on MS-COCO 2017, and 3) Image Segmentation with DeepLabv3 on MS-COCO 2017. Jorge will be compared against SGD, AdamW, Shampoo, and a parallel implementation of Shampoo. Hyperparameters for Jorge will be derived from the pre-optimized SGD baseline using the proposed 'single-shot tuning' rules.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sample efficiency (validation accuracy/IoU/mAP vs. number of epochs)', 'Total wall-clock time to reach a target validation metric', 'Peak validation accuracy after training for a maximum number of epochs']

experiments.Description: To measure the per-iteration computational efficiency of Jorge compared to first-order and other second-order optimizers.
experiments.Method: The per-iteration wall-clock time will be measured for training ResNet-50 on ImageNet and DeepLabv3 on MS-COCO. The comparison will be against SGD and Shampoo. For Jorge and Shampoo, the preconditioner update frequency will be set to every 50 iterations.

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of different learning rate schedules on Jorge's performance and potential for overfitting.
experiments.Method: Jorge will be tested with different learning rate schedules (e.g., step decay, cosine decay, polynomial decay) on tasks like ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10 and DeepLabv3 on MS-COCO. The hypothesis is that Jorge requires a high initial learning rate to escape sharp local minima and that certain schedules might lead to overfitting.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Validation accuracy/IoU progression over epochs', 'Training loss progression over epochs']

risk_factors: This is a major limitation of our method, and one which we plan to fix with a distributed implementation.
As future work, we plan to develop a single-shot hyperparameter bootstrapping strategy from AdamW as well.

title: Ferret-UI Lite: Lessons from Building Small On-Device GUI Agents
name: ferret-ui-lite
short_hypothesis: A compact, end-to-end 3B multimodal LLM can be built to be a competitive GUI agent for on-device deployment by curating a diverse mixture of real and synthetic GUI data, strengthening inference-time performance with chain-of-thought reasoning and visual tool-use, and employing a two-stage training strategy of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards.
related_work: The majority of existing GUI agents are built on large foundation models, which are computationally expensive and have high latency, making them unsuitable for on-device deployment where low latency, privacy, and connectivity are critical. While end-to-end agents that directly map screenshots to actions are an attractive alternative, building competitive small-scale versions remains a challenge. This work explores strategies to adapt modern GUI agent techniques (SFT, synthetic data generation, CoT, RL) to the resource-constrained setting of small, on-device models.
abstract: Developing autonomous agents that effectively interact with Graphic User Interfaces (GUIs) is a challenging open problem, especially for small on-device models. This paper proposes FERRET-UI LITE, a compact, end-to-end GUI agent designed to operate across mobile, web, and desktop platforms. The proposed method involves building a 3B agent by curating a diverse GUI data mixture from real and synthetic sources, strengthening inference-time performance through chain-of-thought reasoning and visual tool-use, and applying reinforcement learning with designed rewards.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's GUI grounding performance, which is the ability to map natural language instructions to target elements on a screen.
experiments.Method: The 3B FERRET-UI LITE model will be evaluated on the ScreenSpot-V2, ScreenSpot-Pro, and OSWorld-G benchmarks. Its performance will be compared against other small-scale models like UI-R1 (3B), UITars (2B), QwenVL 2.5 (3B), and Jedi (3B), as well as larger models such as UITars (7B), GTA1 (7B), and GUI-OWL (7B).

experiments.Description: To conduct an offline evaluation of the model's agentic planning and grounding capabilities in a mobile environment.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Android Control dataset, which features two task types: low-level tasks (single-step instructions) and high-level tasks (multi-step global goals). Performance will be compared against baselines including InternVL-2 (4B), OSAtlas (4B), and Qwen2-VL (7B).

experiments.Description: To conduct an online evaluation of the model's GUI navigation capabilities in fully functional, dynamic environments.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on two benchmarks: (1) AndroidWorld, a dynamic Android environment, with performance compared to models like QwenVL 2.5 (3B), ScaleCUA (3B), and UITars 1.5 (7B). (2) OSWorld-Verified, a benchmark for computer use simulation, with performance compared against models like ScaleCUA (3B), Kimi-VL (3B), OpenCUA (A3B), and Claude-4-Sonnet.

experiments.Description: To investigate the capabilities and limitations of small GUI agents through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Ablation experiments will be conducted to study: (1) The effect of different data mixture ratios of navigation and grounding data. (2) The impact of incorporating synthetic high-resolution data. (3) The effectiveness of synthetic Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data. (4) The contribution of SFT vs. RL training variants, including a zoom-in operation during RL. (5) The impact of different RL reward designs (action type, sparse grounding, dense grounding).

risk_factors: its performance on multi-step navigation remains limited, underscoring the inherent challenges of developing lightweight, on-device agents capable of robust long-horizon reasoning.
While small models can benefit from reinforcement learning, they are sensitive to RL reward designs, underscoring the difficulty of designing robust rewards across heterogeneous UI agentic tasks.
CoT yields structured reasoning, but only in the context of strong grounding and supervision.
Careful RL reward design is critical. Small models are highly sensitive to reward formulation.

title: VAE Approximation Error: ELBO and Exponential Families
name: vae-approximation-error-exponential-families
short_hypothesis: For Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) where both the encoder and decoder are conditional exponential families, the ELBO objective inherently pulls the learned decoder away from the maximum likelihood solution towards a restricted subclass of 'consistent' models (which are RBM-like). This approximation error cannot be reduced by simply using deeper neural networks for the encoder and decoder.
related_work: The VAE approximation error, or the gap between the true and approximate posterior, has been studied empirically. Many works have proposed mitigating this gap by using more expressive encoders, such as those with normalizing flows, hierarchical structures, or autoregressive models. However, these extensions often increase training difficulty and inference cost. There is a lack of theoretical understanding of the coupling between the decoder and the chosen encoder family, particularly for the common case where both are modeled as exponential families. This paper aims to theoretically analyze this coupling and its effect on the learned decoder.
abstract: The importance of Variational Autoencoders reaches far beyond standalone generative models — the approach is also used for learning latent representations and can be generalized to semi-supervised learning. This requires a thorough analysis of their commonly known shortcomings: posterior collapse and approximation errors. This paper analyzes VAE approximation errors caused by the combination of the ELBO objective and encoder models from conditional exponential families, including, but not limited to, commonly used conditionally independent discrete and continuous models. We propose to characterize subclasses of generative models consistent with these encoder families. We hypothesize that the ELBO optimizer is pulled away from the likelihood optimizer towards this consistent subset. Importantly, this subset can not be enlarged, and the respective error cannot be decreased, by considering deeper encoder/decoder networks.

experiments.Description: To illustrate the theoretical findings on a toy example by studying the influence of the encoder's factorization assumption on the learned model when the ground truth is a known Gaussian mixture model.
experiments.Method: A VAE will be trained on data from a ground truth Gaussian mixture model (4 components in 2D). The decoder will use a Gaussian distribution, and the encoder will be a factorized model over the binary representation of the latent code, implemented as a 2-layer MLP. Training will first involve pre-training the encoder with a fixed ground truth decoder, followed by joint optimization of the encoder and decoder to maximize ELBO. The resulting model posterior will be compared to the true posterior and the posterior of an RBM trained on the same data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effect of ELBO optimization on a realistic image generation task using the CelebA dataset.
experiments.Method: A ground truth generative model will be created by training a convolutional GAN on 64x64 CelebA images and adding Gaussian noise to its output. A VAE with a decoder architecture matching the GAN generator and a convolutional encoder will be trained. First, the encoder will be pre-trained by maximizing the conditional log-likelihood on samples from the ground truth model. Then, the encoder and decoder will be jointly trained by maximizing ELBO, starting from the pre-trained components. Two training variants will be explored: one with fixed image noise and one where the noise is a learnable parameter.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Frechet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Visual quality of generated images']

experiments.Description: To compare a proposed theoretically-motivated encoder design against standard practices for the semantic hashing of text documents.
experiments.Method: A Bernoulli VAE model for semantic hashing will be trained on the 20Newsgroups dataset. Three encoder variants will be compared: (e1) a linear encoder on raw word counts (the proposed correction), (e2) a deep 2-layer encoder on word frequencies, and (e3) a linear encoder on word frequencies. These encoders will be evaluated across different latent code sizes (8, 16, 32, 64 bits) and decoder depths (0, 1, 2 hidden layers).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Negative ELBO (NELBO) on training and test sets']

risk_factors: We restricted this study to exponential families. While in richer models discussed in the introduction, the approximation error still exists, it is made small by design, and it is less relevant and harder to analyze it theoretically.
The error is detrimental when the consistent subset is too restrictive.
The VAE approximation error may result in a useful regularization when the respective RBM is a good baseline model.

title: Dynamic Task-Embedded Reward Machines for \\ Adaptive Code Generation and Manipulation \\ in Reinforcement Learning
name: DTERM-for-code-generation
short_hypothesis: A hypernetwork-based framework, named Dynamic Task-Embedded Reward Machine (DTERM), can dynamically modulate reward components for RL-based code generation tasks by using task embeddings. This approach is intended to enable task-aware reward modeling, remove the need for manual reward engineering, and allow for zero-shot adaptation to unseen coding tasks by integrating feedback from compilers and static analysis into a dynamic reward structure.
related_work: Recent work in reinforcement learning for code generation has explored reward shaping with compiler feedback and human preferences. However, these approaches typically use fixed weights for different reward components (e.g., compilation, execution, style) and do not account for the dynamic nature of different coding tasks. While hypernetworks have been used for reward function generation, previous implementations focused on single-task settings without explicit task embeddings. The proposed work aims to bridge this gap by using a hypernetwork conditioned on task embeddings to dynamically generate reward weights, allowing the reward function to adapt to the specific requirements of each coding task.
abstract: Conventional reward models for reinforcement learning on code generation and manipulation tasks are often based on fixed weightings or manual tuning, which lacks flexibility for diverse tasks like translation, completion, and repair. To overcome this, we introduce Dynamic Task-Embedded Reward Machine (DTERM), a new machine learning approach that dynamically modulates reward components using a hypernetwork-driven architecture. This architecture is designed to balance the task-aware configuration of syntactic correctness, semantic correctness, and computational efficiency. The framework combines three key modules: a transformer-based task embedding generator, a modular reward decomposer, and a hypernetwork to generate context-dependent weights for sub-rewards.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of DTERM against static reward baselines on a diverse set of code generation tasks including summarization, translation, and completion.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the CodeXGLUE dataset. The DTERM model, which uses a hypernetwork to generate dynamic weights for sub-rewards based on CodeBERT embeddings, will be compared against three static reward baselines: `Uniform` weights, `Expert-Tuned` weights, and `GradNorm`. All models will be trained using the PPO algorithm.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['BLEU-4 (for summarization and translation)', 'Exact Match (for completion)', 'Overall reward values']

experiments.Description: To evaluate DTERM's performance on competitive programming problem-solving tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the APPS benchmark. The DTERM model will be compared against `Uniform`, `Expert-Tuned`, and `GradNorm` baselines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate DTERM's performance on code repair tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the DeepFix dataset. The DTERM model will be compared against `Uniform`, `Expert-Tuned`, and `GradNorm` baselines.

experiments.Description: To assess DTERM's ability to generate functionally correct code based on hand-written test cases.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the HumanEval benchmark. The DTERM model will be compared against `Uniform`, `Expert-Tuned`, and `GradNorm` baselines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the adaptability and zero-shot generalization of DTERM when applied to unseen coding task types.
experiments.Method: The DTERM model will be applied to a set of unseen tasks not encountered during training. Its performance will be compared against the static baseline approaches (`Uniform`, `Expert-Tuned`, `GradNorm`).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to isolate the contributions of DTERM's key architectural components.
experiments.Method: The full DTERM model's performance on the HumanEval benchmark will be compared against several ablated versions: without the hypernetwork, without task embeddings, without FiLM modulation, without compiler feedback, and using only static prototypes.

title: Listen, Think, and Understand
name: ltu-audio-language-model
short_hypothesis: By integrating a high-performance audio perception model with a large language model and training it on a newly created, large-scale audio question-answering dataset (OpenAQA-5M) using a perception-to-understanding curriculum, it is possible to create a single model that possesses both audio perception and emerging reasoning and comprehension abilities.
related_work: Existing audio models, while proficient at perception tasks like classifying sounds into predefined categories, lack reasoning and understanding capabilities. For example, they can recognize a clock chime but not infer the time. Conversely, modern large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning abilities and possess knowledge about audio, but they cannot perceive audio signals directly. This creates a gap where no single model effectively combines audio perception with deep reasoning. This work aims to bridge that gap by creating a multimodal large language model focused on general audio understanding, unlike prior work that has concentrated on visual or speech modalities.
abstract: The ability of artificial intelligence (AI) systems to perceive and comprehend audio signals is crucial for many applications. Although significant progress has been made, most existing models are designed to map audio inputs to pre-defined, discrete sound label sets. In contrast, humans possess the ability to not only classify sounds but also to listen to finer details, explain predictions, think about what the sound infers, and understand the scene. Such capabilities beyond perception are not yet present in existing audio models. On the other hand, modern large language models (LLMs) exhibit emerging reasoning ability but they lack audio perception capabilities. Therefore, we ask the question: can we build a model that has both audio perception and reasoning ability? In this paper, we propose a new audio foundation model, called LTU (Listen, Think, and Understand). To train LTU, we created a new OpenAQA-5M dataset consisting of 1.9 million closed-ended and 3.7 million open-ended, diverse (audio, question, answer) tuples, and have used an autoregressive training framework with a perception-to-understanding curriculum.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's perception ability on a variety of classic closed-ended audio classification tasks across multiple benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The proposed LTU model will be evaluated on eight audio classification benchmarks: ESC-50, DCASE2017 Task 4, VocalSound, TUT 2017, Beijing Opera, VGGSound, FSD50K, and AudioSet. The model's text output will be compared to label names using text embeddings and cosine similarity. Performance will be benchmarked against the contrastive audio-text model CLAP and a concurrent work.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to generate descriptive captions for audio clips on standard captioning benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The LTU model will be prompted to 'write an audio caption describing the sound'. The generated captions will be evaluated on the test sets of the Clotho v2 and AudioCaps benchmarks. Performance will be compared with SOTA specialized models.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's emerging audio reasoning and comprehension capabilities on open-ended tasks through qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
experiments.Method: A two-stage human subjective evaluation will be conducted via Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT). In Stage 1, human evaluators will assess LTU's answers to open-ended questions generated by GPT-4. In Stage 2, evaluators will assess LTU's answers to human-generated questions. Additionally, an automated evaluation using GPT-4 will be performed to assess the instruction-following rate of the model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Instruction following rate', 'Factual correctness rate', 'Human preference rate']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to justify the design choices of the training framework, including the use of LoRA adapters and the perception-to-understanding curriculum.
experiments.Method: Several versions of the LTU model will be trained and evaluated: 1) with full finetuning of LLaMA instead of LoRA, 2) without the perception-to-understanding curriculum, 3) trained only on closed-ended tasks, 4) without LoRA adapters, and 5) with a frozen audio branch. These models will be evaluated on classification and language tasks.

risk_factors: In this work, we mainly focus on general audio understanding and LTU thus has limited ability to understand speech content, i.e., it is not an automatic speech recognition model.

First, the LLaMA-7B model used in LTU is the smallest model in the LLaMA model family, LLaMA-13B, and larger models are more commonly used and may have a stronger reasoning and understanding ability. Particularly, larger LLM may exhibit emerging properties that the smaller LLM model does not have. While we show LTU exhibits stronger audio reasoning and understanding abilities than existing models, it could have been even stronger if a larger LLM was used.

Second, for the same computational efficiency consideration, we applied a 2× temporal downsampling to make the audio token frequency at 3.2Hz. This may limit the fine-grained temporal reasoning ability of the model.

Third, while we used 8 audio datasets to construct the OpenAQA-5M dataset, only a small portion of them are with temporal-level annotation (i.e., the onset and offset of the sound event), which is important information for GPT-3.5-Turbo to generate time-related questions. Therefore, the capability of the model to temporally localize the sound event might be weaker than its capability to recognize the sound events.

Finally, while we find closed-ended training and the unanswerable training questions greatly mitigate the hallucination issue (the model will answer "I do not know" to unanswerable questions), hallucination and bias may not be completely eliminated and may occur in some cases.

title: Extending Fourier Neural Operators for Modeling Parameterized and Coupled PDEs
name: fno-parameterized-coupled-pdes
short_hypothesis: Minimal architectural modifications to Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) can effectively model parameterized and coupled PDEs. Specifically, a lightweight hypernetwork-based modulation can condition the operator on physical parameters for parameterized dynamics, and a spectral-domain coupling mechanism can efficiently capture cross-variable interactions in coupled systems while preserving the efficiency of standard FNOs.
related_work: While neural operators (NOs) have shown success, most existing studies overlook the prevalent case of parameterized dynamics with fixed initial conditions. Explicit modeling of physical parameters within the FNO family is relatively underexplored; existing methods like HyperFNO infer large parts of the model via a hypernetwork, which can be complex. For coupled systems, prior works have introduced specialized architectures, but there is little systematic guidance on how to minimally extend standard FNOs to handle cross-variable interactions efficiently without sacrificing model compactness.
abstract: Parameterized and coupled partial differential equations (PDEs) are central to modeling phenomena in science and engineering, yet neural operator methods that address both aspects remain limited. We extend Fourier neural operators (FNOs) with minimal architectural modifications along two directions. For parameterized dynamics, we propose a hypernetwork-based modulation that conditions the operator on physical parameters. For coupled systems, we conduct a systematic exploration of architectural choices, examining how operator components can be adapted to balance shared structure with cross-variable interactions while retaining the efficiency of standard FNOs.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed FNO extensions on a parameterized, coupled PDE system representing 1D capacitively coupled plasma (CCP) dynamics with fixed initial conditions. The experiment will be run in three separate settings, varying a single physical parameter (reaction rate R₀, driving voltage V₀, or ion mass mᵢ) at a time.
experiments.Method: The task is to learn a one-step evolution map for the time-dependent PDE system, predicting electron density and electric potential. The proposed models, FNOx (the coupled extension), pFNOx (parameterized via input augmentation), and hpFNOx (parameterized via hypernetwork modulation), will be compared against a suite of baselines: FNOc, CFNO, MWTc, CMWNO, HyperFNO, DeepONets (DONc), and U-Nets (U-Netc). Data is to be generated using a finite-difference solver for the 1D CCP equations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed methods on the Gray-Scott benchmark, a system of coupled reaction-diffusion equations with parameterized dynamics, under varying feed-rate and diffusion coefficient conditions.
experiments.Method: The task is to predict the spatiotemporal evolution of two reacting chemical species in an autoregressive manner. The proposed models (FNOx, pFNOx, hpFNOx) will be compared against baselines including FNOm, FNOc, CFNO, MWTc, CMWNO, DONc, and U-Netc. Data is generated by numerically solving the Gray-Scott equations for different parameter configurations.

risk_factors: it is difficult to fully isolate the influence of coupling strength alone, since changes in νm simultaneously affect both the degree of coupling and the stiffness of the governing dynamics, thereby altering the learning difficulty and the numerical conditioning of the operator.
We note that this analysis is intended to investigate general extrapolation behavior rather than to claim superior OOD performance compared to large-scale or foundation models.

title: Variational Component Decoder for Source Extraction from Nonlinear Mixture
name: sVCD-for-Nonlinear-Source-Extraction
short_hypothesis: A supervised Variational Component Decoder (sVCD), which combines a sequence-to-sequence architecture with variational inference, can effectively extract a single desired source signal from a nonlinear mixture. This is achieved by optimizing a modified, tighter variational lower bound specifically designed for the source extraction task, rather than the standard VAE objective.
related_work: Conventional methods for signal extraction are divided into two categories: Blind Source Separation (BSS), which recovers all sources and is inefficient when only one is needed, and methods that leverage prior knowledge to extract a specific component. While deep learning has been applied to BSS and disentangled representation learning (e.g., VAEs), these approaches are either designed for separating all sources or are not directly applicable to signal mixtures from time-series. Existing semi-blind methods do not seem to suit the specific need of extracting a single source from a nonlinear mixture. This paper proposes a method to fill this gap by focusing on supervised, single-source extraction.
abstract: In many practical scenarios of signal extraction from a nonlinear mixture, only one (signal) source is intended to be extracted. However, modern methods involving Blind Source Separation are inefficient for this task since they are designed to recover all sources in the mixture. In this paper, we propose supervised Variational Component Decoder (sVCD) as a method dedicated to extracting a single source from nonlinear mixture. sVCD leverages the sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) translation ability of a specially designed neural network to approximate a nonlinear inverse of the mixture process, assisted by priors of the interested source. In order to maintain the robustness in the face of real-life samples, sVCD combines Seq2Seq with variational inference to form a deep generative model, and it is trained by optimizing a variant of variational bound on the data likelihood concerning only the interested source.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed sVCD model in extracting a target source from various nonlinear mixtures and compare its performance against state-of-the-art baselines.
experiments.Method: The sVCD model will be evaluated on three distinct datasets: 1) an Artificially Generated Dataset with temporally dependent sources mixed by an MLP, 2) an RF-PPG Dataset for extracting heartbeat waveforms from radar signals, and 3) an EEG-EOG Dataset for isolating eye movement signals from EEG recordings. The proposed model is an RNN-based encoder-decoder with a scaled dot product attention mechanism. It is to be trained with an Adam optimizer. Performance will be compared against two baseline methods: Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and Conv-TasNet.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cosine similarity between the recovered source component and the ground truth source component.']

experiments.Description: To perform an ablation study on the main design components of sVCD to understand their respective contributions to the model's performance.
experiments.Method: The performance of the full sVCD model will be compared against three ablated versions: 1) a 'Seq2Seq' version that skips the variational inference part, 2) a version with only mean regularization instead of full distribution regularization, and 3) a version without the attention mechanism. The evaluation will be conducted on the Artificially Generated, RF-PPG, and EEG-EOG datasets.

risk_factors: The sVCD in this paper is used as a vehicle to validate the overall mathematical framework, but better architectures are likely to be found.

As sVCD is built on RNN structure, there is no strict requirement on the length of input sequence... it is better to take a sufficiently long sequence as it is hard to judge the minimum yet sufficient length a priori.
The RF-PPG data will not be made available to the public to protect the privacy of the subjects.

title: Multi-Action Self-Improvement For Neural Combinatorial Optimization
name: multi-action-self-improvement-nco
short_hypothesis: By extending self-improvement to operate over joint multi-agent actions, where a model predicts complete agent-task assignments jointly at each step and is trained with a set-prediction loss, it is possible to explicitly leverage agent-permutation symmetries. This approach is hypothesized to enhance sample efficiency, improve the ability to learn coordinated behavior, and accelerate solution generation compared to standard single-action self-improvement methods.
related_work: Self-improvement has become a state-of-the-art paradigm in Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO), where models learn by imitating high-quality pseudo-expert solutions they generate. However, existing methods are based on next-token prediction, which is fundamentally limited for multi-agent problems that exhibit agent-permutation symmetries (e.g., vehicle routing, scheduling). By supervising on a single 'best' next action, these approaches implicitly enforce an arbitrary agent order, treating symmetric choices as errors. This reduces sample efficiency and hinders the model's ability to learn coordinated behavior. While other methods like POMO or DPN exploit symmetries, they do so via specialized baselines rather than changing the action generation and learning objective itself.
abstract: Self-improvement has emerged as a state-of-the-art paradigm in Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO), but existing methods are computationally expensive and fail to exploit the structure of multi-agent combinatorial problems. By supervising on single-action trajectories, they fail to exploit agent-permutation symmetries, where distinct sequences of actions yield identical solutions, hindering generalization and the ability to learn coordinated behavior. This work addresses these challenges by extending self-improvement to operate over joint multi-agent actions. The proposed model architecture, MACSIM, predicts complete agent-task assignments jointly at each decision step. To explicitly leverage symmetries, a set-prediction loss is employed to supervise the policy on multiple expert assignments for any given state. This approach is designed to enhance sample efficiency, the ability to learn coordinated behavior, and accelerate the solution generation phase of the self-improvement loop.

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of the proposed MACSIM method on the Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSP), a challenging multi-agent scheduling task.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate MACSIM on FJSP instances. It will be compared against a state-of-the-art CP-SAT solver (OR-Tools), classical priority dispatching rules (FIFO, MOR, MWKR), other learning-based approaches (PPO, DANIEL), and a standard self-improvement baseline (SLIM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective value (Obj.)', 'Gap to best-known solutions', 'Average inference latency']

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of MACSIM on the Flexible Flow Shop Problem (FFSP), another representative multi-agent scheduling task.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate MACSIM on FFSP instances. It will be compared against traditional baselines (Gurobi, Genetic Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization) and neural baselines (MatNet, PolyNet, PARCO, SLIM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective value (Obj.)', 'Gap to best-known solutions', 'Average inference latency']

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of MACSIM on the Min-Max Heterogeneous Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (HCVRP), a multi-agent routing problem.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate MACSIM on HCVRP instances. It will be compared against heuristic baselines (Simulated Annealing, SISR) and other neural baselines (Equity-Transformer (ET), DRLLi, 2D-Ptr, DPN, SLIM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective value (Obj.)', 'Gap to best-known solutions', 'Average inference latency']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization performance of MACSIM by testing models trained on small FJSP instances on larger, unseen instance distributions.
experiments.Method: Models trained on small FJSP instances will be evaluated on larger instances. MACSIM will be compared against OR-Tools, PPO, DANIEL, and SLIM.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to assess the impact of MACSIM's key components, specifically the autoregressive sampling procedure and the skip token.
experiments.Method: On the FJSP task, the full MACSIM model will be compared against variants using different sequence generation modes (random-order, fixed-order) and a variant without the skip token.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed permutation-invariant surrogate loss function against alternative loss formulations.
experiments.Method: On FJSP and FFSP tasks, the performance of MACSIM trained with the proposed set-based cross-entropy loss (L_CE) will be compared to training with a single-agent cross-entropy loss (L_SA), a multi-agent Maximum Likelihood Estimation loss (L_ML), and a Plackett-Luce loss (L_PL).

risk_factors: Although MACSIM substantially improves inference and training time compared to standard self-improvement, its reliance on step-wise encoding results in slower generation than some lightweight neural solvers, which may hinder applicability in latency-critical settings.

title: Aligner, Diagnose Thyself: A Meta-Learning Paradigm for Fusing Intrinsic Feedback in Preference Alignment
name: aligner-diagnose-thyself
short_hypothesis: A meta-learning paradigm can empower a language model to diagnose the reliability of preference pairs by systematically fusing multiple streams of intrinsic feedback—specifically preference consistency, learning difficulty, and generation confidence. This allows the model to learn an adaptive reweighting scheme for training samples, making the alignment process more robust to noisy preference labels than methods relying on single heuristics.
related_work: Existing methods for robust preference alignment fall into two categories. The first uses coarse-grained adjustments like global noise estimates (e.g., cDPO, rDPO), which lack instance-specific precision. The second leverages single-heuristic criteria, such as perplexity difference (PPLDiff) in PerpCorrect, to identify noisy samples. However, these single heuristics are often myopic and fail to capture the multifaceted nature of preference noise. This work builds on meta-learning for robustness, which typically trains a small 'meta-net' to reweight samples based on their training loss. The proposed novelty is to feed a richer, multi-dimensional 'diagnostic vector' into this meta-learner, instead of just a single signal like loss, to achieve a more holistic assessment of sample reliability.
abstract: The alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences is critically undermined by noisy labels in training datasets. Existing robust methods often prove insufficient, as they rely on single, narrow heuristics such as perplexity or loss, failing to address the diverse nature of real-world noise. We challenge this limited-scope approach by introducing a new paradigm where models learn to diagnose thyself, systematically fusing multiple streams of intrinsic feedback for a holistic reliability assessment of each preference pair. We instantiate this paradigm through a meta-learning methodology that learns to adaptively reweight samples based on a rich diagnostic vector. This vector captures three complementary perspectives: preference consistency, learning difficulty, and generation confidence.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the proposed diagnostic fusion approach outperforms state-of-the-art robust alignment baselines across various synthetic noise conditions.
experiments.Method: The method will be evaluated on the Golden HH and OASST1 preference datasets. Noisy preferences will be simulated by randomly swapping 'chosen' and 'rejected' labels for a fraction є ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4} of the training samples. The models used will be Llama-2-7B and Phi-2. The approach will be compared against baselines including Vanilla DPO, cDPO, rDPO, DR-DPO, and PerpCorrect (in both static and dynamic settings). A small, clean meta-dataset (M=100) will be held out for the meta-learning process.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to determine if the fusion of multiple diagnostics is necessary and to disentangle the individual contributions of each diagnostic component (preference consistency, learning difficulty, generation confidence).
experiments.Method: This experiment will be run on the Golden HH dataset with a 30% noise condition. Several variants of the proposed method will be evaluated, each restricted to a single diagnostic input: 'Ours (PPLDiff only)', 'Ours (Loss only)', and 'Ours (Uncertainty only)'. These will be compared to the full fusion model.

experiments.Description: To validate the scalability and real-world applicability of the proposed method on large-scale datasets with naturally occurring noise, beyond synthetic label-flipping.
experiments.Method: The approach will be evaluated on large-scale datasets with natural noise: HuggingFace H4 StackExchange Preferences (10.8M samples) and GPT4All (0.8M samples). Additional evaluations will be performed on WebGPT and Chatbot Arena, which contain organic human annotation noise. The method will be compared against Vanilla DPO, DR-DPO, and PerpCorrect (Dynamic).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's robustness against sophisticated adversarial noise patterns designed to exploit weaknesses in diagnostic-based methods.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will be conducted on the Golden HH dataset with a 30% noise rate under three adversarial scenarios: 1) Targeted High-PPL Flipping, 2) Strategic Annotation Targeting, and 3) an Adaptive Attack where a white-box adversary crafts noise to minimize the diagnostic signature. The full fusion method will be compared against Vanilla DPO, PerpCorrect (Dyn.), DR-DPO, and a PPL-only variant of the proposed method.

risk_factors: A crucial question is how our method performs if the meta-dataset itself is not perfectly clean.

Expert curation is effective but costly: Manual verification by domain experts yields the highest purity (97%) and best performance (+0.8%), but requires 2-3 hours of expert time—acceptable for safety-critical applications.

Training our full fusion method for one epoch on the Golden HH dataset with Llama-3-8B required approximately 8 hours on a single A40 GPU. In comparison, standard DPO took approximately 6 hours. The overhead is primarily due to the dynamic computation of diagnostics and the bi-level optimization loop.

title: Speeding up Policy Optimization with Vanishing Hypothesis and Variable Mini-Batch Size
name: ppo-vanishing-hypothesis-variable-minibatch
short_hypothesis: Incorporating two forms of vanishing noise into the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm—a hypothesis term in the reward function and a variable mini-batch size that increases over time—will reduce training time while preserving convergence properties and final performance.
related_work: Policy-based reinforcement learning algorithms like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) are known to be sample inefficient and have long training times. Prior work in stochastic optimization, such as simulated annealing, has shown that deliberately injecting noise that vanishes over time can speed up the optimization process without compromising convergence. However, the application and theoretical justification of such techniques for modern deep RL algorithms like PPO are not well-established. This work aims to adapt the concept of vanishing noise to PPO by introducing it in two specific ways: through a variable mini-batch schedule and a hypothesis-guided reward term.
abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms, despite their good performance, suffer from long training times. This work outlines a novel method to reduce the training time of algorithms like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The approach is evaluated on a practical astronomical problem: placing observatory stations in the Solar System to maximize the observation of space objects. To speed up training, the method applies noisy evaluation by incorporating additional noise into the reward function. This noise takes the form of a 'hypothesis term' and a 'varying mini-batch size'. To maintain theoretical convergence guarantees, both noise sources are designed to vanish as training progresses.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the proposed vanishing noise techniques can speed up the training of a PPO agent for an optimal sensor placement task without degrading performance.
experiments.Method:
The task is to find the optimal placement for a fixed number of observatory stations on Earth to maximize the coverage of celestial bodies in the Solar System over a specified time interval. The problem is modeled as a Markov decision process and solved with a PPO agent implemented in the Unity ML-Agents Toolkit. The experiment will compare the training speed of four methods:
1. Standard PPO (baseline).
2. PPO with a vanishing hypothesis term added to the reward.
3. PPO with a variable mini-batch size that increases during training.
4. PPO with both the hypothesis term and the variable mini-batch size.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training time (in hours) to reach a target reward level.', 'Reward earned over training time.']

risk_factors: The adjustments of the parameters discussed throughout this section are task-dependent and will be given properly in section 4.
As a relatively new approach, in spite of its very popular and well-applicable nature, the theoretical foundations [for PPO] have already just begun recently.

Though numerous very efficient strategies have been proposed to improve exploration, unfortunately, the theoretical preservation of the convergence has been rarely proved and even in such cases strict limitations had to be applied.

title: Fluid: Scaling Autoregressive Text-to-image Generative Models with Continuous Tokens
name: fluid-continuous-token-ar-models
short_hypothesis: Autoregressive text-to-image models will scale more effectively and produce higher quality images if they operate on continuous tokens instead of discrete ones, and if they generate tokens in a random order with bidirectional attention (like BERT) rather than a fixed raster-scan order (like GPT).
related_work: Autoregressive (AR) models are standard for language but lag behind diffusion models for text-to-image generation. Existing AR vision models often use discrete tokenizers (VQ), which may cause information loss and limit performance. It is also unclear whether the fixed, sequential generation order (raster-scan) optimal for language is suitable for visual content. The scaling laws observed in LLMs have not been clearly demonstrated for AR vision models, particularly concerning evaluation metrics beyond validation loss.
abstract: Scaling up autoregressive models in vision has not proven as beneficial as in large language models. This work investigates this scaling problem in the context of text-to-image generation, focusing on two critical factors: whether models use discrete or continuous tokens, and whether tokens are generated in a random or fixed raster order using BERT- or GPT-like transformer architectures. Inspired by an empirical study of these factors, the paper proposes Fluid, a random-order autoregressive model that operates on continuous tokens, to bridge the scaling gap between vision and language models.

experiments.Description: To conduct a comprehensive empirical study on the scaling behavior of autoregressive text-to-image models by analyzing two key factors: token representation (continuous vs. discrete) and generation order (random vs. raster).
experiments.Method: Four variants of autoregressive models will be constructed, combining the two factors: (1) Raster Order + Discrete Tokens, (2) Random Order + Discrete Tokens, (3) Raster Order + Continuous Tokens, and (4) Random Order + Continuous Tokens. Models will be scaled from 150M to 3B parameters and trained on a subset of the WebLI dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Validation loss on the MS-COCO 2014 training set', 'Zero-shot Frechet Inception Distance (FID) on MS-COCO', 'GenEval score', 'Qualitative visual inspection of generated images']

experiments.Description: To benchmark the proposed model, Fluid (a random-order model with continuous tokens), against leading text-to-image generation systems.
experiments.Method: The Fluid model will be scaled up to 10.5B parameters and trained on the WebLI dataset. It will be compared against previous systems including diffusion models (LDM, DALL-E 2, DALL-E 3, Imagen, SD3, Transfusion, RAPHAEL) and other autoregressive models (CM3Leon, Show-o, Muse, Parti).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Zero-shot FID on MS-COCO 30K', 'GenEval benchmark overall score']

risk_factors: Since both FID and GenEval scores have lower/upper bounds, this linear relationship cannot hold indefinitely. Future work should explore the limits of this correlation.

title: Articulation in Motion: Prior-free Part Mobility Analysis for Articulated Objects By Dynamic-Static Disentanglement
name: aim-prior-free-articulation
short_hypothesis: By analyzing an object interaction video instead of two static states, a proposed dual-Gaussian representation can disentangle static and dynamic components. This clean separation of motion cues will enable a subsequent sequential RANSAC algorithm to perform robust, prior-free part segmentation and articulation analysis, automatically determining the number of parts and their kinematics.
related_work: Recent methods for articulated object analysis typically rely on observing the object in two distinct states (start and end). These approaches perform per-point segmentation and optimize articulation, but often require a priori knowledge of the number of parts. Their performance degrades when there is no clear geometric correspondence between the two states, such as when new parts are revealed during motion (e.g., the interior of a refrigerator). This reliance on two static states and prior knowledge about the part count limits their application and robustness.
abstract: Articulated objects are ubiquitous in daily life. Our goal is to achieve a high-quality reconstruction, segmentation of independent moving parts, and analysis of articulation. Recent methods analyse two different articulation states and perform per-point part segmentation, optimising per-part articulation using cross-state correspondences, given a priori knowledge of the number of parts. Such assumptions greatly limit their applications and performance. Their robustness is reduced when objects cannot be clearly visible in both states. To address these issues, in this paper, we present a new framework, Articulation in Motion (AiM). We infer part-level decomposition, articulation kinematics, and reconstruct an interactive 3D digital replica from a user–object interaction video and a start-state scan. We propose a dual-Gaussian scene representation that is learned from an initial 3DGS scan of the object and a video that shows the movement of separate parts. It uses motion cues to segment the object into parts and assign articulation joints. Subsequently, a robust, sequential RANSAC is employed to achieve part mobility analysis without any part-level structural priors, which clusters moving primitives into rigid parts and estimates kinematics while automatically determining the number of parts. The proposed approach separates the object into parts, each represented as a 3D Gaussian set, enabling high-quality rendering.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed method (AIM) on part segmentation, reconstruction quality, and articulation estimation accuracy against state-of-the-art two-state methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a synthetic dataset rendered from PartNet-Mobility objects, including challenging two-part, three-part, and multi-part objects where interior parts are gradually revealed. AIM will be given a multi-view scan of the start state and an interaction video (200-500 frames). Baselines will be given 100 random views of the start and end states. The method will be compared against baselines: PARIS†, DTA, and ArtGS. A baseline version of the proposed method, Ours-b, which uses a single deformable 3DGS, will also be evaluated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Part segmentation performance using 3D Intersection-over-Union (IoU).', 'Reconstruction quality using bi-directional Chamfer Distance (mm).', 'Articulation estimation accuracy using Axis Angle Error (°), Axis Position Error (0.1m), and Part Motion (° or m).']

experiments.Description: To analyze the effectiveness of individual components of the AIM framework through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on complex objects from the synthetic dataset (Storage-47648, Table-31249). The full model will be compared against several ablated versions: one without the start state scan, one without the static-during-motion detection (SDMD) module, one without the dual-Gaussian representation (using a single deformable 3DGS), and one without sequential RANSAC (attempting DB-SCAN or K-means instead).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average Axis Angle Error (Ang_avg)', 'Average Axis Pose Error (Pose_avg)', 'Average Part Motion Error (Motion_ang/avg)', 'Average Chamfer Distance on moving parts (CD-m_avg)', 'Average 3D IoU on dynamic parts (3D IoU_d_avg)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's performance on real-world data captured from a first-person perspective.
experiments.Method: Data will be captured using Meta Project Aria Glasses, recording a user interacting with an oven and a storage cabinet. The video will be processed using COLMAP to estimate camera poses. The performance of AIM will be qualitatively assessed on these sequences.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of rendered views against ground truth.', 'Visual inspection of rendered part masks from the dual-Gaussian representation.', 'Qualitative assessment of the estimated articulation parameters.']

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed method against a pre-trained segmentation-driven approach to demonstrate its effectiveness beyond the self-contained setting.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will use an overlapping subset of objects from the official Video2Articulation dataset, including four two-part objects and one three-part object. The proposed method will be compared against the Video2Articulation baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Axis Angle Error (°)', 'Axis Position Error (0.1m)', 'Chamfer Distance on moving parts (CD-m)', 'Chamfer Distance on static parts (CD-s)']

risk_factors: We do not require the use of depth sensing, as done by most prior art techniques; however, we do utilise a video that captures the DoFs of the object's parts.
Capturing the whole geometry of internal parts, such as drawers, an extended blade of a knife, or the blades of a pair of scissors, requires the full disassembly of the articulated object.
The generated geometry is limited to the visible geometry and, as such, may be limited in its application for developing interactive models.

From the real-world data, we observe that when motion introduces structural ambiguities—particularly those caused by specular reflections, such as the glass door of the oven in the video—our vanilla 3DGS-based reconstruction in AIM can be affected.

title: Graph Transformers on EHRs: Better Representation Improves Downstream Performance
name: GT-BEHRT
short_hypothesis: A hybrid model, GT-BEHRT, that leverages a graph transformer to create rich, time-aware visit embeddings and a BERT-based model to capture temporal sequences of these visits will produce more robust patient representations and improve performance on downstream predictive tasks, especially for patients with longer medical histories. This approach can be enhanced by a two-step pre-training strategy tailored for both graphical and temporal representation learning.
related_work: Prior work on EHRs falls into two main categories: Transformer-based and GNN-based methods. Transformer-based approaches (e.g., BEHRT) treat medical codes as words and visits as sentences, but struggle to represent the relationships between visits effectively, often relying on weak positional encodings. GNN-based approaches capture the graphical relationships between medical codes within a visit but often assume prior knowledge of medical ontologies or fail to consider the temporal patterns between visits. Integrating these two families of methods is a natural next step, but has been challenging in practice due to EHR data characteristics like high sparsity.
abstract: Following the success of transformer-based methods across various machine learning applications, their adoption to healthcare predictive tasks using electronic health records (EHRs) has also expanded extensively. Similarly, graph-based methods have been shown to be very effective in capturing inherent graph-type relationships in EHRs. Although integrating these two families of approaches seems like a natural next step, in practice, creating such a design is challenging. This is partly due to known EHR problems, such as high sparsity, making extracting meaningful temporal representations of medical visits challenging. In this study, we propose GT-BEHRT, a new approach that leverages temporal visit embeddings extracted from a graph transformer and uses a BERT-based model to obtain more robust patient representations, especially on longer EHR sequences. The graph-based approach allows GT-BEHRT to implicitly capture the intrinsic graphical relationships between medical observations (concepts), while the BERT model extracts the temporal relationships between visits. As part of our method, we also present a two-step pre-training strategy for learning better graphical and temporal representations.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's performance on an in-hospital mortality prediction task.
experiments.Method: The GT-BEHRT model is to be evaluated for binary classification on the MIMIC-IV dataset to predict patient death during an ICU stay. The model will be compared against several baselines: VGNN, HypEHR, Dipole, HiTANet, BEHRT, CEHR-BERT, and CEHR-GAN-BERT. The experimental setup involves an 80/10/10 train/validation/test split, with the process repeated five times with different random seeds.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's performance on a prolonged ICU length of stay prediction task.
experiments.Method: The GT-BEHRT model is to be evaluated for binary classification on the MIMIC-IV dataset to predict whether a patient's ICU stay will exceed 72 hours. The model will be compared against the same set of baselines as the mortality task (VGNN, HypEHR, Dipole, HiTANet, BEHRT, CEHR-BERT, and CEHR-GAN-BERT). The experimental setup involves an 80/10/10 train/validation/test split, with the process repeated five times with different random seeds.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's performance on a heart failure prediction task.
experiments.Method: The GT-BEHRT model is to be evaluated for binary classification on the All of Us dataset to predict the occurrence of heart failure within the next 365 days. The model will be compared against the same set of baselines (VGNN, HypEHR, Dipole, HiTANet, BEHRT, CEHR-BERT, and CEHR-GAN-BERT). The experimental setup involves an 80/10/10 train/validation/test split, with the process repeated five times with different random seeds.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's performance on patients with longer medical histories to test the hypothesis that visit-level embeddings are more robust for long sequences.
experiments.Method: The performance of GT-BEHRT and all baseline models is to be compared on a subset of the test data containing only patients with 10 or more visits.

experiments.Description: Conduct an ablation study to evaluate the effectiveness of each key component of the GT-BEHRT architecture and pre-training strategy.
experiments.Method: The performance of several variants of the GT-BEHRT model is to be compared. The variants will involve removing specific components: 1) the two-stage pre-training (NAM and VTP+MNP), 2) only the NAM stage, 3) only the VTP+MNP stage, and 4) replacing the Graph Transformer with a simple linear layer for visit embeddings.

risk_factors: We believe fairness mitigation techniques (Wan et al., 2021) could improve these results.

title: HYPOCRITE: Homoglyph Adversarial Examples for Natural Language Web Services in the Physical World
name: hypocrite-homoglyph-attacks-on-nlp-services
short_hypothesis: Replacing English characters with visually similar international characters (homoglyphs) can generate effective adversarial examples for commercial natural language web services. This method, termed HYPOCRITE, can cause misclassification in a black-box setting by creating noise that disrupts AI engines while remaining imperceptible to humans.
related_work: Prior research has demonstrated adversarial attacks against NLP models, including white-box character-level crafting and black-box sequence generation. Homoglyph attacks have also been proposed for attacking neural text detectors. However, a methodology specifically designed to generate homoglyph adversarial examples targeting real-world sentiment analysis services by perturbing entire text units (word, sentence, paragraph) remains to be fully explored.
abstract: Many companies provide advanced AI services in the form of Machine-Learning-as-a-Service (MLaaS), but security vulnerabilities like adversarial examples persist. This paper proposes HYPOCRITE, a framework to generate homoglyph adversarial examples for natural language web services in the physical world, with the goal of disrupting these services. The key idea is to replace English characters with other international characters that look visually similar, thereby introducing noise to the AI engines. Using this approach, parts of a text can be replaced to cause misclassification through black-box attacks on natural language web services.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the HYPOCRITE framework in generating both non-targeted and targeted adversarial examples for commercial sentiment analysis web services under a black-box setting.
experiments.Method: The attack will be performed on the sentiment analysis task using the IMDB dataset. To analyze the effect of text length, the dataset will be divided into seven sections based on character count, and 96 reviews will be randomly sampled from each section. The attacks will target four real-world sentiment analysis services: Amazon Comprehend, Google Cloud Natural Language AI, IBM Watson Natural Language Understanding, and Microsoft Text Analytics. The proposed HYPOCRITE method will be compared against a baseline homoglyph attack method from Wolff & Wolff (2020).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the imperceptibility of the generated homoglyph adversarial examples to human observers.
experiments.Method: A user study will be conducted on Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk). Participants will be presented with adversarial texts generated by HYPOCRITE from the IMDB dataset and will be asked to identify the words that have been altered with homoglyphs. The study will use samples from different text length groups to investigate the effect of text length on human perception.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Attack success rate (defined as the proportion of attack words correctly identified by participants)']

risk_factors: To test the performance of our attacks, we used sentiment analysis APIs provided by real world companies. However, every time we checked the results using APIs, the use of APIs should be payed. Therefore, we had to sample a part of the whole dataset, and also could not try the further experiments which deal with perturbing letters to find which letters are important to the score of sentimental analysis.

In our work, we perturbed the whole words to generate adversarial examples. However, every single letter in the words could be perturbed with a letter which looks the same but has a different unicode value.

title: DISTRIBUTED MULTI-AGENT DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
name: DG-MAPPO
short_hypothesis: A fully distributed MARL framework, DG-MAPPO, which leverages a novel Distributed Graph Attention Network (D-GAT) for multi-hop communication, can learn effective cooperative policies using only local observations, peer-to-peer communication, and shared/averaged rewards, thereby removing the need for centralized critics or privileged global information during training.
related_work: The dominant paradigm in MARL is Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE), used by methods like MAPPO and MADDPG. However, CTDE relies on privileged global state information during training, which creates scalability bottlenecks and a train-test mismatch. While some fully distributed learning techniques exist, they often retain some form of centralization, such as simple value function averaging or shared network parameters. Similarly, GNN-augmented MARL approaches have improved communication but still depend on centralized critics or value decomposition. This leaves a research gap for a MARL framework where agents can learn cooperative behaviors using only local observations and peer-to-peer communication, without any reliance on centralized components.
abstract: Centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) has been the dominant paradigm in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), but its reliance on global state information during training introduces scalability, robustness, and generalization bottlenecks. In practical scenarios, distributed approaches allow agents to adapt using only local information and peer-to-peer communication. We present a distributed MARL framework that removes the need for centralized critics or global information. Firstly, we develop a novel Distributed Graph Attention Network (D-GAT) that performs global state inference through multi-hop communication, where agents integrate neighbor features via input-dependent attention weights in a fully distributed manner. Leveraging D-GAT, we develop the distributed graph-attention MAPPO (DG-MAPPO) – a distributed MARL framework where agents optimize local policies and value functions using local observations, multi-hop communication, and shared/averaged rewards. This distributed MARL framework aims to provide a principled and scalable solution for robust collaboration, eliminating the need for centralized training or global observability, enabling agents to learn and act solely through peer-to-peer communication.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of DG-MAPPO on a suite of cooperative multi-agent tasks against strong CTDE baselines under both default and sparse communication settings.
experiments.Method: The proposed DG-MAPPO model will be evaluated on the StarCraftII Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) benchmark. Tasks include: 3m, 8m, MMM, 5m vs 6m, 8m vs 9m, 10m vs 11m, 25m, MMM2, 6h vs 8z, and 3s5z vs 3s6z. The model will be compared against strong CTDE baselines: MAPPO, HAPPO, and MAT-Dec. An additional experiment will assess performance in highly sparse settings by reducing the communication range on the 'Hard+' scenarios (6h vs 8z and MMM2).

experiments.Description: To evaluate DG-MAPPO's performance in a complex, dynamic environment with a changing communication topology.
experiments.Method: The proposed DG-MAPPO model will be evaluated on the Google Research Football (GFootball) benchmark, specifically in the 'Academy 3 vs 1 with Keeper' scenario. It will be compared against CTDE baselines including HAPPO and MAT-Dec.

experiments.Description: To evaluate DG-MAPPO's robustness in continuous-control settings with a fixed, sparse communication topology where agents can only exchange information with physically adjacent joints.
experiments.Method: The proposed DG-MAPPO model will be evaluated on the Multi-Agent MuJoCo benchmark, specifically the 'Multi-HalfCheetah (6x1)' task. It will be compared against CTDE baselines including HAPPO and MAT-Dec.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to assess the importance of individual components of the DG-MAPPO framework: the attention mechanism for message aggregation, the number of communication hops, and the consensus loss regularizer.
experiments.Method: The study will be performed on selected 'Hard' and 'Hard+' SMAC environments (10m vs 11m, 6h vs 8z, and MMM2). The experiments will involve comparing variants of DG-MAPPO: (1) using mean-based aggregation versus attention-augmented aggregation, (2) varying the number of communication hops, and (3) training with and without the consensus loss.

risk_factors: DG-MAPPO, by design, uses the same communication mechanism during both training and execution. At inference time, agents continue to exchange feature vectors h^i with neighbors via the D-GAT module. While this incurs a non-zero communication overhead during deployment, the execution-time communication cost remains bounded and tunable by design.

title: Improving RAG Question Answering Generation with Synthetic Data
name: rag-qa-synthetic-data-finetuning
short_hypothesis: Fine-tuning a RAG generator on synthetically generated question-answering data can effectively substitute for real data, potentially matching or improving performance, especially under imperfect retrieval conditions. It is also hypothesized that a synthetically tuned model will exhibit superior generalization performance compared to its counterpart trained on real data.
related_work: Prior research has explored boosting Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) performance by fine-tuning the generator, retriever, or both. Some studies have used synthetic data for this purpose. However, a direct, novel comparison of fine-tuning a RAG generator on real versus synthetic data, particularly analyzing the impact of retriever quality and using LLM-as-a-judge for correctness, has not been thoroughly investigated. This work aims to fill that gap by focusing on the specific impact of synthetic data on answer correctness and generalization, rather than just overall F1 scores.
abstract: To improve large language models (LLMs) for question answering (QA) tasks, system architects often use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) or fine-tuning. However, many applications lack sufficient high-quality real data for fine-tuning the RAG generator. This work studies the degree to which synthetic data can effectively substitute real data for this purpose. Using GPT-4o, a synthetic version of the HotpotQA training set is generated. A Llama-3 generator is then fine-tuned separately on both the real and synthetic datasets. The plan is to evaluate these models using a range of metrics to understand their performance.

experiments.Description: To evaluate and compare the performance of a RAG generator fine-tuned on synthetic data versus real data on an in-domain multi-hop QA task.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the HotpotQA dev set. A base Llama-3.1 8B Instruct model is compared against two fine-tuned versions: 'G-real', fine-tuned on the real HotpotQA training set, and 'G-synthetic', fine-tuned on a synthetically generated version of the HotpotQA training set. The evaluation is conducted within a RAG system using two different retriever setups: an imperfect MPNet retriever and an idealized GoldenRetriever.

experiments.Description: To test the generalization performance of the fine-tuned models on an alternative, unseen QA task with a similar open-domain style.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the RepLiQA dataset. The same models (base Llama-3.1, G-real, G-synthetic) and retriever setups (MPNet, GoldenRetriever) from the in-domain evaluation are used to assess performance on this new task.

experiments.Description: To test the generalization performance of the fine-tuned models on a specialized, out-of-domain QA task.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the BioASQ dataset, which consists of multi-hop biomedical QA pairs. The same models (base Llama-3.1, G-real, G-synthetic) and retriever setups (MPNet, GoldenRetriever) are evaluated to measure generalization to a specialized domain.

risk_factors: Since performance is highly dependent on the synthetic data distribution, one change to our generation prompt could have led to drastically different results.
We also do not train on distractor, or irrelevant, documents as our goal is to primarily compare the impact of fine-tuning on real versus synthetic data.
We do not contribute any additional methods for generating multi-hop candidate passage pairs to the literature; however, the HotpotQA paper and others have proposed methodologies for this task.
The performance of fine-tuned RAG generators can depend on the retrieval quality. This is a key finding that, to our knowledge, has not been well explored in prior research.

title: Prototypical Information Bottlenecking and Disentangling for Multimodal Cancer Survival Prediction
name: Prototypical Information Bottlenecking and Disentangling
short_hypothesis: A framework, Prototypical Information Bottlenecking and Disentangling (PIBD), can mitigate redundancy in multimodal cancer survival prediction. This is achieved by using a Prototypical Information Bottleneck (PIB) module to reduce intra-modal redundancy by modeling prototypes for different risk levels to guide instance selection, and a Prototypical Information Disentanglement (PID) module to reduce inter-modal redundancy by decoupling data into modality-common and modality-specific components under the guidance of a joint prototypical distribution.
related_work: Prior work in multimodal cancer survival prediction uses tensor-based or attention-based fusion methods (e.g., MCAT, MOTCat, SurvPath). While some methods attempt to alleviate redundancy through alignment, they are often prone to losing modality-specific information. Methods for single modalities, like Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) for pathology, do not typically include constraints to remove redundant information. Although information theory concepts like the Information Bottleneck (IB) and disentanglement exist, they have not been previously applied to address the specific intra-modal and inter-modal redundancy challenges in multimodal cancer survival analysis.
abstract: Multimodal learning significantly benefits cancer survival prediction, especially the integration of pathological images and genomic data. Despite advantages of multimodal learning for cancer survival prediction, massive redundancy in multimodal data prevents it from extracting discriminative and compact information: (1) An extensive amount of intra-modal task-unrelated information blurs discriminability, especially for gigapixel whole slide images (WSIs) with many patches in pathology and thousands of pathways in genomic data, leading to an "intra-modal redundancy" issue. (2) Duplicated information among modalities dominates the representation of multimodal data, which makes modality-specific information prone to being ignored, resulting in an "inter-modal redundancy" issue. To address these, we propose a new framework, Prototypical Information Bottlenecking and Disentangling (PIBD), consisting of Prototypical Information Bottleneck (PIB) module for intra-modal redundancy and Prototypical Information Disentanglement (PID) module for inter-modal redundancy. Specifically, a variant of information bottleneck, PIB, is proposed to model prototypes approximating a bunch of instances for different risk levels, which can be used for selection of discriminative instances within modality. PID module decouples entangled multimodal data into compact distinct components: modality-common and modality-specific knowledge, under the guidance of the joint prototypical distribution.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed PIBD framework for multimodal cancer survival prediction against unimodal, multimodal, and information theory-based state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: The PIBD model is to be trained for disease-specific survival prediction on five TCGA datasets: Breast Invasive Carcinoma (BRCA), Bladder Urothelial Carcinoma (BLCA), Colon and Rectum Adenocarcinoma (COADREAD), Stomach Adenocarcinoma (STAD), and Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (HNSC), using both histology (WSI) and genomics (pathway) data. The performance will be compared against three groups of baselines: (1) Unimodal methods: MLP, SNN, SNNTrans for genomics; ABMIL, AMISL, TransMIL, CLAM-SB, CLAM-MB for histology. (2) Multimodal methods: Porpoise, MCAT (with both concatenation and Kronecker product fusion), MOTCat, SurvPath, and a prediction-level CoxPH model.
