(3) Information theory-based methods: CLAM-SB-FT, MIB, DeepIMV, and L-MIB.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Concordance index (C-index) and its standard deviation', 'Kaplan-Meier (KM) curves', 'Log-rank test p-values']

experiments.Description: To validate the contribution of the proposed Prototypical Information Bottleneck (PIB) and Prototypical Information Disentanglement (PID) modules through ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Two sets of ablations are planned. First, to ablate PIB, baselines using direct average pooling (AP) and a non-disentangled TransMIL encoder will be compared with versions of the same baselines that incorporate the PIB module. Second, to ablate PID, the full PIBD model will be compared against a baseline that uses the PIB module with a non-disentangled TransMIL encoder (i.e., without PID).

experiments.Description: To assess the interpretability of the learned prototypes in the PIB module by visualizing their distributions and performing intervention experiments.
experiments.Method: First, random sampling will be performed on each learned prototype, and the resulting high-dimensional vectors will be reduced to a 2D plane using t-SNE for visualization to check for separability. Second, intervention experiments will be conducted during inference by either removing the identified positive prototype or randomly removing a negative prototype to observe the impact on predictive performance.

risk_factors: During this approximation, the choice of an appropriate similarity metric can contribute to better aligning spatial distributions, which warrants further investigation in future research endeavors.

title: Locality-aware Gaussian Compression for Fast and High-quality Rendering
name: locality-aware-gaussian-compression
short_hypothesis: A novel locality-aware 3D Gaussian representation can effectively compress 3D Gaussian Splatting models by decomposing attributes into explicit and implicit groups. The implicit, spatially-coherent attributes (normalized scale, rotation, opacity, residual SH coefficients) can be encoded using a compact neural field, specifically a multi-resolution hash grid, to minimize storage while preserving local detail. This allows for high compression without modifying the original, efficient 3DGS rendering pipeline.
related_work: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) produces high-quality, real-time renderings but requires significant storage. Previous compression methods include pruning, quantization, and anchor-based approaches. Quantization methods often degrade quality when applied aggressively as they rely on global statistics. Anchor-based methods achieve good compression but introduce rendering overhead, slowing them down. Other locality-exploiting methods only partially leverage the local coherence of Gaussian attributes. There is a need for a compact 3DGS representation that achieves a high compression ratio and fast rendering speed by fully exploiting the spatial coherence of Gaussian attributes without altering the original rendering pipeline.
abstract: We present LocoGS, a locality-aware 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) framework that exploits the spatial coherence of 3D Gaussians for compact modeling of volumetric scenes. To this end, we first analyze the local coherence of 3D Gaussian attributes, and propose a novel locality-aware 3D Gaussian representation that effectively encodes locally-coherent Gaussian attributes using a neural field representation with a minimal storage requirement. On top of the novel representation, LocoGS is carefully designed with additional components such as dense initialization, an adaptive spherical harmonics bandwidth scheme and different encoding schemes for different Gaussian attributes to maximize compression performance.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed LocoGS framework against existing compact 3D Gaussian frameworks on standard novel-view synthesis benchmarks, comparing rendering quality, compression ratio, and rendering speed.
experiments.Method: The LocoGS model (in 'Ours' and 'Ours-Small' variants) will be evaluated on the Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending datasets. The performance will be compared against baselines including the original 3DGS, Scaffold-GS, and various compression approaches: CompGS, Compact-3DGS, C3DGS, LightGaussian, EAGLES, SSGS, and HAC.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to verify the effectiveness of each individual component of the LocoGS pipeline, including the locality-aware representation, dense initialization, adaptive SH bandwidth, and various compression schemes.
experiments.Method: Variants of the LocoGS model will be evaluated on the Mip-NeRF 360 dataset. The study will start with a baseline of 3DGS with pruning and standard compression, then introduce the locality-aware representation, and incrementally add other components: dense initialization, adaptive SH bandwidth, G-PCC for positions, and compression for explicit and implicit attributes.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scalability of LocoGS on large-scale scenes, which require an excessive number of Gaussians.
experiments.Method: The LocoGS model will be evaluated on the Mill-19 dataset (scenes: Building, Rubble) and compared against the original 3DGS approach.

risk_factors: Despite its remarkable performance, our representation takes longer to train than existing methods, approximately one hour for each scene.

In addition, our representation learning process requires more memory space than 3DGS due to the gradient computation of the hash grid and Gaussian attributes, which poses challenges for large-scale scenes.
Our method assumes a cube-shaped, object-centric scene for setting the hyperparameters of the hash grid, which may be less effective for complex structures in large-scale scenes.

title: Autoregressive Graph Network for Learning Multi-step Physics
name: autoregressive-gn-physics
short_hypothesis: A latent space encoded with a causal graph, induced by an autoregressive encoder, will enable particle-based machine learning surrogate models to learn long-range dependencies and obey conservation principles more effectively than models using an undirected graph representation of temporal states.
related_work: Current Graph Networks (GNs) for physics simulation can predict trajectories but tend to be unstable and accumulate error in the long run when trained for one-step prediction. While concatenating a history of previous states improves accuracy, it does not explicitly capture temporal symmetries like the arrow of time. Sequential models such as RNNs, LSTMs, and Transformers can model these temporal dependencies but require sequential computations, which come with significant memory overhead and computational cost as the lookback length increases. This work aims to combine the temporal inductive bias of sequential models with the computational efficiency of parallelizable, feed-forward GNNs.
abstract: In this work, we propose an Autoregressive Graph Network (AGN) that learns forward physics using a temporal inductive bias. Currently, temporal state space information is provided as additional input to a GN when generating roll-out physics simulations. While this relatively increases the network's predictive performance over multiple time steps, a temporal model enables the network to induce and learn temporal biases. In dynamical systems, the arrow of time simplifies possible interactions in the sense that we can assume current observations to be dependent on preceding states. Our proposed GN encodes temporal state information using an autoregressive encoder that can parallelly compute latent temporal embeddings over multiple time steps during a single forward pass. We plan to perform case studies that compare multi-step forward predictions against baseline data-driven one-step GNs as well as multi-step sequential models across diverse datasets that feature different particle interactions.

experiments.Description: To benchmark the forward dynamics prediction performance of the proposed Autoregressive Graph Network (AGN) against other one-step prediction models over long-term roll-outs.
experiments.Method: The models will be trained on one-step target acceleration predictions. Once trained, they will be used to generate 1000 time-step roll-out simulations. The proposed AGN model (in scalar and vector variants) will be compared against several baselines: Graph Network (GN), Graph Lagrangian Network (GLN), Graph Hamiltonian Network (GHN), and Graph Transformer Network (GTN). The evaluation will be conducted on a suite of particle physics datasets: 2D Spring, 2D Damped, 2D Gravity (-), 2D Gravity (r^-2), 2D Charge, 3D Spring, 3D Damped, 3D Gravity (+), 3D Gravity (r^-2), and 3D Charge.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean Squared Error (MSE) on multi-step roll-out predictions.']

experiments.Description: To compare the forward simulation performance of AGN against sequential and multi-step prediction models.
experiments.Method: Models will be compared in a multi-step setting where they are given k states as input to predict k acceleration targets, with k fixed to 5. The proposed AGN (scalar and vector) will be compared against single-step (SS) versions of GN and GTN, and multi-step (MS) versions of GTN and Gated Graph Recurrent Neural Networks (GGRNN). The same 10 particle physics datasets will be used.

experiments.Description: To evaluate how well the proposed model conserves energy over long trajectories compared to sequential and non-sequential baselines.
experiments.Method: Using the trajectories generated from the multi-step prediction experiment (k=5), the total energy deviation will be computed. The AGN models (scalar and vector) will be compared against GN (SS), GTN (SS), GTN (MS), and GGRNN (MS) on the 10 particle physics datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the autoregressive approach when combined with physics-constrained GNNs (Lagrangian and Hamiltonian).
experiments.Method: The AGN model will be modified to create an Autoregressive Graph Lagrangian Network (AGLN) and an Autoregressive Graph Hamiltonian Network (AGHN). These models (in scalar and vector variants) will be trained and evaluated on the 10 particle physics datasets. The baselines for this experiment will be the standard Graph Lagrangian Network (GLN) and Graph Hamiltonian Network (GHN).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean roll-out error (MSE).', 'Mean energy accumulation error (Energy MSE).']

title: Offline Congestion Games: How Feedback Type Affects Data Coverage Requirement
name: offline-congestion-games-feedback-types
short_hypothesis: For offline congestion games, where the action space can be exponentially large, it is possible to efficiently recover an approximate Nash Equilibrium (NE) by designing novel data coverage assumptions tailored to different types of feedback (facility-level, agent-level, and game-level), thereby avoiding the prohibitive sample complexity incurred by existing assumptions for general-sum games.
related_work: Existing theoretical work on offline multi-agent games proposes a unilateral coverage assumption for learning a Nash Equilibrium (NE). However, when applied to congestion games, this assumption results in a sample complexity that scales with the number of actions, which can be exponentially large and thus impractical. While online learning in congestion games has been studied, the offline setting, where an algorithm learns from a fixed, pre-collected dataset, is less understood, especially regarding how different types of reward feedback (e.g., per-facility, per-agent, or per-game) affect the data requirements for efficient learning.
abstract: This paper investigates when one can efficiently recover an approximate Nash Equilibrium (NE) in offline congestion games. The existing dataset coverage assumption in offline general-sum games inevitably incurs a dependency on the number of actions, which can be exponentially large in congestion games. We consider three different types of feedback with decreasing revealed information. Starting from the facility-level (a.k.a., semi-bandit) feedback, we propose a novel one-unit deviation coverage condition and show a pessimism-type algorithm that can recover an approximate NE. For the agent-level (a.k.a., bandit) feedback setting, we show the one-unit deviation coverage condition is not sufficient. On the other hand, we convert the game to multi-agent linear bandits and propose that with a generalized data coverage assumption in offline linear bandits, we can efficiently recover the approximate NE. Lastly, we consider a novel type of feedback, the game-level feedback where only the total reward from all agents is revealed. We propose that with a stronger version of the data coverage assumption for linear bandits, we can recover an approximate NE.

experiments.Description: To empirically verify the theoretical results for the facility-level feedback setting by investigating: (i) if the proposed algorithm can recover the Nash Equilibrium (NE) from datasets satisfying the proposed coverage assumption, (ii) how fast the algorithm converges as sample size increases, and (iii) how the convergence rate varies with the number of players.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a modified version of the Braess paradox, a canonical congestion game with 5 facilities (roads) and a variable number of players (m). The proposed Surrogate Minimization algorithm (Algorithm 1) is evaluated. Offline datasets are generated using two different exploration policies: 1) a `random` policy where each player selects actions uniformly, and 2) a `facility` policy designed to satisfy the proposed `Assumption 2` (one-unit deviation coverage) by covering all facility configurations achievable from unilateral deviations from the NE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance gap of the output policy, which measures its distance from being an NE. This will be plotted against the dataset sample size (n) for different numbers of players (m).']

title: On the Usefulness of Embeddings, Clusters and Strings for Text Generation Evaluation
name: dissecting-mauve-cluster-approximation-vs-divergence
short_hypothesis: The success of the MAUVE evaluation metric is not due to its newly proposed AUC divergence measure, but rather its approximation method which substitutes distributions over strings with distributions over embedding-based clusters. Therefore, classical divergences (like KL or JS) paired with this cluster-based approximation should also serve as high-performing evaluation metrics.
related_work: Many automatic metrics for language generation, such as cross-entropy and perplexity, correlate poorly with human judgments. The recently proposed MAUVE metric is an exception, showing high correlation. MAUVE theoretically measures an information-theoretic divergence between the true and generated distributions over strings. However, in practice, this is intractable and is approximated by measuring the divergence between distributions over clusters derived from text embeddings. The reasons for MAUVE's success are not well understood, specifically whether its performance comes from its novel divergence measure or its cluster-based approximation scheme.
abstract: A good automatic evaluation metric for language generation ideally correlates highly with human judgements of text quality. Yet, there is a dearth of such metrics. One exception is the recently proposed MAUVE. In theory, MAUVE measures an information-theoretic divergence between two probability distributions over strings. In practice, as this divergence is uncomputable, MAUVE approximates it by measuring the divergence between multinomial distributions over clusters instead, where cluster assignments are attained by grouping strings based on a pretrained language model's embeddings. This work investigates why MAUVE works so well, proposing that its success is due to the cluster-based approximation rather than its novel divergence. The research plan is to test if classical divergences, when paired with MAUVE's proposed cluster-based approximation, can serve as better evaluation metrics. A probing analysis is also planned to understand what features (e.g., syntactic, coherence) are encoded by these cluster-based distributions.

experiments.Description: To determine if the cluster-based probability distributions (pc, qc) used in MAUVE are faithful approximations of the true string-based distributions (pw, qw).
experiments.Method: For several GPT-2 model variants (small, medium, large, xl), the ground-truth distribution `qw` is compared against two estimators: a cluster-based estimator `qc` (using GPT-2 XL embeddings and K-means) and a string-based n-gram estimator `qw_hat`. The comparison is made on a set of generated strings.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Spearman correlation between the probabilities assigned by the true distribution `qw` and the estimated distributions (`qc` and `qw_hat`).']

experiments.Description: To compare how various string-based and cluster-based divergence measures correlate with human judgments of text quality, in order to determine if the cluster-based approximation is the main reason for MAUVE's success.
experiments.Method: Systems for open-ended text generation (4 variants of GPT-2 with ancestral and nucleus sampling) are compared against human-generated text from the WebText test set. Divergences are computed in two ways: 1) String-based, using 5-gram models to estimate distributions. 2) Cluster-based, using GPT-2 XL embeddings and K-means clustering. Baselines include Forward KL (Aexp), Backward KL (A<-), Jensen-Shannon (AJS), and the AUC divergence from MAUVE (AAUC), each applied to both string- and cluster-based distributions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Correlation (Pearson and Spearman) of each divergence metric's scores with human judgement scores for 'Interestingness', 'Sensibility', and 'Human likeness'."]

experiments.Description: To probe what linguistic attributes are encoded in the cluster assignments derived from pre-trained language model embeddings.
experiments.Method: This experiment uses texts annotated for sentiment (Yelp Polarity), authorship (News Category), and topic (20 NewsGroup). For each attribute, a clustering function is learned on either WebText or the task's training set. The task is to predict the attribute of a text in the test set based solely on its cluster assignment. This is tested using embeddings from GPT-2 (small, medium, large, XL) and BERT (base, large).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Prediction accuracy for the linguistic attribute (sentiment, authorship, topic).']

experiments.Description: To assess how different types of text perturbations impact the divergence scores calculated using the cluster-based approximation method.
experiments.Method: A reference corpus of human-written text (from WebText) is compared against several modified versions of another human-written corpus. The modifications include: text truncation, article removal, stopword removal, sentence-level permutation, and word-level permutation. A baseline comparison is also made against text generated by GPT-2 XL. The effect of these perturbations on various divergence metrics (Forward, Backward, Exponentiated, JS, AUC) is to be measured.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Divergence scores (Δ) between the reference and perturbed corpora, analyzed as a function of the number of clusters used in the approximation.']

risk_factors: Importantly, we run our experiments only on English text, which is a notable limitation of our work; future work should verify that findings hold across languages.

title: Decomposition Polyhedra of Piecewise Linear Functions
name: cpwl-decomposition-polyhedra
short_hypothesis: The problem of finding a minimal decomposition of a continuous piecewise linear (CPWL) function into a difference of two convex CPWL functions can be made tractable by fixing an underlying polyhedral complex. Under this assumption, the set of all such decompositions forms a polyhedron, whose geometric properties (vertices, bounded faces) correspond to desirable properties of the decomposition (minimality, irreducibility).
related_work: It is a well-known fact that any continuous piecewise linear (CPWL) function can be expressed as a difference of two convex CPWL functions (a DC decomposition). This idea is used in DC programming and for analyzing neural networks. However, a crucial open question is how to find a decomposition where the convex components are as simple as possible (i.e., have a minimal number of linear pieces). While many explicit constructions exist, none guarantee minimality. A recent attempt by Tran & Wang (2024) provided an optimal construction for the 2D case and suggested a generalization, but a finite procedure for finding a minimal decomposition in general dimensions is not known. This paper proposes a new theoretical perspective using polyhedral geometry to make the problem more tractable.
abstract: In this paper we contribute to the frequently studied question of how to decompose a continuous piecewise linear (CPWL) function into a difference of two convex CPWL functions. Every CPWL function has infinitely many such decompositions, but for applications in optimization and neural network theory, it is crucial to find decompositions with as few linear pieces as possible. This is a highly challenging problem. To make the problem more tractable, we propose to fix an underlying polyhedral complex determining the possible locus of nonlinearity. Under this assumption, we propose to prove that the set of decompositions forms a polyhedron that arises as intersection of two translated cones.

experiments.Description: To analyze existing decomposition methods and a recent conjecture using the proposed polyhedral framework.
experiments.Method: The proposed framework will be used to analyze the minimality of decompositions generated by existing methods, specifically the 'hyperplane extension' and 'local maxima decomposition' from Kripfganz & Schulze (1987). A counterexample will be constructed to test the conjecture by Tran & Wang (2024) that their optimal 2D construction method generalizes to higher dimensions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Theoretical proof of suboptimality (i.e., showing that existing constructions do not yield vertices of the decomposition polyhedron) and formal refutation of the conjecture.

experiments.Description: To apply the geometric framework to the problem of decomposing a general set function into a difference of two submodular functions.
experiments.Method: The research will establish a vector space isomorphism between CPWL functions compatible with the braid arrangement and set functions (via the Lovász extension). The main theorems about decomposition polyhedra (e.g., that minimal solutions are vertices) will then be translated to the setting of submodular functions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Derivation of a corollary stating that the set of decompositions of a general set function into a difference of submodular functions forms a polyhedron, and that minimal decompositions correspond to its vertices.

experiments.Description: To develop improved neural network constructions for representing both convex and non-convex CPWL functions.
experiments.Method: For convex CPWL functions, the plan is to blend two incomparable previous constructions (Hertrich et al., 2021 and Chen et al., 2022) to allow a user-chosen trade-off between network depth and width. For non-convex CPWL functions, the proposed approach is to first decompose the function into a difference of two convex functions using the paper's framework, and then apply the new blended construction to each of the two resulting convex functions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Theoretical bounds on the resulting neural network's depth, width, and size.

risk_factors: We emphasize that the focus of our paper is fundamental research by building a theoretical foundation to tackle Problem 1.1 and connecting it with other fields. As such, our paper does not imply any direct improvement for a practical task, but it might prove helpful for that in the future.
In particular, it is beyond the scope of our paper to provide any implementation of a (heuristic or exact) method to decompose a CPWL function into a difference of two convex ones.
On the theoretical side, the approach of fixing an underlying compatible polyhedral complex imposes some restriction on the set of possible solutions and can therefore be seen as a limitation.

title: Numerical Sensitivity and Robustness: Exploring the Flaws of Mathematical Reasoning in Large Language Models
name: llm-math-perturbation-robustness
short_hypothesis: Injecting semantically irrelevant sentences into mathematical reasoning problems will reveal flaws in LLMs' understanding. Specifically, LLMs will be more sensitive to perturbations containing numerical information than those without, and their performance will degrade as the intensity (number) of perturbations increases. Furthermore, removing the core question from a problem will show that LLMs rely on memory or pattern matching rather than true logical reasoning.
related_work: Existing research suggests that LLMs rely on complex pattern matching rather than formal mathematical reasoning, and their performance degrades when minor perturbations are applied. However, prior work has not clearly distinguished whether performance degradation is due to the semantic content of perturbations or the sensitivity of LLMs to numbers. Furthermore, existing studies only apply a low intensity of perturbation (one or two sentences), failing to evaluate the robustness of LLMs under varying and more complex perturbation intensities, which is inconsistent with real-world scenarios.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have made significant progress in the field of mathematical reasoning, but whether they have true mathematical understanding ability is still controversial. To explore this issue, we propose a new perturbation framework to evaluate LLMs' reasoning ability in complex environments by injecting additional semantically irrelevant perturbation sentences and gradually increasing the perturbation intensity. At the same time, we use an additional perturbation method, core questioning instruction missing, to further analyze the LLMs' problem-solving mechanism. The goal is to reveal the shortcomings and limitations of current LLMs in their reasoning capabilities, which is of great significance for the further development of LLMs.

experiments.Description: To investigate if LLMs are more sensitive to perturbations containing numerical information compared to those without numbers.
experiments.Method: Mathematical reasoning problems from the GSM8K and AIME25 datasets are perturbed by inserting a single semantically irrelevant sentence. Two types of perturbation sentences are used: one containing numerical information and one without. The performance of various LLMs (Qwen, DeepSeek, OpenAI GPT-4o/OpenAI-03, Meta Llama-3, Google gemma/Gemini) is to be evaluated on the original and perturbed problems.

experiments.Description: To explore the robustness of LLMs' mathematical reasoning in the face of increasing perturbation intensity.
experiments.Method: Problems from the GSM8K and AIME25 datasets are perturbed by injecting an increasing number of semantically irrelevant sentences. A five-level progressive system is designed: baseline (one sentence), low-level (two sentences), medium-level (three sentences), equal-number (same number as original sentences), and excessive (twice the number of original sentences). The performance of various LLMs (Qwen, DeepSeek, OpenAI GPT-4o/OpenAI-03, Meta Llama-3, Google gemma/Gemini) is to be evaluated across these intensity levels for both numerical and non-numerical perturbations.

experiments.Description: To explore whether LLMs rely on memory and pattern matching rather than true logical reasoning to solve problems.
experiments.Method: The final sentence of each question in the GSM8K dataset, which explicitly states the goal to be solved, is removed. The performance of various LLMs (Qwen, DeepSeek, OpenAI GPT-4o/OpenAI-03, Meta Llama-3, Google gemma/Gemini) is to be evaluated on these 'core questioning instruction missing' samples.

risk_factors: this also shows that there may be potential data leakage problems in the training process of LLMs.

title: The World is Changing: Improving Fair Training under Correlation Shifts
name: fairness-correlation-shift-preprocessing
short_hypothesis: A novel pre-processing step, which adjusts the training data ratio among labels and sensitive groups to reflect a shifted correlation, can be used to boost the performance of existing in-processing fairness algorithms when the correlation between labels and sensitive attributes (correlation shift) differs between training and deployment.
related_work: Existing in-processing fairness algorithms typically assume that training and deployment data distributions are identical. This assumption is often violated in practice, particularly when the statistical bias (correlation) between labels (y) and sensitive attributes (z) changes over time, which can degrade model fairness. While prior work has investigated other types of distribution shifts (e.g., covariate, concept shifts), they do not explicitly model this 'correlation shift'. The proposed work introduces this notion and develops a pre-processing step designed to adjust the training data to a target correlation, aiming to improve the performance of subsequent in-processing methods, unlike existing pre-processing techniques that simply aim to remove bias entirely.
abstract: While many techniques for model fairness have been proposed, most of them assume that the training and deployment data distributions are identical, which is often not true in practice. In particular, when the bias between labels and sensitive groups changes, the group fairness of the trained model is directly influenced and can worsen. To solve this problem, we first analytically show that existing in-processing fair algorithms have fundamental limits. We introduce the notion of correlation shifts, which can explicitly capture this change in bias. Second, we propose a novel pre-processing step that samples the input data to reduce correlation shifts, enabling in-processing approaches to overcome their limitations. We formulate an optimization problem for adjusting the data ratio among labels and sensitive groups to reflect the shifted correlation. A key advantage is decoupling the roles of pre-processing (correlation adjustment) and in-processing (unfairness mitigation).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework's effectiveness in improving the accuracy and fairness of existing in-processing algorithms when the test data has a different (y, z)-correlation than the training data.
experiments.Method:
The proposed pre-processing method will be applied before training with several in-processing fairness algorithms. The performance will be compared against baselines.
- Datasets: A synthetic dataset, ProPublica COMPAS, and AdultCensus. Test data will be constructed to have a shifted correlation, either by re-sampling, modifying the sensitive attribute 'z', or by using a newer dataset (ACSIncome) for testing a model trained on an older one (AdultCensus).
- Model: Logistic Regression.
- Baselines: 
  1. Vanilla (non-fair) training (LR).
  2. In-processing-only training: Fairness Constraints (FC), Adversarial Debiasing (AD), and FairBatch (FB).
  3. Two-step training: Reweighing (RW) pre-processing followed by an in-processing algorithm (e.g., RW+FC).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy', 'DP disparity (Demographic Parity)', 'EO disparity (Equalized Odds)', 'Maximum of DP and EO disparities for multi-metric evaluation']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework's performance when the correlation of the test data is varied systematically.
experiments.Method: The proposed method combined with the FairBatch (FB) baseline will be evaluated on a synthetic dataset where the test data's correlation constant is varied from 10% to 70% of the training data's correlation. This will be compared against the performance of the in-processing-only FB baseline.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework's robustness when the exact shifted correlation of the test data is unknown or misspecified.
experiments.Method:
Two scenarios will be tested on a synthetic dataset:
1. Misspecified correlation: The true test correlation is fixed, but the correlation value provided to the algorithm is varied to simulate estimation error.
2. Correlation range: The algorithm is provided with a range of possible correlation values [α, β] instead of a single point estimate.

experiments.Description: To conduct a preliminary evaluation of the framework's potential to handle noisy group attributes and poisoning attacks.
experiments.Method:
The framework's performance will be evaluated under two robustness scenarios:
1. Noisy Groups: A portion (10-50%) of the sensitive group attributes in the training data will be randomly flipped.
2. Poisoning Attack: 10% of the training labels for a specific group will be flipped to degrade fairness.

risk_factors: We note that our framework implicitly assumes that the distribution of input feature x does not change drastically – see details in Sec. A.6.
We focus on supporting two metrics and leave the support of more metrics as future work.

title: The Semantic Hub Hypothesis: Language Models Share Semantic Representations Across Languages and Modalities
name: semantic-hub-hypothesis
short_hypothesis: Models acquire cross-lingual and cross-modal capabilities by learning a shared representation space across heterogeneous data types, which places semantically similar inputs near one another, even if they are from different modalities or languages.
related_work: The proposed hypothesis follows the hub-and-spoke model from neuroscience, which posits that semantic knowledge in the human brain is organized through a transmodal semantic 'hub' that integrates information from various modality-specific 'spoke' regions.
abstract: Modern language models can process inputs across diverse languages and modalities. We hypothesize that models acquire this capability through learning a shared representation space across heterogeneous data types (e.g., different languages and modalities), which places semantically similar inputs near one another, even if they are from different modalities/languages. We term this the semantic hub hypothesis, following the hub-and-spoke model from neuroscience which posits that semantic knowledge in the human brain is organized through a transmodal semantic "hub" which integrates information from various modality-specific ``spokes'' regions.

experiments.Description: To test if model representations for semantically equivalent inputs in different languages are similar in the intermediate layers and can be interpreted.
experiments.Method: Compare model representations for semantically equivalent inputs in different languages. Use the logit lens to interpret the shared space using the model's dominant pretraining language.

experiments.Description: To determine if the tendency to form a shared semantic space extends to other data types beyond natural language.
experiments.Method: Analyze representations for semantically equivalent inputs across data types including arithmetic expressions, code, and visual/audio inputs.

experiments.Description: To test if the shared representation space is actively utilized by the model during input processing, rather than being a vestigial byproduct of training.
experiments.Method: Perform interventions in the shared representation space for an input in one data type and observe the effects on model outputs for semantically equivalent inputs in other data types.

title: Zero-Shot Coordination via Semantic Relationships Between Actions and Observations
name: attention-for-semantic-zsc
short_hypothesis: Attention-based architectures that jointly process a featurized representation of both the observation and the action have a better inductive bias for exploiting semantic relationships for zero-shot coordination, resulting in more human-compatible policies compared to standard feedforward or recurrent architectures.
related_work: Standard multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods like self-play often fail at zero-shot coordination (ZSC) because they learn arbitrary, private conventions that do not generalize to new partners. Prior ZSC methods like Other-Play (OP) and Off-Belief Learning (OBL) have been proposed to address this, but they either require hand-coded symmetries (OP) or do not explicitly leverage the semantic relationship between action and observation features. While attention mechanisms are common for modeling input-output relationships in other domains, their application to exploit shared action-observation features in Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (Dec-POMDPs) for coordination is novel.
abstract: An unaddressed challenge in zero-shot coordination (ZSC) is taking advantage of the semantic relationship between the features of an action and the features of observations. Humans use these relationships intuitively, for instance by pointing to a desired object, but this remains a challenge for multi-agent reinforcement learning agents. This work investigates the effect of network architecture on the propensity of learning algorithms to make use of these relationships in human-compatible ways. The proposal is that attention-based architectures that jointly process a featurized representation of the observation and the action will have a better inductive bias for exploiting semantic relationships for zero-shot coordination.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the role of different neural network architectures on cross-play generalization and the ability to create human-interpretable conventions in a novel coordination game.
experiments.Method: Agents are trained via self-play using independent Q-learning in a novel two-player Dec-POMDP called the 'hint-guess game'. This game requires a 'Hinter' and a 'Guesser' to coordinate using shared features of cards. The proposed 'Action-In' attention architecture is compared against several baselines: a standard feedforward DQN, a recurrent RDQN, two alternative attention architectures ('Obs' and 'Action-Out'), and two recent ZSC methods, Other-Play (OP) and Off-Belief Learning (OBL).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cross-play (XP) performance (pairing agents from independent training runs)', 'Self-play (SP) performance (pairing agents from the same training run)', 'Policy analysis via conditional probability matrices of hint-given-target and guess-given-hint']

experiments.Description: To examine in a fine-grained way whether the proposed agents exhibit sophisticated coordination behavior beyond simple feature matching, using hand-crafted diagnostic scenarios.
experiments.Method: The 'Action-In' model is evaluated on four specific, hand-crafted scenarios within the 'hint-guess' game designed to probe distinct coordination dimensions: 'Exact Match', 'Feature Similarity', 'Mutual Exclusivity', and 'Similarity + Exclusivity'.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Percentage of agent choices corresponding to the human-interpretable choice', 'Percentage of games where the Guesser correctly guesses the target card']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the zero-shot coordination performance of the proposed AI agents when paired with human partners.
experiments.Method: An experiment is conducted with human participants playing the 'hint-guess' game. Hints generated by humans playing as the 'Hinter' are fed to 'Guesser' agents. The performance of the proposed 'Action-In' Guesser agent is compared against a baseline DQN Guesser agent and human-human pairings.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average game score (success rate) for human/Action-In, human/DQN, and human/human pairs', "Agreement rate between the AI guesser's choice and a human guesser's choice for the same hint"]

risk_factors: we currently assume that action features are provided as part of the problem setting.

while we have focused on the hint-guess game, which is a specifically designed challenge for exploiting features for coordination, our attention-based method can also be tested with humans in more complex settings, e.g. in the benchmark games Hanabi or Overcooked

humans are able to use similarity across different modalities (vision and phonetic sound in the “Bouba” and “Kiki" example) using abstract representations of learned features, and how AI can do this represents another direction for future work.

title: DV-3DLane: End-to-end Multi-modal 3D Lane Detection with Dual-view Representation
name: dual-view-multimodal-3d-lane-detection
short_hypothesis: Learning multi-modal features from images and LiDAR in dual-view spaces, specifically perspective view (PV) and bird's-eye-view (BEV) simultaneously, will improve 3D lane detection performance. This can be achieved by: 1) a bidirectional feature fusion strategy to integrate features into each view; 2) a unified query generator that leverages lane-aware knowledge from both views; and 3) a 3D dual-view deformable attention mechanism to aggregate discriminative features from both PV and BEV spaces into the queries.
related_work: Current 3D lane detection methods are predominantly vision-centric, relying on monocular images. These approaches often suffer from depth information loss and lighting variations. Many use Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) to transform features to Bird's-Eye-View (BEV), but this introduces misalignment on non-flat roads due to the rigid planar assumption. In contrast, LiDAR provides accurate geometric cues. However, existing multi-modal methods for autonomous driving tasks tend to fuse features into a single representation space (e.g., BEV), which may constrain the model's ability to leverage modality-specific information from different views. There is a research gap in exploiting multi-modal features from both perspective view (PV) and BEV spaces simultaneously for 3D lane detection.
abstract: Accurate 3D lane estimation is crucial for ensuring safety in autonomous driving. However, prevailing monocular techniques suffer from depth loss and lighting variations, hampering accurate 3D lane detection. In contrast, LiDAR points offer geometric cues and enable precise localization. This paper presents DV-3DLane, a novel end-to-end Dual-View multi-modal 3D Lane detection framework that synergizes the strengths of both images and LiDAR points. We propose to learn multi-modal features in dual-view spaces, i.e., perspective view (PV) and bird's-eye-view (BEV), effectively leveraging the modal-specific information. To achieve this, we introduce three designs: 1) A bidirectional feature fusion strategy that integrates multi-modal features into each view space, exploiting their unique strengths. 2) A unified query generation approach that leverages lane-aware knowledge from both PV and BEV spaces to generate queries. 3) A 3D dual-view deformable attention mechanism, which aggregates discriminative features from both PV and BEV spaces into queries for accurate 3D lane detection.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall performance of the proposed DV-3DLane framework for 3D lane detection against previous state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: The DV-3DLane model, in Base, Large, and Tiny configurations, will be evaluated on the OpenLane benchmark. It will be compared against a set of baselines including 3DLaneNet, GenLaneNet, PersFormer, Anchor3DLane, M²-3DLaneNet, and LATR.

experiments.Description: To analyze the contribution of each key component of the proposed architecture through ablation studies.
experiments.Method: On the OpenLane-300 subset, the effectiveness of the Unified Query generator will be tested by comparing the proposed method against variants using random initialization, only PV queries, and only BEV queries. The 3D Dual-view Deformable Attention will be evaluated by comparing against single-view sampling (PV space, BEV space) and alternative fusion strategies (DeepInteration, FUTR3D). The effect of Bidirectional Feature Fusion will also be studied.

experiments.Description: To explore the impact of using multiple modalities (image and LiDAR) versus single modalities within the proposed framework.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted using single-modality versions of the model, specifically an 'Image-Branch' only and a 'LiDAR-Branch' only. These will be compared to the full multi-modal DV-3DLane model and a baseline (LATR) augmented with LiDAR input. The 'Image-Branch' will also be evaluated on the Apollo dataset.

title: Towards Reliable Backdoor Attacks on Vision Transformers
name: vit-backdoor-cat
short_hypothesis: The perceived vulnerability of Vision Transformers to existing backdoor attacks is overstated due to the inappropriate adaptation of defenses from CNNs. A more reliable backdoor attack can be created by adding small, adversarially generated perturbations to the trigger pattern to reduce the difference in intermediate channel activations between benign and triggered inputs, thus evading detection by defenses.
related_work: Previous studies have investigated backdoor attacks on Vision Transformers (ViTs) and found that existing attacks are powerful enough to bypass common defenses developed for CNNs. However, these findings may be over-optimistic because the defenses were not properly adapted to the architectural and training-process differences of ViTs (e.g., using SGD for fine-tuning defense on an AdamW-trained model). This leaves a gap in understanding the true robustness of ViTs and in developing more elusive attacks that can bypass properly adapted defenses.
abstract: Backdoor attacks, which make Convolution Neural Networks (CNNs) exhibit specific behaviors in the presence of a predefined trigger, bring risks to the usage of CNNs. These threats should be also considered on Vision Transformers. However, previous studies found that the existing backdoor attacks are powerful enough in ViTs to bypass common backdoor defenses. This study investigates the existing backdoor attacks/defenses and posits that this kind of achievement is over-optimistic, caused by inappropriate adaption of defenses from CNNs to ViTs. The authors hypothesize that existing backdoor attacks can still be easily defended against with proper inheritance from CNNs. Furthermore, they propose a more reliable attack: adding a small perturbation on the trigger is enough to help existing attacks become more persistent against various defenses.

experiments.Description: To re-evaluate the effectiveness of existing backdoor attacks against Vision Transformers when common defense mechanisms are properly adapted for the ViT architecture and training paradigm. This includes investigating the impact of optimizer choice (AdamW vs. SGD), fine-tuning epochs, and pruning granularity.
experiments.Method: A ViT-B model, pre-trained on ImageNet-1k and fine-tuned on CIFAR-10, will be subjected to four backdoor attacks: Badnets, Blend, CLB, and SIG. The effectiveness of several defense methods (Fine-Tuning (FT), Fine-Pruning (FP), Neural Attention Distillation (NAD), Adversarial Neuron Pruning (ANP), and Adversarial Weight Masking (AWM)) will be evaluated, with specific adaptations for ViTs.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Channel Activation attack (CAT) in bypassing properly adapted defenses on various ViT architectures, under both white-box and black-box settings.
experiments.Method: The CAT method will be used to enhance standard backdoor attacks (Badnets, Blend, CLB, SIG) on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The attacks will be launched from a ViT-B source model against a range of target models: ViT-B, DeiT-S, Swin-B, Cait-S, and XciT-S. The performance will be measured against adapted defenses (FT, FP, NAD, ANP, AWM).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed CAT attack on the large-scale ImageNet dataset and compare its effectiveness against existing ViT-specific backdoor attacks and defenses.
experiments.Method: The CAT method will be combined with Badnets and Blend attacks on a ViT-B model trained on ImageNet. Its performance will be compared against baseline attacks (vanilla Badnets/Blend, TrojViT, DBIA) when subjected to various defenses, including a ViT-specific defense, Attention Blocking (AB).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to analyze the contribution of the two key components of the proposed CAT method: adversarial perturbation on triggers (PA) and random masking of perturbation (RMP).
experiments.Method: The experiment will compare the performance of three attack configurations: 1) vanilla backdoor attacks, 2) attacks with PA only, and 3) attacks with both PA and RMP. The evaluation will be conducted on ViT-B and Swin-B target models against FP and AWM defenses.

risk_factors: Furthermore, the proposed method is a strong attack to existing defense, thereby increasing potential risks in practical applications.

title: An Open Quantum Chemistry Property Database of 120 Kilo Molecules with 20 Million Conformers
name: QO2Mol-Dataset
short_hypothesis: By creating and releasing a large-scale, open-source quantum chemistry dataset (QO2Mol) with extensive molecular and elemental diversity, a large number of conformers, and high-precision quantum mechanical properties, we can accelerate advancements in computational chemistry, material science, and drug discovery.
related_work: Existing public quantum chemistry datasets are often constrained by limited elemental diversity (e.g., ANI-1 is restricted to H, C, N, O), small molecular size (e.g., QM9 has a maximum of 9 heavy atoms), or a limited number of conformations per molecule. For instance, PCQM4Mv2 provides insufficient labels for complex tasks beyond HOMO-LUMO gap prediction, and V2DFT, while diverse in molecules, has a lower sampling density of the potential energy surface compared to the proposed work. This creates a shortage of comprehensive datasets needed for training robust, large-scale AI models for molecular science.
abstract: Artificial intelligence is revolutionizing computational chemistry, bringing unprecedented innovation and efficiency to the field. To further advance research and expedite progress, we introduce the Quantum Open Organic Molecular (QO2Mol) database — a large-scale quantum chemistry dataset designed for researches on organic molecules under an open-source license. The database comprises 120,000 organic molecules and more than 20 million conformers, encompassing 10 different elements (C, H, O, N, S, P, F, Cl, Br, I), with heavy atom counts exceeding 40. Each conformation was computed at B3LYP/def2-SVP level of theory to derive quantum mechanical properties, including potential energy and forces. The molecules included in the dataset are based on fragments from compounds in ChEMBL, ensuring their structural relevance to real-world compounds. The extensive variety of molecular structures and elemental compositions represented in the dataset can facilitate construction of potential energy surface and various downstream tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the interpolation performance of benchmark models on the potential energy prediction task using the main, large-scale portion of the dataset.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained and evaluated on `subset A` of the QO2Mol dataset, which contains 20 million conformations from over 110,000 molecules. The task is to predict the potential energy of a given molecular conformation. The benchmark models to be evaluated include invariant models (SchNet, SphereNet, DimeNet++, GemNet) and equivariant models (Equiformer, EquiformerV2, eSCN).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the extrapolation capability of benchmark models by testing them on a more challenging set of larger, drug-like molecules.
experiments.Method: Models trained on `subset A` will be used to predict potential energies on `subset B` of the QO2Mol dataset. Subset B consists of 2.4 million conformers from approximately 1,400 representative drug molecules with a higher heavy atom count (30 to 34). The same set of benchmark models (SchNet, SphereNet, DimeNet++, GemNet, Equiformer, EquiformerV2, eSCN) will be used.

risk_factors: Despite the richness and diversity of the dataset, it may not cover all possible molecular configurations or adequately represent certain chemical environments.

title: Unraveling Neural Cellular Automata for Lightweight Image Compression
name: GNCA-for-Image-Compression
short_hypothesis: The weights of a lightweight Neural Cellular Automata (NCA) model can serve as the compressed representation of an image, which can be reconstructed from a single pixel seed. To scale this to high-resolution images, a Grid NCA (GNCA) strategy is proposed, where an image is divided into patches, and a separate, lightweight NCA is trained in parallel for each patch.
related_work: State-of-the-art image compression methods typically rely on large deep neural networks (autoencoders, CNNs, GANs) which have significant storage and computational demands. The size of these models is often overlooked when calculating the final compressed size. While Neural Cellular Automata (NCAs) have been used for image restoration and synthesis, their potential for image compression remains largely unexplored. This work aims to fill this gap by proposing NCAs as a lightweight, parallelizable alternative where the model parameters themselves constitute the compressed data.
abstract: Neural Cellular Automata (NCA) are computational models capable of learning complex behaviors through local interactions. While NCAs have been applied to tasks like image restoration and synthesis, their potential for image compression remains largely unexplored. This paper aims to unravel the capabilities of NCAs for lightweight image compression by introducing a Grid Neural Cellular Automata (GNCA) training strategy. Unlike traditional methods that depend on large deep learning models, NCAs offer a low-cost, compact, and highly parallelizable alternative with intrinsic robustness to noise. The proposed method will be evaluated on the COCO 2017 dataset against standard codecs like JPEG, JPEG-2000, and WebP.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of NCA model parameters (hidden layer size, number of channels) on reconstruction quality and model size, and to evaluate how the proposed Grid NCA (GNCA) strategy scales with increasing image size compared to a standard NCA.
experiments.Method: An NCA model based on Mordvintsev et al. (2020) will be trained on images from the COCO 2017 dataset. For parameter analysis, the hidden layer size and number of channels will be varied on a 40x40 pixel image. For scaling analysis, the GNCA strategy will be compared against a standard NCA on images of sizes 40x40, 80x80, and 120x120 pixels.

experiments.Description: To compare the compression performance and reconstruction quality of the proposed Grid Neural Cellular Automata (GNCA) approach against traditional compression methods.
experiments.Method: Three versions of the GNCA model (GNCA-small, GNCA-medium, GNCA-large) with varying parameter counts will be trained on 30 random images from the COCO 2017 dataset, resized to 40x40, 80x80, and 120x120 pixels. The performance will be compared against JPEG, JPEG-2000, and WebP codecs at their maximum quality settings.

risk_factors: Training Time: NCAs require a significant amount of training time for each individual image, which limits their applicability in real-time compression scenarios. This makes the current approach more suitable for offline compression rather than dynamic, real-time environments.

Model-per-Image Limitation: In this work, we used NCA based on Mordvintsev et al. (2020), which trains one model per image. We chose this method because it provided higher-quality reconstruction with a low number of parameters. However, strategies to train one model to many images (Hernandez et al., 2021) can be explored in future works.

title: Optimizing Q-Learning Using Expectile Regression: A Dual Approach to Handle In-Sample and Out-of-Sample Data
name: EIQL-expectile-regression-offline-rl
short_hypothesis: A novel offline RL algorithm, Extended Implicit Q-Learning (EIQL), can improve performance by stochastically combining in-sample updates from Implicit Q-Learning with out-of-sample Bellman updates. This dual approach is intended to leverage the maximization capability of the Bellman update for potentially valuable out-of-sample actions while mitigating error extrapolation risks by controlling the frequency of such updates.
related_work: Current offline RL methods face a distribution shift problem. Some approaches penalize out-of-distribution (OOD) actions to ensure pessimism, but balancing this to avoid suboptimal solutions is difficult. Other methods, like Implicit Q-Learning (IQL), use in-sample learning with expectile regression to avoid querying unseen actions altogether. This work addresses the gap of how to safely incorporate potentially beneficial OOD actions, which IQL avoids, without suffering from the error extrapolation that plagues standard Q-learning.
abstract: Offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) presents unique challenges, primarily due to the constraint of learning from a static dataset without additional environmental interaction. Traditional methods often face limitations in effectively exploiting the available data, particularly when navigating the exploration-exploitation trade-off inherent in RL. This paper introduces a novel algorithm inspired by Implicit Q-Learning, designed to extend the utility of the Bellman update to actions not explicitly present in the dataset. Our approach, termed Extended Implicit Q-Learning (EIQL), strategically incorporates actions beyond the dataset constraints by allowing selection actions with maximum Q. By doing so, it leverages the maximization capability of the Bellman update, while simultaneously mitigating error extrapolation risks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Extended Implicit Q-Learning (EIQL) algorithm against prior offline RL methods across a wide range of domains and dataset compositions from the D4RL benchmark.
experiments.Method: EIQL will be tested on the D4RL benchmark. This includes Gym-MuJoCo locomotion environments (halfcheetah, hopper, walker2d) on random, medium, medium-replay, medium-expert, and expert datasets; Adroit tasks (pen-human-v0, pen-cloned-v0); Franka kitchen tasks (kitchen-complete-v0, kitchen-partial-v0, kitchen-mixed-v0); and AntMaze tasks (umaze, medium-play, medium-diverse, large-play, large-diverse). The performance will be compared against baselines including Behavioral Cloning (BC), TD3+BC, Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), and Implicit Q-Learning (IQL).

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of using different expectile levels in the value function update on policy performance and learning dynamics, particularly in scenarios with different reward characteristics.
experiments.Method: The EIQL algorithm will be run with various expectile levels on three distinct datasets: `walker2d-medium-replay-v2`, `antmaze-large-play-v2`, and `kitchen-partial-v0`. The learning dynamics will be compared across these settings and against the baseline IQL.

risk_factors: In future work, we plan to explore the potential of our method to improve other in-sample learning techniques, with the aim of assessing the generalizability of our approach across different domains and settings.

title: ECAttention: Adaptive and Principled Feature Expansion-Compression with Linear Efficiency
name: ECAttention
short_hypothesis: A novel attention mechanism, ECAttention, can be designed based on the geometric insight of the MCR² objective to achieve effective and principled feature expansion and compression. By operating on the column and null spaces of the feature matrix, and using randomization with Cholesky decomposition, this method can achieve nearly linear complexity. Furthermore, by introducing trainable weights for expansion and compression modules, the model can self-adaptively balance these two forces across layers.
related_work: The maximal coding rate reduction (MCR²) objective provides a principled framework for learning low-dimensional subspace representations. Prior work like ReduNet, which directly derives operators from the MCR² gradient, suffers from scalability issues. More recent methods like CRATE and ToST improve scalability but introduce limitations. CRATE's compression module (MSSA) is theoretically flawed as it increases the compression term it aims to minimize. ToST achieves linear complexity by using only the diagonal elements of the feature correlation matrix, but this neglects correlations between dimensions, failing to capture fine-grained data structures. Both approaches also lack an explicit expansion module and a self-adaptive mechanism to balance expansion and compression across layers.
abstract: The maximal coding rate reduction (MCR²) objective has been proposed to learn low-dimensional subspace representations by minimizing the compression term for intra-group compactness and maximizing the expansion term for inter-group separation. Several studies have leveraged MCR² to design principled, interpretable deep models by following or approximating its gradient to derive layer structures. However, these approaches remain limited in achieving fully principled and effective compression and lack self-adaptive control over the strength of expansion and compression across layers. In this work, we introduce ECAttention, a novel attention mechanism that incorporates principled expansion and compression modules inspired by the geometric insight of MCR². Geometrically, gradient-based updates of MCR² move features along directions shaped by the underlying data structure. Our method proposes to efficiently capture this structure using randomization combined with Cholesky decomposition to guide feature updates with nearly linear complexity. By introducing two trainable weights per layer, ECAttention is designed to self-adaptively regulate the strengths of compression and expansion.

experiments.Description: To validate whether the proposed expansion and compression modules achieve their intended effective operation of expanding and compressing feature representations.
experiments.Method: Synthetic toy data is generated from K mutually orthogonal subspaces. The proposed expansion and compression modules are applied separately to this data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Layer-wise coding rate for expansion R(Z)', 'Layer-wise coding rate for compression Rc(Z|U[K])', 'Cumulative distribution of eigenvalues (CDE) of the feature covariance matrix ZZT']

experiments.Description: To determine if compressing features by subtracting the null-space projection is a more stable and effective optimization strategy compared to directly projecting onto the column space.
experiments.Method: Two compression strategies are compared: 1) subtracting the null-space projection, and 2) directly projecting onto the column space. The experiment is conducted on a toy dataset.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the task performance and interpretability of the ECA transformer on real-world visual data against existing methods.
experiments.Method: ECA models of varying scales (ECA-S, ECA-M, ECA-B+, etc.) are pre-trained on the ImageNet-1K dataset and subsequently fine-tuned on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Oxford Flowers-102, and Oxford-IIT-Pets datasets. The performance is to be compared against ToST, CRATE, and DeiT baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Top-1 accuracy', 'Visualization of head-wise membership distribution (attention maps)']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether the ECA transformer exhibits self-adaptive behavior in balancing expansion and compression across layers.
experiments.Method: The ECA-S model is trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiment involves varying the rank hyperparameter 'r' of the random matrix used for column space approximation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Analysis of the learned expansion weights (alpha) across layers', 'Analysis of the learned compression weights (beta) across layers']

risk_factors: Although the framework shows some adaptability to random matrix size, this study does not quantify the potential information loss under varying matrix sizes.
Besides, due to limitations in computational resources and time, it remains to be investigated whether our framework can achieve strong performance on larger-scale tasks.

title: On the Optimality of Activations in Implicit Neural Representations
name: sinc-activations-for-inrs
short_hypothesis: From the perspective of sampling theory, the sinc activation function is theoretically optimal for signal encoding in shallow Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). We propose that using sinc as an activation function will enable INRs to optimally reconstruct signals while preserving smooth gradients, outperforming other common activations like sinusoids, Gaussians, and wavelets.
related_work: Prior work on Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) has explored various techniques to capture high-frequency signals, addressing the spectral bias of standard networks. These include positional encodings, sinusoidal activations (SIREN), Gaussian activations (GARF), and wavelet-based activations (WIRE). However, these methods were developed and analyzed in isolation. This fragmented approach has made it difficult to draw connections, conduct effective comparisons, and establish a unified theoretical framework for understanding the properties and optimality of different activation functions in INRs.
abstract: Implicit neural representations (INRs) have recently surged in popularity as a class of neural networks capable of encoding signals as compact, differentiable entities. To capture high-frequency content, INRs often employ techniques such as Fourier positional encodings or non-traditional activation functions like Gaussian, sinusoid, or wavelets. Despite the impressive results achieved with these activations, there has been limited exploration of their properties within a unified theoretical framework. To address this gap, we propose to conduct a comprehensive analysis of these activations from the perspective of sampling theory. We hypothesize that, particularly in the context of shallow INRs, sinc activations are theoretically optimal for signal encoding. Additionally, we plan to establish a connection between dynamical systems and INRs and leverage sampling theory to bridge these two paradigms, exploring the application of INRs in modeling such systems with minimal need for explicit regularizations.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance and parameter robustness of different INR activations for image reconstruction.
experiments.Method: INRs with different activations (sinc, SIREN, ReLU with Positional Encoding, Gaussian, Wavelet) will be trained on a sub-sampled set of the DIV2K dataset. The models will be 4-layer networks with a width of 256. A grid search will be performed to find a single optimal hyperparameter setting for each activation type, which will then be used across all images and various sampling rates to train the models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of sinc-activated INRs for novel view synthesis in the context of Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF).
experiments.Method: The performance of sinc-INRs will be evaluated on the novel view synthesis task using the real synthetic dataset from Mildenhall et al. (2021). The proposed method will be compared against INRs using other activations such as Gaussian, Sinusoid, and Wavelet.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of sinc-INRs to recover the dynamics of latent variables in chaotic dynamical systems from partial, noisy, and sparse observations.
experiments.Method: Using data from dynamical systems (Vanderpol, Limit cycle, Lorenz, Duffing), the experiment will compare two approaches for recovering the system's attractor. The baseline 'classical method' involves constructing a Hankel matrix from discrete samples and using its eigenvectors. The proposed method first uses a sinc-INR to encode the discrete measurements into a continuous signal, which is then re-sampled to create a more robust Hankel matrix. The methods' robustness will be tested by adding varying levels of noise to the input samples.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of using sinc-INRs to improve the discovery of governing equations for dynamical systems via the SINDy algorithm.
experiments.Method: Using data from the Lorenz and Rossler systems with varying levels of noise, the experiment will compare two methods for computing the time derivatives required by the SINDy algorithm. The baseline method will use spectral derivatives. The proposed method will first train a 4-layer sinc-INR on the time-series data and then compute the derivatives analytically by calculating the network's Jacobian. The resulting derivatives from both methods will be used in the SINDy algorithm to recover the system's governing equations.

risk_factors: The primary limitation of Theorem 2.4 lies in its applicability solely to signals within the domain of V(F).
A critical problem entailed with INRs is that they are sensitive to the hyperparameters in activation functions (Ramasinghe & Lucey, 2022).
The theorem does not provide conditions as to what the best n is and in practise when D is not known it does not provide conditions for the embedding dimension d.

title: GPUDrive: Data-driven, multi-agent driving simulation at 1 million FPS
name: GPUDrive-Simulator
short_hypothesis: By creating a GPU-accelerated, data-driven, multi-agent driving simulator capable of generating over a million steps per second, it will become feasible to apply sample-hungry but effective reinforcement learning algorithms to the design of autonomous planners, a task currently bottlenecked by the need for billions of simulation samples.
related_work: Multi-agent learning has been successful in games but has had limited impact on real-world multi-agent planning, such as autonomous driving. This is partly because such planning requires playing a human-compatible strategy, and partly because generating the billions of samples needed for modern learning algorithms is difficult with existing simulators. Current hardware-accelerated RL frameworks (e.g., JaxMARL, Jumanji) primarily feature fully cooperative or competitive tasks, not the mixed-motive settings of driving. Existing driving simulators like MetaDrive, nuPlan, and Nocturne are not GPU-accelerated or do not scale to the required throughput. While JAX-based simulators like Waymax are GPU-accelerated, they have limitations in sensor modality support and the number of parallel worlds they can run. The proposed work aims to fill this gap by providing a highly scalable, GPU-accelerated simulator that is data-driven, supports various sensors, and is designed for mixed-motive multi-agent RL research.
abstract: Multi-agent learning algorithms have been successful at generating superhuman planning in various games but have had limited impact on the design of deployed multi-agent planners. A key bottleneck in applying these techniques to multi-agent planning is that they require billions of steps of experience. To enable the study of multi-agent planning at scale, we present GPUDrive. GPUDrive is a GPU-accelerated, multi-agent simulator built on top of the Madrona Game Engine, designed to be capable of generating over a million simulation steps per second. Observation, reward, and dynamics functions are written directly in C++ and lowered to high-performance CUDA. Despite these low-level optimizations, GPUDrive is intended to be fully accessible through Python, offering a seamless and efficient workflow for multi-agent, closed-loop simulation. The simulator is intended for training reinforcement learning agents on datasets like the Waymo Open Motion Dataset.

experiments.Description: To measure the raw simulation speed and sample throughput of the GPUDrive simulator and compare its performance against other relevant simulators.
experiments.Method: The simulator's performance will be benchmarked on consumer-grade (RTX 4080) and datacenter-class (A100) GPUs using scenarios from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset. Performance will be compared against Nocturne (a CPU-based simulator) and Waymax (a JAX-based GPU simulator). The experiment will also evaluate the performance impact of different sensor modalities, specifically a radial filter and a GPU-accelerated LiDAR scan.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Agent Steps Per Second (ASPS)', 'Controlled Agent Steps Per Second (CASPS)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the impact of the simulator's speed on the end-to-end workflow of training reinforcement learning agents and to evaluate the feasibility of scaling training to thousands of scenarios.
experiments.Method: Reinforcement learning agents will be trained using an Independent PPO (IPPO) algorithm on a subset of scenarios from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset (WOMD). The end-to-end training time to solve scenes will be compared against training in the Nocturne simulator. The experiment will also study how the time to solve scenarios scales as the total number of unique training scenarios is increased from 32 to 1024.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Goal-reaching rate as a function of training steps and wall-clock time', 'Time-to-completion to reach a 95% goal-reaching rate', 'Amortized time to solve a single scenario']

risk_factors: Absence of a map. The current version of the simulator does not have a well-defined notion of lanes or a higher-level road map which makes it challenging for algorithmic approaches that require maps.
Convex objects only. Collision checking relies on the objects being represented as convex objects.

Unsolvable goals. Due to incorrect labels of some road points in the Waymo dataset, such as an exit to a parking lot being labeled as an impassable road edge, some agent goals (roughly 2%) are unreachable.
Initialization modes: In many scenarios, a significant portion of agents (25-75%) are already at or very close to their goal positions, such as parked cars.
Agent performance: Training agents to navigate without crashing in any scenario, matching human capabilities, remains an unresolved challenge.

title: CoTabBench: A Real-World Benchmark for Question Answering over Weakly-Structured and Heterogeneous Tables
name: cotabbench
short_hypothesis: Existing Large Language Models, which are primarily evaluated on well-structured tables, will exhibit significant performance degradation on a new benchmark, CoTabBench, which features real-world tables with complex, irregular structures and heterogeneous content. A model fine-tuned on a purpose-built instruction dataset, CoTabInstruct, will demonstrate more robust performance on these challenging tables.
related_work: Current table QA benchmarks (e.g., WTQ, TableFact, FinQA) predominantly use regular, clean tables. While some research has addressed specific challenges like hierarchical structures (HiTab) or domain-specific content (FinQA, AIT-QA) in isolation, a comprehensive benchmark that systematically evaluates model performance against the compound challenge of simultaneous structural irregularity and content heterogeneity found in real-world tables is a critical gap.
abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly propelled their capabilities in table-based question answering. However, existing benchmarks predominantly feature well-structured tables, failing to address the complexities of real-world data, which is often weakly-structured and contains highly heterogeneous content. This discrepancy limits the evaluation of model robustness on diverse and challenging formats. To bridge this gap, we introduce CoTabBench, a large-scale, multi-domain, and intricate benchmark featuring real-world, weakly-structured tables. We further propose a novel complexity assessment framework, which quantitatively validates the inherent structural and content-based challenges within CoTabBench. Furthermore, we introduce CoTabInstruct, a large-scale training corpus, and present CoTabLLM, a 7B model trained on it.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art Large Language Models on question answering over complex, weakly-structured, and heterogeneous tables using the newly proposed CoTabBench benchmark. The evaluation is broken down into three specific tasks: Row and Column Counting, TableQA, and Hallucination Detection.
experiments.Method: The benchmark, CoTabBench, contains over 2,700 tables and 8,600 QA pairs across 10 domains, sourced from scientific literature (LaTeX) and public websites (HTML). The proposed model, CoTabLLM, is a 7B parameter model (Qwen2.5-7B or LLaMA3-8B) fine-tuned on the CoTabInstruct dataset (over 11,000 tables and 32,000 QA pairs) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). This model will be evaluated against a wide range of over 20 baseline LLMs, including proprietary models (GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1-mini, Qwen-Plus/Turbo, Gemini 2.5 Flash), open-source models (Llama series, Qwen series, DeepSeek-V3, Kimi K2), reasoning-focused models (o4-mini, DeepSeek-R1), and existing table-specialized models.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy for the Row and Column Counting task.', 'Accuracy for the Hallucination Detection task.', 'Exact Match (after text normalization) for the TableQA task.']

experiments.Description: To quantitatively validate that the proposed CoTabBench benchmark is more challenging than existing benchmarks by using a novel complexity assessment framework.
experiments.Method: A new framework is introduced to measure table complexity along two dimensions: Structural Irregularity and Content Heterogeneity. Structural Irregularity is quantified using metrics like Merged Cell Ratio (MCR), Merged Cell Span (MCS), and Header Depth Index (HDI). Content Heterogeneity is quantified using an LLM-as-a-judge to score Cell Content Complexity (CCC), Data Type Diversity (DTD), and Semantic & Domain Complexity (SDC). These metrics will be calculated for CoTabBench and compared against prior benchmarks like WTQ and InfoTabs.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Comparison of MCR, MCS, and HDI scores.', 'Comparison of CCC, DTD, and SDC scores.', 'Pearson correlation between LLM-as-a-judge scores and human expert ratings for content complexity.']

title: Antithetic Noise in Diffusion Models
name: antithetic-noise-diffusion
short_hypothesis: Pairing every Gaussian noise sample `z` with its negation `-z` (antithetic sampling) in diffusion and other generative models will consistently produce strongly negatively correlated output samples. This negative correlation can be leveraged to create more efficient estimators for uncertainty quantification and to improve other applications like image editing and generation diversity.
related_work: Most prior work on improving diffusion models has focused on architectural/training developments or accelerated sampling.
