The initial Gaussian noise, a key ingredient, has received little attention. The few works that do study initial noise are typically task-specific optimizations. This paper proposes a systematic and universal study of how manipulating the initial noise itself, specifically through antithetic pairing, shapes the outputs of diffusion models, a perspective orthogonal to prior efforts.
abstract: We systematically study antithetic initial noise in diffusion models, proposing that pairing each noise sample with its negation produces strong negative correlation. We hypothesize this phenomenon holds across datasets, model architectures, conditional and unconditional sampling, and even other generative models such as VAEs and Normalizing Flows. To explain it, we propose a symmetry conjecture that the learned score function is approximately affine antisymmetric (odd symmetry up to a constant shift). We hypothesize this negative correlation can lead to more reliable uncertainty quantification. We plan to demonstrate these gains on tasks including estimating pixel-wise statistics and evaluating diffusion inverse solvers. We will also provide extensions with randomized quasi-Monte Carlo noise designs for uncertainty quantification, and explore additional applications of the antithetic noise design to improve image editing and generation diversity. Our proposed framework is training-free, model-agnostic, and adds no runtime overhead.

experiments.Description: To investigate the core hypothesis that antithetic initial noise (z vs. -z) produces negatively correlated outputs compared to independent noise (z1 vs. z2) across a wide range of generative models.
experiments.Method: Image pairs will be generated under two sampling schemes: PN (positive vs. negative noise) and RR (random vs. random noise). This will be evaluated across: (1) unconditional diffusion models (U-Net and DiT on LSUN-Church, CelebA-HQ, CIFAR-10), (2) conditional diffusion models (SD 1.5, DiT), (3) other generative models like an unconditional VAE on MNIST and a conditional Glow model on CIFAR-10, and (4) consistency models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of antithetic sampling for improving the efficiency of uncertainty quantification when estimating pixel-wise image statistics.
experiments.Method: Standard Monte Carlo (MC), Antithetic Monte Carlo (AMC), and Randomized Quasi-Monte Carlo (RQMC) estimators will be used to estimate statistics. The evaluation will use unconditional models (on CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Church) and conditional models (Stable Diffusion 1.5 on Pick-a-Pic/DrawBench prompts, DiT on ImageNet classes). The performance of AMC and RQMC will be compared against the MC baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Pixel value mean', 'Perceived brightness', 'Contrast', 'Image centroid', '95% confidence interval (CI) width', "Relative efficiency (squared ratio of MC CI width to the new estimator's CI width)"]

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficiency of the antithetic estimator in quantifying uncertainty for diffusion inverse problem solvers on reconstruction tasks.
experiments.Method: The Antithetic Monte Carlo (AMC) estimator will be compared to the standard Monte Carlo (MC) estimator. Two popular solvers, Diffusion Posterior Sampling (DPS) and Decomposed Diffusion Sampler (DDS), will be evaluated. For DPS, tasks include human face reconstruction (inpainting, Gaussian deblurring, super-resolution) on CelebA-HQ. For DDS, the task is medical image reconstruction from the fastMRI dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Confidence interval lengths for reconstruction quality metrics (L1 distance, PSNR)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether using antithetic noise pairs improves generation diversity compared to independent noise pairs, without degrading image quality.
experiments.Method: Image pairs will be generated using antithetic noise (PN) and independent random noise (RR) for both unconditional (CIFAR-10, LSUN-Church, CelebA-HQ) and conditional (Stable Diffusion 1.5, DiT) diffusion models.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Pairwise SSIM (lower is more diverse)', 'Pairwise LPIPS (higher is more diverse)']

experiments.Description: To test if applying the antithetic noise strategy to an existing image editing algorithm can improve its performance.
experiments.Method: The antithetic noise strategy will be integrated into the FlowEdit algorithm. In its setting with two noise samples, the two independent random samples will be replaced with an antithetic noise pair (Z and -Z). This will be compared against the original random sampling method on prompts from the official FlowEdit repository.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['CLIP score (semantic text-image alignment)', 'LPIPS (perceptual distance to source)']

risk_factors: A limitation of our work is that while the symmetry conjecture is supported by both empirical and theoretical evidence, it remains open in full generality.
In addition, our method is not a cure-all: the LPIPS diversity improvements in Table 4 are consistent but modest.

While we obtain large gains for pixel-wise statistics, the improvements for perceptual or text-alignment metrics such as MUSIQ and CLIP Score are small, since the strong nonlinearity of transformer decoders attenuates most of the negative correlation present in the diffusion outputs.

title: Squeeze Training for Adversarial Robustness
name: Squeeze Training with Collaborative Examples
short_hypothesis: Jointly optimizing a model on both adversarial examples (high-loss points) and collaborative examples (low-loss points) found within the ε-neighborhood of a benign example, by penalizing their maximum possible output discrepancy, will regularize the non-flat regions of the loss landscape and improve adversarial robustness.
related_work: Adversarial training (AT) is a common defense against adversarial examples, typically formulated as a min-max optimization problem to find and train on high-loss adversarial examples (e.g., vanilla AT, TRADES, MART). While these methods focus on the 'plateau' regions of the loss landscape, the 'valley' regions, which contain low-loss 'collaborative examples', are less explored. The proposed research hypothesizes that incorporating these collaborative examples into the training process can further enhance adversarial robustness, a direction not explicitly taken by prior AT methods.
abstract: The vulnerability of deep neural networks (DNNs) to adversarial examples is related to non-flatness and non-smoothness of normally obtained loss landscapes. Training augmented with adversarial examples (a.k.a., adversarial training) is considered as an effective remedy. In this paper, we highlight that some collaborative examples, nearly perceptually indistinguishable from both adversarial and benign examples yet show extremely lower prediction loss, can be utilized to enhance adversarial training. A novel method is therefore proposed to achieve new state-of-the-arts in adversarial robustness.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Squeeze Training (ST) method in improving adversarial robustness compared to state-of-the-art adversarial training techniques.
experiments.Method: Models (ResNet-18, Wide ResNet) will be trained on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN datasets. The performance of ST will be compared against baselines including vanilla AT, TRADES, and MART.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Clean accuracy', 'Robust accuracy under white-box attacks: FGSM, PGD-20, PGD-100, C&W, and AutoAttack (AA)']

experiments.Description: To assess the compatibility and potential for further improvement by combining Squeeze Training (ST) with other recent advances, specifically Adversarial Weight Perturbation (AWP).
experiments.Method: The ST learning objective will be combined with AWP (ST+AWP) and trained on ResNet-18 models using CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN datasets. This will be compared against the TRADES+AWP baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Clean accuracy', 'Robust accuracy under white-box attacks: FGSM, PGD-20, PGD-100, C&W, and AutoAttack (AA)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the performance of Squeeze Training (ST) when augmented with additional unlabeled data, by combining it with Robust Self-Training (RST).
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be combined with RST (termed ST-RST) to train a WRN-28-10 model on CIFAR-10 along with 500K unlabeled images from the 80 Million Tiny Images dataset. Performance will be compared to other methods that use the same unlabeled data, including RST, GAIR-RST, AWP-RST, BAT-RST, and RWP-RST.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Clean accuracy', 'Robust accuracy under PGDRST and AutoAttack (AA)']

experiments.Description: To perform an ablation study on the choice of the regularization function (discrepancy metric) used within the Squeeze Training (ST) framework.
experiments.Method: ST and TRADES will be trained on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 using different regularization functions: JS divergence, squared l2 distance, and symmetric KL divergence. The trade-off between clean and robust accuracy will be analyzed by varying the scaling factor β.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Clean vs. Robust Accuracy trade-off curves, with robust accuracy evaluated by AutoAttack']

risk_factors: Since the adversarial examples and collaborative examples are both required in ST, the computational complexity in its inner optimization increases.

Our ST adopts the same regularization loss for inner and outer optimization, and we also observed that, if not, moderate gradient masking occurs, i.e., higher PGD-20 accuracy but lower classification accuracy under more powerful attacks, just like in Gowal et al. (2020)'s.

title: Entropy Voting Between Capsules
name: entropy-voting-capsules
short_hypothesis: A novel voting method for capsule networks, called entropy voting, can be formulated from an information-theoretic perspective by modeling capsules as Monte Carlo sampled continuous random variables. This method aims to maximize the marginal differential entropies of all capsules while minimizing the relative entropy (Kullback-Leibler divergence) between child and parent capsules, leading to a more efficient and robust routing mechanism.
related_work: Previous work on capsule networks has focused on improving the routing algorithm through methods like dynamic routing, expectation-maximization, attention mechanisms, and variational Bayesian inference. However, these approaches often suffer from computationally inefficient routing (iterative, attention-based, or sub-network-based) and potential training instability due to non-differentiable activation functions like 'squash'. This paper identifies a gap in addressing these computational and stability issues, and questions the prevalent use of data augmentation in methods that are theoretically designed to reduce its necessity.
abstract: Capsule networks offer a promising solution in computer vision by addressing the limitations of convolutional neural networks (CNNs), such as data dependency and viewpoint challenges. Unlike CNNs, capsules reduce the need for data augmentation by enhancing generalization from limited training data. We explore capsules from the perspective of information theory, viewing them as Monte Carlo sampled continuous random variables. We use marginal differential entropy to measure the information content of capsules, and relative entropy to model the agreement between lower-level and higher-level capsules. The proposed entropy voting method aims to maximize capsule marginal entropies and to minimize their relative entropy.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed entropy voting method on the MNIST handwritten digit classification task, comparing it against state-of-the-art capsule networks.
experiments.Method: The proposed model, consisting of convolutional layers followed by capsule layers with entropy voting, will be trained and tested on the MNIST dataset. The experiment will be conducted both with and without data augmentation (random affine transformations). The model will also be evaluated with and without a reconstruction regularizer. Baselines for comparison are CapsNet (Sabour et al., 2017), Efficient-CapsNet (Mazzia et al., 2021), and HVC-CapsNet (Byerly et al., 2021).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed entropy voting method on the CIFAR10 object recognition task.
experiments.Method: The proposed model will be trained and tested on the CIFAR10 dataset. The experiment will be conducted both with and without data augmentation (random affine transformations) and with/without a reconstruction regularizer. Baselines for comparison are CapsNet (Sabour et al., 2017) and HVC-CapsNet (Byerly et al., 2021).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed entropy voting method on the SVHN street view house numbers recognition task.
experiments.Method: The proposed model will be trained and tested on the SVHN dataset. The experiment will be conducted both with and without data augmentation (random affine transformations) and with/without a reconstruction regularizer. The baseline for comparison is CapsNet (Sabour et al., 2017).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed entropy voting method on the smallNORB 3D object recognition task across different input resolutions.
experiments.Method: The proposed model will be trained and tested on the smallNORB dataset using two input sizes: 48x48 and 32x32. The experiment will be conducted both with and without data augmentation (random affine transformations) and with/without a reconstruction regularizer. Baselines for comparison are Efficient-CapsNet (Mazzia et al., 2021) for the 48x48 experiment and CapsNet (Sabour et al., 2017) for the 32x32 experiment.

risk_factors: While CIFAR10 can probably be considered the most complex of the datasets used in the experiments, it does raise a question about the relationship between image resolution and its content; the more complex the content of an image is, the more useful a higher resolution is.
From the experimental results one might conclude that our method performs well only on simpler datasets, such as MNIST.

Bigger capsules did not offer better predictive performance in the end, so there is a trade-off between stability and predictive accuracy when it comes to the original smallNORB dataset, and potentially other higher resolution datasets, too.

title: Monitoring Latent World States in Language Models with Propositional Probes
name: propositional-probes-for-latent-world-states
short_hypothesis: Language models (LMs) faithfully represent their input contexts in a latent world model. These latent world states can be extracted as logical propositions using 'propositional probes'. These probes work by compositionally extracting lexical concepts from token activations and binding them together into propositions by identifying a 'binding subspace' where activations for bound tokens have high similarity.
related_work: Prior work has focused on probing for smaller lexical concepts (e.g., color, gender) or for propositional beliefs in specific, narrow environments (e.g., Othello, TextWorld). Other research has identified 'binding vectors' in LM activations but could not identify the 'binding subspace' they live in or their geometric relationships. This work proposes to bridge this gap by exploiting compositionality to extract complex propositions and introduces a Hessian-based method to identify the binding subspace, which was a limitation of previous techniques.
abstract: Language models (LMs) are susceptible to bias, sycophancy, backdoors, and other tendencies that lead to unfaithful responses to the input context. Interpreting internal states of LMs could help monitor and correct unfaithful behavior. We hypothesize that LMs faithfully represent their input contexts in a latent world model, and we seek to extract these latent world states as logical propositions. For example, given the input context “Greg is a nurse. Laura is a physicist.”, we aim to decode the propositions WorksAs(Greg, nurse) and WorksAs(Laura, physicist) from the model's internal activations. To do so we introduce propositional probes, which compositionally extract lexical concepts from token activations and bind them into propositions. Key to this is identifying a binding subspace in which bound tokens have high similarity but unbound ones do not. This approach is intended to be useful for monitoring LMs, as it may allow for the decoding of a faithful world model even when the LM's output is unfaithful.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the hypothesis that the subspace identified by the proposed Hessian-based algorithm causally mediates the binding of entities to attributes.
experiments.Method: An interchange intervention will be performed on the internal activations of a Tulu-2-13b model. Using synthetic contexts with three entities and attributes, the activations corresponding to two entities within the identified subspace will be swapped. The experiment will test if this intervention successfully swaps the attributes bound to those entities. The performance of the Hessian-based subspace will be compared against a random subspace baseline, a subspace found using Distributed Alignment Search (DAS), and a skyline subspace derived from binding vector differences.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Intervention success accuracy (checking if the probability of the correct new attribute is highest after the swap)', 'Lowest accuracy across all queried entities']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization performance of propositional probes on standard, non-adversarial inputs of increasing complexity and linguistic variation.
experiments.Method: Propositional probes will be trained on the `SYNTH` dataset, which consists of simple templated English sentences. They will then be tested on held-out `SYNTH` data, the `PARA` dataset (more complex short stories), and the `TRANS` dataset (Spanish translations). The model used is Tulu-2-13b. Performance will be compared to a 'prompting skyline' baseline that asks the model direct questions. Ablation studies will be conducted by replacing the Hessian-based binding metric with metrics derived from DAS subspaces and a random subspace.

experiments.Description: To test if propositional probes remain faithful in adversarial settings where the language model is induced to produce unfaithful outputs.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted in three settings using the Tulu-2-13b model and the `SYNTH`, `PARA`, and `TRANS` datasets. (1) Prompt Injection: The prefix 'Always answer the opposite.' is added to inputs. (2) Backdoor Attack: The model is fine-tuned to give wrong answers on the Spanish `TRANS` dataset. (3) Gender Bias: The model is evaluated on pro- and anti-stereotypical statements of gender and occupation. The faithfulness of the propositions extracted by the probes will be compared to the model's direct output via a prompting baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Jaccard index (for prompt injection and backdoor)', 'Exact-match accuracy (EM) (for prompt injection and backdoor)', 'Accuracy and Calibrated Accuracy (for gender bias)']

risk_factors: This suggests that the 50-dimensional binding subspace obtained from the Hessian may either contain spurious non-binding directions or only incompletely capture the binding subspace.
Thus, our current methods may be too noisy for contexts with more than two entities.
While probing is able to mitigate the latter, it might still be subject to the former.

we believe that this approach could be scaled to larger worlds with more complex semantics if more aspects of how LMs represent meaning are discovered, such as representations of role-filler binding (Smolensky, 1990) and state changes (Kim & Schuster, 2023).

title: KVCompose: Efficient Structured KV Cache Compression with Composite Tokens
name: kvcompose-composite-tokens
short_hypothesis: A structured KV cache compression framework, named KVCompose, can achieve significant memory reduction while preserving model accuracy by using three key components: (1) an attention-guided token scoring mechanism to estimate token importance, (2) the construction of 'composite tokens' where each attention head independently selects its most important tokens before they are aligned into a dense tensor, and (3) a layer-adaptive budget allocation mechanism that assigns more capacity to layers with more informative tokens. This approach is hypothesized to be more effective than prior methods while remaining fully compatible with standard inference engines.
related_work: Prior work on KV cache compression falls into three categories. 1) Structured Eviction methods (e.g., TOVA, StreamingLLM, SnapKV, PyramidKV) remove entire tokens, ensuring compatibility with standard inference engines but often relying on rigid heuristics, fixed budgets, or uniform per-head token selection, which can be suboptimal. 2) Semi-Structured Eviction methods (e.g., DuoAttention) offer more flexibility by grouping heads but can require offline optimization and have coarse granularity. 3) Unstructured Eviction methods (e.g., KVzip, Ada-variants) achieve high compression by producing sparse KV caches but require specialized compute kernels, limiting their practical deployment. The research gap lies in creating a method that offers the flexibility of fine-grained, adaptive token selection similar to unstructured methods, but within a structured eviction framework that remains compatible with existing hardware and inference pipelines.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) rely on key-value (KV) caches for efficient autoregressive decoding; however, cache size grows linearly with context length and model depth, becoming a major bottleneck in long-context inference. Prior KV cache compression methods either enforce rigid heuristics, disrupt tensor layouts with per-attention-head variability, or require specialized compute kernels. We propose a KV cache compression framework based on attention-guided, layer-adaptive composite tokens. The method aggregates attention scores to estimate token importance, selects head-specific tokens independently, and aligns them into composite tokens that respect the uniform cache structure required by existing inference engines. A global allocation mechanism further adapts retention budgets across layers, assigning more capacity to layers with informative tokens. The proposed approach is designed to be a practical and scalable solution for efficient long-context LLM deployment, remaining fully compatible with standard inference pipelines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the KVCompose method in preserving task accuracy under various compression ratios and to compare its robustness against state-of-the-art structured and semi-structured eviction methods. The experiment will assess the benefits of layer-adaptive composite token selection over fixed or heuristic schedules and test the method's robustness across different models and evaluation setups (task-aware vs. task-agnostic).
experiments.Method: The evaluation will be conducted on the Ruler-4096 dataset from the kvpress benchmark, which includes 13 task types across four categories: Needle in a haystack (NIAH), Aggregation tasks (CWE, FWE), Multi-hop tracing (VT), and Question Answering (QA). Three open-source LLMs will be used: LLAMA-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and Qwen3-14B. The proposed method will be compared against several baselines: TOVA, StreamingLLM, SnapKV, PyramidKV, DuoAttention, and ExpectedAttention.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy-compression curves, plotting task accuracy as a function of the compression ratio (r).', 'Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the accuracy-compression curve to provide a single robustness metric.', 'Maximum achievable compression ratio for a given performance degradation tolerance (ε0).', 'Average accuracy over all questions for a given task, scored on a continuous scale from 0.0 to 1.0.']

risk_factors: Although unstructured variants can offer slightly higher accuracy, they require custom kernels and are impractical for deployment.

While our primary focus is on structured eviction methods, we also examine an unstructured variant of our approach and compare it against unstructured KV cache compression strategies. The results are reported in Appendix A.2.

title: Machine Unlearning in Low-dimensional Feature Subspace
name: subspace-unlearning-sun
short_hypothesis: It is possible to perform machine unlearning by learning a projection matrix that maps features into a low-dimensional subspace. This subspace is optimized using a PCA-inspired objective to maximize information about the remaining data while minimizing information about the data to be forgotten. This approach, named SUbspace UNlearning (SUN), is intended to be more efficient and privacy-preserving by operating as a plug-in module that avoids direct data access and modification of the original model's parameters.
related_work: Existing approximate Machine Unlearning (MU) methods typically update the parameters of the original model to align its outputs with an ideal retrained model. These methods often require frequent and massive access to both the forgetting and remaining data, which incurs significant computational overhead and privacy risks. Furthermore, they modify the pretrained model's parameters for each unlearning request, making it inflexible and computationally expensive to handle multiple requests. This work aims to address these challenges of frequent data access and direct model modification.
abstract: Data privacy in modern neural networks attracts growing interest in Machine Unlearning (MU), which aims at removing the knowledge of particular data from a pretrained model and meanwhile maintaining good performances on the remaining data. In this work, a new perspective upon low-dimensional feature subspaces is presented to investigate MU. We first demonstrate the potentials of separating the remaining and forgetting data in a low-dimensional feature subspace. Then, such separability motivates us to seek a subspace range(U) on the features of the pretrained model for unlearning, where the information of the remaining data is preserved and that of the forgetting data is therein diminished, leading to the proposed new method named SUbspace UNlearning (SUN). Compared to mainstream MU methods that require direct and massive access to the training data for model updating, SUN offers two key advantages. (i) SUN avoids frequent data visits and optimizes U involving two covariance matrices, which only requires one-shot feature fetching and thereby alleviates data privacy risks and computation. (ii) SUN in implementation simply serves as a plug-in module to the pretrained model without modifications to its original parameters, reducing the parameter number and computational overhead.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed SUN method on multi-class and single-class unlearning tasks against standard baselines.
experiments.Method: For multi-class unlearning, a Swin-T model will be evaluated on the Tiny-ImageNet dataset, with the task of unlearning 4 out of 200 classes. For single-class unlearning, a ResNet50 model will be evaluated on the ImageNet-1K dataset, with the task of unlearning 1 out of 1000 classes. The method will be compared against a pretrained model, a fully retrained model (exact MU), Fine-Tuning (FT), Gradient Ascent (GA), Random Labeling (RL), SalUn, Bad Teaching (BT), Learn to UnLearn (L2UL), and DELETE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Acc_fg (accuracy on forgetting training data)', 'Acc_rm (accuracy on remaining training data)', 'Acc_fg^te (accuracy on forgetting test data)', 'Acc_rm^te (accuracy on remaining test data)', 'Membership Inference Attack (MIA) success rate', 'Avg.G. (average gap to the exact retrained model across accuracy and MIA)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the robustness of the proposed method under an extreme unlearning scenario where a very high ratio of training samples is removed.
experiments.Method: A Swin-T model will be evaluated on the Tiny-ImageNet dataset. The task is to unlearn 180 out of 200 classes. The method will be compared against the same set of baselines as the main class-unlearning experiments.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method to instance-wise unlearning.
experiments.Method: A Swin-T model will be evaluated on the Tiny-ImageNet dataset. The task is to unlearn 1,000 randomly selected training samples (1% of the data). The method will be compared against the same set of baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Acc_tr (accuracy on training data subsets)', 'Accte (accuracy on the whole test dataset)', 'MIA', 'Avg.G.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method on real-world application tasks, specifically face recognition and emotion recognition.
experiments.Method: For face recognition, a ResNet50 model will be evaluated on the VGGFace2 dataset, with the task of forgetting one of 200 identities. For emotion recognition, a ResNet18 model will be evaluated on the RAF-DB dataset, with the task of forgetting the 'fear' emotion out of 7 total emotions. The method will be compared against the standard set of baselines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of different MU methods in a practical scenario where multiple unlearning requests are made in a continual order.
experiments.Method: A Swin-T model will be evaluated on the Tiny-ImageNet dataset. The experiment will consist of a 4-round continual unlearning process, where 2 classes are randomly selected to be forgotten in each round. The method will be compared against RL, BT, and DELETE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Acc_rm (accuracy on remaining data)', 'Acc_fg (accuracy on current forgetting data)', 'Acc_fgp (accuracy on previously forgotten data)', 'Test set versions of the above accuracy metrics']

risk_factors: SUN is currently applied to discriminative models for classification, and not yet extended to generation, as generative models are more susceptible to the internal changes in feature layers.
It would be interesting to further investigate the subspace unlearning for image generation in future work.

title: Emergent Global OOD Performance in Multimodal Mammography Models
name: scale-emergent-ood-mammography
short_hypothesis: Increasing parameter scale alone, within a single architecture family and without adding extra training data, will yield emergent out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness in a CLIP-trained vision-language model for mammography, particularly at clinically relevant low-FPR operating points.
related_work: Prior work in mammography AI has demonstrated strong screening performance but often faces challenges with out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization across different institutions, demographics, and equipment. While some studies have explored vision-language pretraining (e.g., CLIP-style models) to improve robustness, they often introduce other architectural changes or use richer text data. A research gap exists in isolating parameter scale as a single experimental factor to understand its direct impact on OOD performance when trained on a fixed, single-institution dataset and evaluated zero-shot on international cohorts.
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization is a central barrier to deploying mammography AI fairly across health systems, especially in LMICs. We test whether parameter scale alone, within one architecture family and without adding extra images, yields emergent robustness in a CLIP-trained vision–language model (VLM). Using EMBED-open, we plan to train a CLIP VLM on cohort-1 only. The model's ViT patch-16 image tower will span 6M, 22M, 86M, and 307M parameters. Multimodality is limited to short, de-identified captions (sex, age, ethnicity, manufacturer, view) with a frozen biomedical text encoder. Downstream clinical targets will be modeled via linear probes on learned embeddings. We will evaluate the models in a zero-shot setting on five international OOD cohorts—DMID (India), VinDr-Mammo (Vietnam), KAU-BCMD (Saudi Arabia), CMMD (China), and INBreast (Portugal). The hypothesis is that this scale-driven approach, achieved without extra data, task labels during training, or architectural changes, will lead to emergent global OOD robustness.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effect of model scale on in-domain (ID) performance. This experiment will measure how performance on the source dataset's held-out cohort changes as the model's parameter count increases, while all other factors (data, architecture family, training objective) are held constant.
experiments.Method: A CLIP-style vision-language model (VLM) will be trained on the EMBED-open cohort-1 dataset. The model's image tower is a ViT patch-16 backbone of four different sizes: XXS (~6M), S (~22M), M (~86M), and L (~307M) parameters. The text tower is a frozen Bio-ClinicalBERT. After contrastive pretraining, the image tower is frozen, and a regularized logistic regression linear probe is fit on EMBED-open cohort-2 to predict a cancer label. This probe is then evaluated on the same cohort-2.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that increasing model scale alone induces emergent zero-shot out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness. The models of varying scales, trained only on a single US-based dataset, will be evaluated on five international datasets without any fine-tuning or calibration.
experiments.Method: The same set of four VLM models (XXS, S, M, L) trained on EMBED-open cohort-1 and the same linear probe fit on EMBED-open cohort-2 will be used. This fixed setup (model + probe) will be applied directly to five international OOD cohorts: DMID (India), VinDr-Mammo (Vietnam), KAU-BCMD (Saudi Arabia), CMMD (China), and INBreast (Portugal). The evaluation is strictly zero-shot, with no fitting or threshold tuning on the target OOD data.

risk_factors: Zero-shot OOD AUROCs are still close to chance on several cohorts.
We did not evaluate calibration (ECE) or OOD detection, report confidence intervals, or perform per-cohort threshold transfer.
Label harmonization across datasets used a unified but heuristic mapping (pathology keywords or BI-RADS ≥4) that may not match local labeling protocols.
We studied 2D images with minimal text; DBT and richer reports could further change the scaling curve.
Finally, we used a single linear probe; few-shot target-domain adaptation may reveal steeper OOD scaling.

title: Hyperspherical embedding for novel class classification
name: hyperspherical-embedding-nsl-for-novel-classes
short_hypothesis: A methodology based on the Normalized Softmax Loss (NSL) can be applied to the novel class classification problem by training a network, freezing its parameters, and then inferring the classification weights for new classes by averaging the feature vectors of a few labeled samples from those new classes, thus allowing classification without fine-tuning.
related_work: Metric learning is a common approach for identifying novel classes. Methods like triplet loss, contrastive loss, and prototypical networks enforce a metric structure on the latent space, causing classes to form disjoint clusters. However, these approaches often require pairwise learning, which compares pairs of inputs to learn similarity. This requirement limits scalability due to the large number of possible pairs, creating a need for a more efficient method that does not rely on pairwise training.
abstract: Traditional deep neural networks work well on the closed set problem. For learning tasks involving novel classes, known as the open set problem, the metric learning approach has been proposed. However, while promising, common metric learning approaches require pairwise learning, which significantly increases training cost while adding additional challenges. This paper presents a method in which the similarity of samples projected onto a feature space is enforced by a metric learning approach without requiring pairwise evaluation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed NSL-based approach for classifying novel classes in a disjoint setting, where the model only has to distinguish between unseen classes.
experiments.Method: Datasets: CIFAR-10 and FASHION MNIST. A VGG-based model is trained with NSL on a subset of classes (10 - K*). It is then tasked with classifying the remaining K* unseen classes, where K* is varied from 2 to 5. This is compared against baseline methods using Triplet loss and Contrastive loss, whose embeddings are fed to a k-nearest neighbours model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the test set of unseen classes']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed approach in a joint setting, where the model must classify both the original 'seen' classes and the newly introduced 'unseen' classes.
experiments.Method: Datasets: CIFAR-10 and FASHION MNIST. A model is trained with NSL on 10 - K* classes. It is then evaluated on its ability to classify all 10 classes (the original seen classes and the K* unseen classes). This is compared against baselines using Triplet loss and Contrastive loss.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on seen classes', 'Accuracy on unseen classes']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the NSL approach on a real-world, long-tail distribution dataset for open-set classification in both disjoint and joint scenarios.
experiments.Method: Dataset: A subset of the Pl@ntNet dataset. A custom CNN model is trained on common classes (those with more than N samples, where N is one of {200, 100, 50, 25}). The task is to then classify rare/unseen classes by inferring their weights. The evaluation will be performed over an average of 30 runs.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of the proposed weight inference method against a standard incremental learning approach in a few-shot scenario.
experiments.Method: Dataset: CIFAR-10. A ResNet50 model is trained with the NSL constraint on a subset of classes. For a set of novel classes, a small number of samples (one, five, and twenty-five shots) are used. The performance of inferring weights directly (proposed method) is compared against an incremental learning baseline that fine-tunes the classification layer on these few shots while freezing other network weights.

risk_factors: This simple theoretical result does not hold anymore when there is more than one novel classes (i.e. when K* > 1).
However, when including multiple classes, our proposal may not yield the optimum set of weights for each new neuron.

title: A Scalable Inter-edge Correlation Modeling in CopulaGNN for Link Sign Prediction
name: scalable-inter-edge-correlation-copulagnn
short_hypothesis: Modeling the latent statistical dependency among edges in a signed graph using a Gaussian copula can be made computationally tractable and scalable. This can be achieved by 1) representing the edge-edge correlation matrix as a Gramian of low-dimensional edge embeddings to reduce parameters, and 2) reformulating the conditional probability distribution using the Woodbury matrix identity to reduce inference cost. This approach is hypothesized to achieve significantly faster convergence while maintaining competitive prediction performance.
related_work: Regular Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are not directly applicable to link sign prediction because negative edges violate the homophily assumption. Existing signed GNNs (SGNNs) handle this by using auxiliary structures based on sociological principles (e.g., balance theory) or by treating positive and negative edges separately. However, these approaches often result in slow convergence and inefficient memory usage, and they tend to overlook the direct statistical dependencies that may exist between edges.
abstract: Link sign prediction on a signed graph is a task to determine whether the relationship represented by an edge is positive or negative. Since the presence of negative edges violates the graph homophily assumption, regular graph methods have not been applicable without auxiliary structures. We aim to directly model the latent statistical dependency among edges with the Gaussian copula. However, a naive modeling of edge-edge relations is computationally intractable. To address this, we propose to 1) represent the correlation matrix as a Gramian of edge embeddings, significantly reducing the number of parameters, and 2) reformulate the conditional probability distribution to dramatically reduce the inference cost.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall link sign prediction performance and scalability of the proposed method (CopulaLSP) against a range of state-of-the-art baselines on real-world datasets.
experiments.Method: The task is link sign prediction on the BitcoinAlpha, BitcoinOTC, WikiElec, WikiRfa, SlashDot, and Epinions datasets. The proposed model, CopulaLSP, will be compared against baselines including GCN, SGCN, SNEA, SDGNN, TrustSGCN, SLGNN, SE-SGformer, and SGAAE. The SNEA model will be used as the primary backbone encoder for the proposed method.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['AUC', 'Macro F1', 'Epoch to converge', 'Time per epoch', 'Total time', 'Time speedup', 'GPU memory']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of the model's two key components: the Gramian-based correlation matrix and the Woodbury reformulation for efficient inference.
experiments.Method: 1) Gramian Correlation: The performance of the proposed model using a learned Gramian-based correlation will be compared against a variant using a fixed identity matrix (i.e., no inter-edge correlation). 2) Woodbury Reformulation: The inference efficiency of the model will be evaluated with and without applying the Woodbury reformulation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['AUC', 'F1', 'Epoch to converge', 'Training time', 'Inference time', 'Inference speedup', 'Inference GPU usage']

experiments.Description: To analyze the model's behavior on a synthetic dataset designed to have structurally symmetric but semantically distinct communities, a scenario where node-centric models are expected to fail.
experiments.Method: A synthetic graph with two symmetric communities (positive intra-group edges, negative inter-group edges) will be used. The proposed CopulaLSP model will be compared against the SNEA baseline. The learned components of CopulaLSP (edge embeddings, marginal parameters, correlation matrix) will be visualized to understand how it distinguishes the edge signs.

risk_factors: We have assumed a static graph structure throughout this work, as it is inherent to semi-supervised learning on graphs. Applying our approach to dynamic and bipartite graphs would be a promising future direction, e.g., for social recommendation systems.

title: Factored Representation for Neuro-Symbolic AI
name: factored-representation-for-neuro-symbolic-ai
short_hypothesis: Forcing a pre-trained LLM to maintain a structured internal state using a formal representation can lead to comparable reasoning performance, but with significantly improved interpretability, making the model's thought process more transparent and verifiable.
related_work: Large language models (LLMs) are powerful but their internal decision-making is opaque and unverifiable, posing a challenge to trustworthiness. The field of Neuro-Symbolic AI aims to bridge data-driven neural networks with structured symbolic systems to create more robust and interpretable models. A key focus is knowledge representation. While prior work has integrated symbolic methods by altering the internal architecture of neural networks, this research explores applying structured representations to pre-trained LLMs through prompting and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, without requiring architectural changes.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of tasks, yet their internal decision-making processes remain largely opaque, posing a significant challenge to their trustworthiness in complex reasoning scenarios. We explore the hypothesis that compelling a pre-trained LLM to maintain a structured internal state using a formal, symbolic representation can enhance interpretability without degrading its reasoning performance. To investigate this, we employ two primary techniques: prompt fine-tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) using LoRA. Thus, we prompt a variety of LLMs to articulate their reasoning steps using various structured formalisms, including basic semantic triples, lists of attribute-value pairs, and first-order logic. We also fine-tune a pre-trained LLM on a structured representation that the LLM is subsequently prompted to use as an internal representation during reasoning to solve a task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of prompting LLMs to use various formal, structured representations for their reasoning steps on their core reasoning capabilities.
experiments.Method: Pre-trained LLMs (Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and gpt-oss-20b) are prompted using zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT) techniques. The prompts instruct the models to utilize different formalisms: First-Order Logic (FOL), Propositional Logic (Prop), semantic triples (SPO), and alists. The models' performance is then evaluated on reasoning benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the GSM8K and MMLU benchmarks.', 'Adherence score, measuring the percentage of model outputs that contain the prompted representation.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate if fine-tuning an LLM on a specific structured representation (alists) improves its ability to use that structure and affects its reasoning performance.
experiments.Method: The Qwen3-4B model is fine-tuned using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with LoRA on a custom-generated dataset (ALIST8.5K) of natural language and alist pairs. The resulting fine-tuned models (Qwen3-4B-ALIST-16 and Qwen3-4B-ALIST-32) are then evaluated on reasoning benchmarks, both with and without being explicitly prompted to use the alist format.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the GSM8K and MMLU benchmarks.', "Adherence to the alist representation in the model's output."]

risk_factors: Our results and evaluation are restricted by our use of only two reasoning benchmarks.
A key challenge we came up against in our research was the lack of verification of models' outputs.
Another limitation of our work is the dataset we've used for fine-tuning. The quality of our dataset is entirely dependent on the capabilities of our chosen model – in this case OpenAI's o4-mini.
In this work, we only explore prompt fine-tuning and LoRA-based finetuning.

title: Symbolic Learning Enables Self-Evolving Agents
name: agent-symbolic-learning
short_hypothesis: By treating a language agent as a symbolic network and applying principles analogous to backpropagation and gradient descent using language-based losses and gradients, it is possible to jointly and holistically optimize all of its symbolic components—prompts, tools, and workflow—in a data-centric manner. This holistic optimization will be more effective than methods that optimize individual components in isolation.
related_work: Current language agent development is 'engineering-centric', requiring extensive manual effort to design and stack prompts and tools into workflows. While some automated optimization methods exist (e.g., DSpy, AgentOptimizer), they typically optimize components like prompts or tools in isolation. This can lead to local optima and compromised performance for the entire agent system. The research gap is the lack of a framework that can jointly and holistically optimize all symbolic components of an agent system in a data-centric way, similar to how neural networks are trained end-to-end.
abstract: A fundamental limitation of current language agents research is that they are model-centric, or engineering-centric, requiring substantial manual engineering efforts from human experts rather than automatically learning from data. We believe the transition from engineering-centric to data-centric, i.e., the ability of language agents to autonomously learn and evolve in environments, is the key for them to possibly achieve AGI. In this work, we introduce agent symbolic learning, a systematic framework that enables language agents to optimize themselves on their own in a data-centric way using symbolic optimizers. Specifically, we consider agents as symbolic networks where learnable weights are defined by prompts, tools, and the way they are stacked together. Agent symbolic learning is designed to optimize the symbolic network within language agents by mimicking two fundamental algorithms in connectionist learning: back-propagation and gradient descent. Instead of dealing with numeric weights, agent symbolic learning works with text-based weights, loss, and gradients.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the agent symbolic learning framework on standard LLM benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be evaluated on the HotpotQA ('hard' split), MATH, and HumanEval benchmarks. The models used will be GPT-3.5 and GPT-4. Tools will be disabled for these experiments to ensure a meaningful comparison with existing literature. The performance will be compared against several baselines: GPTs, Agents, Agents w/ AutoPE, and DSPy.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['F1 and exact match for HotPotQA', 'Accuracy for MATH', 'Pass@1 for HumanEval']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework's ability to optimize an agent for the complex, open-ended task of creative writing.
experiments.Method: The task requires the agent to write a coherent 4-paragraph passage that ends with 4 given random sentences. The proposed method will be evaluated using both GPT-3.5 and GPT-4. Baselines for comparison will include GPTs, Agents, Agents w/ AutoPE, and Tree-of-Thought (ToT).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['GPT-4 score to evaluate the generated passages.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework's ability to optimize an agent for the complex task of software development from a product requirement document (PRD).
experiments.Method: The task requires the agent to develop executable software for several games (Flappy bird, Tank battle game, 2048 game, Snake game, Brick breaker game). The proposed method will be compared against GPTs and Agents baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A numerical score from 1 to 4 quantifying the executability and correctness of the generated software, where 1 is execution failure, 2 is successful execution, 3 is conformance to workflow, and 4 is flawless alignment with expectations.']

risk_factors: The scope of experiments in this paper is not super large enough to cover most agentic tasks in the real world. They are rather proof-of-concept experiments showcasing the effectiveness of the proposed method.
Another limitation is that the experiments are done with text-only models and tasks, while experiments with multi-modal agents and tasks would be very interesting.
As for the boarder impact, we would like to point out that enabling language agents to self-evolve in the wild poses certain safety risks.

title: Taking a Step Back with KCal: Multi-Class Kernel-Based Calibration for Deep Neural Networks
name: KCal
short_hypothesis: A new Kernel-based calibration method, KCal, which operates on the penultimate-layer latent embeddings of a DNN rather than its logits or softmax outputs, can produce fully calibrated multi-class probabilities. By learning a metric space and using kernel density estimates, this method is proposed to achieve better calibration with theoretical guarantees, while maintaining or improving classification accuracy.
related_work: Existing deep neural network calibration methods often fall into categories like scaling (e.g., Temperature Scaling), binning (e.g., Histogram Binning), or loss regularization. However, these methods often lack theoretical guarantees for full calibration, may reduce classification accuracy, or only address confidence calibration (calibrating the probability of the predicted class) rather than full multi-class calibration. While kernels have been used for uncertainty quantification or as regularizers, they have not been proposed for directly calibrating predictions via a trained kernel density estimator on latent embeddings.
abstract: Deep neural network (DNN) classifiers are often overconfident, producing miscalibrated class probabilities. In high-risk applications like healthcare, practitioners require fully calibrated probability predictions for decision-making. That is, conditioned on the prediction vector, every class’ probability should be close to the predicted value. Most existing calibration methods either lack theoretical guarantees for producing calibrated outputs, reduce classification accuracy in the process, or only calibrate the predicted class. This paper proposes a new Kernel-based calibration method called KCal. Unlike existing calibration procedures, KCal does not operate directly on the logits or softmax outputs of the DNN. Instead, KCal learns a metric space on the penultimate-layer latent embedding and generates predictions using kernel density estimates on a calibration set. We first analyze KCal theoretically, showing that it enjoys a provable full calibration guarantee.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the calibration performance of KCal against state-of-the-art methods on standard computer vision benchmark datasets.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use multiple image benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and ImageNet. Pretrained models such as ViT, MLP-Mixer, and Inception ResNet V2 will be used as the base networks. KCal will be compared against a suite of baseline methods including Uncalibrated (UnCal), Temperature Scaling (TS), Dirichlet Calibration (DirCal), Mutual-information-maximization-based Binning (I-Max), Gaussian Process Calibration (GP), Intra Order-preserving Calibration (IOP), Splines-based Calibration (Spline), Focal-loss-based calibration (Focal), and MMCE-based calibration (MMCE).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy', 'Class-wise Expected Calibration Error (CECE)', 'Expected Calibration Error (ECE)', 'Brier Score']

experiments.Description: To evaluate KCal on health monitoring datasets where full calibration is crucial for diagnostic applications, including settings with patient-wise data splits.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use three health monitoring datasets: IIIC (ictal-interictal-injury-continuum classification), ISRUC (sleep staging classification), and PN2017 (ECG rhythm classification). For IIIC and ISRUC, both random splits and patient-wise splits ('IIIC (pat)', 'ISRUC (pat)') will be evaluated. A CNN (ResNet) will be trained for IIIC and ISRUC, and a top-performing model from the 2017 PhysioNet Challenge (MINA) will be used for PN2017. KCal will be compared against the same set of baselines as the computer vision experiments: UnCal, TS, DirCal, I-Max, GP, IOP, Spline, Focal, and MMCE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy', 'Class-wise Expected Calibration Error (CECE)', 'Expected Calibration Error (ECE)', 'Brier Score']

risk_factors: The major limitation of KCal is the need to store the entire calibration set, which is a small overhead with the dimension reduction step and potential improvements.
